## Thread: Is there a way to concatenate strings? — 69 this month / 86 total

_Thread started [2001-03](2001-03.md#t-f60fc53a87)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T08:40:19+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC65CD3.ED87ED40@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <E2nx6.2544$bY4.7822@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Open close is particularly expensive on MVS (z/OS). I once did a
benchmark of Unix vs MVS and the Unix pgm ran in less time than
it took MVS to open the input file. 

But it is always relatively expensive compare to read/write and
nowmal processing.

Tim Josling

John H Sleight Jr wrote:
> In article <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl>, Judson McClendon says...
> >
> >Opening and closing files rank among the most expensive I/O operations
> >you can do, except on groups of records.
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T17:26:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UWNx6.171$al2.15170@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <E2nx6.2544$bY4.7822@www.newsranger.com> <3AC65CD3.ED87ED40@melbpc.org.au> <QQtx6.89800$Xt3.12317513@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
"Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com> wrote:
> I'm not sure that open & close operations are particularly important,
> when considering performance, unless you have a very high volume
> of files and a very small number of records in each.

The discussion was about a program that used OPEN/CLOSE in a loop.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T23:44:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u3Px6.94739$Xt3.12846729@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <E2nx6.2544$bY4.7822@www.newsranger.com> <3AC65CD3.ED87ED40@melbpc.org.au> <QQtx6.89800$Xt3.12317513@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <UWNx6.171$al2.15170@news2.atl>`

```
I generally don't spend too much time on individual programs,
I design application architecture.

While the total time - per operation -  may be greater for open
& close,  they shouldn't feature greatly in the overall scheme of
an application. I wouldn't want my staff wasting time on opens &
closes. BUT - if the app. included a high number of files, I would
investigate the data organisation, to amalgamate related data sources.

It's like saying that your car travels slowly, when reversing out of the
garage - true, but unimportant - unless you are driving 100 yards
down the road.


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:UWNx6.171$al2.15170@news2.atl...
> "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com> wrote:
> > I'm not sure that open & close operations are particularly important,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

```
Hi Rufio,

The pgm I was working on, as originally designed, did 80,000 open/closes for the
20,000 accts it processed. That was Judson's point; at that level of use the
overhead is consierable, to say the least.

Regards, Jack.   


In article <u3Px6.94739$Xt3.12846729@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>, Rufio says...
>
>I generally don't spend too much time on individual programs,
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…

```

- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <E2nx6.2544$bY4.7822@www.newsranger.com> <3AC65CD3.ED87ED40@melbpc.org.au> <QQtx6.89800$Xt3.12317513@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <UWNx6.171$al2.15170@news2.atl> <u3Px6.94739$Xt3.12846729@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <UYPx6.3392$bY4.8201@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Wow - interesting data design - wonder if they've got any job openings!

I'll certainly agree that doing that volume of open/close operations will
take time. In the past few years I've supported perhaps 15-20 applications
and they probably only total a few hunder or perhaps a thousand file
open/closes. So it never occurred to me to find something of this nature.

Not sure I'd admit to any sort of involvement with something like this
though.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T05:38:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <E2nx6.2544$bY4.7822@www.newsranger.com> <3AC65CD3.ED87ED40@melbpc.org.au> <QQtx6.89800$Xt3.12317513@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <UWNx6.171$al2.15170@news2.atl> <u3Px6.94739$Xt3.12846729@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <E0Sy6.520829$Pm2.8389449@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Well thanks Donald - I thought I'd been coding for the last couple
of decades, but actually I was knitting a mohair tank-top.

At the Appllication Design level, I determine the  nature of the data
capture process, to create a combination of files that can be most
efficiently processed, to provide the desired result. This is how I have
been able to increased efficiency, making applications run 20-50 times
as fast as before, wilst still providing the same functionality, with
improved accessibility and robustness.What part of this is bad?

As a critical test - I support my own designes, and I don't have too
many sleepless nights.

My entry in this thread came about because I was not convinced that
a single program reading several thousand different files was a good idea.

I defy you to prove that a well designed program can read thousands of
files of DIS-SIMILAR DATA. If the data is simlar and/or related - it
can be merged into a single input, or a reduced number of inputs.

Not only is streamlining of similar data good for the current design, it
also
reduces the effort involved in subsequent development, when new data
is added.

P.S. the reason I'm writing this at 10:30pm, rather than being fast asleep
in bed,
is that the programmers who wrote the upstream application didn't follow my
advice. Their application is now puking it's guts up; and my app will run in
90
minutes, when the file is created upstream..


"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:E0Sy6.520829$Pm2.8389449@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Perhaps you should write some code, then perhaps you would understand the
> problem.  At the "application architecture" level, do you think deciding
on
> file level lockout vs record leval lockout might be affected by open/close
> time, or don't you get involved at that level of detail?
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > I'm not sure that open & close operations are particularly
important,
> > > > when considering performance, unless you have a very high volume
> > > > of files and a very small number of records in each.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

"Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com> wrote in message
news:QxTy6.105764$Xt3.14640982@news1.rdc1.az.home.com...
> Well thanks Donald - I thought I'd been coding for the last couple
> of decades, but actually I was knitting a mohair tank-top.
>
Interesting.  In your last post, you said:

"I generally don't spend too much time on individual programs,  I design
application architecture."

If you do not write programs, then just what have you been coding "for the
last couple of decades"?  This is a programming NG, and the discussion was
about the ratio of time for opening and closing files, vs the time for
reading the files.  Your statements showed a complete lack of knowledge of
the ramifications of that ratio at both the programming level and at the
architecture level.  It is, in fact, at the architectural level that the
biggest effects take place.

Quite aside from that, it is impossible to design well at the architectuaral
level without any decisions about how it is to be coded.  Not that Cobol
shops lack for PHMs making marketing points while spouting  goblelty-gook.

> At the Appllication Design level, I determine the  nature of the data
> capture process, to create a combination of files that can be most
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The fact that it is close to meaningless. Every design of a 500,000 line
program for the last thirty years could start with the same line. Every one
of them does something different. It is typical advertising crap, not
anything meaningfull.  It certainly does not have anything to do with the
ration of open/close times to the ratio of read times, an any platform, or
in any language.

> As a critical test - I support my own designes, and I don't have too
> many sleepless nights.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> can be merged into a single input, or a reduced number of inputs.

Can it really?  Perhaps if the application is a batch run it could.
Realtime apps do not have the luxury of waiting for the files to become
available, get collected into a batch, then get merged into a single large
file.  Besides, there is no such thing as thousands of files of dis-similar
data, in any application.  At the very least, it is all in binary. Any web
browser can read *millions* of binary files, scattered all over the world,
each having very little to do with the other.  If it is going to be merged
into a single input at one level, then it *has* to be read by the merging
program somewhere else, or do you do that by hand?  For someone that claims
to be a better system architect than programmer, you show an amazing lack of
knowledge about how programs fit together to make systems.

>
> Not only is streamlining of similar data good for the current design, it
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> is that the programmers who wrote the upstream application didn't follow
my
> advice. Their application is now puking it's guts up; and my app will run
in
> 90
> minutes, when the file is created upstream..

What happened?  Did they not merge it into 80 column card images for you?
BTW, I thought you did not get involved with that, that you just made sure
the upstream guys and the downstream guys all talked the same language.

>
>
> "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
> news:E0Sy6.520829$Pm2.8389449@news20.bellglobal.com...
> > Perhaps you should write some code, then perhaps you would understand
the

> on

open/close
> > time, or don't you get involved at that level of detail?

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

the
> > > garage - true, but unimportant - unless you are driving 100 yards
> > > down the road.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting
life."
> > > >
> > > >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

- **Date:** 2001-04-06T01:50:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B6F2994F.656%jeffbaynard@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <E2nx6.2544$bY4.7822@www.newsranger.com> <3AC65CD3.ED87ED40@melbpc.org.au> <QQtx6.89800$Xt3.12317513@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <UWNx6.171$al2.15170@news2.atl> <u3Px6.94739$Xt3.12846729@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <E0Sy6.520829$Pm2.8389449@news20.bellglobal.com> <QxTy6.105764$Xt3.14640982@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <T_1z6.520927$f36.15879875@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
in article T_1z6.520927$f36.15879875@news20.bellglobal.com, Donald Tees at
donald_tees@sympatico.ca wrote on 4/5/01 1:32 PM:

> For someone that claims
> to be a better system architect than programmer, you show an amazing lack of
> knowledge about how programs fit together to make systems.

> it is impossible to design well at the architectuaral
> level without any decisions about how it is to be coded.

Donald, you are preaching to the choir on this one.

In my circle of (fairly geeky) friends, we have a joke that systems
architects (or business system analysts as our company calls them) are
programmers who don't have much ability to write code.

Our company expects the IT application group to work as 2 teams.  The
'systems analysts' figure out what needs to be done, how it is to be done,
and writes the 'specifications' for said work.

But like you said, how can anyone who doesn't know the structure of good
code actually suggest a good design?  HELLO!!!! Of course they can't.  Some
of our 'analysts' actually think that running an FTP job or using MS Access
is programming.  Yeah, and I'm Elvis Fuggin Presley.

Now let's take that one step further.... take said environment (workplace)
above and make the developers solely oncall for the production code.  All of
it.  Analysts do not have to carry a pager.  Normally it may not be a big
deal, but my company does $4 billion in sales yearly.  If I fuck up bad
enough, it's a 'blue' slip for me.  Being in Point of Sale is no help.

Anyways, my question is, if the 'analysts' job is to design the system, then
why should I be solely on call for it?  There are certainly bugs caused by
poor design.  And if I'm working with someone who sucks (which happens too
often round here)... then I'm screwed.

All that with no increase or changes in pay.  I think it sucks and it's
really pissing me off.

Jeff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T12:31:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Hiz6.522810$f36.15965021@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <E2nx6.2544$bY4.7822@www.newsranger.com> <3AC65CD3.ED87ED40@melbpc.org.au> <QQtx6.89800$Xt3.12317513@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <UWNx6.171$al2.15170@news2.atl> <u3Px6.94739$Xt3.12846729@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <E0Sy6.520829$Pm2.8389449@news20.bellglobal.com> <QxTy6.105764$Xt3.14640982@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <T_1z6.520927$f36.15879875@news20.bellglobal.com> <B6F2994F.656%jeffbaynard@home.com>`

```
One of the biggest problems in IT is, and always has been, what do you do
with people that cannot code their way out of a paper bag.  You sound like
you have found the biggest single use for such people ... get a really large
comany, and make them all "managers/analysts/systems architects/system
engineers/etc/etc".  There is s solution, however.  Once a company gets so
bloated that they cannot function anymore, then they hire "consultants" to
make it all work.

Now what you have to remember, is that in order to justify the consultant,
the managers have to point out that the consultant is a *HUGELY* qualified
person, rather than just an amazingly well qualified person like themselves.
That means *REALLY BIG BUCKS*.  The bigger the bucks, the better the
qualifications, obviously.

So the trick is to become a consultant.  This will allow you to do
absolutely nothing for several years, while you watch how various big
companies completely fuck up IT departments.  It is sort of like school,
except you get paid really well.

Unfortunately, it becomes boring after a while.  I mean, like, after you've
realized that most of them average a line of code every three days, and you
have completed so much work in advance that you are well into their Y3K
problem, you will realize that there is more to life, and get a job as a
just a programmer.

At that point, having seen it all, you will be able to make smart-assed
remarks on C.L.C., and have Bill correct you.  You will be able to leap tall
buildings with a single line of code, and enjoy writing it.  If you are
really lucky, you can start your own company, and sell what you write.

Donald

in run time from 10 mins to 3+ hrs!

Regards, Jack.

AFAIK the same kind of overhead is present on PC based systems using 
the Merant NetExpress ISAM files. We ran into this with an application
where 20 file open/close actions increased the run time of a 
particular routine from 1.5 seconds to 43 seconds. Changing the 
routine to leave the files open left us with the 1.5 second runtime.

Lorne

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T23:18:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7HOx6.507965$Pm2.8049776@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <E2nx6.2544$bY4.7822@www.newsranger.com> <3AC65CD3.ED87ED40@melbpc.org.au> <VeKx6.3188$bY4.8197@www.newsranger.com>`

```
I tend to benchmark that as a first thing on Cobol platforms, as it is
important to know when designing file systems.  I have quite low
transactions rates (in the dozens per second, tops), so can get away with
file level lock-out for I-O. That approach, while extremely solid, makes
opn/close times quite crucial.

Mind you, I would always bury it in an OOP object so that I could invisibly
change it later.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

- **From:** Keith Odam <0dam-k@iname.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T07:26:07+09:30
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACB9877.3B60A1B7@iname.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> "John H Sleight Jr" <John_member@newsranger.com> wrote:
> >I mentioned to the PM that wrote the specs that opens/closes
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >could be used to reduce the opens/closes.
[ ... ]
> >the job ran in 10 MIN!!!!
> Opening and closing files rank among the most expensive I/O
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to READ/WRITE/READ/WRITE).
[ ... ]
> My son recently wrote a strikingly similar program.  Though he
> has only been programming professionally for less than three
> years, he spotted the potential performance hit immediately and
> coded it using a table the first time.

  When discussing with my son I have contrasted the C/UNIX
expectation of infinite amounts of memory with simply using files.
It seems to me simpler from the outset to use an indexed sequential
file and need no alternative handling for when the available
entries exceed the size of a table in memory.  Of course, buffer
management permits the number of IS records held in memory (and
not physically written to file) to be adjusted for performance.
  A temporary IS file needs to be initialised before it can be
used for IO, but then the file need not be closed and opened
again between each use. After all, you don't destroy and recreate
a table when coding for the memory-bound alternative, do you?
  Indexed sequential was part of the 1974 standard and widely
implemented in versions of COBOL-68, but perhaps I have been
spoiled by my fortunate environment.
```

"Keith Odam" <0dam-k@iname.com> wrote:
>
>   When discussing with my son I have contrasted the C/UNIX
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> spoiled by my fortunate environment.

Wow!  You believe that substituting the overhead of adding/deleting
hundreds of thousands of random records in an indexed file (along
with the index entries) will improve the speed here? :-)

[*mount soapbox*]

I'm not blasting Keith, his post was just the last straw that pushed
me over the edge in this thread.  You can decide for yourself if any
of the following applies to you. :-)

I am appalled at the lack of understanding vis-a-vis efficiency shown
by some of the posts in this thread.  I realize that most programmers
today don't have to fret over cycles and bits the way we old timers
had to do.  But that is no excuse for having only the vaguest (if any)
idea of the costs of various approaches one might take to solve a
problem!

Listen people, anyone who has programmed more than a year *ought* to
know the following:

1. Processing an entire file using random I/O is *MUCH* slower than
   using sequential I/O, simply because the I/O subsystem anticipates
   sequential reads and attempts to pre-fill the file's input buffers.
   Reading a sequential file from beginning to end is *many times*
   faster than doing the same to a random file.

2. Creating/deleting records in an indexed file structure is *very*
   expensive, relative to reading/writing a sequential record,
   because the index tables must be searched and updated for each
   add or delete, in addition to doing the actual add/delete, as well
   as other internal I/O.  And the index tables, as well as the data
   file, are all random access.

3. File open and close operation are *expensive*, for several reasons.
   An open requires at least a partial directory search, which can
   involve many directory I/O's (and remember, the directory is a
   shared resource with heavy activity) which may cause operating
   system overlays as well.  An open output also requires creation of
   a new directory entry, which could be several I/O's.  Both open and
   close operations require activity in whatever area is used to track
   use of shared resources, which can cause additional I/O's.

4. Memory access is millions of times faster than disk I/O.

5. Operating systems calls are (at best) hundreds of times slower
   than in-memory processes.

If you doubt the above statements, simply write a few test programs
and you will see for yourself.  It boggles my mind that there are
experienced programmers out there who have never bothered to find out
how expensive various operations are.  In my 32 years programming, I
have written literally thousands of programs for the sole purpose of testing just such things.  If you want to always be only a 'get
by'
programmer all your life, and always have a boss looking down your
neck, that's okay.  But if you want to earn well into 6 figures every
year, and be your own boss if you want, like some of us around here,
guessing is *not* good enough.  You have to *KNOW*.  And that means
getting your head out and learning how and why and what is *really*
going on inside that box.  If you aren't willing to do that, don't
ever complain about how bloated and slow software is getting to be
these days, because that's how it gets that way. :-)

[*dismount soapbox*]

Whew, that makes me feel better. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T17:52:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kh2z6.520971$f36.15880676@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <3ACB9877.3B60A1B7@iname.com> <VU0z6.94$be.8301@news1.atl>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:VU0z6.94$be.8301@news1.atl...
> "Keith Odam" <0dam-k@iname.com> wrote:
> >
…[66 more quoted lines elided]…
> have written literally thousands of programs for the sole purpose of
testing just such things.  If you want to always be only a 'get
> by'
> programmer all your life, and always have a boss looking down your
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> --


Well said.  Perhaps I am just getting grumpy in my old age, but I have less
and less patience with people that claim to be programmers so good that they
do not write programs anymore, they just design them and give them to a
lacky to program.  99% of the time, the designs are on par with what I
scribble on beermats in a bar when drunk.

The only thing more annoying are the people that want you to spend two years
coding up their latest brainstorm (free of course) because they *just know*
that their idea will *make us both rich*. Invariably it is something
wonderfully innovative, like putting porn on the internet.

A program *is* a specification.  A specification on how to handle data that
is detailed well enough that even dumb machine can follow it. Anything less
is not designed, but simply roughed out.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T14:05:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3acd24cf_5@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <3ACB9877.3B60A1B7@iname.com> <VU0z6.94$be.8301@news1.atl> <Kh2z6.520971$f36.15880676@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

Donald Tees wrote in message ...

In response to an excellent post by Judson...
>
>Well said.  Perhaps I am just getting grumpy in my old age, but I have less
>and less patience with people that claim to be programmers so good that
they
>do not write programs anymore, they just design them and give them to a
>lacky to program.  99% of the time, the designs are on par with what I
>scribble on beermats in a bar when drunk.

Jeez Don, I do my BEST work on barmats... keep them all over the house!

I wonder if anyone ever picks up the mats when you've finished in the bar
and sends them off to be programmed...?<G>

Maybe that's how they got the idea for Windows..?<G>

>
>The only thing more annoying are the people that want you to spend two
years
>coding up their latest brainstorm (free of course) because they *just know*
>that their idea will *make us both rich*. Invariably it is something
>wonderfully innovative, like putting porn on the internet.
>
ROFL!

I get an average of 2 of these a month and TOTALLY relate to what you are
saying...

The BEST one was for an on-line duty free shop at a new PLANNED
international airport here in our little Sleepy Hollow. I said "OK" on time
and materials (at what we call "Mate's Rates" <a heavily reduced fee as a
favour to someone you know, often reciprocated by the other party>)

As I am technically on holiday I didn't feel it was fair to charge the full
rates and, besides, he couldn't have afforded it.  The person concerned was
really miffed that I wouldn't GRAB this opportunity to "make a killing" and
should do all the work for nothing (After all, he had provided the bright
idea, right?).

While I enjoy my work, I haven't quite got to the point of doing OTHER
PEOPLE's projects for nothing (although I have done a few of my own for
nothing; it wasn't intended, it just worked out that way...<G>). He declined
and said he'd find a student who would be glad to do it. I smiled and
sincerely wished him well.

Four months later they were still arguing about what should be on it, and
the student was protesting that it wasn't possible to do what was being
asked. Five months later the Airport failed planning and was shelved...

We are still friends, but never discuss it and I don't think he is likely to
ask me to do anything more...

>A program *is* a specification.  A specification on how to handle data that
>is detailed well enough that even dumb machine can follow it. Anything less
>is not designed, but simply roughed out.
>
Absolutely!

Pete.

http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <3ACB9877.3B60A1B7@iname.com> <VU0z6.94$be.8301@news1.atl> <Kh2z6.520971$f36.15880676@news20.bellglobal.com> <3acd24cf_5@127.0.0.1>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Donald Tees wrote in message ...
>
> In response to an excellent post by Judson...

OK you guys, Judson, Don and Pete - good points. However when you were all three
young (phew!), we can take it Don and Pete definitely got drunk - don't know
about you Judson - unless you got at the altar wine <G>

And then there's Jeff Baynard - wouldn't be an exaggeration to say he is more
than slightly pissed off would it ?

But you first three - you've made the leap - you do your own thing - which
necessitated you embryoing into an Anlyst/Programmer. Meanwhile there are snake
oil salesmen offering themselves as 'consultants' - I personally use every word
in Roget's Thesaurus before I'd admit to that one !

Each in your own way, in your 'private' capacity, you've headed up a project. I
referred to this topic in another thread, but nobody took me up on it. Like it
or not, in a big organization there have to be middlemen, between the end user
and the programming team, (use the dirty word 'systems analyst' - I know Don
hates it with a passion).

Now the degree to which those middlemen operate is debatable. Today, some,
probably with a foot in each camp, do a good job. But just as a programmer can
get frustrated, so too can they if the end users are absolutely f...........
assholes - and this is not a category in which there is a short supply !

The other trend is of course, turn a programmer into an analyst and problem
solved - as Gershwin's song goes, "It aint necessarily so".  I've had people in
that category - do an excellent job at programming, but come out zip on the
'in-between', God forbid I use the phrase, but they just 'aren't smooth enough',
or have the vision to look forward, beyond the current dumb question the user is
asking.

Here's one, where I should have blown the whistle. I got out of weekly trips
from Taunton up to Essex to deal with our new subsidiary Caters supermarkets.
One of my systems 'lieutenants' went instead. As an analyst, being tuned into
vibes he kept coming back to me and saying, "It aint gonna happen. It aint gonna
happen". No proof, no facts, just verbal observation stuff - but I understood
him.  I had nothing to forward, but I should have written to both the Chairman
of the Board and the CEO, bypassing EVERYBODY - and strongly made that point.
The proverbial would have hit the fan - they may well have given me the boot, or
possibly thought, maybe this guy is sincere, let's investigate. I didn't do that
- the result - the total company had a FRIGGIN DISASTER with a threat from the
auditors to qualify the accounts if it wasn't cleaned up by the following year.
Who took the blame as scapegoat - my boss the DPM !

Now put an unseasoned programmer turned analyst into that little 'playette' -
guess how he would come out.

Don't want to go on - BUT - P.E.C.D. - DO NOT <REPEAT> NOT - misread me. I still
hate 'CON-sultants' with a passion !

Jimmy
```

understood
> him.  I had nothing to forward, but I should have written to both the
Chairman
> of the Board and the CEO, bypassing EVERYBODY - and strongly made that
point.
> The proverbial would have hit the fan - they may well have given me the
boot, or
> possibly thought, maybe this guy is sincere, let's investigate. I didn't
do that
> - the result - the total company had a FRIGGIN DISASTER with a threat from
the
> auditors to qualify the accounts if it wasn't cleaned up by the following
year.
> Who took the blame as scapegoat - my boss the DPM !
>
> Now put an unseasoned programmer turned analyst into that little
'playette' -
> guess how he would come out.
>
> Don't want to go on - BUT - P.E.C.D. - DO NOT <REPEAT> NOT - misread me. I
still
> hate 'CON-sultants' with a passion !
>


The problem of how to "manage" a programming project/team is a huge one.  I
personally think that the only good solution is to have the best programmer
lead the team. I definitely use juniours to proof read, and check for bugs.
In fact, I currently have my installer, who is not a programmer, working on
help screens for the system I am working on. He is also looking for stuff
like tabbing sequences on the screens that do not make sense, and
awkwardness in flow.

One thing that few shops do, and I am a firm believer in, is that the *head*
of the team (me, in this case), actually go out in the field and work with a
user for a few weeks.  I am always astonished at how a few tweaks here and
there can cut user annoyances in half.  Those changes are seldom major
(though they might be).  Often, it is a simple matter of changing a few
sequences here and there, and adding a few new routines that make life
easier.

I make my living by selling what I write.  That means that people have to
buy it, or I starve.  Sitting with the end user and trying to make the
system do all the work for me provides most of the feedback for further
changes.  I completely agree with Jeff regarding the "help desk".  Answering
the same question three days in a row at 6 in the morning should result in a
change.  If the people responsing for setting change priorities are not
involved in the problem solving, then the help desk stays as busy as ever.
```

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <3ACD3265.89C881C4@home.com>...
>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>OK you guys, Judson, Don and Pete - good points. However when you were all
three
>young (phew!), we can take it Don and Pete definitely got drunk - don't
know
>about you Judson - unless you got at the altar wine <G>
>

I've been drunk ever shinsh...<hic>

Have you ever been SO drunk you kissed a woman on the navel?

I've been drunker....

>And then there's Jeff Baynard - wouldn't be an exaggeration to say he is
more
>than slightly pissed off would it ?
>

No, I read his post.

"You are responsible for the condition you are in".
(This is a sign they used to have behind the Grill at "Fat Jacks", my
favourite hamburger place on the entire Planet, Ventura Boulevarde at Studio
City, don't miss it...) While you can argue this is hamburger philosophy, I
reckon it goes much deeper....

Jeff, my advice is...

"Deal with it. Talk to your Manager. Have a free and frank discussion. If
you get no satisfaction, start looking for another job or register with an
Agency and go freelance.
(Check out the excellent article about contracting in the USA on
COBOLReport...<no, I DIDN'T write it, I said it was "excellent"  <G>> it is
absorbing reading. Made me think, and I have been freelancing for 26 years.)

Don't stay in a job if you are unhappy there. Either change the situation or
move on."


>>>
>But you first three - you've made the leap - you do your own thing - which
>necessitated you embryoing into an Anlyst/Programmer. Meanwhile there are
snake
>oil salesmen offering themselves as 'consultants' - I personally use every
word
>in Roget's Thesaurus before I'd admit to that one !
>
<<<
If you are giving people advice, and they pay you for it, they are
consulting you. There is good and bad advice and there are good and bad
consultants. The label doesn't change the suitcase.

Having said that, it makes me smile when I hear people who do trivial things
like install OFFICE call themselves "systems consultants" ... but then I
once heard a seven year old telling her friend she had "programmed" her
computer by getting Windows to change the background. When I thought about
it, I realised she was right.  I can't see any point in getting too incensed
about these labels.

I guess if you ask someone for advice on installing OFFICE, the person has a
right to call themselves a consultant.


>Each in your own way, in your 'private' capacity, you've headed up a
project. I
>referred to this topic in another thread, but nobody took me up on it. Like
it
>or not, in a big organization there have to be middlemen, between the end
user
>and the programming team, (use the dirty word 'systems analyst' - I know
Don
>hates it with a passion).
>
These are what I would call "Applications Analysts" or as they are sometimes
referred to, "Business Systems Analysts". I believe the term"Systems
Analyst" should be reserved for the poor souls who actually do this job and
analyse system performance and software.

I didn't realise Don was so passionate, Jimmy. <G>

Do you have emotional attachments to other words, Don <G>?

Jimmy is absolutely correct, that, if you are operating in a Waterfall
environment, there will be a requirement for "Middlemen" and that means that
things get lost in "translation" between Business and Technical (this is one
of the big shortcomings with the Waterfall).

In RAD environments where teams are composed of both Business and IT people
there is no need for such a Middle layer. Joint Workshops prototype
functionality and if the Business don't like it, it is changed on the spot.
I noticed Don touched on this when he emphasised the importance of going
onto user sites and working with them during project development. It is
exactly the same idea we use in Corporations with RAD projects, and I'm a
believer.  It's too big a subject for this discussion (I'm working on a book
on it, but it may take longer than the new COBOL standard <G>), but the
bottom line is that smarter software and Methodologies can eradicate the
"System Architect" role altogether <sighs of relief all round <G>> If anyone
reading this wants more information, mail me and I'll send you a paper about
it.

I wouldn't confuse Project Management with "Systems Analysis", Jimmy. The
skill sets are totally different. When "Systems Analysts" are allowed to
"head up a project" the results are usually disastrous. (Mind you, they can
be with a bad Project Manager, too...)

>>>
>Now the degree to which those middlemen operate is debatable. Today, some,
>probably with a foot in each camp, do a good job. But just as a programmer
can
>get frustrated, so too can they if the end users are absolutely
f...........
>assholes - and this is not a category in which there is a short supply !
>
<<<
If you think of your end users in these terms you probably won't be
disappointed.

I see my end users as customers. Sometimes they are difficult, but the
Business won't exist without them, and sometimes I am difficult
too...<Shock! Horror! gasps of "Never!">

>>>
>
>Don't want to go on - BUT - P.E.C.D. - DO NOT <REPEAT> NOT - misread me. I
still
>hate 'CON-sultants' with a passion !
>

You have made your distaste abundantly clear. Although I'm officially
designated as a "consultant", I certainly don't emphasise the "CON", and I'm
not in the least bit offended.

I see myself as more of an "Insultant", actually...<G>. Sometimes, abuse can
be a great motivator...

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T18:29:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jWnz6.503$BX5.188118@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <3ACB9877.3B60A1B7@iname.com> <VU0z6.94$be.8301@news1.atl> <Kh2z6.520971$f36.15880676@news20.bellglobal.com> <3acd24cf_5@127.0.0.1>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> wrote in message
news:3acd24cf_5@127.0.0.1...
>
> Jeez Don, I do my BEST work on barmats... keep them all over the house!
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Maybe that's how they got the idea for Windows..?<G>

Not that I am necessarily a Microsoft-lover, but I have been impressed with
the both design and performance of Windows since my first Windows system
(WFWG, aka Win 3.11, for me, 1996). (Ok, so MS has had some stability
problems. But hell, I have had some stability problems myself!).

When I first started looking to write Windows programs about two years ago,
I was impressed with the API - the completeness, the documentation, the
error-handling, the system-level controls, the whole schmear.

Granted, for the sequential mindset (most non-Windows programmers),
migrating to the Windows message ("event") paradigm is a hard thing to do:
It just don't make sense sometimes that your function calls itself
recursively without your knowledge before it completes itself. (Just
re-reading that that sentence is weird enough!).

But once I got the "feel" of working in the message-driven and reentrant
environment, programming became programming - that is, you still have a job
to do, and there's lots of tools with which to do it. The major tool: the
increased primary storage (memory) under Win/32: that lets you imagine all
kinds of stuff you'd dismiss out-of-hand in another environment.

For the first time FOR REAL, the programmer can concentrate on the
application and not the system interface, thanks  to the comprehensive and
open application services provided by the Windows API.

My $0.02.

MCM
```

Agree totally, Michael. I actually LOVE Windows... It is great fun to
develop applications for.

Doesn't alter the fact that Users don't necessarily see it that way...

On reflection, I could've used a better target than Windows for my comment
(if I'd said "Unix" I would've been lynched and Richard Plinston is not so
far away from where I'm writing this that I could escape his wrath...<G>),
but it was said with tongue in cheek and no actual slur on Windows was
intended...

(And I have no difficulty with Event Driven programming...<G>)

Pete.

problems. But hell, I have had some stability problems myself!).

When I first started looking to write Windows programs about two years ago,
I was impressed with the API - the completeness, the documentation, the
error-handling, the system-level controls, the whole schmear.

Granted, for the sequential mindset (most non-Windows programmers),

recursively without your knowledge before it completes itself. (Just

But once I got the "feel" of working in the message-driven and reentrant

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3ACEBDA7.D0BB811C@Azonic.co.nz...
> Michael Mattias wrote:
> >
> > Not that I am necessarily a Microsoft-lover, but I have been impressed
with
> > the both design and performance of Windows...

No, no, no!

I really DO like working in Windows. I posted about the same thing somewhere
maybe a year ago; if there still was a deja.com newsgroup archive you could
search it. Plus, I've said some nice things about Windows in some of the
articles I've published in "BASICally Speaking."

Lots of memory, multi-tasking, DLLs for common routines, Windows has it all.
The MSDN library has all the doc for the API calls, even if it is already
starting to 'lean' toward the ".NET" stuff even though the main MS-piece is
not scheduled for delivery until 4Q '01. (And MS's track record on meeting
scheduled release date? YECCCH!!)

Now, Microsoft APPLICATIONS programs, that's a horse of another color. As a
publisher of applications, Microsoft makes a heck of an operating system.
(MS-Word(r) is the world's WORST word processor!).

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

they have all been running for at least two weeks since last reboot. Perhaps
you should take the time to learn how to do the same.

On Sat, 07 Apr 2001 16:34:20 GMT, "Donald Tees"
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> enlightened us:

>I have seven machines here, all running windows.  A quick check shows that
>they have all been running for at least two weeks since last reboot. Perhaps
>you should take the time to learn how to do the same.
>

It is not me.  I don't do anything out of the ordinary except run my
software.  It's possible that I've overtaxed my PC, but I don't think
so.  I get GPF's at least once a day, usually with either Netscape or
I.E. when they are coming down.

I get system freezes at various times in various programs.  Some days
nothing happens.  Other days, this happens two or three times.

I've seen Windows just go out to lunch when it is loading it's system
programs on boot up.  A re-boot and things are fine.

It ain't me!


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Read the facts and sign a petition at:

http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
I have experienced the exact same symptoms as you.  However they were only
on one machine, allowing me to trace the problem.  It was a sporadic memory
error.  I fiddled with changing the memory, but ended up replacing the
motherboard.  Same OS, booted off the disk without even telling it that the
computer had changed, and I have not had a problem since.

power spikes or brownouts.  They are getting quite cheap, and if they solve
the problem, then will pay for themselves in a day or two.
<the one thing you cannot fix is the slow boot><G>

quite noisy.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <3ACB9877.3B60A1B7@iname.com> <VU0z6.94$be.8301@news1.atl> <Kh2z6.520971$f36.15880676@news20.bellglobal.com> <3acd24cf_5@127.0.0.1> <jWnz6.503$BX5.188118@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3ACEBDA7.D0BB811C@Azonic.co.nz> <Y2Dz6.38$3J2.42312@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <BFB3C71171A6DB85.5E29D1DB23B403E7.715B1DD83C2ABE99@lp.airnews.net>`

```

I've used all of the DOS versions, and Windows 3.1, 95, and 98.  As much 
as I would like to concur with the opinions expressed in this chain of 
discussion, I would like to relate my conclusions:

1) Windows is a complex design with great potential for Microsoft.
2) It demonstrates the good and bad of OO (I'm not really qualified to say 
this about these two subjects.)
3) Using any version of Windows is like learning to live with one's partner. 
It takes a long time to understand the relationship.
4) Any mixing of DOS and Windows by me gets ME into trouble with the 
Windows method.

It took me some time to come to these conclusions, and mind changing is 
one option. However, the thing that helped me accept Windows for what it 
is was the use of my Norton Utilities.  Frequently running of this software 
has taught me how interrelated Windows and DOS are to this point. I can 
see why MS wants to remove DOS completely to achieve a higher 
reliability reputation. My current concern with the way Windows is going is 
will they provide the utilities to let the single user be the system 
programmer also.  This is on top of the inclination of Windows to expand 
to do everything.  I've been a proponet of Indegrated Data Store, but there 
are limits to good clean interfaces that make me feel most of my problems 
with 98, and software in general is with ME, and the absents of proper 
documentation of the software. Here again, my proper might be different 
than "yours", but my benchmark comparison is to a description of how to 
make some dish for a meal.  If I follow it using all of the rules for 
substition, and size requirements, the taste and quality of the results will 
approximate the intended.  I believe the Windows Operating System is in 
the hands of too many cooks.

Warren Simmons

```
The thing that I think you are missing, Warren, is that the programer has to
look at the *entire* OS as part of the OS.  You are thinking in terms of an
OS plus "user programs".  Under OOP, when as proper OOP user application is
implimented, it should become part of the OS.  The next programmer
can/should be able to use it as a subroutine library.

When you think in those terms, then MicroSoft Word becomes the report writer
module for the OS.  The power of the individual programmer that can use it
as a module instead of as a user application, becomes that much greater.

<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
> The thing that I think you are missing, Warren, is that the programer has 
to
> look at the *entire* OS as part of the OS.  You are thinking in terms of an

application is
> implimented, it should become part of the OS.  The next programmer

report writer
> module for the OS.  The power of the individual programmer that can 
use it
> as a module instead of as a user application, becomes that much 
greater.

> "Warren G. Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote in 
message
> news:1103_986663276@micron-450...

I admit to feeling most user programs are not a part of the OS.  A division 
of responsibility helps to keep things right, OR highlights the failure to 
follow instructions in the use of other systems (applications.)  I believe it 
is very "nice" to be able to call Word to do some writing, etc.  I don't 
believe it should be fully contained within the operating system.  As I 
recall, Windows began with three other editors (I don't know the degree of 
integration) because they did not feel someone who needed to write 
something needed all the bagage the other write support carried around 
with it.  Had they made one program with options for lessor or expanded 
needs, it might have been easier to understand why they did it.  Yet, I feel 
they don't use all of the DLL s in Word to support the others, and one, 
"edit" has a different interface for dos.

This may not fully reflect my feelings about this subject, I'm often at a loss 
for words. (Don't count these. <G>)  My feelings stem from years 
exposure to attempts by some to place all the responsiblity for some 
"system" on one and only one vendor.  Sometimes a vendor would step up 
to the task only to be over ruled by the home office, or some other 
condition "outside of their control."   To me it becomes a case of putting all 
your eggs in one basket.  What do you do if a vendor fails to meet your 
needs, and has no program in place to fill that need.  From what I read, as 
an example, Intel's next processor (IV ) has stuff for the guy who wants to 
make movies, but nothing else for others of import.  MS, trying to build a 
single source for everything you need in my view, would be better off 
creating some interface standards, and tending to business on an 
application by application basis.

And, what of the added ability of things interwined to break or be broken 
into.

I believe MS began as an operating system company with ideas to expand 
in all directions, but found opportunity to apply resources to the OS to aid 
the others to the disadvantage of others.  I feel that had they developed 
the operation system as a system unto it's self, the design would have 
made it easy by not required to be part of the OS.  I am pleased to see 
other OS's come into the market.  From this group's conversations of 
email, I've switched to Opera instead of IE, and Eudora instead of OE. My 
life has been easier, less worry about security problems, and on the dark 
side, I'm carrying lots of unused software because there is no way 
published by MS to remove it.
\
Warren

division

it

> integration) because they did not feel someone who needed to write
> something needed all the bagage the other write support carried around

I agree with what you are saying, but I think we are in a transition. The
real problem with OOP is the traditional chicken and egg problem.  You
cannot use OOP properly without the entire thing being OOP.

You need an OOP OS, an OOP compiler, an OOP linker, and a fully OOP
interface puting it all together.

Right now, we have bits and pieces. As each part becomes more compliant, the
whole is getting easier to use correctly.

```
"Warren G. Simmons" wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> I admit to feeling most user programs are not a part of the OS.  A division
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> "edit" has a different interface for dos.

You might want to look into converting/upgrading to Win2000 Pro, which
has no DOS underpinnings at all. I did 3 months ago and have been *very*
happy with it. You need to research your device drivers because most
current ones won't work with W2K. OTOH, W2K ships with a lot of working
drivers. You can get bitten in unexpected places, e.g., I anticipated
problems, perhaps, with the drivers for my DVD & CD-RW drives but not
for my Trinitron monitor. Just the opposite in real life. The only real
problem I had was with the DVD-ROM drive, which I simply couldn't get to
work under W2K, even trying it with a commercial DVD player software
product ($40!). The problem here was the drive, which was a Samsung
SD-612 (the one Dell's been shipping since last spring). I bought a
Toshiba drive (SD-M1502, $71 plus S&H) and Matrox's updated DVD reader
for W2K ($10) and it works like a dream, much better than the Samsung
ever did under Win98SE. (I have a Matrox video card).

W2K is extremely stable and much more secure than the DOS-based versions
of Windows. I haven't had any blue screens of death, either. I did see
some "unexpected_kernel_mode_trap" failures involving my DVD-ROM drive,
which resulted in automatic reboots. The fix was to reinstall W2K, which
I was planning to do anyway to get rid of all the test versions of DVD
players I'd tried and to give me a nice, tidy Registry. W2K also keeps
an Event Log, to which it logs many routine events, plus all failures.
By examining the Event Log I could see the exact failure on my DVD drive
and the first time it occurred (02/17). 

As always, YMMV,
LiamD
```

```
Had to respond to this...

Don, it is uncanny...

I arrived at exactly the same conclusions you describe about using MS WORD a
few weeks ago.

I no longer have ANY COBOL printing in  new programs and am gradually
getting round to removing the existing stuff.

Output goes direct to a WORD document. From there the user can Print it,
Mail it, Fax it, Change fonts, orientation, etc. with NO effort on my
part....Love it!

And you are right that it is easier to do this with OO programs. (Most of
mine are PowerCOBOL which creates the Word Document, then invokes Word with
the document preloaded when the User asks to view or print it. They can
preview and/or print using the facilities of Word and it doesn't cost me a
single line of code...(apart from invoking it in the first place...)

I even have things like renewal notices set up with Word Templates and
simply hit the Database with PowerCOBOL, creating a table which is used as a
Data Source to Word Mail Merge...It took months off the development time.)

As far as I'm concerned the COBOL Report Writer and even the assignment of
files to a Printer, is obsolete. I don't care whether they implement it or
not...

Now, I realise that many will argue that this is NOT transportable. Guess
what? The people who are using it don't care. They are not about to throw
out Windows and go Unix. (Half their existing applications would not run if
they did...)

Furthermore, It is a fairly simple matter to convert the base document back
to a print file, in the unlikely event that this was necessary.

It is like you said, the Word Package becomes just like an OS subroutine.
You invoke it and use it just like you would any other OS service.

Pete.

> mine are PowerCOBOL which creates the Word Document, then invokes Word
with
> the document preloaded when the User asks to view or print it. They can
> preview and/or print using the facilities of Word and it doesn't cost me a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> simply hit the Database with PowerCOBOL, creating a table which is used as
a
> Data Source to Word Mail Merge...It took months off the development time.)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> out Windows and go Unix. (Half their existing applications would not run
if
> they did...)
>
> Furthermore, It is a fairly simple matter to convert the base document
back
> to a print file, in the unlikely event that this was necessary.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >look at the *entire* OS as part of the OS.  You are thinking in terms of
an
> >OS plus "user programs".  Under OOP, when as proper OOP user application
is
> >implimented, it should become part of the OS.  The next programmer
> >can/should be able to use it as a subroutine library.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >module for the OS.  The power of the individual programmer that can use
it
> >as a module instead of as a user application, becomes that much greater.
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Jeff Baynard <jeffbaynard@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T21:31:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B6F4FF9C.6AE%jeffbaynard@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <3ACB9877.3B60A1B7@iname.com> <VU0z6.94$be.8301@news1.atl> <Kh2z6.520971$f36.15880676@news20.bellglobal.com> <3acd24cf_5@127.0.0.1> <jWnz6.503$BX5.188118@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3ACEBDA7.D0BB811C@Azonic.co.nz> <Y2Dz6.38$3J2.42312@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <BFB3C71171A6DB85.5E29D1DB23B403E7.715B1DD83C2ABE99@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Even my 450mhz G4 Mac here at home is more stable (and a smidge faster) than
my PIII 650mhz NT computer at work.  My cable modem gives our 10BaseT
network at work a run for it's money.

The problem with NT is that it tries to do too many things at once.  If you
move too fast, it will lock up.  It crashes cuz it gets confused.  The mac
is not pre-emptive, so it does things one at a time and doesn't get confused
(as much).

The C-64 NEVER crashed though, unless the program itself was truly buggy.
Good ol RUN/STOP - RESTORE will do the trick.  Most hacker type people these
days seem to have owned and used a C64 at some point.  I had one...

Jeff


> Windows, specifically Win 98, is the only operating system I've worked
> on that crashes at least once a day.  Usually it is more.  And we're
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> and never anything close with my old reliable Commodore 64!
```

- **Date:** 2001-04-08T08:01:43+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD00CD7.1F4FD91F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <3ACB9877.3B60A1B7@iname.com> <VU0z6.94$be.8301@news1.atl> <Kh2z6.520971$f36.15880676@news20.bellglobal.com> <3acd24cf_5@127.0.0.1> <jWnz6.503$BX5.188118@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3ACEBDA7.D0BB811C@Azonic.co.nz> <Y2Dz6.38$3J2.42312@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <BFB3C71171A6DB85.5E29D1DB23B403E7.715B1DD83C2ABE99@lp.airnews.net>`

```
> On Sat, 07 Apr 2001 11:42:48 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
> >No, no, no!
> >
> >I really DO like working in Windows. 

Compared to what ? MS-DOS ? Mainframes ?

> >. Plus, I've said some nice things about Windows in some of the
> >articles I've published in "BASICally Speaking."

Well, you may have had to say nice things about Windows or they wouldn't
have published it   ;-)

> >Lots of memory, multi-tasking, DLLs for common routines, Windows has it all.

So have many other systems, for much longer, and mostly done much
better.  

Windows 3.x and 9x isn't really multitasking.  In fact Windows works
primarily as one large program that does dynamic loading of 'modules'
rather than being an OS that loads independent programs.

Windows DLLs are the cause of more problems than solutions.  It has got
to the point where Windows now checks all the DLLs on a regular basis
and restores what _it_ feels are the correct ones regardless of what the
system admin has done.  With XP the system will silently apply updates
over the network.  While this may make the OS more stable (it gets
unstable because of the DLL hell that MS created), it tends to break
other programs - non-MS programs.

What else have you worked with ?

> >The MSDN library has all the doc for the API calls,

Actually it doesn't have them _all_, there are some that MS keeps to
itself.

> > even if it is already
> >starting to 'lean' toward the ".NET" stuff even though the main MS-piece is
> >not scheduled for delivery until 4Q '01. (And MS's track record on meeting
> >scheduled release date? YECCCH!!)

And .NET is all about taking a tax on internet transactions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T21:24:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qALz6.14049$Kr1.1175686@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <3ACB9877.3B60A1B7@iname.com> <VU0z6.94$be.8301@news1.atl> <Kh2z6.520971$f36.15880676@news20.bellglobal.com> <3acd24cf_5@127.0.0.1> <jWnz6.503$BX5.188118@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3ACEBDA7.D0BB811C@Azonic.co.nz> <Y2Dz6.38$3J2.42312@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <BFB3C71171A6DB85.5E29D1DB23B403E7.715B1DD83C2ABE99@lp.airnews.net> <3AD00CD7.1F4FD91F@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz
>
> Windows 3.x and 9x isn't really multitasking.  In fact Windows works
> primarily as one large program that does dynamic loading of
'modules'
> rather than being an OS that loads independent programs.

Windows 98 upward is preemptively multi-tasked (has Apple done that
yet?). The 9x series is not, I don't think, time-sliced or priority
driven; everybody gets a piece. The OS has to wait on a call for
services before it will shift focus to another task.

You can try a tight, infinite loop while another program is
incrementing a counter. The counter will move while the loop is
running. Another example: Format a diskette in DOS under Win95 and the
machine is locked for two minutes; under Win98 you get back some
control during the format.

I do have to reboot my Win98 machine, due to the ill-behaved nature of
Fujitsu's debugger. Not the case with Win2K (What! you say, you have
Fujitsu COBOL on TWO machines? Shame! Shame! Relax, it's Version 5 and
I'm only using one machine at a time.)

In our experience, Win2K (aka NT 5) is extremely stable and a real
pleasure to work with.
```

that the Marketplace is voting for Windows and, as that is where I want to
be, I HAVE to develop for it...

Having said that, I think it is OK. But I have never developed for UNIX or
LINUX or any OS ending in 'X', so I accept that I am not qualified to judge
on Operating System superiority.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-08T13:22:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bCZz6.69$fP5.36170@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <3ACB9877.3B60A1B7@iname.com> <VU0z6.94$be.8301@news1.atl> <Kh2z6.520971$f36.15880676@news20.bellglobal.com> <3acd24cf_5@127.0.0.1> <jWnz6.503$BX5.188118@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3ACEBDA7.D0BB811C@Azonic.co.nz> <Y2Dz6.38$3J2.42312@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <BFB3C71171A6DB85.5E29D1DB23B403E7.715B1DD83C2ABE99@lp.airnews.net>`

```
>
> Windows, specifically Win 98, is the only operating system I've worked
> on that crashes at least once a day.  Usually it is more.  And we're
> talking crashes, freeze ups and just plain going to sleep.

There is a known problem with Win 98 (which I run at home) with memory
fragmentation. This is what causes the annoying "Insufficient Memory to run
this application. Close one or more programs and try again" message even
when nothing is running except the "base" system. Then when you try to
restart, that kinda hangs up and you end up dicking around for twenty
minutes with scandisk, et al.

Never had this problem with 95, NT or 2K, though.

But as a platform for writing applications, Win/any is a terrific platform.
(Unless you're a Luddite).

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Anonymous <anonymous@anonymous.anonymous>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T02:51:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ad07b5e_2@anonymous>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <3ACB9877.3B60A1B7@iname.com> <VU0z6.94$be.8301@news1.atl> <Kh2z6.520971$f36.15880676@news20.bellglobal.com> <3acd24cf_5@127.0.0.1> <jWnz6.503$BX5.188118@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3ACEBDA7.D0BB811C@Azonic.co.nz> <Y2Dz6.38$3J2.42312@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <BFB3C71171A6DB85.5E29D1DB23B403E7.715B1DD83C2ABE99@lp.airnews.net> <bCZz6.69$fP5.36170@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael,

as I mentioned to Richard, I'm not looking to start a war here, but I'm
quite amazed at some of the stuff being posted.

"Win 98 crashes at least once a day..."

So install and configure it correctly.

Don't use ME.

I run Win 98 (in 32 Megs of real memory, on an AMD K6 (Pentium 333,
equivalent) notebook)and it crashes SO infrequently I can't remember the
last time it did. I run it EVERY day for MOST of the day, develop complex
GUI applications on it, write heaps of prose on it, handle mail and
Newsgroups on it, and run a spectrum of applications on it.
(Maybe the problem is with Intel...<G>)

And I use your abhorred MS WORD (97) on a daily basis, and find it
excellent, so I guess it all comes down to personal preference.

I have NEVER encountered the memory fragmentation you describe, but I would
expect it if I was cutting and pasting VERY frequently... I have NEVER seen
the "Insufficient memory" message you allude to.

Someone else (can't remember who) said the Fujitsu Debugger causes it to
hang. This is a known problem and a fix is available for it from the
www.adtools.com site. Since applying the fix, it hasn't happened on my
system. (Guess it "fixed" it...<G>)

It is interesting to me that people will blame the OS and system software in
general, when they make no attempt to isolate or identify and alleviate
whatever it is that is causing the problem.

Who would tolerate an OS crashing "at least once a day"? You either get it
fixed very quickly or bin it...

Is it me?

Pete.


Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>>
>> Windows, specifically Win 98, is the only operating system I've worked
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

SkippyPB wrote in message ...
>On Mon, 9 Apr 2001 02:51:52 +1200, Anonymous
><anonymous@anonymous.anonymous> enlightened us:
>
Don't you just love that e-mail address?

Sorry, folks it is indeed me, Pete Dashwood, messing around with various
News Feed servers in an effort to find one that is stable and gives an
updated view of CLC... I think I've cracked why this one is anonymous...it
has a number of pornographic feeds on it which I honestly haven't had time
to check out....yet <G> Anyway, to the business at hand... (don't even THINK
about going there...<G>)
>
>You put up with it because there is no other choice at this point.
>
I sympathize with your point, Steve. What about the people who supplied your
system?
Don't they care that they have a customer who is unable to use the product?
Have you notified them? Or has it only started misbehaving since you added
software beyond what they supplied?

A week ago I went to update my McAffee .DAT files and the download and
update proceeded without problem, just as it always has for the past couple
of years. When I tried to reboot, the system was unable to load Windows and
stopped at the C:\ prompt. (I don't consider this a Windows problem as
Windows had not yet loaded...) I uninstalled the McAffee software and
everything worked perfectly. I immediately went to a secret site and did an
online scan for viruses (there is a similar facility available publicly from
Trend Anti-virus at their site), all clear.

Yet a simple update of virus data files had rendered the system inoperable.
I am now using a different anti-virus system and have had no problems since.
I have neither the time nor inclination to investigate EXACTLY what
happened, I just needed to get it running again. I was off the air for about
1 hour.

This is no reflection on McAffee, I have always found their products and
services to be very good. Maybe my virus engine was too old, I don't know
(or care). It runs fine now with a different anti-virus engine.

Blackice is something I have always been suspicious of. If it doesn't report
potential attacks, it could be seen to be useless... Ask yourself what
anyone stands to gain by attacking your system? If you have Cookies enabled,
there are two points of view on using software such as Blackice:

1. I need Blackice to detect possible hacks through the Cookie doorway.

2. As I have given the Internet an open invitation to place (what is
ostensibly, but can you be sure?) data on my system, is there any point in
running a hack detector, when the doors are wide open anyway?

Which position you adopt probably depends on how paranoid you are<G>

Seriously, I would suggest trying the system with the removal of both
Blackice and McAffee, and seeing if it makes any difference. Obviously, this
will leave you feeling vulnerable to virus attck (if not to hack attack) so
why not check your system at the Trend site before closing your Browser (it
is an incremental check so takes less time once you have run it). Do this
for a few days and see if you get better stability. If you do, consider an
anti-virus alternative. (I would drop Blackice altogether unless you are
actully carrying out secret Government or Commercially sensitive work, or
have something else of "cybervalue" on  your system<G>).

If you don't, then get the hardware back to the vendor, pronto. As Don
pointed out, it could be a hardware problem.

Windows is NOT intended to crash once a day....

>I am beginning to believe it may be memory related.  Generally I only
>run 1 program at a time, but I do have McAfee Virus and BlackIce
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well if it freezes,   Blackice HAS to be a suspect...<G>

Pete.


  --------== Posted Anonymously via Newsfeeds.Com ==-------
     Featuring the worlds only Anonymous Usenet Server
    -----------== http://www.newsfeeds.com ==----------
```

```
Didn't MS recognise the stability problems with its OS's at the launch of
W2K ?. Sad truth is until recently I developed all software under a DOS
partition using a 11 year old compiler. Things started going down hill
back then !. I still plan to develop as much as I can within DOS, only
switching to windows for the final production tasks.

That out the way... I've just installed W2K on my laptop with VMware
running a virtual W98 machine. This is rock solid and has the bonus
that W2K/W98 are networked so I keep my source on the W2K side
and develop within W98. If W98 does mess up I can simply restore
a backup of the virtual machines disk image. If you haven't already
looked at VMware then you should as they have a eval for download
at www.vmware.com.

I have developed on DOS, WINx, SCO Unix & Linux and so far
have to say that DOS rules followed by Unix with WINx bringing up
the rear (or should that be sneaking up your rear ?).

The Microsoft advertisements for Windows 2000 at one had quotes of 
"independant bench marks" showing that Windows 95/98 had a lifetime of
2 and a bit days from bootup before they crashed, and Windows NT a 
lifetime of 5 and a bit days.

Microsoft was using this as a pitch to sell people the "new, more 
stable, etc, etc," Windows 2K

So Microsoft is one (admitedly biased :-) source for the authenticity 
of these "my system always crashes" reports...

Most people don't "bin it" because they are true believers in the 
"Microsoft Way".

Lorne Sunley

##### ↳ ↳ Re: update:: still need help!

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T08:42:47+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC6DBF7.D123B637@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<19%w6.163945$p66.48044471@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <9E3x6.164558$p66.48395631@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
For Example: John Smith wrote:
> 
> At the very end, no matter what I have declared, it tells me that I have
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> always at the end, even if I add another variable!


If the PIC sizes are counted then the total is 76, however this
indicates that you have failed to understand what the 'OCCURS 50'
means.  While .CM-Case-Sales-Rec is 9 bytes as you apparently counted,
there are 50 of them giving 450 characters for this part of the record. 
Similar for others.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: update:: still need help!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T00:10:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC6748B.D023A08A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<19%w6.163945$p66.48044471@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <9E3x6.164558$p66.48395631@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <3AC6DBF7.D123B637@Azonic.co.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:

> For Example: John Smith wrote:
> >
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> Similar for others.

Haven't been following this thread closely - but following on from what Richard
has written, and hoping you got his point about the occurs 50. Now I know you
probably got your format from a text book or sample - BUT - you are talking a
FIXED RECORD LENGTH. Delete that damned line that's really causing your problem
:-

RECORD CONTAINS 76 CHARACTERS.

Should your instructor 'insist' on this line - then come up with "Why ?", "Why
?", "Why ?'.

Leaving that line in - and having corrected to the true number of characters -
what happens if subsequently you change it to read occurs 55 times - now you've
got to recalculate the wretched record size.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: update:: still need help!

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T17:47:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LQJx6.3168$bY4.8226@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<19%w6.163945$p66.48044471@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <9E3x6.164558$p66.48395631@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Hi John,

Hate to tell you this, but I think you've got it all wrong.

Reading the specs, I got the idea that the prof wanted you to populate the index
file with the data available in the ip rec. I may be wrong, but give it some
thought.

In spite of the fact that he wanted fields for actual qtys and dollars sold, I
don�t think he expected them to be populated from this problem (or it was
covered in a part of the specs I didn't see). Maybe the next pgm(s) he assigns
will do that. I think each table he wants has only three occurrences. The same 3
that are in the ip rec (monthly tgt sales). Here�s what I think the rec layouts
should look like. I may not have the tables defined properly, but you can
correct them.

Note that now you�re back to a 49 byte ip rec and a 76 byte index rec. How about
that?


01  ip-rec.
05  ip-acct                           pic  x(005)
05  ip-name                           pic  x(025)
05  ip-phone                          pic  x(010)
05  ip-tgt-sales-tbl.
10  ip-tsale    occurs  3             pic s9(003). 


01  op-idx-rec.
05  op-acct                           pic  x(005)
05  op-name                           pic  x(025)
05  op-phone	                      pic  x(010)
05  op-tgt-sales-tbl.
10  op-tsale   occurs  3              pic s9(003). 
05  op-act-sales-tbl.
10  op-asale   occurs  3              pic s9(003). 
05  op-dlr-sales-tbl.
10  op-dsale   occurs  3              pic s9(004)v99. 

The PD below should give you an idea about how to populate the index file, you
can meld it into the rest of your logic. Hope this helps.

Jack.

PROD-DIV.
initialize op-idx-rec
read ip-file			
perf 100-pop-index-file
until ip-eof
finish up.

100-pop-index-file.
move all flat stuff
perf varying gsub from +1 by +1 until gsub > +3
move ip-tsale(gsub)  to op-tsale(gsub)
end-perf
yada yada yada

write op-rec
read ip-file.



In article <9E3x6.164558$p66.48395631@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>, For Example: John
Smith says...
>
>I found where part of the problem was, however I am still stuck with the
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

I wonder if the restriction is in record length?  Certainly no Cobol that I have run into has such a restriction, but you never know with student stuff.  I would try defining the table in working storage, and reading the elements into it one at a time.

Donald

  "awkisall" <awkisall@qwest.net> wrote in message news:3AC4F24B.F1C7AA28@qwest.net...
  Try indexing your tables?  E.G., 
         10 CM-CASE-SALES-REC 
              OCCURS 50 TIMES 
              INDEXED BY CASE-SALES-IND. 
      . 
      . 
      . 
        10 CM-CASE-TARGET-SALES-REC 
             OCCURS 50 TIMES 
             INDEXED BY TARGET-SALES-IND. 
    
  For Example: John Smith wrote: 

    I am trying to make my first indexed table.  I am to define these three 
    items as tables.  however when I compile them I get Restrictions where 
    indicated.  There MUST be a way to go around this or my teacher would not 
    have assigned it.  Does anyone see where or what to do? 
    Tanks. 

     01 CUSTOMER-MASTER-REC. 
        05 CM-CUSTOMER-ACCOUNT-NUM  PIC 99999. 
        05 CM-CUSTOMER-NAME   PIC X(25). 
        05 CM-CUSTOMER-TELE-NUM  PIC X(10). 
        05 CM-CASE-SALES-TABLE. 
           10 CM-CASE-SALES-REC 
       OCCURS 50 TIMES. 
              20 CM-CS-COLA    PIC 999. 
              20 CM-CS-DIET-DRINKS  PIC 999. 
              20 CM-CS-ENERGY-SUPP   PIC 999. 
               05 CM-CASE-TARGET-SALES-TABLE. 
               $ 
    restriction 
           10 CM-CASE-TARGET-SALES-REC 
       OCCURS 50 TIMES. 
              20 CM-CT-COLA    PIC 999. 
              20 CM-CT-DIET-DRINKS  PIC 999. 
              20 CM-CT-ENERGY-SUPP   PIC 999. 
        05 CM-DOLLAR-SALES-TABLE. 
         $ 
    restriction 
           10 CM-DOLLAR-SALES-REC 
       OCCURS 50 TIMES. 
              20 CM-DS-COLA    PIC 9999V99. 
                     20 CM-DS-DIET-DRINKS  PIC 9999V99. 
              20 CM-DS-ENERGY-SUPP   PIC 9999V99. 

    $ 

    restriction
```

have watched you over the past year, I see a young (I suspect) person that

with you.

go.

abilities that I see in you.

group would concur.

Floyd Johnson

- **From:** Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-04T19:54:25+00:00

Deek40 wrote:
> 
> I deserved that:)  Naw serious though, it's normal to have errors when you
> first write a program?  I thought once you got good you didnt get errors.
> 
> Deek

I have been coding for sometime now, I am instantly suspicious when a
program clean compiles on it's first attempt (I am occasionally golden),
often I have typos in such words
as prefrom (perform) or I am missing a  statistical counter (or misnamed
it).

	Susan A

A compiler error is wonderful! Black Box debugging is a challenge!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and my college

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T13:41:10+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACC67E5.CA740FE6@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<wSax6.2182$bY4.7651@www.newsranger.com> <20010331014848.01277.00001226@ng-fr1.aol.com> <3ACB7BF1.5482E329@boeing.com>`

```
Susan Allen wrote:

> I have been coding for sometime now, I am instantly suspicious when a
> program clean compiles on it's first attempt (I am occasionally golden),
> [..]
> or I am missing a  statistical counter (or misnamed it).

One of the advantages of Cobol is that it _does_ give an error when you
misspell names.  With some other languages they silently create a new
variable of the wrong name and then use that.  This can be quite
difficult to debug.  I prefer to get a number of errors rather than a
debug nightmare.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and my college

- **From:** "John R. Green" <johninatlanta@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T14:35:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACA17E3.EF1FA335@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<0D455311F8D9BF83.F93FCE8F33304347.A5CB880501B036FD@lp.airnews.net> <20010330155408.01673.00001545@ng-fo1.aol.com>`

```
I have been developing applications on computers for almost 25 years.  You learn to
avoid the most common mistakes.  What is most important is to understand COBOL (or
any language) well enough to be proficient at fixing the errors once they occur.
That takes time, but you will get there.

Long live COBOL!!!

John
