```
SkippyPB wrote:

> Personally, I believe that 1 year of Cobol in college is not enough.
> At the university I attended I was required to take two years of
> Cobol.

I took one semester of CoBOL as a freshman.  After I graduated and was a USAF pilot,
I got my first real job programming in CoBOL.  I wonder how good I was - to me now
CoBOL is the easiest language out there, but that's because I speak it natively.
Nothing teaches though like using it on the job.   What do you do in two years of
CoBOL?
...

> Someone suggested that Java would be good to take.  I think you'd be
> better served learning Unix first then Java.  Also, you should take a
> fair amount of math, accounting and economics.

Why Unix?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and my college

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T14:09:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D654BE9E5F5174DC.02DB1E8EEC177BE8.D7B7D65926B99340@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<20010329172002.28981.00000810@ng-ft1.aol.com> <0D455311F8D9BF83.F93FCE8F33304347.A5CB880501B036FD@lp.airnews.net> <3AC88178.61FB3D7A@brazee.net>`

```
On Mon, 02 Apr 2001 07:41:13 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
enlightened us:

>SkippyPB wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The first year (2 semesters) was spendt learning the mechanics and
syntax.  The programs we had to write were very basic.  More time was
spent in lecture than lab.  The second year (also 2 semesters) was
spent doing many more complicated things with Cobol.  A lot less
lecture time and much more lab time.  

>> Someone suggested that Java would be good to take.  I think you'd be
>> better served learning Unix first then Java.  Also, you should take a
>> fair amount of math, accounting and economics.
>
>Why Unix?

Because it is a base for many other things.  Understanding Unix gives
a better understanding of server systems in general.

Regards,

Steve

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and my college

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T07:33:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC87F93.2A63862B@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010329172002.28981.00000810@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```
Deek40 wrote:

> 1)My school has just reported we are the final cobol students for the school,
> they aren't teaching it next year.  While I enjoyed cobol they are saying cobol
> is dead and they are adding visual basic and i believe java and python.

Of course, from you're school's perspective "dead" means "people don't want to pay
them money to learn CoBOL".


> 2) A quick question, just for talk assume i graduate college and decide to
> pursue cobol.  Right now I know i am far from knowing everything about cobol,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> normal?

Continuing Education is more likely to offer CoBOL, as its customers have seen the
real world.

If I were hiring, I would consider other evidence that you could program.  And if I
were an OO shop, I would want evidence that you "got" OO.   Generally, companies
don't like to hire people without real work experience.  Obviously this has
exceptions.   Entry level programmers are assumed to need close supervision as they
learn the job.   So if CoBOL isn't your #1 language, it doesn't make much
difference for entry level jobs.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and my college

- **From:** "David Binkowski" <dcb@wwa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-23T02:50:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XLME6.1127$yW2.16942@ord-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<20010329172002.28981.00000810@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```
Is this a school that has no Enterprise-scale equipment to teach you guys
on?
What do I have to say about their decision?  Java - Great!  Did you know it
doesn't just run on mainframes, it runs on PC's too?  And Python, I don't
know
the language but I know C/C++ so I imagine its not too big a stretch, and
like
Java, ITS PORTABLE due to its interprative nature.  So all the better.  It
runs
on PC's, mainframes, and everything in between.  Visual Basic???  The only
thing I can say for VB is that its a bad language that teaches you bad
habits,
but at least its not portable.  <Insert rim-shot here>

I laugh when people tell me they want to become experts in ONE language,
and especially one language that only works on a very proprietary and
specific
platform, that being Microsoft Windows and Intel.  I don't believe VB has
the ability to compile or run on anything but that specific combination.  It
would be okay if Microsoft were to really maintain their illicit hold on the
world,
but I don't see that hapenning.  I DO see more open-source development and
more upscaling to systems that can handle real I/O and do internet business.
Well, in any case, I believe learning MORE languages is always better, so if
they fail to instruct you in COBOL, you may have to just read up on it
yourself.
In the end, you teach yourself anyway.

They aren't teaching COBOL any more?  I never really liked Cobol, but I
acknowledge its gotten alot better with Cobol/II and Cobol/370 language
constructs.  I accept it because now you can do much better string handling
and SET ADDRESS / pointers makes it more friendly to the CPU.  I'm a
native Assembler, C, Pascal, PL/1, and BASIC (shhhh) programmer, so
COBOL didn't appeal to my nature when I learned it in college.  BUT, now
I accept it as a reality.

"Deek40" <deek40@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010329172002.28981.00000810@ng-ft1.aol.com...
>
>
> 1)My school has just reported we are the final cobol students for the
school,

cobol

cobol,
> this is my 2nd semester and i still get errors and other stuff but for the
most
> part understand the concepts and can fix most of the errors.  Is it
absolutely
> necessary for a undergraduate student to master the language with a
semester of
> it behind them?  Or would a company send for more training, because after
these
> past semesters in my class i only see maybe 1 or 2 people that mastered
the

>

> Deek
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and my college

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-23T03:57:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%KNE6.258306$3J6.17096142@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<20010329172002.28981.00000810@ng-ft1.aol.com> <XLME6.1127$yW2.16942@ord-read.news.verio.net>`

```

"David Binkowski" <dcb@wwa.com
>
> I laugh when people tell me they want to become experts in ONE
language,
> and especially one language that only works on a very proprietary
and
> specific
> platform, that being Microsoft Windows and Intel.  I don't believe
VB has
> the ability to compile or run on anything but that specific
combination.  It
> would be okay if Microsoft were to really maintain their illicit
hold on the
> world,
> but I don't see that hapenning.


Straw-man argument. The WinTel platform - maybe 80% of the world's
installed computer base - cannot carry the negative connotation of
'proprietary platform.' WinTel is, rather, the de facto standard
platform.

That Java will run on a toaster or PDP-8 is cute.

program.

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <3ac746d7.79903032@news1.attglobal.net>...

>Pete - did you even look at the specification of the feature?  How do
>you know it's confusing?
>
That's very fair comment, Thane.

No, I didn't look at it. And I don't intend to until it is released. Life's
too short and brain cells are precious at my age...<G>

Consider this:

A group of people who are not amenable to suggestion or reason, take 10
years to produce a document couched in Legalistic Gobbledygook, with no
examples, pictures or explanations.
(And I know this is true from the bits I HAVE looked at, and from posts by
people who have been using COBOL for years and are now bewildered by it...)

 Then they expect us to read it and comment on it in a few weeks. And if we
do, they'll take another 2 years to think about it...

Now if they had "trickled out" what they were doing, say every 6 months or
so, I for one would be very happy to read, consider and comment on it.

I am reluctantly persuaded that nothing I can say or do will make one jot of
difference to it . How did I reach that conclusion? Read the posts to
softwaresimple and the non-responses...

But don't get me started on this... I don't want to start on the new
Standard or the committees responsible for it (and you don't want me to
either...<G>).

Actually Ed Arranga has just published my response to the papers you and
three others published on COBOLReport.com. I never intended this to be an
"article"; it was simply a response. But I stand by it. (And BTW, it is NOT
personal...I'm just EXTREMELY pissed off with what is being done to COBOL
and I reckon SOMEONE (ideally, the people responsible for the current
fiasco) should answer for it.

Now, <takes a deep breath> addressing your post here...

"How do I know it's confusing" when I haven't read it?

Because I am assured by people whose opinions I respect and who HAVE read it
that it contradicts what has been established for the last 40 years.

"I didn't want to confuse the student who is just starting - but for those
who don't follow what is in the draft COBOL Standard, there is now a *new*
format of the WRITE statement that actually *does* allow you to WRITE the
file-name (rather than the record-name).  There are even some "tricks" so
that if there are multiple 01-level records under the FD, it can figure out
"which" record type to write.

It will DEFINITELY take me a long time to get used to this new "enhancement"
(once it becomes available)."

THAT's what I was responding to... I find myself in total agreement. Why was
the committee wasting time on stuff that isn't broken? (Oops..there I go
again...sorry. It's OK, I'm calm now...<G>)

It really doesn't matter whether you add facilities to READ a RECORD or
WRITE a FILE (especially not to newcomers...) but it DOES matter that TIME
was spent on it, when there were a HUGE number of other things that REALLY
needed attention.

I guess it's more "fun" to sit and discuss upsetting the existing applecart
than it is to do some code samples, or provide some diagrams, or even look
at new features...

I agree totally with Bill's comment above. I too will find it confusing. So
I just won't use it. And neither will many other COBOL programmers.

So what exactly was achieved by the time wasted on it?

Sadly,

Pete.

```

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T14:13:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<josephk-109023.20370625032001@news.m.iinet.net.au> <3abe943a_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <josephk-B06FCB.21530826032001@news.m.iinet.net.au> <3ac07b83_1@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> wrote:

>
>Nowadays, I develop components in preference to Classes. But I NEED OO to do
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>in the new age of component based technology.

hohum. i think that oo is good, but usually for porting purposes, oo
only is used to tell the compiler which operating system and hardware
is running. i'd venture to say alot of lines of code areen't going
anywhere.

db/isam handling, file handling, hardware detection, and a good
interface library.

then you have some process control, and i'm thinking that tansactions
are good, but in reality, you have conflicts with transactions and
database transactions.

what would be the rollback method for writing to a tape? a rewind? if
it's a disk, you could move the disk file back, but then you'd need a
class which distinguished (at runtime for hotpluggables) between
rewindable files and non.

what about rolling back variables? keep two instances in storage? if
you have a large number of calculations and no memory, re-calc them?

how do you andle a cpu crashing with multiple processors, versus
single processor?

actually, as computers move from mainframes to pcs, alot of these
queswtions have been solved at the easiest level possible, but leave
the system specifics to the programmer, who probably cannot figure out
what to do if there is a problem, which prevents hardware porting.

remove the spam

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD35860.88DAD8EF@home.com>`

```


G Moore wrote:

>
> what would be the rollback method for writing to a tape? a rewind? if
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Whether you are talking Structured or OO ' rolling back' a tape sounds a bit like
pushing the envelope to me. Agreed, I'm out of touch - the only mainframes I see
are in movies <G>

As a designer, not programmer, I've always thought of tapes as being
'transactional', not 'historical'. Used either for inputting transactions to an
application, or for security backing up NCR CRAM or RCA MCF and currently hard
disks. Subject to disk storage constraints, I can appreciate there will be
circumstances where the historical data sits on mag tapes as part of a library,
but would have thought that for 'Enquiry', queries are either made sequentially
against the tapes, or the transactions are put on to a hard disk to allow multi
query SQL syntax.

With hard disk technology I can hardly think that people think of 'Master File'
updating as being a mag tape scenario.

So why would you want to rollback a tape ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO's Failure

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T15:29:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ac74711.79961302@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<josephk-109023.20370625032001@news.m.iinet.net.au> <3abe943a_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <josephk-B06FCB.21530826032001@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
On Mon, 26 Mar 2001 21:53:08 +0800, Joseph J Katnic
<josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:

>This may be true in COBOL OO, but Smalltalk - the original OO requires 
>GET and SET methods. Obviously Newer implementations are doing away with 
>this requirement.
>

That will teach you to believe everything you read.  COBOL DOES
require Get and Set methods for properties --- However, these are
IMPLICIT and don't have to be explicitly coded unless you wish to
override the default get and set methods to do something different.

>E.g. Create a CLASS Screen. Define objects Screen, window etc.
>Define methods MOVE, DISPLAY, SET, DELETE etc.
>You see we have created extensions to the language to move windows 
>around a screen etc. Now MOVE can mean MOVE a window around a screen as 
>well as the traditional meaning. 

With the way the syntax is defined there's no chance that you'll get
the two "moves" confused.  Some have been critical of this actually -
one cannot create new COBOL syntax using OO.

>Actually, what really happens is that the programmer gives up searching 
>the source library after about 30 minutes and just writes the code 
>anyway.

This is a problem with all OO languages that COBOL has tried to
address to some degree in the draft standard.  We have published
"Interfaces" so that you (and the compiler) can easily see what's
there and conformance is ensured.  In addition, while the format of
the "repository" is not mandated in the draft, vendors (see Fujitsu's
excellent class browser for instance) will be free to utilize the
repository to make these "find the right code" searches easier.

>No, But that's the great hope of OO isn't it. The holy grail of 
>re-usability that the IC industry has used to reduce the prices of it's 
>products.

I see it as speeding development and there is a savings there.  I also
see it as more reliable.  However, the other consideration is that if
you start purchasing hundreds of different classes and componantes,
and you want to resell your product you may be facing a large quantity
of royalties etc.


>When I write slackness, I don't mean that they aren't busy or 
>overworked. They are just busy and overworked on the wrong things.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>be there!

That is exactly why the COBOL standards groups have never restricted
extensions to the laguage and as far as I can see, actively encourage
it.

>I see what you are getting at.
>The class is like a template that gets applied to the data to create 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>of data.

This is correct.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO's Failure

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T17:05:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9a88l9$rje$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<josephk-109023.20370625032001@news.m.iinet.net.au> <3abe943a_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <josephk-B06FCB.21530826032001@news.m.iinet.net.au> <3ac74711.79961302@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:3ac74711.79961302@news1.attglobal.net...
> On Mon, 26 Mar 2001 21:53:08 +0800, Joseph J Katnic
> <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just so it is clear, there are GET and SET methods (both implicit and
explicit) for properties, but you do NOT need to use an method invocation to
actually "use" them.  There is a "new" format of identifiers that allows you
to implicitly USE these methods.

For example, if "prop-1" is an object property (defined in class "classj-2")
defined to have both GET and SET methods and it is "appropriately" defined
in the repository, you can say either:

 Move "ABC" to prop-1 of class-2
      or
  Move prop-1 of class-2 to some-data-item

(you can also use "object references" where this example uses the
class-name)

This is called an "object property" and is defined on page 99 of the FCD.
```

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <3ac74711.79961302@news1.attglobal.net>...
>On Mon, 26 Mar 2001 21:53:08 +0800, Joseph J Katnic
><josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:

You missed the point here Thane. (And I think I disagree with what you are
saying also...)

We were talking about getting/setting STRUCTURES (multiple properties) with
a single GET or SET. This can certainly be done. You may have to write the
Method...

I'm not sure what you mean by implicit use of GET/SET...

If I establish an Object Reference to an Object, I have programs which can
reference the properties without using GET/SET provided they are public
properties.

I can do it with a straight COBOL MOVE.

move "property" of  Object Reference  to whatever...

Now, it is possible that the facilities of OLE are being invoked under the
covers, but the fact remains that a COBOL programmer (well, a PowerCOBOL
programmer, actually) does not need to use GET/SET.

If you use the *OLE Class provided by Fujitsu, then I agree you MUST use GET
and SET, but this is a requirement of the OLE Class, NOT OO COBOL.

<remainder snipped, as no issue...>


Pete.

<a id="t-e90ec3595c"></a>
## Thread: What's differernt between Micro Focus COBOL and Fujitsu COBOL V4 ? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### What's differernt between Micro Focus COBOL and Fujitsu COBOL V4 ?

- **From:** "Calvin Chan" <calvin@tokyo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T23:44:24+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9a7idc$qgj8@imsp212.netvigator.com>`

```
What's differernt between Micro Focus COBOL and Micro Focus COBOL ?

Make sure Micro Focus COBOL program can be complied and run in Micro Focus
COBOL ????
```

#### ↳ Re: What's differernt between Micro Focus COBOL and Fujitsu COBOL V4 ?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T17:51:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC76A66.97880083@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9a7idc$qgj8@imsp212.netvigator.com>`

```


Calvin Chan wrote:

> What's differernt between Micro Focus COBOL and Micro Focus COBOL ?
>
> Make sure Micro Focus COBOL program can be complied and run in Micro Focus
> COBOL ????

Started to answer this - then thought I'd gone nuts ! Your text keeps saying
Micro Focus - whereas the title mentions Fujitsu V 4 <G>

Unfortunately there's no quick answer other than 'suck it and see'. Let's
assume you are asking about the difference between Fujitsu V4 and Net Express
which is the LATEST Micro Focus. (There are two here who have both F/J and N/E
- Judson and Thane - perhaps they can expand).

If you restrict yourself to the STANDARD '74 or '85 features - don't use
vendor extensions - it's a fair bet it will compile cleanly, either direction.
(Just recently I took a Fujitsu V ????? Structured program written by Thane
and compiled it cleanly in Net Express).

Now if you move up to what we refer to as the '97 Standard, (includes
Intrinsic functions etc.), that's a slightly grey area, but really boils down
to the EXTENSIONS the respective vendors have introduced. Both allow for
CBL_FILE routines - but I'm guessing they probably have different syntax and
possibly return different results (?). Similarly both as an extension have
Screen Section - I know Merant has CBL_screenAttributes and CBL_moveMouse
routines; I don't know if F/J has equivalents - If the answer is 'Yes' - it's
a fair bet that they are different

Both allow for using comp-5 - though I think they are different. Merant(Micro
Focus), as an extension allows you to code :-

78 ThisIsMyConstant                   value 22.
78 MySecondConstant                value 'Hello World'.

01 MyRecord                                pic x(ThisIsMyConstant).

Note : the examples above are replaced by the NEW syntax CONSTANT in the draft
of COBOL 200X.

The above is just a 'sampling' which immediately springs to mind.

Chances are, using an 'up to date' coded program in one or the other - you are
going to get an hiccup when you try to convert.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: What's differernt between Micro Focus COBOL and Fujitsu COBOL V4 ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T17:15:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9a899v$360$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9a7idc$qgj8@imsp212.netvigator.com>`

```
Are you going from Micro Focus to Fujitsu - or Fujitsu to Micro Focus?
There are different problems depending on which way you are "going"?  Either
way, the place that I would start would be with the Fujitsu manual called,

 "Micro Focus to Fujitsu User's Guide"

which is available (online) from the Fujitsu web page (I believe)
```

Luis Gomez-Almeida wrote:

> I agree with the statements below. While saying that "programming is not
> difficult" is clearly an exageration (probably with the intention of
> increasing the effect), what is true is that the main issue is to understand
> the business.

It depends on the definition of "programming".  A good programmer can take
accurate specs (the only detail needed is the business rules) and code half
asleep, waking up a bit to write a good test plan.   The trick is getting the
good specs, and that's where we need to understand the business.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Programmers wanted

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Keith Odam <0dam-k@iname.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T07:25:18+09:30
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACB9846.B02047FB@iname.com>`
- **References:** `<mfa4ctclr1covtu517f3o0i0g8r5ia2lei@4ax.com> <20010328224608.19033.00003984@ng-ci1.aol.com> <R7Gw6.562984$JT5.15589588@news20.bellglobal.com> <9a0reb$f35$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`

```
I feel someone who has learnt to program is "a coder".
  A "programmer" needs to understand systems analysis and design.

  My son's course includes the subject "Computer Programming" in
which they learn C.  Java is not a subject in its own right, but
a by-product of the course "Network Programming in Java".

  COBOL is the by-product of courses like "Systems Engineering
Life Cycle and Systems Maintenance".  It may sound pretentious,
but it seems to emphasise that the programs written in COBOL are
components in a system rather than as some coders seem to think
the program they have written is the be-all and end-all.
  This course includes the lovely definition:  "Existing systems
which must be maintained are called legacy systems". (From the
lecture notes distributed by Roger Tagg (based on Ian
Sommerville Software Engineering, 5th edition, 1995).)
  "must be maintained" doesn't mean they fail and need fixing,
of course, but that they are the systems which must run or the
company is out of business.

Luis Gomez-Almeida wrote:
> I agree with the statements below. While saying that "programming
> is not difficult" is clearly an exageration (probably with the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> regards to the fact that what is needed is a railway bridge?
[ ... ]
> > "YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
> > news:20010328224608.19033.00003984@ng-ci1.aol.com...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > why of what changes or enhancements are being made. Ah well :)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACBC18D.AB6CB576@home.com>`
- **References:** `<mfa4ctclr1covtu517f3o0i0g8r5ia2lei@4ax.com> <20010328224608.19033.00003984@ng-ci1.aol.com> <R7Gw6.562984$JT5.15589588@news20.bellglobal.com> <9a0reb$f35$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <3ACB9846.B02047FB@iname.com>`

```


Keith Odam wrote:

> I feel someone who has learnt to program is "a coder".
>   A "programmer" needs to understand systems analysis and design.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the program they have written is the be-all and end-all.<snip>

I've been reticent to jump in on this one - BUT - just for the hell of
it !

When Noah was still collecting wood for the Ark, and after Alan Turing
et al. had done their thing on ADP, around about 1960 it became viable
for the new magic boxes to be used commercially, at a reasonable price,
(still dear, but at a reasonable price).

In the experimental stuff you had 'boffins, beards and Jesus boots
(sandals) who loved playing with ones and noughts. (Jeepers - when I
first got into this racket there was an article in the UK papers, 'Ford
Motor Company were employing a nurse to look after the well being of the
'freaks' who were programming") So when it came to staffing these new
computer installations - from whence were you going to draw staff ? Well
of course, take a bunch of bright young kids, mathematically inclined,
(most definitely NOT yours truly), train them in machine code and then
assembler - hey presto - programmers ! Whoa there - waddya gonna program
? Who can 'think' how to get debits and credits into one of these
'funny' boxes

So we now have a Systems Analyst, (as the title originally meant).
People who could 'analyse' the business problem and by God and by
guessing, attempt to translate it into some meaningful program
specification that those youngsters could use as a base. The source for
these analysts, not totally, but primarily people in accounting who had
done their penance pushing around sheaves of paper - and were thinking
there's got to be a better way.

It's not an exaggeration to say that early analysts were systems
designers, politicians, psychologists etc. No they couldn't produce you
a diploma to prove all these things, but they sure as hell could show
you the bruises ! OK, so they all had different qualities going from
excellent to lousy. The young guys doing the programming, "What do those
jerks know ?". Well most did know - and it invariably was the 'long
history' of getting an application off the ground, often doing a lot of
fighting with users - including in those days numerous Luddites.

Of course it's changed now, with a mixture of job titles,
analyst/programmer, application programmer etc., (take your pick), and
no longer being in the 'big commercial world' I get the impression the
original concept of a 'systems analyst' has disappeared. Certainly by
melding the two job titles, it offers a more attractive career path to
the young upcoming programmers.

Maintenance - Y-U-C-K !  Fortunately I only have to change my own goofs.
But Paul, tackling 4 to 6 different applications per day - my deepest
sympathies. Eileen, one application that she has got her teeth into, and
is learning it back to front and upside down.  I think Eileen's case
represents an interesting situation - if she is solely devoted to
maintaining this particular 'beast', isn't this a case where perhaps
somebody should say, "We've got to bite the bullet and rewrite the damn
thing. It's costing us a fortune just maintaining it, our corporate
marketing philosphy has long since changed, and a review and rewrite may
well be cheaper, in the long run".

Not knowing how the game is currently played, i.e. analyst v programmer
role - just how many of you, if your efforts are primarily programming,
have the opportunity to get to comprehend the total business problem.

In the cae of those who have branched out on their own, using titles
like 'consultant etc., it is understood. They have had to, successfully
or otherwise, embrace both analysis and programming. But the rest of you
folks...........?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Programmers wanted

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T04:31:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

>From: "James J. Gavan" jjgavan@home.com 
>Date: 4/4/01 5:52 PM Pacific Daylight Time

> I think Eileen's case
>represents an interesting situation - if she is solely devoted to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Actually Jimmy I was brought aboard with 2 others in late 1995 to rewrite the
beast, and let me tell you rewrite we did.  I did all the on-line and when the
other 2 were given the boot finished all the batch.  In the on-line programs I
had at least 20 that didn't use a map perse - they used cursor positions!  They
hadn't been maintained or enhanced since 1989 because of that - rewrote those
maps and all.

I took me almost 6 months to rewrite the heart of the beast - taking the thing
from 8800 lines of code to 3200 and that was about 2 years ago. 

Right now I'm salivating over rewriting part of another system that's about to
collapse under it's own weight of bandaids.  I just have to convince the higher
up that it would be worth the effort.  I do do maintenance on that system at
times and sneak in a bit of rewrite each time :).  Not much - but at least it
eases it's pain a bit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Programmers wanted

- **From:** Merlin43PhD@netscape.net (Dwight Scott Miller)
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T17:50:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-BjBuC03mfq7p@localhost>`
- **References:** `<99rt6c$qdh$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <78pw6.1001$cI1.7824@iad-read.news.verio.net> <mfa4ctclr1covtu517f3o0i0g8r5ia2lei@4ax.com>`

```
On Sun, 28 Mar 3901 20:22:47, Binyamin Dissen 
<postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

> I doubt it.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
YES! YES! YES!

I get constant griping from my introductory computer classes that I am 
asking too much of them.  They want to know why I ask them questions 
like, "How many of my stores in Florida are in the Site Survey stage?" 
instead of "What do you right click on to get the menu to left click on 
to get the submenu to right click on to..."

I had never sat down at a PC running Microsoft code until about two 
weeks before the Fall 2000 semester started and now I am teaching Mickey
based classes, employed as the database czar in a Mickeysoft Access 
shop, and programming many wondrous things in Fujitsu COBOL with Flexus 
SP2  (a really *nice* COBOL GUI) on various Windoze machines.

How am I doing these things and getting high praise and reasonable 
money?  BECAUSE I HAVE 40 YEARS OF EXPERIENCE IN MANUFACTURING, 
MILITARY, AND ACADEMIC SYSTEMS!  My PhD is in applied queueing theory 
and I am a kick butte data miner.  I don't know squat about C++ or Java,
but IF I NEED TO, I CAN LEARN THEM IN LESS THAN 6 WEEKS.

I like COBOL.  It is easy to read. It is easy to take up sombody else's 
code and run with it.  It is well implemented on a plethora of platforms
(pardon the alliteration), and it is mutable enough to absorb the most 
esoteric advances in comp. sci. theory.
```

wrote:

##### ↳ ↳ Re: uncompile cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T20:51:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9a8lqm$jfv$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<OrMx6.115$233.323@coltserv-news.interno.es.colt.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-dFT6EJVddZTE@tcpserver>`

```
The question of converting "object code" to COBOL source code is one that
comes up occasionally.  Depending on the platform (operating system and
compiler) THERE may be ways (services) to do this.  HOWEVER, the original
note seemed to indicate that the person asking the question didn't even know
what compiler was used to create the object code.  In that case, I think
things are "pretty hopeless".

If you DO know the compiler and operating system, then "repost" with that
information and we MIGHT be able to help.

P.S.  If all you want to know is which compiler was used, you might want to
look at your object code in hex/ASCII "conversion" mode and see if any
"eye-catchers" happen to include the vendor name.  This does happen with
some (most?) object code - but is certainly no guarantee.
```

>
> P.S.  If all you want to know is which compiler was used, you might
want to
> look at your object code in hex/ASCII "conversion" mode and see if
any
> "eye-catchers" happen to include the vendor name.  This does happen
with
> some (most?) object code - but is certainly no guarantee.

That will almost certainly work to identify the compiler used. We
wrote a routine to 'sanitize' EXE and DLL code by removing the word
"COBOL" everywhere it appears just because the compiler makers were so
friggin' proud of their handiwork they included the name of their
company over 50 times in one runtime DLL.

Talk about program bloat. Feh!
```

Bill, this topic comes up in most of the BASIC-language newsgroups as well.
My usual response is the best way to do this is bite the bullet and restore
your backup.

Second choice is a quote which came originally from Dave Navarro, Jr.,
formerly of PowerBASIC, Inc and now with WebGeek Communications: " Trying to
create source code from compiled code is like trying to convert 'hamburger'
back into 'cow.'"


MCM

- **References:** `<OrMx6.115$233.323@coltserv-news.interno.es.colt.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-dFT6EJVddZTE@tcpserver> <9a8lqm$jfv$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <oH4y6.1971$Ln6.228145@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
>Bill, this topic comes up in most of the BASIC-language newsgroups as well.
>My usual response is the best way to do this is bite the bullet and restore
>your backup.

>Second choice is a quote which came originally from Dave Navarro, Jr.,
>formerly of PowerBASIC, Inc and now with WebGeek Communications: " Trying to
>create source code from compiled code is like trying to convert 'hamburger'
>back into 'cow.'"

yea, if i had a backup, that'd be the first thing i'd do with object
code. but assuming this is a cobol newsgroup, and some modules may no
longer even exist, perhaps the best way for the original poster to
handle the problem is to make it assembler, then move from assembler
to system and database calls(which are usuaully the majority of cobol
code) and take the rest and do a hand convert (keep in mind the types
of variables also).

after you get it to the db/os level, you can convert (with some
certainty) the rest to C, which is a few abstractions under cobol.
then move from c to cobol.

of course, if you know what calls the object code, you can give that
info to the programmer re-building the code, so they have some clue of
what they are doing. heck, they may even be able to just build new
code and "throw" errors by email, and shutdown the server on a regular
level when something unexpected comes up.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com 
remove the spam

points in agreement snipped

spambots pick up these spambots addys

nonesofar
```

<a id="t-cc8344a99d"></a>
## Thread: One week left - for comments on draft COBOL Standard (in US) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### One week left - for comments on draft COBOL Standard (in US)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-01T21:08:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9a8n00$jjp$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI - there is now just ONE WEEK left to get your comments in (if any) on
the draft of the next COBOL Standard (if you want to submit them as US
comments - comments in other countries may have different "due" dates).

The deadline in the US is April 9, 2001.

Given past experience/input, it would be my best *guess* that:

 A) This draft *will* be approved as it is at the end of the review (and
response) period.
 B) That there will be a MINIMUM of 5 (and more likely 10 or more) years
until another "Full revision" is done.  (Although "individual"
features/functions may WELL be added before that)
 C) When/if any "corrections" to problems that are missed in this review
period MIGHT be fixed, is totally unknown (but highly unlikely before
2004/2005 - at the EARLIEST).
 D) Which vendors will actually implement this standard (in part or whole)
and when is unknown at this time; similarly it is unknown when (if ever)
such vendors would STOP supporting the current "standard" or
version/releases of their COBOL products.

If you are interested in how to "see" the process for reviewing and making
comments, I would like to point you to an earlier "article" that I did that
can still be seen at:

 http://www.cobolreport.com/columnists/bill/01222001.htm

As with the "reaffirmation" of the current COBOL Standard, it does NOT hurt
to send in a comment just saying that you want the whole thing approved or
that you don't.  It is MOST useful if you can give "specific" comments (on
problems especially) - but that isn't a requirement. Comments on "new"
features that you would like added are HIGHLY unlikely to be accepted for
this revision - but may be added to a "candidate" list for the next
revision.  Items that you want REMOVED from this revision are also medium
unlikely to be removed, but that is still a THEORETICAL possibility.  Things
that are optional (Processor Dependent) would probably NOT be added to the
"required" list; things that are currently required *might* (but probably
would NOT) be put in the optional (processor dependent) list.

Bottom-Line:
  What you see is what you will get
     or more accurately
  What you don't look at now, is what you will STILL be getting (so why not
look now).
```

#### ↳ Re: Anyone moving their system to Linux from Microfocus Cobol on SCO Open Server ?

- **From:** qIroS <qIroS@tlhIngan.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T18:52:25+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mbehct48p9oj7drftarqd4ick5v3j4ge3h@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3ac82b13@nexus.comcen.com.au>`

```
ghItlh "Stephen B." <steveno@spamcfs.com.au> 

>Anyone moving their system to Linux from Microfocus Cobol on SCO Open
>Server?
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have no idea about licensing as it's a commercial issue and that's
another department.

However, during an experiment in late 1999, [that was never taken
forward] I moved a very big product (around 30 million lines of COBOL,
I think) from OCDS 4.1 on mainstream unix (Dynix actually) to OCDS 4.1
on Linux with not one single COBOL platform issue [many many database
access issues but that's another story; the COBOL itself was fine].
The level of compatibility is very high. You should be aware of
endianess issues though if your SCO system is non intel (and if you
have any code which relies on this such as pointer manipulation or
reference modified or redefined access to binary storage).

There were issues with a whole bunch (around 15,000) lines of C code
that was linked into the runtime. The C code needed about 40 hours
work to move it from unix vendor specific C to something GCC was happy
with, and most of this was tinkering with the screen handling (which
uses curses/ncurses).

Not too shabby really. 

Really though, we need a good compatible open source COBOL on
GNU/Linux. Tiny Cobol is getting there but it's far from complete.
I'm trying marginally (i.e. not) successfully to contribute to Tims
GCC project (if only I could consistently get the EXISTING work to
build!!).

Of course, as usual, YMMV.
```

## Thread: Covert COBOL Computational Field To Numeric Field In FoxPro — 4 this month / 12 total

_Thread started [2001-03](2001-03.md#t-74f05b8884)._

#### ↳ Re: Covert COBOL Computational Field To Numeric Field In FoxPro

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T15:41:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.databases.xbase.fox,microsoft.public.fox.programmer.exchange
- **Message-ID:** `<9aa6j1$f3u$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<tbmvaplgjd9d8e@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <tbmvaplgjd9d8e@corp.supernews.com>, Billy Moeller
<bmoeller@pldi.net> writes:
>I am converting a Mainframe COBOL program to PC Visual FoxPro.  There is
>COMPUTATIONAL field in COBOL that I need converted to NUMERIC field in
>FoxPro.  If there is anybody that could help I would appreciate it.
>
If you have a PIC 9(11) COMP that makes one question what kind of COMP field
it is.  For instance COMP-2, COMP-3?
If it is a packed number, I would guess COMP-3. An 11 digit COMP-3 takes up
only 6 character positions.  Where as a normal pic 9(11) takes up at least
11 positions, so you have to move the COMP-3 to a PIC 9(11).  Just redefining
it gets you a bunch of garbage.  Sometimes we redefine it as alpha and sort
it as alpha to make it sort faster, but you wouldnt be able to read it.


>WORKING STORAGE
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>


 -----  Posted via NewsOne.Net: Free (anonymous) Usenet News via the Web  -----
  http://newsone.net/ -- Free reading and anonymous posting to 60,000+ groups
   NewsOne.Net prohibits users from posting spam.  If this or other posts
made through NewsOne.Net violate posting guidelines, email abuse@newsone.net
```

>>FoxPro.  If there is anybody that could help I would appreciate it.

If you have exact specifications as to what you need done I would be
willing to help you.

- Rick C. Hodgin

> If you have a PIC 9(11) COMP that makes one question what kind of COMP
field
> it is.  For instance COMP-2, COMP-3?
> If it is a packed number, I would guess COMP-3. An 11 digit COMP-3 takes
up
> only 6 character positions.  Where as a normal pic 9(11) takes up at least
> 11 positions, so you have to move the COMP-3 to a PIC 9(11).  Just
redefining
> it gets you a bunch of garbage.  Sometimes we redefine it as alpha and
sort
> it as alpha to make it sort faster, but you wouldnt be able to read it.
>

<a id="t-da8fcc8a29"></a>
## Thread: Month table??? kinda long — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Month table??? kinda long

- **From:** "Seekers462" <Seekers462@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T21:25:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m76y6.6388$rk4.483742@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

I am trying to write a program that has a month table as an embedded table.
Ex:
02                                   PICX(3) VALUE "JAN".
Etc....
up to the Dec listed in the table.
The table then has this below:
01 MONTH-TABLE REDEFINES MONTH-TABLE-DATA.
     02 MT-MONTH OCCURS  12 TIMES           PIC X(3).

I am trying to insert the month written out onto my output file as follows:
DD-MMM-YYYY.

The date is gathered from an input record and also have it listed in my
working storage area.

I need some ideas as to how to subscript it into an acceptable data that
will print out on paper. My teacher said to break it down within my input
file which is listed as follows:

01  CUSTOMER-RECORD.
      02  CR-DATE-PURCH                                  PIC9(8).

I am suppose to break the date down and include the subscript here but am
not clear on how to do it. The date on the dat file is listed as MMDDYYYY. I
can do the day part and the year part but how do I subscript the month? Does
it need to be done here or in another part? Any suggestions? Our book only
shows how to display on screen not write. Any help would be appreciated...

: )
```

#### ↳ Re: Month table??? kinda long

- **From:** qIroS <qIroS@tlhIngan.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T22:52:48+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ssshctsavb45j4thdjf2uqncs1b94p2jt9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<m76y6.6388$rk4.483742@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
ghItlh "Seekers462" <Seekers462@hotmail.com> 

>
>I am trying to write a program that has a month table as an embedded table.
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>: )

I'm not sure why you couldn't do:

01 CUSTOMER-RECORD.
   02 CR-DATE-PURCH.
      03 CR-DATE-PURCH-MM   PIC 99.
      03 CR-DATE-PURCH-DD   PIC 99.
      03 CR-DATE-PURCH-YYYY PIC 9999.

or if you absolutely must keep CR-DATE-PURCH as a display numeric
type:

01 CUSTOMER-RECORD.
   02 CR-DATE-PURCH PIC 9(8).
   02 CR-DATE-PURCH-MMDDYYYY redefines CR-DATE-PURCH.
      03 CR-DATE-PURCH-MM   PIC 99.
      03 CR-DATE-PURCH-DD   PIC 99.
      03 CR-DATE-PURCH-YYYY PIC 9999.

then you can use CR-DATE-PURCH-MM as a subscript for your table.

It might also be good if you did numeric first, and then range checks
second on these values in case you have something stupid on your file;
You don't want a S0C4 or whatever you might get from an out of bounds
table access on your platform.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Month table??? kinda long

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T01:16:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cw9y6.212$jz.15685@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<m76y6.6388$rk4.483742@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <ssshctsavb45j4thdjf2uqncs1b94p2jt9@4ax.com>`

```
Adding to what qIroS wrote, you might want to rename or redefine
CR-DATE-PURCH-MM again to something more manageable, like MSUB, or move it to
another field called MSUB. It makes the subscripted moves, etc. less cumbersome.


Jack 



In article <ssshctsavb45j4thdjf2uqncs1b94p2jt9@4ax.com>, qIroS says...
>
>ghItlh "Seekers462" <Seekers462@hotmail.com>

```

#### ↳ Re: Month table??? kinda long

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T22:33:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC8FDD8.E52E25B3@home.com>`
- **References:** `<m76y6.6388$rk4.483742@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


Seekers462 wrote:

> I am trying to write a program that has a month table as an embedded table.
> Ex:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> shows how to display on screen not write. Any help would be appreciated...

As suggested in the earlier reply you could breakdown the reference to the date
into d/m/y levels. But where you are really stuck is comprehending tables -
isn't it.
So two variations on a theme :-

01 MonthTable1 pic x(36) value "JanFebMarAprMayJunJulAugSepOctNovDec".
01 MonthTable2 redefines MonthTable1.
    05 MonthName occurs 12 pic x(03)..

OR :-

01 MonthTable1.
    05 pic x(03) value "Jan".
    05 pic x(03) value  "Feb"
    05 pic x.......
    05 ........
    05 pic x(03) value "Dec".
01 MonthTable2 redefines MonthTable1.
    05 MonthName occurs 12 pic x(03)..

Obviously the first is quicker - shorthand. But if using the first it can get a
little misleading, if you are using numerics. Use commas, colons, semi-colons,
exclamation marks or whatever, so your eye can visually split the values.

01 PayRate1 pic x(24) value "10.50,11.50,12.50,13.50,".
01 PayRate2 redefines PayRate1.
     05 Rate occurs 4.
          10 HourlyRate pic 99.99.
           10 filler           pic x.            *> You don't give a damn what's
in this pic x

So back to your date problem. Good idea to do what is referred to as Xtreme
Programming (1) is the input-date numeric - if No do something (2) is the Month
between 1 and 12 - if No do something.

Given you've got a valid month :

    If your input date is '03292001' then :-

    move MonthName (CR-PURCH-MONTH) to ............, which is
   ( move MonthName (03) to .........)

Your book will actually tell you more than you think <G> - you haven't latched
on to the words to search for. Check the index for TABLES, (Arrays in other
languages), and SUBSCRIPTS/SUBSCRIPTING.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Month table??? kinda long

- **From:** awkisall <awkisall@qwest.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T23:55:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC973F9.32250735@qwest.net>`
- **References:** `<m76y6.6388$rk4.483742@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
A simple month array might go:

01 WS-MONTH-ARRAY.
    05 FILLER         PIC X(03)   VALUE  'JAN'
    05 FILLER         PIC X(03)   VALUE  'FEB'
      .
      .
      .
    05 FILLER         PIC X(03)   VALUE  'DEC'
01 WS-MONTH-ARRAY-R   REDEFINES WS-MONTH-ARRAY.
    05 WS-MONTH       PIC X(03) OCCURS 12.

To subscript make a working-storage item (MONTH-SUB?) that can handle the
occurances, ideally with a binary halfword pic.

To re-arrange input dates break them down into 03 (preferably 15) level elements
FD-MM
                   FD-DD
                   FD-CCYY

then move these elements to elements of an WS field with elements under an 01
level layed out as WS-DD
                                        WS-MM
                                        WS-CCYYY
(they need unique identifiers).

You might not need this intermediate step, but...

then use your month array and your FD or re-arranged WS date field to populate
another (your) DD-MMM-YYYY output field, once again moving element to element
under an 01.

MOVE WS-MM                  TO WS-MONTH-SUB

MOVE WS-DD                  TO OT-DD
MOVE WS-MONTH (MONTH-SUB)   TO OT-MMM
MOVE WS-CCYY                TO OT-CCYY








Seekers462 wrote:

> I am trying to write a program that has a month table as an embedded table.
> Ex:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> others here for?
```

what works best...thanks...
: )

## Thread: Student needs a simple answer — 15 this month / 21 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Student needs a simple answer

- **From:** "Gary Crowley" <gc817@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T22:59:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lv7y6.24030$PR.200487@news1.wwck1.ri.home.com>`

```
Gentlemen (and technobabble whizzes):

I'm starting my 1st semester in Cobol programming. While I'm not a computer
science major (CIS major), I have just one question to ask the holy sages of
Cobol world..... when writing a program to create a payroll report, I
declare input variables that are in the data files (employee-no, emp-name,
hours-worked, and hourly-pay). Now, I am having such a brainfart right now
about where to declare my output variables (gross pay and tax-amount). Is it
suppose to be in the file section or the working-storage section??? Could
some programming master show me the way to the light at the end of the
tunnel??? I'm just beginning to dive into the Cobol language. Even though my
college does not require CIS students to take advance Cobol, I intend to
take it to increase my edge when applying for jobs when I graduate.
BTW, I nearly split my sides reading some of the previous news article about
Moses breaking his back and cursing as he kicked the 11th commandment down a
precipice...(very funny Mr. Dashwood)

Regards,

G. Crowley
```

#### ↳ Re: Student needs a simple answer

- **From:** ar656@freenet.carleton.ca (Luis Gomez-Almeida)
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T20:13:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ab4f4$8l8$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`
- **References:** `<lv7y6.24030$PR.200487@news1.wwck1.ri.home.com>`

```
First of all, in COBOL there are gentlemen and ladies.  Or guys and
guyettes, if you prefer.

Variables on files (input or putput) are declared with the files.  data that
is used in the program, but not part of any file is declared in working
storage.

Regards,
Luis

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Student needs a simple answer

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T01:00:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1h9y6.209$jz.14745@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<lv7y6.24030$PR.200487@news1.wwck1.ri.home.com> <9ab4f4$8l8$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`

```
Hi Gary,

As you will learn, or probably have already learned, there's usually more than
one solution to every programming problem. So, here goes.

You can also code your input/output data descriptions in WS rather than the FD.

But when you read/write the rec you use into/from:

read ip-file into ws-ip-rec
write op-rec  from ws-op-rec

In this case, when you define the rec in the FD you usually code it:

01  fd-ip-rec         pic x(nnn).

There's no need for detail because you've provided it in the WS rec description.


There are advantages/disadvantages to this approach:

You can access the data in WS before the file is opened and after it's closed or
reaches EOF; in certain stuations this can be helpful.

A move to the FD rec to/from the WS rec is generated by the compiler, making the
I/O less efficient.   

Now, aren't you sorry you asked? ;-) Good luck.

Jack


In article <9ab4f4$8l8$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>, Luis Gomez-Almeida says...
>
>First of all, in COBOL there are gentlemen and ladies.  Or guys and
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student needs a simple answer

- **From:** ar656@freenet.carleton.ca (Luis Gomez-Almeida)
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T22:40:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9abd20$m04$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`
- **References:** `<lv7y6.24030$PR.200487@news1.wwck1.ri.home.com> <9ab4f4$8l8$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <1h9y6.209$jz.14745@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Actually, the solution given by John is highly recommended in some shops.
In the rest is mandatory. The reason for this is that if the program abends
(abnormally ends, or just craps out) whatever was in the record being read
by the program might not show in the dump, but what is in working storage
does.

I am convinced that these answers will confuse you even more, but that is
what we are here for :)

Regards,
Luis

Steve

Well for starters, full marks for being a smooth bastard  coming on with an

> Gentlemen (and technobabble whizzes):
>

Now for the simple answer. The 12th Commandment is 'Thou shalt do thine own

#### ↳ Re: Student needs a simple answer

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T07:12:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC9CC42.D3302978@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<lv7y6.24030$PR.200487@news1.wwck1.ri.home.com>`

```


Gary Crowley wrote:

> Gentlemen (and technobabble whizzes):
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> G. Crowley

If you're writing a file, it doesn't make any difference anymore, although lots
of shops still have a standard of using working storage, and that's what I use.
If you're writing a report, almost everybody uses working storage.   I don't use
Report Writer, so here's an example of creating output to a report.

01   R1-HEADER1.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(10)    VALUE SPACES.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(12)    VALUE '    GROSS'.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(05)    VALUE SPACES.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(12)     VALUE '     TAX'.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(10)     VALUE SPACES.

01   R1-HEADER2.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(10)    VALUE SPACES.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(12)    VALUE '     PAY '.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(05)    VALUE SPACES.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(12)     VALUE '   AMOUNT'.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(10)     VALUE SPACES.

01   R1-DETAIL.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(10)    VALUE SPACES.
      05   R1-GROSS-PAY    PIC   $,$$$,$$$.99.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(05)    VALUE SPACES.
      05   R1-TAX-AMOUNT  PIC $,$$$,$$$.99.
      05   FILLER        PIC X(10)     VALUE SPACES.

01  LINE-COUNT      PIC 999        VALUE 100.

...

MOVE GROSS-PAY      TO R1-GROSS-PAY.
MOVE TAX-AMOUNT TO R1-TAX-AMOUNT.

IF LINE-COUNT > 66
      WRITE OUTPUT-RECORD FROM R1-HEADING1 AFTER ADVANCING PAGE
      WRITE OUTPUT-RECORD FROM R1-HEADING2 AFTER ADVANCING 1
      MOVE SPACES TO OUTPUT-RECORD
      WRITE OUTPUT-RECORD AFTER ADVANCING 1
      MOVE 3        TO LINE-COUNT
END-IF

ADD 1        TO LINE-COUNT.
WRITE OUTPUT-RECORD FROM R1-DETAIL AFTER ADVANCING 1.


Note, if you only have one format for the output line, the 05 levels could just
as easily have been in the File description.  (At one time this caused
troubles).
```

#### ↳ Re: Student needs a simple answer

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-04T14:18:49+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aca8a18_1@my.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<lv7y6.24030$PR.200487@news1.wwck1.ri.home.com>`

```

Gary Crowley wrote in message ...
>Gentlemen (and technobabble whizzes):
>
Ha! Totally missed the Ladies...but that's Ok; Don reckons they're zeros
anyway...<G>

(You met my ex, Don...?)

>I'm starting my 1st semester in Cobol programming. While I'm not a computer
>science major (CIS major), I have just one question to ask the holy sages
of
>Cobol world.....

Now be very careful. The Oracle may answer spuriously, or in a riddle, or it
might deliberately mislead... bringing gifts for the Oracle is good...

>when writing a program to create a payroll report, I
>declare input variables that are in the data files (employee-no, emp-name,
>hours-worked, and hourly-pay).

Yes, Glasshopper, you got that right.

>Now, I am having such a brainfart right now
>about where to declare my output variables (gross pay and tax-amount). Is
it
>suppose to be in the file section or the working-storage section???

Ah, Glasshopper, this is NOT a simple question. But then, to be fair, you
said you wanted a simple ANSWER, you never promised the question would be
simple.

You have seen many answers to your question here. The Learned Men have
consulted the Holy Books... and told you to do your own homework (well, at
least one of them has, and not the one who usually does...<Are you OK, Doc?
still with us..?>) While this advice may be unpalatable it is sound.

However, to solve your immediate problem consider the following koan:

"All the citizens of Fileio live in neatly ordered streets, in little
houses, which have numbers on the letter boxes.

There are two kinds of citizens, based on the direction their lives are
taking; the Inners and the Outers. They prefer to keep themselves to
themselves and so the Inner community lives across town from the Outer
community.

However, in the middle of the community is a wild strip of unsettled land
which also has some houses on it. These houses tend to be derelict as they
were never built to last. They actually represent previous attempts by the
Inners and Outers to change their directions.

As this would be Heresy in either community, people who feel the Great Call
to do this, go away quietly, removed from their neighbours and retreat to
the Wilderness.

Very occasionally, some citizens renounce both Innerhood and Outerhood and
become permanently resident in the Wilderness.  They can interact with other
Inners and Outers, who are really just "passing through", while they decide
their life direction in answer to the Great Call.

The wise Sages and Philosophers of Fileio have debated for Centuries the
question of who has got it right: Is it better to be an Inner, an Outer, or
a recluse in the Wilderness?

Given that you can live anywhere, where should you choose? Does it matter?
Why choose at all?"

Ponder this, Glasshopper, and your problem will be resolved.

>Could
>some programming master show me the way to the light at the end of the
>tunnel???

Truly it is written that the light at the end of the tunnel is from an
oncoming train...

>I'm just beginning to dive into the Cobol language. Even though my
>college does not require CIS students to take advance Cobol, I intend to
>take it to increase my edge when applying for jobs when I graduate.
>BTW, I nearly split my sides reading some of the previous news article
about
>Moses breaking his back and cursing as he kicked the 11th commandment down
a
>precipice...(very funny Mr. Dashwood)
>

"For there is no harm in laughter" - Sir Arthur Wing Pinero

Good luck with your studies,

Pete.

-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

<a id="t-5da00b904a"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu Cobol v6.1 — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Cobol v6.1

- **From:** jets@nbnet.nb.ca (Tony M. Mina)
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T23:54:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ac90e9a.5080611@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
I received my Enterprise v6.1 several weeks ago and haven't installed
yet due to several  horror stories I heard regarding the installation
and registration process. My main concern is the defrag. I spoke to a
certain Alex of the tech support group  about this and he acknowledged
that it might destroy the registration which invalidate the use of the
software. His solution is to call them to get another registration
key. I defrag my machine at least once a month and I cannot afford to
install the key everytime I defrag my  machine.

I do appreciate very much if anyone in this newgroup can post their
experiences with this product. I will not  install my copy until
Fujitsu can resolve this issue and I will return the product if I have
to.

Thanks

Tony M. Mina
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol v6.1

>I received my Enterprise v6.1 several weeks ago and haven't installed
>yet due to several  horror stories I heard regarding the installation
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

Is this really true? A product 'designed' for systems such as Windows
9x/ME that are based on the worst file system known to man for
fragmenting will burp and loose your registration details if you
defrag?!

Don't think I'd be using that product. Blimey even the NetExpress
license dosen't have a fit with a defrag. Now reinstalling windows;
There's a different kettle of ball games all together.

Use something else! IBM VA Cobol, Merant NX. Try and find another
product to fit your requirements. That's my opinion anyway.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol v6.1

- **From:** "Paulo Vieira" <pvieira@emporsoft.pt>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T11:14:16+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ac801$lb8$1@venus.telepac.pt>`
- **References:** `<3ac90e9a.5080611@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
Hi,
My saga with Fujitsu COBOL V6.1:
Got a copy on my mail box.
Decide where would I install it. You know, I work with 3 computers, althoug
with only one brain (the computers are located one at my office, another at
home and another in a laptop case). Since I live in Portugal which is known
for having the highest rates for phone calls among EU countries, I decided
not to risk being put on hold for half an hour in a call to the US, just to
get my installation moved from one machine to another; so I installed with
no activation key (evaluation install) just to get a look of the beast.
First try: take a look at the documentation: It wasn't there, just the
README files, no manuals or guides.
Second try: build a sample. No it doesn't build. A lot of .obj files are
missing after the installation.
First clever thing I did: Take a closer look at the new features included in
this release. Althoug I am a Fujitsu customer for several years now, I only
got to know that there as a new release coming by an email message which
arrived the very same day the CD did. So I browsed, browsed and browsed
again...
Second and most clever thing I did: Put the CD back in it's envelope and
forget the whole thing. I'l stick to my V5 until a .NET compiler comes and
then evaluate the situation again. If the quality of CRM at Fujitsu remains
the same, I'l just drop it.

Conclusion: I am in no way against a sort of protection in software. But I
feel betrayed (after having bought my first Fujitsu compiler for US $750,
and paying US $500 anualy for maintenance fees), to have to spend another US
$6,600 just to be able to work at home and perform some field maintenance to
my customers.

The following is taken directly from the FAQ at www.adtools .com

"3. There appears to be a lot of value in the Fujitsu COBOL package, yet
your prices seem to be a lot lower than your competitors? What's the catch?

Answer: Fujitsu believes that COBOL programmers deserve a reasonably priced
COBOL compiler with high performance, unmatched reliability, superior
scalability, and royalty free COBOL run-times.
"
I am not familiar with prices at other vendors, but come on "REASONABLY
PRICED" ? - those guys must be kiding.

Until the COBOL compiler vendors understand that their main competitors
aren't the other COBOL compiler manufacturers, COBOL will continue to loose
adepts and importance. Their true maket is problem solution, which already
has a lot of tools targeted to it. Expand the market instead of grabbing
market share!

Paulo Vieira
Programmer on business days :(
Sailor on weekends :)

"Tony M. Mina" <jets@nbnet.nb.ca> wrote in message
news:3ac90e9a.5080611@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca...
> I received my Enterprise v6.1 several weeks ago and haven't installed
> yet due to several  horror stories I heard regarding the installation
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol v6.1

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T11:41:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GFiy6.512602$Pm2.8219213@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3ac90e9a.5080611@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca> <9ac801$lb8$1@venus.telepac.pt>`

```
I have five machines on my desk.  When I buy a tool, I buy it so *I* can use
it, not so one of my computers can use it.  That is like buying a book that
I can only read with my left eye while standing in the kitchen on my left
foot.

If Fujitsu is so concerened about copyright, then why do they not simply cut
a fresh CD for each user, hard coded with something like "for the sole use
of Donald Tees", then prosecute if it is miss-used. It might cost them an
extra dollar per copy.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol v6.1

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T22:07:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ae16g$8pto$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ac90e9a.5080611@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca> <9ac801$lb8$1@venus.telepac.pt>`

```
Gee, someone else installed 6.1 and all of a sudden found that they could no
longer build?

Thane, do you want to jump into this?

As I had mentioned in a previous post, I just selected the "standard"
install option and all of a sudden, not only *.obj files were missing, but
*.dll files were missing also.  Yes, I can call Fujitsu support and try to
work through all this, but to be honest, we've licensed the product for 3+
years, and I'll be danged if I call support for some change in their install
procedure, since I've never had any problems in the past with upgrades.

Reminds me of a problem I had with Disney "Mickey Saves the Day Software"
for my 3 year old son.  I actually had to E-mail support three times to let
them know that the software wouldn't run on my PC (trust me, the PC I run
ain't exactly a Tandy 1000!).  After being told a number of times that
"there is definitely something wrong with my PC", one kind support person
from the Disney Interactive staff E-mailed me to let me know there was a
"patch" out on their Web site that I might want to try.  So I downloaded and
installed it, and lo and behold, it fixed whatever was wrong with my
hardware :~)  And this was after I basically decided to put the software on
the shelf.  Kudos go out to Disney on this one.  IMHO, when PC programmers
start learning that they cannot just arbitrarily load whatever they want
into the \\windows\system directory, we'll all be a lot better off!

And this is precisely why my home version of Fujitsu 6.1 is still sitting in
the mailer. I'm being extremely cautious based on what I ran into with the
version we license at my work place.  Plus being a mainframe kind of guy, to
paraphrase Arnold... "We'll be back".  Who needs this kind of aggravation?

Denny

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol v6.1

- **From:** "Basim Kadhim" <kadhim@fs.fujitsu.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T09:36:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ac9fa51$1@news01.bendcable.com>`
- **References:** `<3ac90e9a.5080611@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
Tony,

I apologize for our poor communication with you on this matter. Fujitsu's
software licensing mechanism is, in general, immune to the effects of
defragmentation utilities. The defragmentation utility included in
Symantec's Norton Utilities, called Speed Disk, is unfortunately one
exception. Speed Disk moves hidden licensing files, which causes license
loss. The licensing files are hidden system files that reside in the same
directory as the Fujitsu product. Even with Speed Disk, you can follow the
following steps to avoid license loss:

1. Open Speed Disk, and choose File->Options->Customize->Unmovable Files.
2. Specify that the *.ent, *.rst, and *.key files cannot be moved.
3. Choose File->Options->Optimization->Save to save the new profile.

You should now be able to safely defragment your disk with Speed Disk
without losing your Fujitsu COBOL license. Note that these procedures are
not necessary with most other defragmentation utilities, including the
default defragmenter available with Windows.

Basim Kadhim
Chief Architect, COBOL Product Group
Fujitsu Software

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol v6.1

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T21:38:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9pry6.1948$B84.182646@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3ac90e9a.5080611@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
According to the technical specifications and rationale of the copy
protection scheme used by Fujitsu, the install process scatters
several small hidden files around your disk - mostly in directories
with twelve or more current entries. The locations of these files are
remembered and their continued presence on your drive checked. If
these spoors are missing, well, you're toast.

This is but ONE of several techniques used in the Fujitsu copy
protection scheme, but I bet it's the one that is most sensitive to
defragmentation.

All in all, a most unsatisfactory situation.


"Tony M. Mina" <jets@nbnet.nb.ca> wrote in message
news:3ac90e9a.5080611@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca...
> I received my Enterprise v6.1 several weeks ago and haven't
installed
> yet due to several  horror stories I heard regarding the
installation
> and registration process. My main concern is the defrag. I spoke to
a
> certain Alex of the tech support group  about this and he
acknowledged
> that it might destroy the registration which invalidate the use of
the
> software. His solution is to call them to get another registration
> key. I defrag my machine at least once a month and I cannot afford
to
> install the key everytime I defrag my  machine.
>
> I do appreciate very much if anyone in this newgroup can post their
> experiences with this product. I will not  install my copy until
> Fujitsu can resolve this issue and I will return the product if I
have
> to.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol v6.1

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T18:10:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9adl60$ha8$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ac90e9a.5080611@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca> <9pry6.1948$B84.182646@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3ACA55C9.C969B230@bigfoot.com>`

```
I am still waiting for confirmation of this - BUT the information that I
have SO FAR is:

1) "normal" Windows "defrag" does *not* cause a problem.

2) any "defrag" that allows for the movement of "read-only" files MIGHT
cause a problem

3) some versions/releases of Norton Utilities *do* have an "option" for
moving read-only files.

I'll keep everyone "informed" if I get more "definitive" answers.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: BATCH COBOL/DB2 SUBROUTINE PROBLEM

- **From:** awkisall <awkisall@qwest.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-02T21:42:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC954C0.91C7FC8A@qwest.net>`
- **References:** `<3AB31D47.13A7611E@qwest.net> <3ab375d5_4@my.newsfeeds.com> <3ABE53A7.C48E3504@qwest.net> <3abee22f_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

Hello.  I sent the below last Friday but it never got posted. Am re-sending if
for no other reason than to make sure you receive my thanks.

Our meeting today was moved back to Thursday.  Meanwhile, our DBA sent me a
vituperous e-mail concerning my design (I'd asked him questions akin to those
asked here).  Ah! politics! Especially for a junior programmer trying who is
only trying to learn--COBOL and DB2 that is--but I guess I'm learning more.

The below:

Yes, we are batch processing.

You've helped a great deal.  Explaining the problem helped me too.  Obviously,
I'm new to DB2, have been attempting to write this little subroutine upon
receiving minimal specs from a senior analyst who's not much more familiar with
DB2 than I am (at least in batch), and my SQL was initially written with manuals

in front of me.

Ironically, my ideas have paralleled your suggestions.  After explaining what I
was doing, I thought of not closing the cursor as in your suggestion 2.1 (it
jumped out of the screen at me as I typed my 1, 2, 3 etc. outline--if I close
the cursor it's not going to be controlling anything!).

This worked, but, as you correctly stated, not in an ideal manner.  When the
first calling program is running, any other task attempting to use the held row
will wait, but only for 1 second of CPU time, then everything goes to hell.
After waiting for more than 1 second, the second program ends its call, somehow
managing an SQL return code of zero, starts writing checks at check #1, and of
course ABENDS because it is coded to do so if the check numbers aren't within a
certain range.  The calling program currently runs in less that 1 second CPU
time, so it "works", but this remains unacceptable.  And this is without the
possibilty of page locks, as you mentioned.  These would defeat the purpose of
the subroutine and table.

We'd also thought of a flag early on, but for some reason dismissed it in favor
of the row lock.  We have a meeting scheduled with our DBA for Monday, April 2.
I'm going to revisit possibly adding a flag column to the table.

JCL dependencies would be ideal were it not for the politics and human
(operators) elements involved.

Thanks again,

Greg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BATCH COBOL/DB2 SUBROUTINE PROBLEM

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T18:45:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACA1A03.9BAD509A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB31D47.13A7611E@qwest.net> <3ab375d5_4@my.newsfeeds.com> <3ABE53A7.C48E3504@qwest.net> <3abee22f_4@news.newsfeeds.com> <3AC954C0.91C7FC8A@qwest.net>`

```


awkisall wrote:

> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Absolutely nothing to do with your programming problem, but you are coming smack up
against that topic that they don't teach at school - People 101. There are good
guys, bad guys, and somewhere in the middle another category assholes, who really
aren't sure what they want to be. Unfortunately, just like the rest of us, you are
going to get burned, learning about people the hard way.

Let me give you a couple of examples, hilarious, but also tragic, if you think about
it. Like most military, the RAF has Annual Confidential Reports, an assessment of
each individual commissioned officer, to determine if he goes forward in his career
with a further promotion.

So me in Egypt 1951-54 at Middle East HQ. Sat in Iraq (way before Saddam) is Air
Officer Commanding Iraq,  Air Vice Marshal J.G. Hawtrey, (guess that makes him a 3
star general). Name doesn't matter, the old bastard is long dead. Somewhat
eccentric, the old bachelor even rode a horse to inspections complete with jodhpurs
- not part of standard RAF dress, nor was the goddam horse ! Anyway our AVM has the
final say, as local commander, on ALL reports for his officers.

On a Wing Commander (Lt.Col.) "As an officer this gentleman would make a good
farmer....."

On a Flight Lieutenant (1st Lt ???), "This young officer is mad about the Air Force,
mad about flying, mad about rugby, in fact quite mad ....."

I could go on........ It still happens today in the military on both sides of the
Atlantic, and even worse, occurs much too fequently in politics and commerce -
leading to bad planning of economies or even successful companies taking a nose
dive.

Just be aware that you are going to constantly, but regrettably, run into politics,
or prima donnas protecting their own turf, and as above, complete jerks. Carefully
make you own judgments, and for your career's sake, use reasoned arguments to make a
point but avoid head on clashes with 'superiors'. The alternative is of course to
move on if you come up against a brick wall.

I'm now 70 - so I really don't give a s----- what anybody thinks, although for the
most part, I follow the maxim 'Do unto others as they do unto you'. (Translation,
you want to be treated nicely, treat others nicely).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
