```
Thanks for the good laugh and sage advice.  After having read both volumes of IBM's
Application Programming and SQL Guide (there are those who consider me obsessive) and
receiving Mr Dashwood's suggestions, I believe I have sufficient well reasoned arguments
to fly the flag, as it were.

Is it do unto others as they would do to you? or wait until the right time to do to them
as they did you?

I lean heavily toward as they would do to you, but haven't really been thinking about
these sorts of questions lately, just been trying to figure out how to close up one
little gap in two otherwise elegant new programs (calling and called, the entire context
is breakthrough development in our environment) that will make our users very happy.

And I will continue to conduct myself accordingly. (Running a flag pattern:)

Ciao,

Greg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BATCH COBOL/DB2 SUBROUTINE PROBLEM

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-04T23:24:05+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3acb0b81_2@my.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB31D47.13A7611E@qwest.net> <3ab375d5_4@my.newsfeeds.com> <3ABE53A7.C48E3504@qwest.net> <3abee22f_4@news.newsfeeds.com> <3AC954C0.91C7FC8A@qwest.net>`

```
Greg, I was touched by your response as it caused me to remember a young man
in another lifetime who had very similar problems with a DBA (so decided to
solve THAT particular problem by becoming one...if nothing else it showed
the bastard that ANYONE could do his  precious job...<G>)

Years later, in another Country, I had a run in with another DBA because I
had designed an IMS Database with 4 levels of hierarchy (the software
supports 16) but she had been told on a course at IBM that three was the
number... It was like the Holy Hand Grenade for those of you who are Monty
Python fans..."Three shall be the number...Not 2 or 4..the number shall be
3...". This was a multi-million dollar system for a huge international Bank;
I spent 4 months sweating the design and then she just smiled and said "We
won't implement it; it violates our standards."

The fact that it NEEDED 4 levels was never an issue.

Three months later (after it going to the top man in the Bank (who would
have had no idea what DBA or IMS stands for) ) they approved the 4 levels
and she never spoke to me again...in fact she would turn around so as not to
pass me in a corridor (mind you I have had that same effect on other women
too...so maybe it wasn't just technical pride <G>)

You can see how stupid it becomes when the Boss (who should be spending his
time thinking about Third World debt) makes a decision he is hopelessly
unqualified to make, based on the fact that I am costing him a large amount
of money so I must know something... (He was right, of course...<G>)

Some comments below...

Pete.
awkisall wrote in message <3AC954C0.91C7FC8A@qwest.net>...
>"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:


>>>
>Hello.  I sent the below last Friday but it never got posted. Am re-sending
if
>for no other reason than to make sure you receive my thanks.
>
>Our meeting today was moved back to Thursday.  Meanwhile, our DBA sent me a
>vituperous e-mail concerning my design (I'd asked him questions akin to
those
>asked here).  Ah! politics! Especially for a junior programmer trying who
is
>only trying to learn--COBOL and DB2 that is--but I guess I'm learning more.
>
<<<
I appreciate your thanks, but I'd appreciate more 10 minutes alone with your
DBA...

Anyone who stifles or suppresses the enthusiasm or desire to learn of others
is an "enemy" of the corporation and should be dealt with as such... (On my
projects he'd get one private warning... and you'd get to sit down with him
and have your concerns addressed properly. Even more importantly, both of
you would be required to approach the Business and find out what the REAL
requirements and constraints on Cheque Processing are...Terrible if we
actually got to asking "customers" what was wanted and needed...Before you
know it the whole place would be thinking IT was a SERVICE...<G>)

Unfortunately, sometimes people who are coasting in an established position
feel threatened when someone comes along and asks simple basic questions
which could change the way things are being done. The guy told you
originally to "hold the cursor" and that was totally spurious advice for the
reasons I explained to you.  How much better if he had sat down and gone
through with you the requirements and the implications of various solutions.

What happened here is a perfect example of "Technocracy" ("Do what I tell
you because I'm an expert and much too important to be held to account by
the likes of you...")

Don't worry about it. Just stay polite and resolve NOT to be that way when
the roles are reversed.

>The below:
>
>Yes, we are batch processing.
>
>You've helped a great deal.  Explaining the problem helped me too.

Greg, this is EXTREMELY important. NEVER be afraid to explain a problem to
someone else. I have been called on to manage guys with expertise I didn't
have. (This is not a good situation for any Manager, but it CAN be handled).
How can you help and support them if you don't understand what they are
doing?

Get them to explain it to you. At first they'll think it is pointless but if
you persevere it is amazing how the lights come on. So, you see, it works
for both the talker and the listener. Walking through a problem with another
party is a  very valid solution strategy.



>Obviously,
>I'm new to DB2, have been attempting to write this little subroutine upon
>receiving minimal specs from a senior analyst who's not much more familiar
with
>DB2 than I am (at least in batch), and my SQL was initially written with
manuals
>
>in front of me.
>
>Ironically, my ideas have paralleled your suggestions.  After explaining
what I
>was doing, I thought of not closing the cursor as in your suggestion 2.1
(it
>jumped out of the screen at me as I typed my 1, 2, 3 etc. outline--if I
close
>the cursor it's not going to be controlling anything!).
>
>This worked, but, as you correctly stated, not in an ideal manner.  When
the
>first calling program is running, any other task attempting to use the held
row
>will wait, but only for 1 second of CPU time, then everything goes to hell.
>After waiting for more than 1 second, the second program ends its call,
somehow
>managing an SQL return code of zero, starts writing checks at check #1, and
of
>course ABENDS because it is coded to do so if the check numbers aren't
within a
>certain range.  The calling program currently runs in less that 1 second
CPU
>time, so it "works", but this remains unacceptable.  And this is without
the
>possibilty of page locks, as you mentioned.  These would defeat the purpose
of
>the subroutine and table.
>
>We'd also thought of a flag early on, but for some reason dismissed it in
favor
>of the row lock.  We have a meeting scheduled with our DBA for Monday,
April 2.
>I'm going to revisit possibly adding a flag column to the table.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Put your case for the additional flag. Make the point that holding the lock
is not viable (it isn't...)
The DBA will probably resist (it means work for him) so politely ask if he
has another idea. ANY other idea he comes up with, ask him to write it down
in a memo to you. Then do what he says. Keep the memo on file. When they
come with fire and pitchforks looking for your blood, use it as a parachute.
If you never have to use it, great. Everybody wins...

I too smiled at Jimmy's post, and the points he makes are very valid. (I
especially liked the "mad" comments, Jimmy).

However, only you can decide where the line must be drawn. Sometimes it is
just not possible to please everybody, so, please yourself <G>.(Now, where's
that bloody horse...these jodhpurs are killing me....)

regards,

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BATCH COBOL/DB2 SUBROUTINE PROBLEM

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** awkisall <awkisall@qwest.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-13T23:55:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD7F45C.B82A684@qwest.net>`
- **References:** `<3AB31D47.13A7611E@qwest.net> <3ab375d5_4@my.newsfeeds.com> <3ABE53A7.C48E3504@qwest.net> <3abee22f_4@news.newsfeeds.com> <3AC954C0.91C7FC8A@qwest.net> <3acb0b81_2@my.newsfeeds.com>`

```
I've been waiting to respond until I could give a final (thank goodness not
another in the ongoing saga) chapter to conclude the story.

We had a meeting prior to our meeting with the DBA.  "We" would be my team
leader, project leader and fellow programmers.  This is a big rollout, 7 jobs
running approximately 50? programs (not counting utility steps), with the
linchpin program being the one that calls the subroutine we've discussed.

I'll spare you with the debate.  We decided to insert an Easytrieve in a step
immediately preceding our step with the check writer that would count how many
checks are incoming to write, thus enabling my program to know how how many
checks are to be written in advance, and to use logic as suggested early on by
Mr Mattias, and cleaning up the whole mess.

Now, we're in and out and done within milliseconds.  Good conversational design.

Of course we're making a file to use to query and update a table, and I wonder
why we don't just use a file and loose the table (and subroutine), but I don't
make the calls (pun intended).

I've learned a great deal from all this; whether more about DB2 than life
remains in question..

I came to this newsgroup for help and received far more than I asked for..

Thanks again, again,

Greg



>
```

Excellent Greg...

in the end, you got a (kind of) JCL solution... which was always going to be
best.

I still have a number of reservations about this solution but I'm not going
to express them here.

Given that you must produce a Batch of Cheques, it is an adequate solution.

More importantly, as you pointed out, the "ancillary learning" was valuable.

That's what really matters...

Pete.

## Thread: ODBC questions — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### ODBC questions

- **From:** "Stephen Plotnick" <thepla@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T12:00:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aca03c3_4@news1.prserv.net>`

```
I am using Realia 3.1 Workbench.

I was getting a SQLCODE of a -1 on a fetch. The open and declare cursor
worked with SQLCODE of zero. I tried to use the ODBC trace in the control
panel and got an error ODBC32.dll Unable to access file, verify that the
correct path is given. Does anybody know how to fix this error? I have tried
moving all dll's that start with ODBC to a different directory and doing a
repair with my Office 2000 Premium. This did not fix the problem.

My second question is:
I sent the programs and a test Access DB to CA-Realia support and the
support person ran the program with trace and got an error that one of the
fields was required. The message sent to me from CA-Support was "My trace
log indicated that Fetch failed because the "Indicator variable required but
not supplied in column number 13 ( TVCAT_DISCFLAG).  If you look at the
database that you sent me,  field TVCAT_DISCFLAG was indeed empty.  Once I
inserted a number into this field,  the Fetch command worked fine." In my
Design in the Access Data base I have "required" set to no. I also changed
the "allow zero length" from "no" to "yes" with same results.
```

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-04T13:09:01+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aca7c1d_2@my.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<3aca03c3_4@news1.prserv.net>`

```

Stephen Plotnick wrote in message <3aca03c3_4@news1.prserv.net>...
>I am using Realia 3.1 Workbench.
>
>I was getting a SQLCODE of a -1 on a fetch. The open and declare cursor
>worked with SQLCODE of zero.

What was the SQLSTATE field?

>I tried to use the ODBC trace in the control
>panel and got an error ODBC32.dll Unable to access file, verify that the
>correct path is given. Does anybody know how to fix this error? I have
tried
>moving all dll's that start with ODBC to a different directory and doing a
>repair with my Office 2000 Premium. This did not fix the problem.
>
Moving things around is more likely to compound the problem...(but I
understand what it is like when you're looking at a wall <G>)

>My second question is:
>I sent the programs and a test Access DB to CA-Realia support and the
>support person ran the program with trace and got an error that one of the
>fields was required. The message sent to me from CA-Support was "My trace
>log indicated that Fetch failed because the "Indicator variable required
but
>not supplied in column number 13 ( TVCAT_DISCFLAG).  If you look at the
>database that you sent me,  field TVCAT_DISCFLAG was indeed empty.  Once I
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Did you have Validation Rules on the field? If so you must add "Or is NULL"
to the end of the validation rule text.

"Indicator Variables"  are NOT the same thing as a normal variable in SQL.
These are used to determine whether a field has a NULL value or not and are
only accessible under certain special circumstances. It would appear that
the system believes the field in question to be NULL when you have said this
is OK...

How did you create this table? If you did it with an SQL Create, did you
specify "NOT NULL" for the column? We would expect ACCESS to then define it
as REQUIRED=YES but maybe something happened during the Create...


Try removing the field completely, then do the definition manually in
ACCESS. If there are indexes or relationships attached to the field, this
will highlight them and that could be where the problem lies.

If none of this works, mail me the database and your source code.

pete.

## Thread: Checking a number for uniqueness (Student Program) — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Checking a number for uniqueness (Student Program)

- **From:** "J. Reis" <jreis26@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T14:56:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SXpy6.1243$wG4.317255@news.uswest.net>`

```
Greetings.

Working again on my COBOL class final project.  I have created a lottery
program that randomly draws six numbers.  It also allows people to input
their guesses. There are two things left I would like it to do and need to
get pointed in the right direction.  First, I would like it to make the
numbers it draws for the first five random numbers unique.  I assume that an
IF...OR....THEN...GOTO statement would need to be used.  I separated the
sections in my code to give each random number its own section of the
Procedure Division.  I have tried using these section titles as the
identifiers for performing the check against numbers but I dont think that
is the appropriate way to procede.  The other part of this project which I
would like the program to perform is the matching of guessed numbers to
drawn numbers.  Once again, an IF...OR....THEN....GOTO statement of some
variety needs to be used, but I am not sure how to implement this one.

For my first problem, I think inserting the lines at Line No. 001520
containing -

IF random-number-1 = random-number-2 OR random-number-1 = random-number-3 OR
random-number-1 = random-number-4 OR random-number-1 = random-number-5
THEN GO TO randnum-1
END-IF

should do the work, but will it loop properly if the same number is drawn
twice?  Can I also assume the same type of line comparing the
random-number-x to an entry in the Working Storage section for the user
inputted number would work for detereming a winning combination?
The program code that I have thus far follows this message.  Any help on
this is greatly appreciated.

Justin Reis

@OPTIONS MAIN
000020 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000030 PROGRAM-ID.   LOTTERY.
000040 AUTHOR.       JUSTIN REIS.
000050 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000060 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
000070 SPECIAL-NAMES.
000080       CRT STATUS IS KEYBOARD-STATUS
000090       CURSOR IS CURSOR-POSITION.
000100 SOURCE-COMPUTER.  IBM-PC.
000110 OBJECT-COMPUTER.  IBM-PC.
000120 DATA DIVISION.
000130 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000140 01  KEYBOARD-STATUS.
000150     03 ACCEPT-STATUS   PIC 9.
000160     03 FUNCTION-KEY    PIC X.
000170     03 SYSTEM-USE      PIC X.
000180 01  CURSOR-POSITION.
000190     03 CURSOR-ROW      PIC 9(2)     VALUE 1.
000200     03 CURSOR-COLUMN   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 1.
000210 01  RANDOM-SEED        PIC 9(8)     VALUE ZEROS.
000220 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-1    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000230 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-2    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000231 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-3    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000232 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-4    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000233 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-5    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000234 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-6    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000236 01  RANDOM-GENERATE    PIC V9(2)    VALUE ZEROS.
000240 01  SHOW-NEXT-SCREEN   PIC X        VALUE "Y".
000241 01  PROCESS-FLAG       PIC X        VALUE SPACES.
000242     88  END-PROCESS    VALUE "Y".
000243 01  SCREEN-ITEMS.
000250     03  LOTTO-BALL-1   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 1.
000260     03  LOTTO-BALL-2   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 2.
000270     03  LOTTO-BALL-3   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 3.
000280     03  LOTTO-BALL-4   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 4.
000290     03  LOTTO-BALL-5   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 5.
000300     03  JUSTIN-BALL-6  PIC 9(2)     VALUE 1.
000310     03  ACTUAL-NUM-1   PIC 9(2)     VALUE ZEROS.
000320     03  ACTUAL-NUM-2   PIC 9(2)     VALUE ZEROS.
000330     03  ACTUAL-NUM-3   PIC 9(2)     VALUE ZEROS.
000340     03  ACTUAL-NUM-4   PIC 9(2)     VALUE ZEROS.
000350     03  ACTUAL-NUM-5   PIC 9(2)     VALUE ZEROS.
000360     03  ACTUAL-NUM-6   PIC 9(2)     VALUE ZEROS.
000370 SCREEN SECTION.
000380 01  DATA-ENTRY-SCREEN
000390     BLANK SCREEN, AUTO,
000400     FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 7,
000410     BACKGROUND-COLOR IS 4.
000420     03  LINE 1 COLUMN 7  VALUE "Welcome to Justin's Lottery"
000430         HIGHLIGHT FOREGROUND-COLOR 7 BACKGROUND-COLOR 4.
000440     03  LINE 4 COLUMN 5  VALUE "To play, simply enter 5 unique ".
000450     03  LINE 5 COLUMN 4  VALUE "numbers and the lucky JustinBall".
000460     03  LINE 6 COLUMN 5  VALUE " Numbers must be between 1 and ".
000470     03  LINE 7 COLUMN 7  VALUE " 50 and use two digits.".
000480     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 1  VALUE "Lotto".
000490     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 9  VALUE "Lotto".
000500     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 17 VALUE "Lotto".
000510     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 25 VALUE "Lotto".
000520     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 33 VALUE "Lotto".
000530     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 41 VALUE "POWER".
000540     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 1  VALUE "Ball".
000550     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 9  VALUE "Ball".
000560     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 17 VALUE "Ball".
000570     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 25 VALUE "Ball".
000580     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 33 VALUE "Ball".
000590     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 41 VALUE "BALL".
000600     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 1  VALUE "No. 1".
000610     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 9  VALUE "No. 2".
000620     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 17 VALUE "No. 3".
000630     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 25 VALUE "No. 4".
000640     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 33 VALUE "No. 5".
000650     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 41 VALUE "  |  ".
000660     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 3  PIC 9(2) USING LOTTO-BALL-1
000670         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000680     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 11 PIC 9(2) USING LOTTO-BALL-2
000690         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000700     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 19 PIC 9(2) USING LOTTO-BALL-3
000710         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000720     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 27 PIC 9(2) USING LOTTO-BALL-4
000730         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000740     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 35 PIC 9(2) USING LOTTO-BALL-5
000750         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000760     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 43 PIC 9(2) USING JUSTIN-BALL-6
000770         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000780 01  CONFIRMATION-SCREEN-1.
000781     03  LINE 14 COLUMN 5  VALUE "ARE YOU READY TO PLAY? (Y/N): ".
000782     03  LINE 14 COLUMN 29 PIC X USING SHOW-NEXT-SCREEN.
000783 01  LOTTERY-DRAWING-SCREEN.
000784     03  LINE 17 COLUMN 5  VALUE "Here are today's lucky".
000790     03  LINE 18 COLUMN 6  VALUE "JUSTIN-BALL numbers!".
000800     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 1  VALUE "Lotto".
000810     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 9  VALUE "Lotto".
000820     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 17 VALUE "Lotto".
000830     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 25 VALUE "Lotto".
000840     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 33 VALUE "Lotto".
000850     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 41 VALUE "POWER".
000860     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 1  VALUE "Ball".
000870     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 9  VALUE "Ball".
000880     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 17 VALUE "Ball".
000890     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 25 VALUE "Ball".
000900     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 33 VALUE "Ball".
000910     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 41 VALUE "BALL".
000920     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 1  VALUE "No. 1".
000930     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 9  VALUE "No. 2".
000940     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 17 VALUE "No. 3".
000950     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 25 VALUE "No. 4".
000960     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 33 VALUE "No. 5".
000970     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 43 VALUE "|".
000980     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 3  PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-1
000990         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001000     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 11 PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-2
001010         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001020     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 19 PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-3
001030         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001040     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 27 PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-4
001050         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001060     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 35 PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-5
001070         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001080     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 43 PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-6
001090         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001100 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
001110 JUSTIN-BALL-START.
001111     PERFORM PROCESS-SCREEN
001112 PROCESS-SCREEN.
001113     DISPLAY DATA-ENTRY-SCREEN.
001120     ACCEPT  DATA-ENTRY-SCREEN.
001121     DISPLAY CONFIRMATION-SCREEN-1.
001122     ACCEPT  CONFIRMATION-SCREEN-1.
001130 LOTTERY-START.
001140     MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (9:8) TO RANDOM-SEED
001150     PERFORM RANDNUM-1 THRU RANDNUM-6.
001160 RANDNUM-1.
001180     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001190         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001200         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-1 = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001210     END-PERFORM.
001230 RANDNUM-2.
001240     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001250         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001260         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-2  = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001270     END-PERFORM.
001290 RANDNUM-3.
001300     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001310         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001320         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-3 = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001330     END-PERFORM.
001350 RANDNUM-4.
001360     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001370         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001380         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-4 = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001390     END-PERFORM.
001410 RANDNUM-5.
001420     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001430         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001440         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-5 = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001450     END-PERFORM.
001470 RANDNUM-6.
001480     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001490         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001500         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-6 = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001510     END-PERFORM.
001530     DISPLAY LOTTERY-DRAWING-SCREEN.
001531     STOP RUN.
001540
```

#### ↳ Re: Checking a number for uniqueness (Student Program)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T20:48:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACA36E6.477F0B72@home.com>`
- **References:** `<SXpy6.1243$wG4.317255@news.uswest.net>`

```


"J. Reis" wrote:

> Greetings.
>
…[195 more quoted lines elided]…
> 001540

Justin,

I'm not going to get into random numbers, because that's not my scene. I suspect
some of the math whiz kids will tell you 'there aint no such animal as a random
number' <G>.

My comments are more to do with shorthand. You've got it nicely structured, but
as soon as you find yourself saying, "Hey - I just coded that before", look to
see if you can introduce a shortcut - more concise and less chance of errors -
what we call in OO 'Reusability", although the term is just as legit in
Structured programming..

So taking your source :--

000220 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-1    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000230 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-2    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000231 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-3    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000232 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-4    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000233 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-5    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000234 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-6    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.

Change this to :-

01 RandomTable
    05  RandomNumber occurs 6                        pic 9(02).

Procedure....

initialize RandomTable
perform RANDOM-CALC varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 6

RANDOM-CALC.

    compute RandomGenerate = function Random
    compute RandomNumber(n) = (RandomGenerate * 50) + 1.

That's the general idea, and you should be able to alter it to do any additional
perform you might want, even segregating to two paragraphs if you were using a
different Multiplying factor for certain of the numbers.

Perform RANDOM-CALC
    varying n from x by y until n > z

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Checking a number for uniqueness (Student Program)

- **From:** "Thomas Meehan" <erwin24@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T23:26:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n_sy6.7196$IJ1.609390@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<SXpy6.1243$wG4.317255@news.uswest.net>`

```
First thing that popped into my mind was tabling up the numbers as you draw
them.  Each time you draw a new number you check if it's in the table.  If
it is go back to the paragraph for drawing the number.  If it's not in the
table store it, and see if you have enough numbers.  Either go back to the
drawing paragraph or exit the loop when you have them all selected.


J. Reis <jreis26@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:SXpy6.1243$wG4.317255@news.uswest.net...
> Greetings.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> numbers it draws for the first five random numbers unique.  I assume that
an
> IF...OR....THEN...GOTO statement would need to be used.  I separated the
> sections in my code to give each random number its own section of the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> IF random-number-1 = random-number-2 OR random-number-1 = random-number-3
OR
> random-number-1 = random-number-4 OR random-number-1 = random-number-5
> THEN GO TO randnum-1
…[181 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Austin, Texas is slim pickings for mainframers right now.  Several of the
tech companies (Dell, AMD, Applied Materials, etc.)have had layoffs right
now.  People who had gone to pc based stuff (leaving the mainframe behind)
are taking the few mainframe positions open.  And the cost of living here is
still very high.


Susan

<a id="t-08faf26759"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL product licensing — 28 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL product licensing

- **From:** "Fujitsu COBOL" <cobol@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-03T16:36:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aca5c8a$1@news01.bendcable.com>`

```
As many of you know, with version 6 of the Fujitsu COBOL product, we
introduced a software product license protection mechanism. This new
mechanism was introduced to ensure that Fujitsu Software can continue to
offer customers a competitively priced product and ensure that we can make a
profitable business. As with all such mechanisms, it does introduce some
regrettable inconveniences to our customers. We hope that this note will
help to explain some of the technical as well as business issues surrounding
our current product licensing strategy.

From a technical point of view, several people have expressed concern that
the licensing mechanism is not compatible with certain common operations to
their computers, such as defragmenting hard disks or upgrading hardware. As
recently posted to comp.lang.cobol, most defragmenting programs do not
introduce a problem. The only exception we are aware of is Speed Disk that
is shipped with Norton Utilities. Settings exist in Speed Disk that allow
defragmentation to be performed without affecting the Fujitsu COBOL license.
Upgrading any hardware other than the hard disk on a machine also does not
affect the license.

The registration software for Fujitsu COBOL also has a mechanism for
transferring a license from one machine to another without any interaction
with Fujitsu Software. In some cases, it is necessary to contact Fujitsu
Software to resolve product license issues. We are happy to work with
individuals to quickly resolve any such problems. We believe that you will
find our customer support staff to be very responsive.

As a business model, we have always sold the Fujitsu COBOL product on a
per-installation basis and not on a per-user basis. This did not change in
the latest version of our product. The only thing that changed is that we
now have a mechanism that provides us with some assurances that the product
is being used as licensed. Per-installation licensing is common practice in
the software industry and we believe it meets the needs of most of our
customers. These are choices, however, that we must constantly reevaluate.

We are aware, as someone recently pointed out, that we, as a COBOL vendor,
not only compete against other COBOL vendors, but also against other
languages. As such, we are forced to strike a balance - we believe that we
have the most competitively priced COBOL compiler suite on the market. At
$900 for our base product offering, our product is certainly comparable to
other software products on the market and we do not charge per copy runtime
distribution fees as many of our competitors do.

The market for COBOL is sufficiently different from that of other
programming languages that it is not possible for us to compete with many
other programming languages on price alone. Building COBOL compilers is a
costly investment and we must be able to show profit on our investment. We
do believe that we can continue to make a good business selling our COBOL
development products, which is why we are committed to building products,
such as a compiler for Microsoft's .NET, that will ensure future success for
our customers.

Basim Kadhim
Fujitsu Software
cobol@adtools.com
```

(snip, snip)

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL product licensing

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-04T17:02:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TsIy6.19376$XV.4953292@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3aca5c8a$1@news01.bendcable.com> <tclnko7iu8410d@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Mike Madara" <mojo@chesapeake.net> wrote in message news:tclnko7iu8410d@corp.supernews.com...
> "Fujitsu COBOL" <cobol@adtools.com> wrote:
> 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The only protection scheme apparent in MS Visual C++ is a CD key (serial number). Here's an excerpt from Microsoft's Visual C++ EULA concerning legal copies:

1.1 General License Grant. Microsoft grants to you as an individual, a personal, nonexclusive license to make and use copies of the SOFTWARE PRODUCT for the sole purposes of designing, developing, and testing your software product(s) that are designed to operate in conjunction with any Microsoft operating system product. You may install copies of the SOFTWARE PRODUCT on an unlimited number of computers provided that you are the only individual using the SOFTWARE PRODUCT. If you are an entity, Microsoft grants you the right to designate one individual within your organization to have the sole right to use the SOFTWARE PRODUCT in the manner provided above.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL product licensing

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T11:33:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ae01e28.117655500@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3aca5c8a$1@news01.bendcable.com> <tclnko7iu8410d@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Microsoft offers a ton of different licensing schemes.  So whether
this is legal depends on your agreement with Microsoft.  We have a
site license for these tools, but are required to count installation
and report them to MS.  We use SMS for this.  If an employee connects
to the network, even via dial up, SMS checks their machine and reports
the installed software.  

At any rate, MS is moving to this model -- check out Windows XP.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL product licensing

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-04-04T17:14:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3acb5441.156929933@news>`
- **References:** `<3aca5c8a$1@news01.bendcable.com> <3ACB4F38.5FE85DB8@home.com>`

```
On Wed, 04 Apr 2001 16:44:47 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>A well made case and reasoning understood. But, here perhaps is the important
>thing. Both Fujitsu and Merant - can your demonstrate that COBOLers have
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB
=====================
Hi, Jimmy,

We use a simple copyright-violation detection (not a copy-protection)
mechanism in GUI ScreenIO that causes it to temporarily revert to
"evaluation" mode when we detect you've got the same serial number
installed on more than one computer.  Plus, we pop up a messagebox
notifying you a possible copyright violation has been detected.

It resets to registered mode the next time you run it, so it's just a
reminder - until you move it to yet ANOTHER machine, when you'll be
reminded again.

Typical phone call:

User:  "How come my GUI ScreenIO says it's an evaluation copy?  I
registered it 6 months ago."

Us:  "It's because you're using the same serial number on more than
one machine on your network."

...long pause...

User:  "Um, I'd like to purchase another license."

It happens with about five (5) percent of our installations, maybe a
shade more.  

And, it's not only small shops.  We've encountered this with
governments and large companies, too.  I think users do it for the
sake of expediency most of the time, but, hey, if they need more than
one copy, they really ought to license additional copies.  

No one has commented adversely on how we encourage compliance.  Our
approach is to encourage folks stay honest (most of 'em will, given
the chance) without being punitive about it.  

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL product licensing

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T21:49:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ad4ceae.10421785@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<3aca5c8a$1@news01.bendcable.com> <3ACB4F38.5FE85DB8@home.com> <3acb5441.156929933@news> <3ACB5C46.C2C9D271@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>
>
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy

Jimmy:

Nothing.  None of our production software has licensing routines in
them.  That includes sp2, FormPrint, Thin Client and Web Client.  We
won't put any licensing software in our tools for a couple reasons....

1.  We don't want anything to interfere with the operation of our
editors or our dynamic link libraries.  Although licensing schemes can
be written to minimize these types of problems, we would rather not
have to worry about them interfering and would rather not have the
customer worry about them interfering with the operation of their
software.

2.  We know that we have customers who don't follow our licensing
policies, but we believe that the vast majority of our customers are
honest and are willing to pay us for the software they use.  If only a
very small percentage of the customer base is cheating or attempting
to cheat, then why worry about it anyway?

I'm sure that many out there would consider us naive, but we've been
selling software like this since 1986 and it hasn't hurt us.

We do have a 60 day timeout in our evaluation version, but there is
nothing in any of the production software.

When we have discovered customers who have taken advantage, it is
usually when the customer decides that they don't want to pay AND they
want technical support anyway.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

- **References:** `<3aca5c8a$1@news01.bendcable.com> <3ACB4F38.5FE85DB8@home.com> <3acb5441.156929933@news> <3ACB5C46.C2C9D271@home.com> <3ad4ceae.10421785@news.epix.net> <3AD4DE3B.FEB17F42@home.com> <3ad50e74_1@my.newsfeeds.com>`

```
Pete:

This happened to me just this week.

In the ditch with a software development product. Message is of the
form: "... Internal error. Contact support..."

Zip. E-mail to support desk. Prompt reply: "Your maintenance has
expired, so sorry we can't help..."

Prowl around the office. Yep, maintenance had expired. E-mail the
software vendor along the lines of "This is the second year in a row
you haven't sent an invoice. Will you send an invoice (I have your
pro-forma invoice from last year when we had a similar cock-up) or
should I just mail a payment and hope you can figure out what to do
with an unsolicited check. If the latter, how much?"

I'm still waiting for and invoice/response. Meanwhile, I'm in kind of
a cul-de-sac on a development problem.


>"Peter E. C. Dashwood"
> >
> Hey, what about when you quote them the Registration number, date
and method
> of payment, and they STILL say they can find no record of your
having
> registered..? Company will remain nameless, but NOT Merant or
Fujitsu.
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL product licensing

"Good morning Mr. G. This is Sears. We note your maintenance agreement on
your fridge/freezer/TV etc..... runs out at the end of March. Would you
like to renew ? It will cost you $......."

"Yes".

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL product licensing

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-14T15:44:07+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ad7cc45_4@my.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<3aca5c8a$1@news01.bendcable.com> <3ACB4F38.5FE85DB8@home.com> <3acb5441.156929933@news> <3ACB5C46.C2C9D271@home.com> <3ad4ceae.10421785@news.epix.net> <3AD4DE3B.FEB17F42@home.com> <3ad50e74_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <46MB6.563$x4.41343@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Same thing happened to me with  Fujitsu Maintenance renewal. They didn't
remind me and I forgot about it (It was due around Xmas when everyone has
1000 other things on their minds.)

Have to say, Fujitsu were very quick to reinstate my Maintenance status and
there was a minimum of hassle.

This raises the question of whether we SHOULD expect companies to remind us
(or invoice us) for recurrent things which we are likely to want.

In the scenario you describe, it is really serious. I would've thought that
the fact you were a past customer might have encouraged them to give you the
support and argue about paying for it later. Still, the fact remains that
they don't HAVE to and they can argue it is OUR responsibility to approach
them with money.

I know what it's like when you're blocked on development by this kind of
action. You try every combination you can think of to get round the problem,
when a 5 minute phone call or an e-mail would reveal it to be a known
problem with a known fix...

The bottom line (at least as far as I'm concerned) is that I remember and
support the companies who try to help. The one's who don't (and I reckon
your current one is in that category...) rapidly disappear off my list. In
fact I would spend the time you're waiting for them, in looking at other
prospective vendors or solutions...

It can be summed up in two words which can decide the prosperity or
bankruptcy of any Business (my own included): "Customer Focus".

Hope you get it sorted,

Pete.


Jerry P wrote in message
<46MB6.563$x4.41343@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>...

```

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> wrote in
message news:3ad7cc45_4@my.newsfeeds.com...
> Same thing happened to me with  Fujitsu Maintenance renewal. They
didn't
> remind me and I forgot about it (It was due around Xmas when
everyone has
> 1000 other things on their minds.)
>
> Have to say, Fujitsu were very quick to reinstate my Maintenance
status and
> there was a minimum of hassle.
>
> This raises the question of whether we SHOULD expect companies to
remind us
> (or invoice us) for recurrent things which we are likely to want.
>
> In the scenario you describe, it is really serious. I would've
thought that
> the fact you were a past customer might have encouraged them to give
you the
> support and argue about paying for it later. Still, the fact remains
that
> they don't HAVE to and they can argue it is OUR responsibility to
approach
> them with money.

Pete,

You're right; they have a marginally defensible position in declining
assistance. And I admit to some culpability for not treating my
maintenance fee like a country-club payment (that's an A/R term for
periodic, recurring payments).

Still, following a basic rule of business ("It's easier to get money
from your current customers than from new ones"), I would think that
sending invoices once a year would be a slam-dunk policy. We do it at
my company sixty days before the contract anniversary date. If the
customer doesn't pay, we send a statement. If they still don't pay, we
place the customer on inactive status. We even follow up on that.

In my view, had the company in question spent a bit more effort on
improving their recurring cash flow instead of investing time and
money on a new licensing/sofware-lock scheme, we'd both be better off.
(Shit. Pretend I didn't say that. I was trying so hard to avoid giving
a clue. Pay no attention to the man behind the curtain. Change the
subject quick: "Do you think the US will ever go metric?)

Onward through the fog.
```

Jimmy:

Thanks for the kind words.  I have to give credit to one of my
business partners, Andy Holyoak, for this philosophy....he insists
that we will never have copy protection and converted me to his way of
thinking many years ago.

He has always exuded the utmost of professionalism, although he is the
master of brevity, which is occaisonally miscontrued as apathy or
anger.  Although most have not heard his name mentioned before, Thane,
Donald, Bob Hennessey, Tony Mina and some of the other folks who have
used our products will back me up 100% on this one.

...and Jimmy, you will be very pleased to know that Andy hails from
Shropshire, UK.

It doesn't happen very often, but 2 or 3 times per year we have to
deal with these types of situations.

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL product licensing

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T22:11:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9aj8dt$5ci2$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3aca5c8a$1@news01.bendcable.com>`

```
Basim,

So if one uses the product daily, and one boots up with the now familiar
"blue screen of death" or "missing operating system" due to a hard drive
failure, how exactly does one go about "transferring" said license?  I'm not
trying to be a wise guy about this, but this has happened to our shop in the
past using Attachmate software. And Murphy being Murphy, it always seems to
happen at times when support is not available, i.e. 2am, weekends, etc.,
etc.  We even quoted the reference to the Microsoft license that was
mentioned in another post to Attachmate, and they chose to ignore that
comparison, saying that "rules are rules".  So you won't find any Attachmate
software at our shop today.

The question is,  is there a way to "transfer" a license without actually
uninstalling the software completely?  If so, this would suffice in a
"backup" situation.  We could restore our backups, then transfer the license
back to the backup PC.  Otherwise, to penalize 95% of the honest users of
this product for the misuse of 5% of the dishonest users, is in my opinion,
the kindergarten approach to life.  Our organization is  totally satisfied
with the product you are offering and the price.  And totally honest, since
we actually have "software police" that audit us every 90 days.  Also, we
were a former MicroFocus user and found that your product offered a better
solution at a more reasonable price.

We have absolutely no problem with the current licensing scheme, except for
the disaster backup portion of it. And we offer a disaster recovery service
for banks, so our priority may be somewhat different that the every day
user.

Regards,

Denny Brouse

Fujitsu COBOL" <cobol@adtools.com> wrote in message
news:3aca5c8a$1@news01.bendcable.com...
> As many of you know, with version 6 of the Fujitsu COBOL product, we
> introduced a software product license protection mechanism. This new
> mechanism was introduced to ensure that Fujitsu Software can continue to
> offer customers a competitively priced product and ensure that we can make
a
> profitable business. As with all such mechanisms, it does introduce some
> regrettable inconveniences to our customers. We hope that this note will
> help to explain some of the technical as well as business issues
surrounding
> our current product licensing strategy.
>
> From a technical point of view, several people have expressed concern that
> the licensing mechanism is not compatible with certain common operations
to
> their computers, such as defragmenting hard disks or upgrading hardware.
As
> recently posted to comp.lang.cobol, most defragmenting programs do not
> introduce a problem. The only exception we are aware of is Speed Disk that
> is shipped with Norton Utilities. Settings exist in Speed Disk that allow
> defragmentation to be performed without affecting the Fujitsu COBOL
license.
> Upgrading any hardware other than the hard disk on a machine also does not
> affect the license.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> now have a mechanism that provides us with some assurances that the
product
> is being used as licensed. Per-installation licensing is common practice
in
> the software industry and we believe it meets the needs of most of our
> customers. These are choices, however, that we must constantly reevaluate.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> other software products on the market and we do not charge per copy
runtime
> distribution fees as many of our competitors do.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> such as a compiler for Microsoft's .NET, that will ensure future success
for
> our customers.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL product licensing

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T11:57:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ae02372.119010329@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3aca5c8a$1@news01.bendcable.com> <9aj8dt$5ci2$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I'm really not trying to beat a dead horse, but.... all you have to do
is install the full product on a different machine.  (Your backup
machine for instance)   It will work, unfettered for 30 days.  That is
plenty of time to call the vendor, tell them about the crash and get a
new license.

In ADDITION - if recovery is the main concern, then simply ask for an
additional license key instance and if a machine dies, you can just
"Validate" again and be off and running.

Lastly, I have not tried this for obvious reasons, but I think the
particular prodct used by Fujitsu for theire licens protection is
tolerant of a Windows Re-Install.



On Thu, 5 Apr 2001 22:11:44 -0400, "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
wrote:

```
Hi,
    When I print out source code from my compiler "Merant Microfocus
Personal COBOL for Windows 3.1 v1.1" I get the year 1901 at the bottom of
each sheet of printed source code. This is done by the compiler and is not
set by me. How do I change this. Is it a bug in the compiler. I have asked
Merant but they dont reply. Any help would be greatly appreciated. This is
happening with every program I write.
  B loggs.
```

Get serious guys, this is cobol! ;-)

Your compiler is not Y2K compatible, and I'm not sure if MicroFocus will
upgrade it for you.  They should, as a non-Y2K compliant compiler is not
your prolem, is theirs!  So you may want to push their sales department.

Just to satisfy my curiousity, why in the world do you care that the bottom
of your printed source says 1901 instead of 2001?

Calin, TORONTO
```

but I got a fix for it".

## Thread: First cobol-program — 17 msgs

### Re: First cobol-program

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-04T23:58:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi>`

```
Pretty good for a first attempt, Jake.

Move one line, add one line. Shown below in lower case (Thats non-Capital
letters...)

The errors you get after that should be resolvable.

Pete.

Jarkko Kï¿½hkï¿½nen wrote in message <0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi>...
>Hi!
>I'm writing my first cobol-program... And I'm totally in confusion! Please
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Working-storage section.
01  ownstring   pic x(39). ====> Why would you move an 80 byte input record
 (which has 82 characters defined in it...)
 to a 39 byte field....?

>PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>me into confusion.=)

Try www.adtools.com

Unfortunately, the errors in the above (even after the marked corrections)
will cause
ANY compiler to produce error messages and they are often not helpful to the
newcomer.

Think about things ... counts, logic...

ask yourself questions like:
" How many friends will be on the input file?"
"How many am I reading?"
"Is there any point in setting an end of file flag if I never test it?"
"How many of my friends are actually customers?"
"Should I read the same file I open or can it be a different one?"

Some further major errors in the above are caused by misplaced and improper
full stops. That's why we got them almost removed from the Language and
replaced with "scope delimiters" (like "end-perform" "end-if" and so on...)
Review your use of full stops...
And have a look at level numbers. They are a most important part of COBOL.
A record must start with an "01" level...

You are on the right track and I wish you every success.

Pete


>
>Best regards,
>Jake
>
>

```

#### ↳ Re: First cobol-program

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-04T15:21:45-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com>`

```
On Wed, 4 Apr 2001 23:58:39 +1200, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> enlightened us:


Peter, Peter, Peter, shame on you.  You missed a really big error.
See below.


>Pretty good for a first attempt, Jake.
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>>   RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS.

You should have an 01 level here before the first 05.  Not required,
for an input file the way you are using it, but is generally something
all Cobol programmers do.  Example:

   01 FRIENDS-RECORD.
>>    05 FRIENDS-NAME PIC X(17).
>>    05 FRIENDS-EMAIL-ADDRESS PIC X(39).
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>>    READ CUSTOMER-FILE INTO OWNSTRING

Where is CUSTOMER-FILE defined?

>>     AT END SET EOF-CUST-INPUT TO 'T'

Where is EOF-CUST-INPUT defined?

>>    END-READ
>>
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----


Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Borrow money from a pessimist - they don't expect it back.


DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge
Read the facts and sign a petition at:

http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

law

> exact percentage).  He and VP Dick are still Oil Company CEOs at

Yeah, ain't it grand about the environmental regulations rollback.

I hadn't heard about the toasters, but I can authoritatively state
that no LAW was involved - it would be revoking another over-reach by
a regulatory agency.

Here's a good example: because the 1.6 gallon flush toilet was deemed
insufficient, regulations were being considered in the Clinton
administration (modeled after the California zero-emission auto rules)
to mandate a 'zero gallon' toilet. Dubbed the PRImary enVironmental
Effluvia Elimination standard (PRIVEE, for short), immense savings in
water usage would result.

> DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge

I agree. Strip mine the son-of-a-bitch.


> Read the facts and sign a petition at:
>
> http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Uh, there are very few polar bears in the Artic National Wildlife
Refuge. Mostly they're lost. Polar bears prefer to live in or near the
water (seals are yummy). ANWR is, at best, mud.

Check it out.

http://www.anwr.org/

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> >
> I don't know who sponsors that site (www.anwr.org).....

I suspect the National Rifle Association has something to do with it.
Just kidding.

>
> The whole point of opposing the drilling in the Artic National
> Wildlife Refuge is that it is absolutely not needed.

California had 33 days last year when power demand exceeded 40,000
megawatts. If this year resembles 2000, Californians can expect 33
days of rolling blackouts. We are planning to offer glow-in-the-dark
mousepads to our California customers.


>Why spoil such a pristine area for so little?

It's not 'pristine.' There is at least one town and one military
installation already there. Plus, you can't spoil mud. If ANWR were
the same size as your house, the exploration area would be the size of
a saucer.

On Sat, 07 Apr 2001 20:54:05 GMT, "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
enlightened us:

>
>"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I doubt it.  They'd want the place left as is and opened up so there'd
be some defenseless animals to practise shooting at with their assault
rifles!

>>
>> The whole point of opposing the drilling in the Artic National
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
And drilling in ANWR is not going to help them one bit.  The U.S.
Geological Survey has said it would take at least ten years for any
oil from the refuge to reach west coast refineries, and another 15
years for the oil field to reach its peak production level.

As for California's problems, it is very interesting to me that they
never had these problems until they deregulated public utilities.
Last year's winter was not that much different than this years and
they had no problems in 1999.

Maybe you should give each of your customers 2 glow-in-the-dark
mousepads just in case.

>>Why spoil such a pristine area for so little?
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The Arctic Refuge, known as "America's
Serengeti," is among the world's last truly pristine
wild places and one of the largest sanctuaries for
Arctic animals on the planet. The coastal plain of the
refuge is traversed by a dozen rivers and framed by
the jagged peaks of spectacular mountains. This
spectacular wilderness is a vital birthing ground for
polar bears, grizzlies, Arctic wolves, the vast
Porcupine herd of 130,000 caribou and the highly
endangered shaggy musk ox, a mammoth-like
survivor of the last Ice Age.

The area the oil comapnies want to drill in is "only" 2000 acres.  Yes
small in comparison to the total area of ANWR.  But, those are not
2000 contiguous acres that they want.  They are spread accross dozens
of separate sites and would be supported by a web of roads, pipelines
and processing facilities extending over hundreds of square miles of
fragile wildlife habitat.

Want to see what the oil companies can do to an area like ANWR?  Just
pay a visit to Pruhoe Bay.  The arguements to allow drilling there in
a "small" area are again being used to allow drilling in ANWR.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Borrow money from a pessimist - they don't expect it back.


DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge
Read the facts and sign a petition at:

http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: First cobol-program

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T14:46:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BXjA6.1208$Ak1.147319@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <3ACB7777.7ED67246@home.com> <1EF061510F4212DD.E26B3491FA8454C9.0433824481B06B70@lp.airnews.net> <2qkz6.8822$Kr1.750249@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <42CB70780CE339B9.848FC009E781F571.6C106A3ED0969F4E@lp.airnews.net> <N7Lz6.13998$Kr1.1168620@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <E3A9EF7930C2A4A8.246B4D428748E511.86B6DB3010DB45CA@lp.airnews.net>`

```

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
>
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >I suspect the National Rifle Association has something to do with
it.
> >Just kidding.
> >
>
> I doubt it.  They'd want the place left as is and opened up so
there'd
> be some defenseless animals to practise shooting at with their
assault
> rifles!

Of course. Do you think we're idiots? I'm certainly not going to shoot
at an animal that could shoot back.

> >
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> years for the oil field to reach its peak production level.

Ten to 15 years seems a bit high, the North Slope came in less than
that. But whatever the time interval, future availability affects
current prices.

>
> As for California's problems, it is very interesting to me that they
> never had these problems until they deregulated public utilities.
> Last year's winter was not that much different than this years and
> they had no problems in 1999.

First, the power industry was not deregulated in California - the
politicians just called it that.

Second, what's different this year is that California can't buy the
power it was able to buy in 1999. Best estimates are that California
can count on only 40,000 megawatts of power this summer - in 98 and 99
they peaked at 54,000 megawatts. Some power plants have shut down
because, while they have not been paid, their expenses (e.g., payroll,
taxes, insurance) continue; other power plants can sell their power to
more credit-worthy customers. No, California can expect shortfalls of
up to 10,000 megawatts this summer.
>
> The Arctic Refuge, known as "America's
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> survivor of the last Ice Age.

The Arctic Refuge, also known as 'American's Energy Solution' is a
vast area of mostly nothing. There are no mountains in the coastal
plain (otherwise it would not be called a 'plain'). There are no
'rivers' only snow-melt gullies. There are no significant indigneous
animals that make it a home (there is a lemming, mole. vole, gopher,
or some-such, that hibernates below the ice, coming out once a year to
communicate with prospective mates by biting them on the thigh). The
coastal plain is a unbelievably vast expanse of ice-covered mud.
Sometimes just mud.

>
> The area the oil comapnies want to drill in is "only" 2000 acres.
Yes
> small in comparison to the total area of ANWR.  But, those are not
> 2000 contiguous acres that they want.  They are spread accross
dozens
> of separate sites and would be supported by a web of roads,
pipelines
> and processing facilities extending over hundreds of square miles of
> fragile wildlife habitat.

New comparison: If ANWR were the size of your house, the exploration
area would be the size of a postcard (2,000 sq ft vs. 29 sq in). Cut
the postcard up into 30 postage-stamp sized pieces and scatter the
pieces on your door jamb. Connect the pieces with string. That's about
it.

>
> Want to see what the oil companies can do to an area like ANWR?
Just
> pay a visit to Pruhoe Bay.  The arguements to allow drilling there
in
> a "small" area are again being used to allow drilling in ANWR.

I've been to the North Slope fields, Purdhoe Bay, and Valdez (and
points in-between), working for a company desiging the power
distribution system for the pipeline. Imagine the Sahara. With ice.
```

- **References:** `<2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <3ACB7777.7ED67246@home.com> <1EF061510F4212DD.E26B3491FA8454C9.0433824481B06B70@lp.airnews.net> <2qkz6.8822$Kr1.750249@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <42CB70780CE339B9.848FC009E781F571.6C106A3ED0969F4E@lp.airnews.net> <N7Lz6.13998$Kr1.1168620@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <E3A9EF7930C2A4A8.246B4D428748E511.86B6DB3010DB45CA@lp.airnews.net> <BXjA6.1208$Ak1.147319@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
On Mon, 09 Apr 2001 14:46:57 GMT, "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
enlightened us:

>
>"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >

LOL!!  That a great line and, unfortunately, there are probably some
NRA zealots that really feel that way!

I'm not going to belabor the point about drilling in ANWR.  Obviously
I believe the effect on the area, no matter how small it may seem,
outweighs the benefits.  You do not.  That's the great thing about
freedom of speech.


<<some sniappge>>
>
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The web of gravel pads, service roads, pipelines, and industrial
buildings on Alaska's North Slope is far more extensive and damaging
than envisioned more than 30 years ago when oil production was
authorized there, environmentalists told a panel of scientists Jan. 8
and 9, 2001.

Instead of the single Prudhoe Bay oil field that was originally
planned when oil was discovered there in 1968, oil development now
encompasses 19 separate fields spread over 1,000 square miles, with
twice the number of wells and production drill sites as
originally anticipated, Pamela A. Miller, an environmental consultant,
told the committee of scientists appointed by the National
Research Council. 

In the years since drilling started on the North Slope, operators
there have caused an annual average of 409 spills of crude oil,
diesel fuel, and other substances, said Miller, who runs
Anchorage-based Arctic Connections.

"There's about a spill a day at Prudhoe Bay," she said.   Annual
emissions of nitrogen oxides are measure 56,427 tons, and annual
emissions of methane range from 24,000 metric tons to 114,000 metric
tons, she said. 

Those statistics, gathered from reports by state and federal agencies,
are among the environmental impacts that have been far
greater than what was predicted in the original environmental study
done before construction of the Trans Alaska Pipeline System,
she said. 

Despite the industry's spreading reach over the area, "No analysis of
cumulative impacts of North Slope oil development has ever
been done," said Peter Van Tuyn, an attorney with Trustees for Alaska,
an Anchorage-based environmental law firm. The U.S. Army
Corps of Engineers, for example, has not completed a single full
environmental impact statement on any onshore North Slope
development since 1972, he said.

Prudhoe Bay can never be recovered, only contained.  The damage that
has ocurred can never be undone.  This same kind of damage could and
probably would happen to ANWR.  It is awful hard for the politicans to
say no to the oil companies once they've said yes.

And finally, on ANWR:

The average estimate of economically recoverable oil from the Arctic
Refuge is 3.2 billion barrels of oil or less than 6 monthï¿½s worth of
U.S. supply (this is based on the most recent USGS assessment). 

The truth is any estimate for how much oil might lie beneath the
coastal plain is a best geologic guess.
More than 20 different independent and federal studies have been
conducted there with estimates varying wildly. The Reagan
Administration, for example, projected only a 1 in 5 chance of even
discovering commercial quantities of oil. 

Strangely, the results of the only test well ever authorized in the
Arctic Refuge have been kept confidential  by Chevron for more than 15
years. If Senator Murkowski really believes thereï¿½s so much oil in the
refuge,  then why doesnï¿½t he call on Chevron to release its well data?

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I have a twin brother; he's identical, but I'm not.


DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge
Read the facts and sign a petition at:

http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
SkippyPB wrote:

>
> >> I doubt it.  They'd want the place left as is and opened up so
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> NRA zealots that really feel that way!

I expect just about everybody feels that way.  My meat, however doesn't
come from animals - it comes from the grocery store.


> I'm not going to belabor the point about drilling in ANWR.  Obviously
> I believe the effect on the area, no matter how small it may seem,
> outweighs the benefits.  You do not.  That's the great thing about
> freedom of speech.

There are a couple of other issues here which the president should
consider:
1.  What do we think the future will be like - will we really, really need
the oil then?  If so, will we be better able to keep it clean, or desperate
enough to not care.

1.5.  Are we better off using foreign oil now and keeping our oil as a
reserve?

2.  Would the Bush administration be able to gain big useful brownie points
by making an issue of protecting that area?   The political benefits for
the things he cares about could outweigh the costs of not getting the
oil.   If he's setting this up as a bargaining chip, what would the
Democrats be willing to pay for it?   Or if he's setting this up so that he
can appease voters by giving in - will he actually get votes from
environmentalists?


> The average estimate of economically recoverable oil from the Arctic
> Refuge is 3.2 billion barrels of oil or less than 6 monthï¿½s worth of
> U.S. supply (this is based on the most recent USGS assessment).

6 months is a lot of supply.
```

Steve,

I didn't miss the errors, I just decided to let him find them for himself.

It's called "learning"...<G>

Read the comments (hints) I posted. (They point him at both the misplaced
files and the 01 level errors, as well as a number of other "hard to spot"
errors...some of which you missed "shame on you" <G>)

If you go through and do a thorough analysis of this program and present it
to Jake at this stage in his career you are robbing him of vital information
and not allowing him to develop useful practices which will stand him in
good stead in his future.

Nothing is gained by showing how clever we are, if it leads a newcomer to
simply expect that his problems can be solved immediately by posting to a
Newsgroup...

Now go, and sin no more... <G>

Pete
