<a id="t-2e2d7d7428"></a>
## Thread: Screen error message when executing a program — 3 msgs

### Screen error message when executing a program

- **From:** "J. Reis" <jreis26@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-04T10:25:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C3Hy6.1733$uk.138390@news.uswest.net>`

```
Good day.

I am once again working on my final project for my COBOL programming course
and I have run into a bit of a snag.  Using the Fujitsu Compiler on a PC,
whenever I execute my program, the first screen comes up correctly, but when
I enter that I want to continue, a error message stating "JMP0206I-W SCREEN
ITEM SIZE IS LARGER THAN LOGICAL SCREEN" appears.  I thought this might be
related to my windows linking one after the other rather than blanking the
screen.  So I added the BLANK SCREEN to the last set of commands in the
Procedure Division and it caused this error.  Removing the line, this error
still occurs, but after three screens have been processed.  I am not exactly
sure what this error pertains to but would greatly appreciate getting a
pointer in the correct direction as how best to deal with this particular
error.  Following this email is a copy of the program source code.  Thank
you.

Justin Reis (fledgling COBOL programmer)

000010 @OPTIONS MAIN
000020******************************************************************
000021*  THIS PROGRAM ASKS THE USER FOR A SET OF NUMBERS TO BE ENTERED *
000022*  INTO THE PROGRAM AND THEN RANDOMLY COMPUTES ITS OWN SET OF    *
000023*  NUMBERS.  COMPARING THE TWO SETS, IT DETERMINES A PRIZE VALUE *
000024*  BASED ON ANY TWO MATCHING NUMBERS.                            *
000025******************************************************************
000026 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000030 PROGRAM-ID.   LOTTERY.
000040 AUTHOR.       JUSTIN REIS.
000050 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000060 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
000070 SPECIAL-NAMES.
000080       CRT STATUS IS KEYBOARD-STATUS
000090       CURSOR IS CURSOR-POSITION.
000100 SOURCE-COMPUTER.  IBM-PC.
000110 OBJECT-COMPUTER.  IBM-PC.
000120 DATA DIVISION.
000130 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000140 01  KEYBOARD-STATUS.
000150     03 ACCEPT-STATUS   PIC 9.
000160     03 FUNCTION-KEY    PIC X.
000170     03 SYSTEM-USE      PIC X.
000180 01  CURSOR-POSITION.
000190     03 CURSOR-ROW      PIC 9(2)     VALUE 1.
000200     03 CURSOR-COLUMN   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 1.
000210 01  RANDOM-SEED        PIC 9(8)     VALUE ZEROS.
000220 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-1    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000230 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-2    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000231 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-3    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000232 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-4    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000233 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-5    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000234 01  RANDOM-NUMBER-6    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
000236 01  RANDOM-GENERATE    PIC V9(2)    VALUE ZEROS.
000240 01  SHOW-NEXT-SCREEN.
000241     03  PLAY-JB        PIC X(1)     VALUE "Y".
000242 01  EXITING-SCREEN.
000243     03  QUIT-JB        PIC X(1)     VALUE "N".
000244 01  SCREEN-ITEMS.
000250     03  LOTTO-BALL-1   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 1.
000260     03  LOTTO-BALL-2   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 2.
000270     03  LOTTO-BALL-3   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 3.
000280     03  LOTTO-BALL-4   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 4.
000290     03  LOTTO-BALL-5   PIC 9(2)     VALUE 5.
000300     03  JUSTIN-BALL-6  PIC 9(2)     VALUE 1.
000370 SCREEN SECTION.
000380*******************************************************************
000381*  MAIN SCREEN DISPLAYING GREETING AND ALLOWING USERS TO INPUT    *
000382*  THEIR GUESSES FOR THE LOTTERY DRAWING                          *
000383*******************************************************************
000384 01  DATA-ENTRY-SCREEN
000390     BLANK SCREEN, AUTO, REQUIRED,
000400     FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 7,
000410     BACKGROUND-COLOR IS 4.
000420     03  LINE 1 COLUMN 7  VALUE "Welcome to Justin's Lottery"
000430         HIGHLIGHT FOREGROUND-COLOR 7 BACKGROUND-COLOR 4.
000440     03  LINE 4 COLUMN 5  VALUE "To play, simply enter 5 unique ".
000450     03  LINE 5 COLUMN 4  VALUE "numbers and the lucky JustinBall.".
000460     03  LINE 6 COLUMN 5  VALUE " Numbers must be between 1 and ".
000470     03  LINE 7 COLUMN 7  VALUE " 50 and use two digits.".
000480     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 1  VALUE "Lotto".
000490     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 9  VALUE "Lotto".
000500     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 17 VALUE "Lotto".
000510     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 25 VALUE "Lotto".
000520     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 33 VALUE "Lotto".
000530     03  LINE 9  COLUMN 41 VALUE "POWER".
000540     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 1  VALUE "Ball".
000550     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 9  VALUE "Ball".
000560     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 17 VALUE "Ball".
000570     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 25 VALUE "Ball".
000580     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 33 VALUE "Ball".
000590     03  LINE 10 COLUMN 41 VALUE "BALL".
000600     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 1  VALUE "No. 1".
000610     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 9  VALUE "No. 2".
000620     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 17 VALUE "No. 3".
000630     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 25 VALUE "No. 4".
000640     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 33 VALUE "No. 5".
000650     03  LINE 11 COLUMN 41 VALUE "  |  ".
000660     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 3  PIC 9(2) USING LOTTO-BALL-1
000670         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000680     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 11 PIC 9(2) USING LOTTO-BALL-2
000690         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000700     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 19 PIC 9(2) USING LOTTO-BALL-3
000710         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000720     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 27 PIC 9(2) USING LOTTO-BALL-4
000730         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000740     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 35 PIC 9(2) USING LOTTO-BALL-5
000750         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000760     03  LINE 12 COLUMN 43 PIC 9(2) USING JUSTIN-BALL-6
000771         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
000780*******************************************************************
000781*  SCREEN THAT ASKS USER TO CONTINUE WITH LOTTERY DRAWING         *
000782*******************************************************************
000783 01  CONFIRMATION-SCREEN-1.
000784     03  LINE 14 COLUMN 5  VALUE "Are you ready to play? (Y/N) ".
000785     03  LINE 14 COLUMN 35 PIC X USING SHOW-NEXT-SCREEN.
000786*******************************************************************
000787*  SCREEN THAT DISPLAYS THE COMPUTER GENERATED NUMBERS            *
000788*******************************************************************
000789 01  LOTTERY-DRAWING-SCREEN.
000790     03  LINE 17 COLUMN 5  VALUE "Here are today's lucky".
000791     03  LINE 18 COLUMN 6  VALUE "JUSTIN-BALL numbers!".
000800     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 1  VALUE "Lotto".
000810     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 9  VALUE "Lotto".
000820     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 17 VALUE "Lotto".
000830     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 25 VALUE "Lotto".
000840     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 33 VALUE "Lotto".
000850     03  LINE 20 COLUMN 41 VALUE "POWER".
000860     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 1  VALUE "Ball".
000870     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 9  VALUE "Ball".
000880     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 17 VALUE "Ball".
000890     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 25 VALUE "Ball".
000900     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 33 VALUE "Ball".
000910     03  LINE 21 COLUMN 41 VALUE "BALL".
000920     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 1  VALUE "No. 1".
000930     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 9  VALUE "No. 2".
000940     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 17 VALUE "No. 3".
000950     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 25 VALUE "No. 4".
000960     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 33 VALUE "No. 5".
000970     03  LINE 22 COLUMN 43 VALUE "|".
000980     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 3  PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-1
000990         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001000     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 11 PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-2
001010         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001020     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 19 PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-3
001030         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001040     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 27 PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-4
001050         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001060     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 35 PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-5
001070         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001080     03  LINE 23 COLUMN 43 PIC 9(2) USING RANDOM-NUMBER-6
001090         REVERSE-VIDEO REQUIRED.
001100******************************************************************
001101*  SCREEN THAT COMPARES ANSWERS AND RETURNS WITH CORRECT MATCHES *
001102******************************************************************
001103 01  PRIZE-SCREEN.
001104     03  LINE 26 COLUMN 5 VALUE "Let's see if you matched any".
001105     03  LINE 27 COLUMN 6 VALUE "numbers in today's drawing.".
001106*  FILL IN CALL TO APPROPIATE VALUE FROM PRIZE-VALUE EQUATION    *
001107******************************************************************
001108*  SCREEN THAT ASKS FOR THE EXIT COMMAND                         *
001109******************************************************************
001110 01  EXIT-SCREEN
001111     BLANK SCREEN, AUTO, REQUIRED,
001112     FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 7,
001113     BACKGROUND-COLOR IS 4.
001114     03  LINE 29 COLUMN 5 VALUE "Do you want to quit? (y/n)".
001115     03  LINE 14 COLUMN 35 PIC X USING EXITING-SCREEN.
001116 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
001117 JUSTIN-BALL-START.
001118     PERFORM PROCESS-SCREEN.
001119 PROCESS-SCREEN.
001120     DISPLAY DATA-ENTRY-SCREEN.
001121     ACCEPT  DATA-ENTRY-SCREEN.
001122     DISPLAY CONFIRMATION-SCREEN-1.
001123     ACCEPT  CONFIRMATION-SCREEN-1.
001124         IF   PLAY-JB IS EQUAL TO "N" OR
001125              PLAY-JB IS EQUAL TO "n" THEN
001126              GO TO JUSTIN-BALL-START
001127         ELSE GO TO LOTTERY-START
001130         END-IF.
001131 LOTTERY-START.
001140     MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (9:8) TO RANDOM-SEED
001150     PERFORM RANDNUM-1 THRU RANDNUM-6.
001160*******************************************************************
001161*  THE FOLLOWING SECTIONS PERFORM THE COMPUTATION OF THE RANDOM   *
001162*  NUMBERS                                                        *
001163*******************************************************************
001170 RANDNUM-1.
001180     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001190         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001200         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-1 = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001210     END-PERFORM.
001230 RANDNUM-2.
001240     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001250         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001260         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-2  = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001270     END-PERFORM.
001290 RANDNUM-3.
001300     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001310         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001320         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-3 = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001330     END-PERFORM.
001350 RANDNUM-4.
001360     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001370         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001380         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-4 = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001390     END-PERFORM.
001410 RANDNUM-5.
001420     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001430         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001440         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-5 = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001450     END-PERFORM.
001470 RANDNUM-6.
001480     PERFORM 1 TIMES
001490         COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM
001500         COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER-6 = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 50) + 1
001510     END-PERFORM.
001530*******************************************************************
001531*  THIS SECTION COMPARES THE FIRST FIVE NUMBERS AGAINST EACH      *
001532*  OTHER TO GUARENTEE THEIR UNIQUENESS                            *
001533*******************************************************************
001534 COMPARE-RANDNUM.
001535     IF RANDOM-NUMBER-1 = RANDOM-NUMBER-2 OR
001536        RANDOM-NUMBER-1 = RANDOM-NUMBER-3 OR
001537        RANDOM-NUMBER-1 = RANDOM-NUMBER-4 OR
001538       RANDOM-NUMBER-1 = RANDOM-NUMBER-5 THEN
001539        GO TO RANDNUM-1
001540     END-IF
001541     IF RANDOM-NUMBER-2 = RANDOM-NUMBER-1 OR
001542        RANDOM-NUMBER-2 = RANDOM-NUMBER-3 OR
001543        RANDOM-NUMBER-2 = RANDOM-NUMBER-4 OR
001544        RANDOM-NUMBER-2 = RANDOM-NUMBER-5 THEN
001545        GO TO RANDNUM-2
001546     END-IF
001547     IF RANDOM-NUMBER-3 = RANDOM-NUMBER-1 OR
001548        RANDOM-NUMBER-3 = RANDOM-NUMBER-2 OR
001549        RANDOM-NUMBER-3 = RANDOM-NUMBER-4 OR
001550        RANDOM-NUMBER-3 = RANDOM-NUMBER-5 THEN
001551        GO TO RANDNUM-3
001552     END-IF
001553     IF RANDOM-NUMBER-4 = RANDOM-NUMBER-1 OR
001554        RANDOM-NUMBER-4 = RANDOM-NUMBER-2 OR
001555        RANDOM-NUMBER-4 = RANDOM-NUMBER-3 OR
001556        RANDOM-NUMBER-4 = RANDOM-NUMBER-5 THEN
001557        GO TO RANDNUM-4
001558     END-IF
001559     IF RANDOM-NUMBER-5 = RANDOM-NUMBER-1 OR
001560        RANDOM-NUMBER-5 = RANDOM-NUMBER-2 OR
001561        RANDOM-NUMBER-5 = RANDOM-NUMBER-3 OR
001562        RANDOM-NUMBER-5 = RANDOM-NUMBER-4 THEN
001563        GO TO RANDNUM-5
001564     END-IF.
001565     DISPLAY LOTTERY-DRAWING-SCREEN.
001566*******************************************************************
001567*  THIS SECTION COMPARES THE USER ENTERED NUMBERS AGAINST THE     *
001568*  DRAWN NUMBERS                                                  *
001569*******************************************************************
001570* COMPARE-ALL-NUMBERS.
001571*     IF LOTTO-BALL-1 = RANDOM-NUMBER-1 OR
001572*        LOTTO-BALL-1 = RANDOM-NUMBER-2 OR
001573*        LOTTO-BALL-1 = RANDOM-NUMBER-3 OR
001574*        LOTTO-BALL-1 = RANDOM-NUMBER-4 OR
001575*        LOTTO-BALL-1 = RANDOM-NUMBER-5 THEN
001576*  FILL IN EQUATION FOR ADDING A +1 VALUE TO PRIZE-VALUE          *
001577*        END-IF
001578*     IF LOTTO-BALL-2 = RANDOM-NUMBER-1 OR
001579*        LOTTO-BALL-2 = RANDOM-NUMBER-2 OR
001580*        LOTTO-BALL-2 = RANDOM-NUMBER-3 OR
001581*        LOTTO-BALL-2 = RANDOM-NUMBER-4 OR
001582*        LOTTO-BALL-2 = RANDOM-NUMBER-5 THEN
001583*  FILL IN EQUATION FOR ADDING A +1 VALUE TO PRIZE-VALUE          *
001584*        END-IF
001585*     IF LOTTO-BALL-3 = RANDOM-NUMBER-1 OR
001586*        LOTTO-BALL-3 = RANDOM-NUMBER-2 OR
001587*        LOTTO-BALL-3 = RANDOM-NUMBER-3 OR
001588*        LOTTO-BALL-3 = RANDOM-NUMBER-4 OR
001589*        LOTTO-BALL-3 = RANDOM-NUMBER-5 THEN
001590*  FILL IN EQUATION FOR ADDING A +1 VALUE TO PRIZE-VALUE          *
001591*        END-IF
001592*     IF LOTTO-BALL-4 = RANDOM-NUMBER-1 OR
001593*        LOTTO-BALL-4 = RANDOM-NUMBER-2 OR
001594*        LOTTO-BALL-4 = RANDOM-NUMBER-3 OR
001595*        LOTTO-BALL-4 = RANDOM-NUMBER-4 OR
001596*        LOTTO-BALL-4 = RANDOM-NUMBER-5 THEN
001597*  FILL IN EQUATION FOR ADDING A +1 VALUE TO PRIZE-VALUE          *
001598*        END-IF
001599*     IF LOTTO-BALL-5 = RANDOM-NUMBER-1 OR
001600*        LOTTO-BALL-5 = RANDOM-NUMBER-2 OR
001601*        LOTTO-BALL-5 = RANDOM-NUMBER-3 OR
001602*        LOTTO-BALL-5 = RANDOM-NUMBER-4 OR
001603*        LOTTO-BALL-5 = RANDOM-NUMBER-5 THEN
001604*  FILL IN EQUATION FOR ADDING A +1 VALUE TO PRIZE-VALUE          *
001605*        END-IF
001606*     IF JUSTIN-BALL-6 = RANDOM-NUMBER-6 THEN
001607*  FILL IN EQUATION FOR ADDING A +1 VALUE TO JUSTIN-BALL-PRIZE    *
001608*        END-IF.
001609*******************************************************************
001610*  THE FOLLOWING SECTION DISPLAYS THE WINNING RESULTS OF THE GAME *
001611*******************************************************************
001613*    DISPLAY PRIZE-SCREEN.
001614*    ACCEPT PRIZE-SCREEN.
001615     DISPLAY EXIT-SCREEN.
001616     ACCEPT  EXIT-SCREEN.
001617         IF   QUIT-JB IS EQUAL TO "N" OR
001618              QUIT-JB IS EQUAL TO "n" THEN
001619              GO TO JUSTIN-BALL-START
001620         ELSE CONTINUE
001623         END-IF
001624         EXIT PROGRAM.
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen error message when executing a program

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-04T15:38:07-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<50E902271766C9FF.FBB548C6242C54F6.C8270530086590A5@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<C3Hy6.1733$uk.138390@news.uswest.net>`

```
On Wed, 4 Apr 2001 10:25:44 -0500, "J. Reis" <jreis26@hotmail.com>
enlightened us:

I found a few things Justin.  Not sure if it is everything though.
Look for comments below.

>Good day.
>
…[166 more quoted lines elided]…
>001118     PERFORM PROCESS-SCREEN.

You PERFORM PROCESS-SCREEN and then fall through and do the statements
in that paragraph again.  You do not need the PERFORM.  

>001119 PROCESS-SCREEN.
>001120     DISPLAY DATA-ENTRY-SCREEN.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>001150     PERFORM RANDNUM-1 THRU RANDNUM-6.

You do the PERFORM above then you fall through the same paragraphs.
You do not need the PERFORM at this point.  Also you don't need the 1
TIMES below on your PERFORM.  If there are no TIMES, 1 is assumed.
However, having 1 TIMES does not cause any problems.

>001160*******************************************************************
>001161*  THE FOLLOWING SECTIONS PERFORM THE COMPUTATION OF THE RANDOM   *
…[125 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Borrow money from a pessimist - they don't expect it back.


DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge
Read the facts and sign a petition at:

http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen error message when executing a program

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T03:47:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IVRy6.520793$Pm2.8388476@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<C3Hy6.1733$uk.138390@news.uswest.net>`

```
Line 1114 specifies the highest line your are trying to display. It is line
29.  Likely, you have not set a screen size.  The default is an 80 column by
24 line screen. At run time, the program bombs, because it cannot write the
output.  Note that the screen size is set when you run the program, so you
are quite correct with your code.  What you have to do is set the run time
screen to fit.  I like a blank line at top and  bottom, I think it looks
better, so I would suggest you try a screen size of 30*80.  Once you see
what it looks like, you can narrow it down so the text looks centered.

Run time parameters are specified in a file called cobol85.cbr that sits in
the run area alongside the exe.  Here is a typical one for a system. The
name in the square brackets is the PROGRAM-ID that you used in the program.
(BTW, Yours won't work.  Change:
> 000030 PROGRAM-ID.   LOTTERY.
to
> 000030 PROGRAM-ID.  " LOTTERY".
)


[DISPATCH]
@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE
@NoMessage=YES

[CONTEXT FILE UPDATE]
@CBR_SCR_KEYDEFFILE=COBOL.KBD
@ScrnFont=(Terminal,20)
@ScrnSize=(54,14)

[SelectCustomerTester]
@CBR_SCR_KEYDEFFILE=COBOL.KBD
@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE
@NoMessage=YES
@ScrnFont=(Terminal,20)
@ScrnSize=(80,19)

[SelectScheculeItemTester]
@CBR_SCR_KEYDEFFILE=COBOL.KBD
@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE
@NoMessage=YES
@ScrnFont=(Terminal,20)
@ScrnSize=(80,19)

[SelectTicketTester]
@CBR_SCR_KEYDEFFILE=COBOL.KBD
@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE
@NoMessage=YES
@ScrnFont=(Terminal,20)
@ScrnSize=(80,19)


[WEIGH IN]
@CBR_SCR_KEYDEFFILE=COBOL.KBD
@NoMessage=YES
@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE
@ScrnFont=(Terminal,20)
@ScrnSize=(54,7)

[WEIGH IN.ENTRY]
SioBaud=WSC32.DLL
SioDTR=WSC32.DLL
SioGetc=WSC32.DLL
SioParms=WSC32.DLL
SioReset=WSC32.DLL
SioRTS=WSC32.DLL
SioDone=WSC32.DLL


"J. Reis" <jreis26@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:C3Hy6.1733$uk.138390@news.uswest.net...
> Good day.
>
> I am once again working on my final project for my COBOL programming
course
> and I have run into a bit of a snag.  Using the Fujitsu Compiler on a PC,
> whenever I execute my program, the first screen comes up correctly, but
when
> I enter that I want to continue, a error message stating "JMP0206I-W
SCREEN
> ITEM SIZE IS LARGER THAN LOGICAL SCREEN" appears.  I thought this might be
> related to my windows linking one after the other rather than blanking the
> screen.  So I added the BLANK SCREEN to the last set of commands in the
> Procedure Division and it caused this error.  Removing the line, this
error
> still occurs, but after three screens have been processed.  I am not
exactly
> sure what this error pertains to but would greatly appreciate getting a
> pointer in the correct direction as how best to deal with this particular
…[297 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
ghItlh Ben Logan <bl03@students.stir.ac.uk> 

>can anybody help me with aggregating records in a table, basically to
>summarize a list of data.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>ben

Need a little more than this to go on.

fields grouped by the table key?

Do you mean table as in Database table? Or something in Working
Storage?  If it's in WS, how did it get there?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help

- **From:** Ben Logan <bl03@students.stir.ac.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T16:51:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACC9471.844EF742@students.stir.ac.uk>`
- **References:** `<3ACB8707.5572AC73@students.stir.ac.uk> <8bfncto5d7dh8urltrnajpbn1uto86u8un@4ax.com>`

```
I have an input file with data set out as follows:

10*Wanda     *0002*04999
03*Larsson   *0001*54950
23*Sparkli   *0500*00045
11*Wanda     *0001*04949
10*Larsson   *0003*49995
23*Wanda     *0010*04899
10*Sparkli   *0750*00039
12*Wibbli    *0001*02399
12*Wobbli    *0002*09995

The first number is a shop branch number, then comes the item of
furniture, then the quantity sold and then the unit price.

I have set the FD section to recognise this as follows:

       01  Branch-record.
           05 Branch-no        PIC X(2).
           05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .
           05 Product-name     PIC X(10).
           05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .
           05 Quantity-sold    PIC 9(4).
           05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .
           05 Unit-price       PIC 999V99.

The problem is that I need to read the file and update a table held in
working storage after each line has been read. As you can a see a
particular branch may appear more than once. The table then needs to be
displayed showing each branch number, there total sales, there total
income, and the average selling price. My problem is creating this table
and causing it to be updated after each line is read. I understand the
maths that need to be done in order to compute the figures. If you need
more info let me know.
thansk a lot
Ben


qIroS wrote:
> 
> ghItlh Ben Logan <bl03@students.stir.ac.uk>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Storage?  If it's in WS, how did it get there?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T11:00:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACCA4C0.AFDB6EDC@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3ACB8707.5572AC73@students.stir.ac.uk> <8bfncto5d7dh8urltrnajpbn1uto86u8un@4ax.com> <3ACC9471.844EF742@students.stir.ac.uk>`

```
Please don't respond to me personally again - I use newsgroups not personal
e-mail, and am replying publicly, not privately.

Ben Logan wrote:

> I have an input file with data set out as follows:
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ben

You mean "their total", not "there total".  (If you're asking for the favor,
I get to scratch my pet peeve - at least you didn't confuse "your" with
"you're"!!!)

The table is based upon branch number.  If that branch number has to be a
number between 1 and 99, it is easy.

But I am going to assume a little more difficulty:

01   MY-INDEX           PIC S9(5) BINARY.
01   MY-MAX              PIC S9(5)  VALUE ZERO.
01   MY-LIMIT             PIC S9(5)  VALUE 100.
01   MY-TABLE
      05   MY-RECORD   OCCURS 100.
             10   MY-BRANCH     PIC XX.
             10   MY-QUANTITY    PIC 9(5)
             10   MY-   put whatever you will need to do your calculations.
....


INIT-ROUTINE
      move all '0'    to my-table.
....


READ-ROUTINE.
      PERFORM VARYING MY-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
                              UNTIL   MY-INDEX > MY-MAX
                                      OR MY-BRANCH (MY-INDEX) = BRANCH-NO
      END-PERFORM.
      IF MY-INDEX > MY-MAX
            ADD  1        TO MY-MAX
            MOVE BRANCH-NO     TO MY-BRANCH (MY-INDEX)
            IF MY-MAX > MY-LIMIT
                  DISPLAY ' MY-TABLE OVERFLOW'
                  PERFORM ERROR-ROUTINE
            END-IF
      END-IF

      ADD QUANTITY-SOLD TO MY-QUANTITY (MY-INDEX).
      Do other calculations you need now into the table.



When you are finished, do a perform, walking the table and getting its
output to print.

Ways to improve the program:
1.   Sort the file before you read it.   This will help immensely.  You
won't have to search at all.
2.   Use an index instead of a subscript to use the SEARCH command.
3.   If you have numeric subscripts between 01 and 99, don't do a search at
all - but go directly to the RECORD (BRANCH-NO).   BRANCH-NO needs to be pic
99, not XX, and you need to do an IF ALPHA check to make sure it doesn't
have an invalid value.

Try these all and tell your instructor why you picked the choice you prefer.



I'm sorry I gave too much detail - I don't have enough time to properly
imply what you need to do without showing you.  But if you try my
suggestions, you should learn enough to make up for my lack.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> Please don't respond to me personally again - I use newsgroups not personal
> e-mail, and am replying publicly, not privately.
>
> Ben Logan wrote:

Ben,

Howard is not being snotty. Writing to us direct kind of puts an onus on us -
but writing to the group, anyone can join in and make alternative suggestions.
Think on it -

        Ahhh... tis April
        The Spring is sprung,
        The grass is riz
        I wunder where young Benji iz ?

        Answer :

        I ha'e no doot he's off the campus
        To Stirling city, to create a ruckus
        In tavern yon, he'll ha'e a wee dram
        Hoping wi' a local lassie
        He'll get sassy

Meanwhile us old codgers are looking over his homework <G>.

(Apologies to Robbie Burns, William Wordsworth and all the rest).

So let's paraphrase the assignment you sent to me :-
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Firstly, your instructor is NOT telling you how it would be done in the real
world, but in addition to testing your handling of input/output files, he's
really after seeing if you have a good grasp of table handling, and as he says
the application is developed incrementally. (The real easy way, sort the damned
input and then go from there).

1. Read in-file and display records
2. You have 59 branches/stores - note he says '59' NOT  "1 - 59"
3. You have a max of 500 different products, (could be you only have 50)
4. Your output Reports.txt file can be written as a Line Sequential - which can
then be printed.
5. To test your logic, he is DEMANDING your have two tables (1) Summarize branch
sales and (2) Summarize product sales, which are written to Reports.txt.

For the branches - you could do it quick and dirty with a 100-entry table,
slotting each branch number into the appropriate pigeon hole. It's a two-digit
branch code so not unreasonable to assume the number will be anywhere between 1
and 99. (That's the obvious way - BUT - back when I was with Debenhams, we had
three-digit store numbers but only 60 department stores).

Product sales - This is where Howard's suggestion of an indexed table comes in.
If not comfortable with this, check out what you can turn up on SEARCH by index.

Ah Ha ! The VAT WASN'T  a red herring. Local managers can set their own prices.
(Your instructor has been talking to my beloved Eileen - she makes Arabian
gentlemen in a flea market look like rank amateurs when it comes to price
haggling <G>).

Input file :-

> Branch, Product   Qty     Retail Price
> >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

From : Howard

>
> >The table is based upon branch number.  If that branch number has to be a
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> won't have to search at all.

********
If you think your instructor will buy a SORT - then go that route - but it's my
impression he deliberately pushed you for a sequential read/display, then
following up with table handling.
*********

>
> 2.   Use an index instead of a subscript to use the SEARCH command.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> suggestions, you should learn enough to make up for my lack.

*********
Based on my interpretation of the 'challenge' - you'll have to go with index
searching for the Product summary. Get your indexing right and you are a large
part of the way there.

Now to the source you sent me - Oooh Errr. Lots of problems. I really can't sit
down and rewrite it, so firstly a little bit of pseudo code and then your source
:

        Initialize stuff
        set FileNotFinshed to true
        perform Read-In-Next
        perform until FileFinished
                perform Display stuff
                perform Branch-Table-Add
                perform Product-Table-Add

                if FileNotFinished
                   perform Read-in-Next
                end-if

        End-perform

        Perform Branch-Table-Summarize
            varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ?????? (your max Branch entry)
        *> This is writing the Branch Summary to Reports.txt
        Perform Product-Table-Summarise
            varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ???? (your max Product entry)
        *> This is writing the Product Summary to Reports.txt

See my comments interspersed. You'll really have to give this one a re-think.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
FILE-CONTROL.
    SELECT File-in ASSIGN TO "Sales.txt"
        ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.
FD File-in.
01 Branch-record.
       05 Branch-no        PIC 99.
       05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .  *> you really don't
       05 Product-name     PIC X(10).         *> need to worry
       05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .  *> about value '*'
       05 Quantity-sold    PIC 9(4).          *> They are just
       05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .  *> fillers
       05 Unit-price       PIC 999V99.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 Branch-table.
    03 Branch-info OCCURS 59 TIMES.
       05 Branch-id        PIC 99.
       05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .
       05 Branch-quantity  PIC 9(4).
77 Sub-branch PIC 99.
01 Flags.
    05 File-in-eof-flag PIC A VALUE "N".
        88  File-in-ended  VALUE "Y".
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
Main-para.

*> I 'HATE' your Flag above (just my particular preference).
*> Try using :-
*> 01 FileFlag              pic 9.
*>  05 FileFinished         value 1.
*>  05 FileNotFinished      value 0.
*> You have to do more initializing - hence the reason you got a
*> non-numeric on your first access of Branch-id(x)

INITIALIZE Branch-Table, Sub-Branch     *> Both of these lines
set FileNotFinished to true                           *> are part of Init.
                                                                   *> sometimes
it's a good idea to
                                                                   *> perform
INIT-PARAGRAPH

OPEN INPUT File-in
MOVE ZERO TO Sub-branch.  *> delete - see above
PERFORM Read-record
PERFORM UNTIL File-in-ended
PERFORM VARYING Sub-branch FROM 1 BY 1

*> I'll bet he will hate this - he's probably looking for you to
*> do a search/index rather than a perform

  UNTIL Branch-id (Sub-branch) = Branch-no
    OR Branch-id (Sub-branch) = SPACE

*> Why would you test for SPACE both Branch IDs are numeric.

*> I see you have MF - do you have NetExpress with the Animator
*> or any other M/F compiler with an Animator - it loops below
*> between CONTINUE and END-PERFORM without doing anything

    CONTINUE
  END-PERFORM
IF Branch-id (Sub-branch) = Branch-no
  ADD Quantity-sold TO Branch-quantity (Sub-branch)
ELSE
  MOVE Quantity-sold TO Branch-quantity (Sub-branch)
  MOVE Branch-no TO Branch-id (Sub-branch)
  END-IF
DISPLAY Branch-table
PERFORM Read-record
END-PERFORM
STOP RUN.
Read-record.
READ File-in
    AT END
        MOVE "Y" TO File-in-eof-flag
        CLOSE File-in
END-READ.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Ben,

Don't get discouraged - you're getting  there.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Ben,

You've titillated us so far - and based on what you've written above - any
one of us could give you several different solutions - and the answer may
NOT be what your instructor was after. Care to post the text of the original
assignment - then you'll get an answer which more closely matches the
instructor's question.

(Remember - just like you - we have to 'know' the question in full detail -
before we can figure out the right answer).

Nothing wrong with your input record format - you are reading in a
SEQUENTIAL file, which of course allows for duplicate branch numbers,
product names and different unit selling prices. (So, some assumptions so
far - (1) Branch Number is in range 01 - 99, (2) there can be 'umpteen'
product names ? (3) Each product can have a different selling price per
branch, or is the local retail salesman allowed to make a deal, ("I'll let
you have it without VAT"),  so that you can even have more than one retail
price for the product at the same branch ?)
.
PS: Reference to VAT is a bit of a red herring. Here in Canada, frequent
retail advertising gimmick, "We wont charge you GST". 'Great', thinks the
customer, but 7% of the eventual price is STILL GST <G>.
.
Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T18:06:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uu2z6.520999$f36.15881167@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3ACB8707.5572AC73@students.stir.ac.uk> <8bfncto5d7dh8urltrnajpbn1uto86u8un@4ax.com> <3ACC9471.844EF742@students.stir.ac.uk>`

```
Your first step should be to remove the value statements from your record
description.  They are illegal, and will not compile.  You are going to
*read* this data, not set it up in memory with the compiler.  After you read
it from disk, you may wish to check the validity of the data you read, by
checking to see if the asterisks are there.  To do that, you will have to
give names to the fields, rather than making them filler.

As a hint on where to start, you define the file in the file section, then
you describe the table in the working-storage section.  Once that is done,
you write a procedure that reads the data from the file, and places it in
the table. After loading the table, you can manipulate it as much as you
want with further procedures.  Start by writing a program that reads the
data, and simply displays it on the screen. That should be about a five line
procedure.  Once you have that working, then play with putting it in a
table, and get that working.  When that is complete and tested, then write
the analysis stuff, and jazz up your display.

"Ben Logan" <bl03@students.stir.ac.uk> wrote in message
news:3ACC9471.844EF742@students.stir.ac.uk...
> I have an input file with data set out as follows:
>
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Storage?  If it's in WS, how did it get there?
```

```
Donald Tees wrote:

> Your first step should be to remove the value statements from your record
> description.  They are illegal, and will not compile.

I have used at least one compiler where you just get a warning message here or
in the linkage section.  I have seen it when using a copymember with value
statements.   It will be interesting when we have a new, improved INITIALIZE
command to see if more compilers will allow it.

I kind of like it being allowed in input files even though it doesn't do
anything - I use commented out versions as documentation in parm files.
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T18:21:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9aiunc$m2n$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ACB8707.5572AC73@students.stir.ac.uk> <8bfncto5d7dh8urltrnajpbn1uto86u8un@4ax.com> <3ACC9471.844EF742@students.stir.ac.uk> <uu2z6.520999$f36.15881167@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ACCB748.FAEE9625@brazee.net> <GP2z6.521039$f36.15882017@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
FYI -

Syntax rule 3 of the VALUE clause in the Net Express V3.1 LRM

"In the File Section and the Linkage Section,
and Local-Storage Section, the VALUE clause can be used in data item
entries, but is documentary only. "

This is documented as an MF, OSVS, and VSC2 extension - because it is also a
documented extension for all IBM mainframe compilers (from OS/VS COBOL on).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: value clauses in record or linkage descriptions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T17:33:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9airsh$gim$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ACB8707.5572AC73@students.stir.ac.uk> <8bfncto5d7dh8urltrnajpbn1uto86u8un@4ax.com> <3ACC9471.844EF742@students.stir.ac.uk> <uu2z6.520999$f36.15881167@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ACCB748.FAEE9625@brazee.net>`

```
FYI -

A) a "number" of compilers currently accept (as an extension) VALUE clauses
(other than 88-levels) in both File Section and Linkage Section.  They are
(needless to say) "ignored" in most cases.

B) the draft Standard makes it explicit that you MAY code a VALUE clause
(other than 88-levels) in both File Section and Linkage Section.

C) the new "INITIALIZE TO VALUE" variation of initialize *will* pay
attention to these VALUE clauses.

  ***

General Rules 2 and 3 for the VALUE clause in the FCD are:

"2) In the file section, VALUE clauses take effect only during the execution
of an INITIALIZE statement. The initial value of the data items in the file
section is undefined.

3) In the linkage section, VALUE clauses take effect only during the
execution of an INITIALIZE statement. The initial value of the data items in
the linkage section is specified in 13.6, Linkage section."
```

```

Value statements aren't illegal in file section, they can be used on 88
levels.
```

```
Ok, I've got this far:
        ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT My-file-in ASSIGN TO "Sales.txt"
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
           SELECT My-file-out ASSIGN TO "Report.txt"
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD My-file-in.
       01  Line-in     PIC X(80).
       FD My-file-out.
       01  Line-out    PIC X(80).
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01 Branch-record.
        03 Branch-info OCCURS 59 TIMES.
          05 Branch-no        PIC 99.
          05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .
          05 Product-name     PIC X(10).
          05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .
          05 Quantity-sold    PIC 9(4).
          05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .
          05 Unit-price       PIC 999V99.
       01 Flags.
           05 File-in-eof-flag PIC A VALUE "N".
               88  My-file-in-ended    VALUE "Y".
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       Main-para.
           OPEN INPUT My-file-in
               OUTPUT My-file-out
           PERFORM Read-record
           PERFORM UNTIL My-file-in-ended
           MOVE Line-in
               TO Branch-record
           DISPLAY Branch-record
               PERFORM Read-record
               END-PERFORM
           CLOSE My-file-out
           STOP RUN.
       Read-record.
           READ My-file-in
               AT END
                   MOVE "Y" TO File-in-eof-flag
                   CLOSE My-file-in
           END-READ.

This displays the newly created table, but I am confused as to how I go
about creating a new table with the relevant lines added together so
that any one branch does not have more than one occurence in the table.
I hope you can understand what I am getting at:
we have an input file with data as follows:

10*Wanda     *0002*04999
03*Larsson   *0001*54950
23*Sparkli   *0500*00045
11*Wanda     *0001*04949
10*Larsson   *0003*49995
23*Wanda     *0010*04899
10*Sparkli   *0750*00039

 And I need an output file with data as follows:

Branch	Volume	Total value	Average price
3	1	ï¿½549.50		ï¿½549.50
10	755	ï¿½1,892.33	ï¿½2.51
11	1	ï¿½49.99		ï¿½49.99
23	510	ï¿½714.90		ï¿½1.40

Regards 
Ben
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T19:50:50+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACE100A.7501DB60@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ACB8707.5572AC73@students.stir.ac.uk> <8bfncto5d7dh8urltrnajpbn1uto86u8un@4ax.com> <3ACC9471.844EF742@students.stir.ac.uk> <uu2z6.520999$f36.15881167@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ACCE187.B9726FA6@students.stir.ac.uk>`

```
Ben Logan wrote:
> 
> Ok, I've got this far:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>                ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

Without FILE STATUS clauses the program will terminate with an error
code if anything goes wrong (such as file not found).  It is normal to
have file status and then check this after every file operation (except
CLOSE, UNLOCK unless you are extremely pedantic).
 
>        DATA DIVISION.
>        FILE SECTION.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>           05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .

VALUE clauses shouldn't be in an occuring item.  These are not required
anyway.  Although you imagine these bytes as having this value, they
will only get this value through reading the data in the file.

>           05 Product-name     PIC X(10).
>           05 FILLER           PIC X VALUE "*" .
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>                TO Branch-record

No.  'Branch-Record' (badly named try Branch-Table) is the _whole_
area.  You need to move each record read to 'Branch-Info'. There are 59
of these so you need to specify which one using a subscript.  ie  MOVE
Line-In   TO Branch-Info(record-number)  where record-number is defined
in W-S as, say, PIC 9(4), is initialised to zero and then incremented on
each read before being used.

>            DISPLAY Branch-record

Will display 59 times 24 characters after each record is read.  Just
DISPLAY Line-In.  

>                PERFORM Read-record
>                END-PERFORM
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> that any one branch does not have more than one occurence in the table.

Two ways possible.  if the branch numbers are guaranteed to be 01 to 99
then use this as a subscript to a table that OCCURS 99 (or 100, put
branch zero into 100 if it happens by) with
ADD Line-Quantity-Sold  TO Quantity-Sold(Line-Branch-No) where the
Line-~ fields are defined in the Line-In record under the FD. 
Initialise the table first with MOVE ALL ZERO TO Branch-Table.

Second way is to search the table looking for existing branch.

             PERFORM
                 VARYING Table-Sub FROM 1 BY 1
                   UNTIL Table-Sub = 59     (or whatever the OCCURS is)
                      OR Line-Branch-No = Branch-No(Table-Sub)
                      OR Branch-No(Table-Sub) = ZERO

                 CONTINUE
              END-PERFORM
              MOVE Line-Branch-No      TO Branch-No(Table-Sub)
              ADD line-Quantity-Sold   TO Quantity-Sold(Table-Sub)		
			

Note this leaves the table compact, but not ordered.

> I hope you can understand what I am getting at:
> we have an input file with data as follows:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ben
```

#### ↳ Re: help

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T07:17:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACC7063.93551B41@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3ACB8707.5572AC73@students.stir.ac.uk>`

```
Ben Logan wrote:

> can anybody help me with aggregating records in a table, basically to
> summarize a list of data.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ben

We can help in homework - keyword is help.  Check our FAQ and you will
find out the requirements which are something like:

Show us what you have done so far.
Mention your environment (compiler and OS).
Show us error messages.
Tell us what you have tried, and let us see where you're stuck.
Show us the requirements/specs/pseudo code.

The line between helping you and doing your homework is one which is
important to us.

If you haven't done anything, the best we can do is something like this
pseudo-code:

initialize summary fields
read record until at end
    add amounts from record to summary fields
    loop
at-end write summary fields.

But we don't know your requirements - You write your pseudo code, test it
out with a pencil and paper, then convert it to CoBOL.  Test it again
with pencil and paper and compile and test it in real life.  If you get
stuck, read this message and then come back for assistance.
```

<a id="t-78b8c0fb7e"></a>
## Thread: index vs subscript performance — 3 this month / 22 total

_Thread started [2001-03](2001-03.md#t-78b8c0fb7e)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Keith Odam <0dam-k@iname.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T07:25:56+09:30
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACB986C.F94A3E8F@iname.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news> <99ohjr$va7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <36Uv6.16278$ue1.1378062@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <f201ct8h7slmdmhnolcn8g4dcdag7h8ql3@4ax.com> <Es0w6.603$Uw.74978@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <mh81ctseo5hovj163i676679a8pf04koid@4ax.com> <9xlw6.4394$aP5.356855@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
> "Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com
> > You have proven it in the case where subscripts and
> > indexes are identical.
[ ... ]
> As you say, I proved the case that "subscripts and
> indexes are identical". Substantially more than nothing.

  I think it helps to be clear about your terminology.
A table entry has an occurrence number and an offset.
A subscript is an explicit occurrence number and is
converted to an offset value for addressing and not
"converted into an index". (Your compiler may maintain
an index item in the background, but doesn't need to).  

  The timing values of Jerry's example are not surprising,
as both cases include the arithmetic to increment the data
item (J) used as a subscript while only the indexing case
includes the arithmetic to increment the index.
  "PERFORM 10000 TIMES" can be faster than incrementing
and testing an otherwise unused data item.
  (Binyamin has noted that the example uses the special
case of a single character table element.)

  It seems to me that indexes offer performance differences
that can be large on different platforms while both should
work on all.  As always, code for correct results with
allowance to tune for performance.
```

#### ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-04T14:08:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACB8D39.9703B746@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news>`

```
I have done optimizations of COBOL programs for many years, generally on
the IBM mainframe.  In addition, I have written COBOL compilers.  Of the
many answers offered below, I would say first, that we have found it
possible to save enough money (in the form of reduced CPU time) to
justify keeping an eye on our expensive jobs, and second, that it is
dangerous to generalize about the subject of index items vs. subscripts.

In the COBOL compilers I worked on, I am willing to admit that we kept
index values as binary integers (the same as a "good" subscript).  Thus,
very little performance advantage could be gained from using index
items, unless the programmer was so unwary as to use USAGE DISPLAY, or
omit an operational sign, etc., on the subscript definition.

On the other hand, IBM compilers maintain an index item as the offset
from the start of a table.  In a simple example, with 5 character
entries, the subscript 1 equates to an index value 0, subscript 2 =
index 5, etc.  A subscript, when used, must be loaded into a register,
have 1 subtracted from it, and then be multiplied by the table item
length (at which time it is equal to the index item, if there is one).
Then, either the index item of the computed subscript is added to the
address of the table, and the program is pointing at the right item in
the table.

I have personally optimized programs where I did not touch the Procedure
Division, but only changed USAGE and PICTURE in the Data Division, and
the program ran in only 50% of the original CPU time.

I believe that good standards can be developed in this area, depeneding
on the platform, compiler, etc.  A shop would be foolhardy to ignore the
chance to save run time dollars.

Much more to the point, programmers are capable of making bad code.  In
another example, we found a program that runs in 8 different jobs,
adding rates to records in a sequential data set.  When it was
originally coded, it looked up the rates in a table that was read into
memory.  As time went on, the table could no longer hold all of the rate
info, so code was added to check for table overflow, and if that
occurred, get the information from a DB2 table.  Now, a 9 million record
file does 9 million DB2 accesses.  The number of different keys for
which rates are looked up proves to be under 10,000.  So, a little
rethinking says:
1.  Is the key I want the same as the last key I wanted?  If so, I have
the answer.
2.  Is the key I want in a table I've built of all the answers?  If so,
I have the answer.
3.  If neither of the above cases is true, I need to get the answer from
DB2, and add it to the table (in sequence, so that I can do a SEARCH
ALL).

The net result was a savings of well over $11,000 each time these jobs
run -- a 97% reduction.

A similar case occurred years ago.  A program was reading a sequential
data set and making an IMS call for every record.  We found that the IMS
data base was being unloaded three steps earlier in the job, so we
changed to a sequential two file match, and cut the cost over 90%.

A final word about index vs. subscript:
Improper table handling leads to some of the toughest to find errors
when programs abend.  A shop is well advised to promote and require good
table loading techniques to ensure that little things like table
overflow don't occur.

The smart answer is that you should never get complacent.  Always look
for problems.  You'll usually find something that can be improved.
Colin Campbell
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T11:44:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Th1z6.106$be.8857@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news> <3ACB8D39.9703B746@attglobal.net>`

```
"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote:
>
> On the other hand, IBM compilers maintain an index item as the offset
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the table.

There is no need of subtracting 1 from the subscript in cases where the
table begins at least the length of 1 entry from address 0.  You simply
apply the calculated offset from Table - EntryLength.

> Much more to the point, programmers are capable of making bad code.  In
> another example, we found a program that runs in 8 different jobs,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> ALL).

Do a frequency analysis on the items in the database and load the table
with only the entries that have the highest hit-rate.  Assuming, of
course, that there is a pattern in the hit-rate.  Such data might be
generated from each run, and used to load the table for the next run.
This would allow for gradually changing patterns in the hit-rate.

> The smart answer is that you should never get complacent.  Always look
> for problems.  You'll usually find something that can be improved.

Something can *always* be improved. ;-)  'Optimization' is a misnomer,
for it is actually an iterative approach toward 'optimum', which is
virtually never reached in practice, for any complex procedure.  You
eventually reach a point of diminishing returns where you must begin
to sacrifice efficiency in one area to gain in another. :-)

A few years ago someone posted a simple, elegant string operation.
For fun we worked on it for a while and wound up with code that was
less elegant, but about 100 times faster than the original, elegant
code.
```

```
Richard Plinston wrote:

> One of the things that a programmer needs to learn is that there is a need to
> be completely specific in both reading and writing program specifications.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> languages.

I finally found a copy of this book at Computer Literacy Bookshops in San Jose, CA.
The
bookshop has a nice reading area where one can review a book before purchasing it. So
I spent about an hour looking at the Third Edition of  "Teach Yourself COBOL in 21
Days."
If this is the most recent edition, it is still a pretty poor book on COBOL.   The
code
examples are woefully out-of-date and represent some of the worst practices of COBOL
programming.  Those examples might have been OK twenty years ago, before the arrival
of ANSI standard 95, but they are likely to mislead new COBOL programmers into
poor coding style and bad habits today.    Of course, if you are still using COBOL-68
or COBOL-74, as some companies still are, it might be all right.

To its credit, the book does mention, all too briefly, some of the more current COBOL
capabilities.   This book might not be so bad if someone took the time to update the
long code examples to reflect more contemporary COBOL practice.

On the same shelf, at the same bookstore, was another book from the same publisher,
"Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours."    That is much better book. The code examples
represent much of current COBOL,  the discussions of  scope (not called that in the
book,
but that's not a problem) and control statements is up-to-date.   I wonder why the
editors
at SAMS could not have demanded the same quality of the 21 Days book they have
demonstrated in the 24 Hours book.
```

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T03:32:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3adfac5e.88522525@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3AAE3AD0.3D95B185@liv.ac.uk> <20010313203100.04277.00002352@ng-fi1.aol.com> <3ABF9756.5326984A@ix.netcom.com> <985638124.1204166251@aklobs.org.nz> <3AD9E361.3D169EBD@ix.netcom.com> <3ADAF64E.11238A3A@brazee.net>`

```
Funny Howard!  Actually, my book (The 24 hours one) is setup as 24
"one hour lessons".  I did quite a lot of work on Lesson Plan and
tried mightily to keep the tone conversational -- which by the way is
a "brand" identifyer for the 24 Hours series of books.  In addtion, I
try to keep the reader interested and not overwhelmed.  I reinforce
things already learned as new things are introduced, and the
phraseology is decidedly non technical.  I did that on purpose.  I'm
well pleased with the result, and I had a lot of lattitude in design
-- but in addition I had great support from some very good technical
editors (Howard Hinman) in addition to some great editing advice from
my editors at Sams.  All in all it turned out well and continues to be
a solid month to month performer.  I'm certainly never gonna get rich
off the book - but if things continue on their present track, sometime
in May I will have grossed Fifteen bucks an hour for my work on the
book!

My main goal in writing the book was to create an easy to use, yet
useful and current COBOL "Tutorial".  I'm glad to say the fanmail is
overwhelmingly positive -- I thank my gentle readers for their
support!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Maintenance - Cobol program

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T08:35:08+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACD71AB.2964199@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010404225729.14462.00001921@ng-ca1.aol.com> <3ACC6E41.823F2963@brazee.net> <FA92EFC537A0D2F3.307FFD4512F21CBF.66E462C0551E5711@lp.airnews.net> <3ACCB5B7.B5E71465@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> SkippyPB wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> would want to have it explained in a Cobol book?

In interactive systems the programs that add records, update or delete
them from master files may be called 'File maintenance' programs.  This
is an essential part of any Business system such as adding new products
and customers, changing prices, updating addresses.
```

#### ↳ Re: File Maintenance - Cobol program

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T13:58:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3Yjz6.8706$Kr1.743307@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<20010404225729.14462.00001921@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
For the novice, there is no book that explains the entire subject
clearly. For the expert, virtually all books on the subject are clear.

"BHarris948" <bharris948@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010404225729.14462.00001921@ng-ca1.aol.com...
> Can anyone tell me what cobol book would expain the subject clearly?
```

```
think it has been discussed but not much more. It would not be
that hard. Resources on all open source projects are limited.

DO you have a specific need for this? Would you mind describing
what the requirement is?

My COBOL compiler, COBOLForGCC is nearing the point where I will
have viable subset that will allow cobol programmers to
contribute to the project in their own language (a subset
thereof). I am using the GCC back end, which can generate code
for just about any platform (Z/Series, Palm Pilot, X86, etc),

Tim Josling
```

## Thread: Vs: First cobol-program — 4 msgs

### Re: Vs: First cobol-program

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T07:28:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi>`

```
Did this compile cleanly?   I haven't used many PC compilers but it seemed odd
to see Working-storage before I-O.

I'm also confused by your perform statement.  It appears that you have a
combination of performing a paragraph (oops I mean section) and an in-line
perform.  Use one or the other.

Some compilers give an error if they find a section without a paragraph name.
I myself prefer to not use sections in the procedure division, but other fine
programmers haven't been converted to my enlightened view. 8^)

"Jarkko Kï¿½hkï¿½nen" wrote:

> Thanks to everybody for help! I corrected my mistakes. Here is new version.
> (CUSTOMER-FILE was typo (what?!=)).
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…
>               Encoding: x-uuencode
```

#### ↳ Re: Vs: First cobol-program

- **From:** "Erlend Moen" <erlend.moen@disys.no>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T16:14:52+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ahul0$oq4$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> skrev i news:3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net
> Did this compile cleanly?   I haven't used many PC compilers but it seemed
odd
> to see Working-storage before I-O.

I agree to that...

> I'm also confused by your perform statement.  It appears that you have a
> combination of performing a paragraph (oops I mean section) and an in-line
> perform.  Use one or the other.

...but not to this!

The best reason I have to use sections instead of top-down paragraphs is
just this that I can program "functions" in this way and performing these
just with a simple statement (i.e. PERFORM CONVERT_DATE). I adopted this
from my experience with programming in TurboPascal in early 80's. Since '88
I've used sections in CObOL all the time. In *my* opinion the code is much
cleaner, but it demands that you use obvious section-names and not to
mention variable-names. Another benefit is that it's easy to make "complex"
copy-files and integrate these very easy in many programs.

I know this has been debated over and over here, but I couldn't resist...
:-)

Regards,
Erlend Moen
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Vs: First cobol-program

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T14:49:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kC%y6.4407$Kr1.433547@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net> <9ahul0$oq4$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`

```

"Erlend Moen" <erlend.moen@disys.no>
>
> > I'm also confused by your perform statement.  It appears that you
have a
> > combination of performing a paragraph (oops I mean section) and an
in-line
> > perform.  Use one or the other.
>
> ...but not to this!
>
> The best reason I have to use sections instead of top-down
paragraphs is
> just this that I can program "functions" in this way and performing
these
> just with a simple statement (i.e. PERFORM CONVERT_DATE). I adopted
this
> from my experience with programming in TurboPascal in early 80's.
Since '88
> I've used sections in CObOL all the time. In *my* opinion the code
is much
> cleaner, but it demands that you use obvious section-names and not
to
> mention variable-names. Another benefit is that it's easy to make
"complex"
> copy-files and integrate these very easy in many programs.
>
> I know this has been debated over and over here, but I couldn't
resist...
> :-)

You don't have to PERFORM CONVERT-DATE, you can CALL 'CONVERT-DATE'
and get the target code completely out of the program, but that's
another story.

In this case, we have a homework assignment, not a stock-ticker
quotation system.

Lose the SECTION(s).

In this context, SECTIONS are superfluous, confusing, and a source of
error. In the unlikely event SECTIONs are ever encountered in the
wild, one can take steps at that time.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Vs: First cobol-program

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T09:15:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACC8C21.2355839B@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net> <9ahul0$oq4$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`

```


Erlend Moen wrote:

> Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> skrev i news:3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net
> > Did this compile cleanly?   I haven't used many PC compilers but it seemed
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> I know this has been debated over and over here, but I couldn't resist...

I don't think I was clear.  While I would love to see procedure division
sections go away along with GO TO exit statements, I was referring to the
following code:

   PERFORM READ-MY-DATA-FILE UNTIL END-OF-FRIENDS-FILE = 1
     DISPLAY OWNSTRING
   END-PERFORM

He should either have it an in-line perform or an external perform, not both.


BTW:
When EXIT-PARAGRAPH is available, I suspect you won't mind working in a shop
which uses these instead of sections.
```

```
On Thu, 05 Apr 2001 09:04:50 GMT, "Jarkko Kï¿½hkï¿½nen"
<jarkko.kahkonen@softageneraattori.fi> enlightened us:

Others have pointed out the short comings of this program.  Did you
actually compile this before you posted here?  If so do you not
understand the huge amount of errors you must be getting?  What is it
you would like us to do?  You need to make your request clearer.

Some other stuff is interspersed below.


>Thanks to everybody for help! I corrected my mistakes. Here is new version.
>(CUSTOMER-FILE was typo (what?!=)).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The following 3 lines are misplaced.  

> WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
>   01 OWNSTRING   PIC X(82)
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The following line is not needed and serves no useful purpose.  (And I
agree with Howard on the use of Section).

> PROGRAMBODY SECTION.
>
>  DISPLAY '!Reading friends.dat line by line!'
>
>  OPEN INPUT FRIENDS-FILE.

If the following statement actually executed, the DISPLAY is only
going to show (probably) garbage one time.  You might get the last
record in the file, but chances are you are going to get garbage.

You have coded this like it is an in-line perform yet you wrote a
paragraph by the same name.  

>   PERFORM READ-MY-DATA-FILE UNTIL END-OF-FRIENDS-FILE = 1
>     DISPLAY OWNSTRING
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ciao,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Borrow money from a pessimist - they don't expect it back.


DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge
Read the facts and sign a petition at:

http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

<a id="t-e24c69c56e"></a>
## Thread: Announce: GUI ScreenIO now does Images, Splash screens, Timeouts, Registry ops, and more... — 1 msgs

### Announce: GUI ScreenIO now does Images, Splash screens, Timeouts, Registry ops, and more...

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T19:54:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3acccefa.2805554@news>`

```
For those of you who are interested in developing true Windows
applications in COBOL, we have just posted a new version of GUI
ScreenIO that adds quite a few features.

- Simple display of bitmaps
- Simple "splash screens"
- Easy-to-use timeouts for, say, multiuser applications
- Simple function to read/write to the Windows Registry
- Sounds (whoopee)
- Arrays (aka "repeat groups")
- lots more

For information and/or to download an evaluation copy:

http://www.screenio.com

Thank you.

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

<a id="t-d94825509f"></a>
## Thread: *** "Duplication and distribution of current and future COBOL Standards in Jeopary — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### *** "Duplication and distribution of current and future COBOL Standards in Jeopary

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T15:18:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9aijv5$d0j$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI,
 The draft of the revision of the COBOL Standard says,

"    Copyright notice
This ISO document is a draft International Standard and is
copyright-protected by ISO. Except as permitted under the applicable laws of
the user's country, neither this ISO draft nor any extract from it may be
reproduced, stored in a retrieval system or transmitted in any form or by
any means, electronic, photocopying, recording or otherwise, without prior
written permission being secured.

Requests for permission to reproduce should be addressed to ISO at the
address below or ISO's member body in the country of the requester:
 Copyright Manager
 ISO Central Secretariat
 1 rue de Varemb�
 1211 Geneva 20 Switzerland
    tel: +41 22 749 0111
    fax +41 22 734 0179
        e-mail central@iso.ch

Reproduction may be subject to royalty payments or a licensing agreement.
Violators may be prosecuted."

I have contacted both ANSI and ISO with my concerns on this issue.  Today, I
received the following reply

"Mr. Klein - This is to advise you that the NCITS Secretariat staff will not
be responding further to your questions or requests for information
regarding copyrights. Discussions and disclosures of this type of
information would be inappropriate except in routine business negotiations
with the holders of those copyrights.
  ...

Jim Ryland
Manager, NCITS Standards Operations
p - 202/626-5741
f - 202/638-4922
email:  jryland@itic.org"

If you are interested in the copyright restrictions versus the "traditional
Acknowledgement statement" (in both the current Standard "up for
reaffirmation" and the revision of the Standard) that says (in part),

" Acknowledgment
Any organization interested in reproducing the COBOL standard and
specifications in whole or in part, using ideas from this document as the
basis for an instruction manual or for any other purpose, is free to do so."

Then I suggest that you go to:
  http://www.ncits.org/prsweb.htm

Look for BOTH the section on:
 Programming Language COBOL (Revision of 1985 version)
    and
 Reaffirmation of a Standard. X3.23:1985 [R1991]

Then select the "link" to
  Call for Patents (per ANSI requirements)

and following the instructions there for registering any "pertinent
copyright concerns" that you might have.

NOTE:
  If anyone is interested in the documents that I sent in as my response to
that CALL, just send me a PRIVATE email note - and I will forward them to
you (as Word documents).
```

#### ↳ Re: *** "Duplication and distribution of current and future COBOL Standards in Jeopary

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T10:36:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9akuq7$fvh$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<9aijv5$d0j$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On 5 April 2001 at1:18PM, William M. Klein submitted :

<<I have contacted both ANSI and ISO with my concerns on this issue.  Today,
I received the following reply

"Mr. Klein - This is to advise you that the NCITS Secretariat staff will not
be responding further to your questions or requests for information
regarding copyrights. Discussions and disclosures of this type of
information would be inappropriate except in routine business negotiations
with the holders of those copyrights.
  ...
Jim Ryland
Manager, NCITS Standards Operations
p - 202/626-5741
f - 202/638-4922
email:  jryland@itic.org"
>>

An ellipsis in the original response from Mr. Ryland strikes me as highly
unlikely, and yet you indicate "Today I received the following reply".

Could you verify for us that the ellipsis is actually represented as such in
the orignal reply from Mr. Ryland?

If this ellipsis actually represents an editorial emendation on *your* part,
could you be so kind as to post the material you elided from that reply?   I
for one would be most interested in reading it.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: *** "Duplication and distribution of current and future COBOL Standards in Jeopary

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T14:48:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9al6hv$v32$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9aijv5$d0j$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9akuq7$fvh$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
You are correct that the ellipsis DO reflect an "editorial deletion" on my
part.  I felt that such a use was well enough understood NOT to require that
I begin the quotation with "in part".

The deleted paragraph had NOTHING to do with the copyright issue and as such
I do not plan on posting it to this newsgroup or even sending it on
privately.

If others have an interest in the COPYRIGHT/duplication-and-distribution
issue, I am certain that following the instructions that I have pointed to
will get you whatever response ANSI or NCITS feels most appropriate to your
comments.

Should others be interested in Mr. Ryland's "comments" that do NOT pertain
to the copyright issue, I suspect that you can contact him individually at
the email address (that I have included - so my views would not be taken as
representing someone else's) and he will either let you know or won't - that
is up to him.
```

#### ↳ Re: *** "Duplication and distribution of current and future COBOL Standards in Jeopary

- **From:** "Steven O. Ellis" <soellis@soltec.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-24T20:30:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AE628B3.37A66C20@soltec.net>`
- **References:** `<9aijv5$d0j$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I took ISO up on it and wrote on behalf of my employer to see how one
could
be granted permission to distribute the draft.  I was referred to
Rosemary
Maginniss at ANSI and she received a copy of the the ISO response.  The
ISO response and the ANSI response were as follows.  I have removed all
references to my employer.  I get the impression both bodies would
rather 
ignore the issue.

"Jacques-Olivier Chabot" <chabot@iso.ch> on 04/20/2001 09:10:37 AM

To:   Steven Ellis
cc:   Rosemary Maginniss <RMAGINNI@ANSI.org>
Subject:  Re: [Fwd: ISO/IEC FCD 1989:2001 Copyright]




Mr. Ellis,

I would suggest that you consult Ms. Rosemary Maginniss (see her email
address above) at our member body for USA, ANSI.

Regards

Jacques-Olivier Chabot



Rosemary Maginniss <RMAGINNI@ANSI.org> on 04/20/2001 10:14:09 AM

To:   Steven Ellis
cc:
Subject:  committee draft of COBOL




Dear Sir
ISO forwarded your request to ANSI for response as ANSI is the US member
body and is responsible for exploitation rights to ISO material in the
United States.

It is not our policy to permit dissemination of committee draft
documents
for the reference purpose you described.  Committee drafts are for the
purpose of review and comment by the committee members, and since they
are
subject to quite a few changes and they proceed through the standards
development process, we do not feel they should be used for reference
purposes.

We appreciate your interest and will be very willing to discuss a
networking arrangement for your staff when the final standard is
approved.

Best regards
Rose Maginniss

Rose Maginniss
Director, Electronic Sales & Marketing
Tel:  (212) 642-4999
Fax: (212) 398-0023
e-mail: rmaginni@ansi.org <mailto:rmaginni@ansi.org>
www.ansi.org



"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> FYI,
…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
```

## Thread: strange occurence — 45 msgs

### strange occurence

- **From:** anonymous <mac3@slic.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T15:59:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com>`

```
fujitsu 4
win 98
STUDENT QUESTION???

Hello-

I was closing in on finishing lab project 04 when I noticed
something disturbing.  I have provided partial code from my project
because I'm close to being finished and I don't want to 
give out free lab code over the net.  If what I provided isn't 
enough, I guess I will submit the rest of the code and not 
worry about it.  I can get this program to work one way but
I don't understand why it doesn't work the other way.  This
makes me frustrated.

BTW the error messages I get are program performed an illegal
operation and will be shut down and signal interrupt access violation.
I get these because it is trying to read a data name from a file
that is closed.  The error seems to happen after the program is 
finished reading all the values in the name-file.

Thanks for your help
jim

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
  GENERIC-PARAGRAPH.
  
  	OPEN INPUT  NAME-FILE
               OUTPUT  PRINT-FILE.
	PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE
			
      PERFORM 400-EXIT-PROG04. 					
		
      
	150-READ-NAME-FILE.
      PERFORM UNTIL NAME-SWITCH = 'NO'
      READ NAME-FILE
	AT END
		MOVE 'NO' TO NAME-SWITCH
		PERFORM 400-EXIT-PROG04
	NOT AT END
		PERFORM 200-PROCESS-BOOK-LIST
	END-READ
	END-PERFORM.

The above code works OK.  Notice I explicitly tell it
to quit the program AT END.

Now the problem occurs if I change things around a little bit.
Here is basically the same code with slight modification.
The only difference is close and stop run are contained in a
separate paragraph above and the PERFORM 400-EXIT-PROG04
at the end of read name-file.

In the code below...
In the debugger after it moves 'No' to name switch below,
it starts executing another paragraph instead of terminating
the program. 

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
  GENERIC-PARAGRAPH.
  
  	OPEN INPUT  NAME-FILE
               OUTPUT  PRINT-FILE.
	PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE
	CLOSE	NAME-FILE
		PRINT-FILE.
	STOP RUN.
	
     150-READ-NAME-FILE.
      PERFORM UNTIL NAME-SWITCH = 'NO'
      READ NAME-FILE
	AT END
		MOVE 'NO' TO NAME-SWITCH
										NOT AT END
		PERFORM 200-PROCESS-BOOK-LIST
	END-READ
	END-PERFORM.
```

#### ↳ Re: strange occurence

- **From:** "Tim Cordsen" <tim_cordsen@gmx.de>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T22:42:33+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9aildt$gev$03$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com>`

```

anonymous <mac3@slic.com> schrieb in im Newsbeitrag:
3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com...
> fujitsu 4
> win 98
…[75 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-PERFORM.

Hi, I think you left out the EXIT at the end of the 150-READ-NAME-FILE
section and I am not sure if you need the word SECTION like this

>  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>   GENERIC-PARAGRAPH.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: strange occurence

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T14:55:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACCDBC8.E556B0DE@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <9aildt$gev$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Tim Cordsen wrote:

Many times the last paragraph in a section or in a perform thru is a dummy
paragraph which is just a place to GO TO.   This paragraph may contain EXIT
or CONTINUE.  In shops which allow sections this is often a standard.   But
unless you GO TO it, it doesn't do anything.

I am still curious what was after the last line in his code segment.   If it
is a new SECTION, then the code is OK.  If it is a new paragraph, that
paragraph gets executed.  If it is a new statement, that new statement gets
executed.

This is part of the reason I dislike sections.

> Hi, I think you left out the EXIT at the end of the 150-READ-NAME-FILE
> section and I am not sure if you need the word SECTION like this
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> EXIT.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: strange occurence

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T20:04:28+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACE133C.18079E79@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <9aildt$gev$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Tim Cordsen wrote:
 
> Hi, I think you left out the EXIT at the end of the 150-READ-NAME-FILE
> section and I am not sure if you need the word SECTION like this

No program ever needs to use the EXIT statement (that is EXIT alone and
not in conjunction with PROGRAM etc).  Programs will work identically if
all EXIT statements are removed, they do nothing.  Note especially that
an EXIT statement will not do an exit.

(However some compilers may complain about empty paragraphs).

> >  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
> >   GENERIC-PARAGRAPH.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >      150-READ-NAME-FILE SECTION.

This may cause: 

      Compiler to give error message as PD did not start with a SECTION.
      Loss of control, depending on what the next label is.

> >       PERFORM UNTIL NAME-SWITCH = 'NO'
> >       READ NAME-FILE
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> EXIT.

This EXIT statement may cause a compiler error message as EXIT is valid
only when in a paragraph by itself.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: strange occurence

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)

```
Richard Plinston  <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>Tim Cordsen wrote:
```
