```
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>an EXIT statement will not do an exit.

Ummmmmm... Mr Plinston, my memory is, admittedly, porous but I recall that
this very situation (a PERFROM THRU -EXIT where -EXIT was actually empty
and did not contain an EXIT statement) caused a bit of a headached on a
WANG VS5000 a decade-and-change back; when I suggested inserting the
missing EXIT I was met with scornful replies of 'What, all it does is
generate a NOP, that *can't* be the problem!'... and several hours later,
when all else had failed, it worked.

>
>(However some compilers may complain about empty paragraphs).

E'em worse... the compiler let it pass with nary a peep; it was found only
in ste-level debug processing.

DD
k
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: strange occurence

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T08:26:38+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACEC12E.290D8E24@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <9aildt$gev$03$1@news.t-online.com> <3ACE133C.18079E79@Azonic.co.nz> <Gvgz6.2575$cI1.60767@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
NA wrote:

> >No program ever needs to use the EXIT statement (that is EXIT alone and
> >not in conjunction with PROGRAM etc).  Programs will work identically if
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> when all else had failed, it worked.

That is not the fault of the language, but is probably an implementation
fault in the compiler.  If the system is not using stacks then it may be
doing a PERFORM by modifying the code to change the EXIT to a GO TO
[statement after PERFORM] and then doing a GO TO to the PERFORMED-label.

If the compiler relies on there being an EXIT to be a place holder for
the GO TO return then this is a constraint that should cause the
compiler to issue a warning - even if it is empty paragraph.

I have actually seen someone code:

          IF ( some condition )
              EXIT.
          more code.

(there was no compiler error for this either.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ EXIT paragraphs (was: strange occurence

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T16:23:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9alc6i$4bh$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <9aildt$gev$03$1@news.t-online.com> <3ACE133C.18079E79@Azonic.co.nz> <Gvgz6.2575$cI1.60767@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3ACEC12E.290D8E24@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
The following is the first syntax rule in the draft of the next COBOL
Standard,

"The EXIT statement shall appear in a sentence by itself that shall be the
only sentence in the paragraph."

There may be vendors who have an extension "removing" this restriction, but
I don't recall seeing them.  I would be interested in anyone who knows of an
LRM (Language Reference Manual) that actually DOES support a "variation" of
this rule.

FYI - even in the '85 Standard it was "legal" to have a paragraph header
with NO sentences in that paragraph.  Therefore, any compiler that doesn't
(didn't) support that was non-conforming.

Page IV-35 of the '85 Standard says,
 "A paragraph consists of a paragraph-name followed by a period and a space
and by zero, one, or more successive sentences."

and the "general format" for the Procedure Division "clearly" puts
"sentence" in square brackets - indicating that they are OPTIONAL within a
paragraph.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT paragraphs (was: strange occurence

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T15:32:38-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACE35F6.A65A6D40@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <9aildt$gev$03$1@news.t-online.com> <3ACE133C.18079E79@Azonic.co.nz> <Gvgz6.2575$cI1.60767@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3ACEC12E.290D8E24@Azonic.co.nz> <9alc6i$4bh$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:

> FYI - even in the '85 Standard it was "legal" to have a paragraph header
> with NO sentences in that paragraph.  Therefore, any compiler that doesn't
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> paragraph.

But my compiler gives a warning - which I think is good.  So when I get vendor
written programs with empty paragraphs, I used to add EXIT but now add CONTINUE
to get around the message.   EXIT is a bit more clear, but CONTINUE allows me to
have a "D"  debug statement with it without syntaxing out when DEBUG is on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT paragraphs (was: strange occurence

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T10:40:52+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACEE0A4.FDBA921D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <9aildt$gev$03$1@news.t-online.com> <3ACE133C.18079E79@Azonic.co.nz> <Gvgz6.2575$cI1.60767@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3ACEC12E.290D8E24@Azonic.co.nz> <9alc6i$4bh$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> The following is the first syntax rule in the draft of the next COBOL
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> only sentence in the paragraph."

No change there then.  This is as for Cobol-85, and probably 74, 68 and
61.
 
> There may be vendors who have an extension "removing" this restriction, but
> I don't recall seeing them.  I would be interested in anyone who knows of an
> LRM (Language Reference Manual) that actually DOES support a "variation" of
> this rule.

MF does not impose the restriction, and says that this is also a VSC2
extension.

But the EXIT still does nothing.

> FYI - even in the '85 Standard it was "legal" to have a paragraph header
> with NO sentences in that paragraph.  Therefore, any compiler that doesn't
> (didn't) support that was non-conforming.

I didn't say that it was illegal, I just said that some compilers may
complain about an empty paragraph (as a warning).  I have seen this
behaviour in an old Cobol, it also warned about 'unreachable code' after
an unconditional GO TO in a paragraph.  This was due to a spurious full
stop in an IF statement (not my code) so it was useful.

So then, you do agree with me that EXIT is entirely unrequired and
programs should work identically if they are all removed (except for
'EXIT something' of course).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: strange occurence

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Anonymous <anonymous@anonymous.anonymous>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3acf7b02_3@anonymous>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <3ACEC12E.290D8E24@Azonic.co.nz> <_Rtz6.2671$cI1.62762@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3AD0098C.7FF8BFB2@Azonic.co.nz> <wuKz6.55$DW1.5160@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Doc, Richard, Bill,

I followed this with interest. (I am fully aware of how EXIT <which does
nothing, right?> works, and the code which it doesn't generate on at least 3
platforms <G>)

Could somebody enlighten me as to how I can remove all the EXITS from my
programs and still have them function?

You see, I code SECTIONs and I use GO TO the EXIT paragraph when I want to
escape my section.

Now, I know you folk disapprove and we've discussed it here before, and I
don't want to open it up again, but if you are seriously advising
programmers to remove EXIT, shouldn't the implications be examined a little
more carefully?

On at least one platform I have worked with, EXIT generates a NOP which is
later modified to implement a "return" or "drop thru" (NOT every platform
is/was stack based... and NOT every platform has an equivalent of BALR or
even a register 14 to return from...)

Theory is fine, but many people here want to actually DO something.
Unfortunately that sometimes means that what is ideal in theory is not
necessarily so in practice...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: strange occurence

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T16:49:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ao224$opa$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <3ACEC12E.290D8E24@Azonic.co.nz> <_Rtz6.2671$cI1.62762@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3AD0098C.7FF8BFB2@Azonic.co.nz> <wuKz6.55$DW1.5160@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3acf7b02_3@anonymous>`

```
The answer to your question on HOW to get rid of "GO TO xxxx-EXIT" depends
on which compiler and what release you are using.

If you are using a compiler that supports the (new in the next COBOL
Standard) syntax of
   EXIT PARAGRAPH
      or
   EXIT SECTION

(I know that MERANT already does - but I don't know who else - if anyone
does),

Then replace your
   GO TO xxxx-EXIT
         with
   EXIT PARAGRAPH (or EXIT SECTION - depending on your structure)

Remove the xxxx-EXIT paragraph
Remove the THRU phrase of your PERFORM statement

and voila .... you will have "semantically" identical code.

If your vendor does NOT already support EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION, then contact
your vendor thru "normal enhancement request" channels and ask them WHEN
they will support it (and HOPE that it isn't going to wait until they have a
"full" 200x compiler)
```

```
I like to think of EXIT (which I rarely use) as a compiler directive: it is

like SECTION, except I NEVER use SECTION on purpose).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: strange occurence

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T07:44:12+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD15A3C.7BB37AE4@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <3ACEC12E.290D8E24@Azonic.co.nz> <_Rtz6.2671$cI1.62762@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3AD0098C.7FF8BFB2@Azonic.co.nz> <wuKz6.55$DW1.5160@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3acf7b02_3@anonymous> <aCZz6.68$fP5.36086@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> I like to think of EXIT (which I rarely use) as a compiler directive: it is
> used when the code is compiled, but serves no purpose at run-time. (Kinda
> like SECTION, except I NEVER use SECTION on purpose).

It would be nice if EXIT was used as a compiler directive, but I suspect
that most compilers just ignore it completely.  The end of a PERFORM is
entirely the end of the paragraph, THRU paragraph or SECTION regardless
of where or if the EXIT statement is.

If the compiler took note of EXIT and warned if it wasn't the end of a
logical block (eg was followed by a SECTION label) then it may at least
serve some purpose.  However, this check does not seem possible due to
the ways that Cobol can be written.

At best it should be regarded as a comment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ MORE on EXIT paragraphs/sections (was : strange occurence

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T16:40:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ao1hv$skv$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <3ACEC12E.290D8E24@Azonic.co.nz> <_Rtz6.2671$cI1.62762@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3AD0098C.7FF8BFB2@Azonic.co.nz> <wuKz6.55$DW1.5160@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Well, actually ....
 There is a SLIGHT ambiguity in the '85 Standard on what happens if you do a
PERFORM procedure-name-1 THRU procedure-name-2
statement and procedure-name-2 is an "empty" paragraph or section.  The '85
Standard has the following rules:

 " c. If procedure-name-2 is specified and it is a paragraph-name , the
return is after the last statement of the paragraph.
  d. If procedure-name-2 is specified and it is a section-name, the return
is after the last statement of the last paragraph in the section."

There are *no* rules on what happens if procedure-name-2 has no statements
in it.  THEREFORE, by the usual rules of the Standard, such behavior is
"implicitly undefined".

By the way, there is also a rule that says,
 "If there are two or more logical paths to the return point, then
procedure-name-2 may be the name of a paragraph consisting of the EXIT
statement, to which all of these paths must lead."

So that is (at least one) case where NOT having the "traditional" EXIT
paragraph/section is nonconforming source code.
```

#### ↳ Re: strange occurence

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T14:44:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACCD925.D3C8BF01@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com>`

```
anonymous wrote:

I'm not seeing your problem right now, probably because I don't see the rest of your code, but
I am confused a bit by your code.  You have

PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE

which you execute once, then fall through.   You never return to the close and stop run
commands, could your debugger be showing you executing the next paragraph after your
END-PERFORM?   That's what the code is telling it to do.

I would get rid of that in-line perform and stick the UNTIL clause on

PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE UNTIL NAME-SWITCH = 'NO'


> fujitsu 4
> win 98
…[75 more quoted lines elided]…
>         END-PERFORM.
```

#### ↳ Re: strange occurence

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-05T17:48:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9aisog$not$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com>`

```
Is NAME-SWITCH defined in the File Section or the Working-Storage section?
Although some compilers/run-times allow it, it is (generally) NOT a good
idea to put your "end-of-file" switch in the File section (i.e. use a part
of your file definition) but is MUCH "safer" to put it in Working-Storage
where you can "access it" both before and after the file is opened and
closed.

Also, (just a "style" question),

Both of you examples have your mainline code including the line:
  PERFORM 400-EXIT-PROG04

HOWEVER, you entire   400-EXIT-PROG04 paragraph is an inline PERFORM
statement starting with:
 PERFORM UNTIL NAME-SWITCH = 'NO'
and ending with
 END-PERFORM

Is there any reason that you don't change your "mainline" PERFORM statement
to
  PERFORM 400-EXIT-PROG04
    UNTIL NAME-SWITCH = 'NO'
and get rid of the "inline perform" within that paragraph?  I don't think
that will "solve" this problem, but it is an "odd" (to me) construction that
you have now.
```

#### ↳ Re: strange occurence

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T01:24:57+00:00

- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com>`

```
Hi Mous,

You left out the NOT AT END clause; this ececutes 200-... at the worst possible
time: after EOF, when there's no pointer to your i/p rec fields.

Hope this helps, Jack.



In article <3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com>, anonymous says...
>
>fujitsu 4
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
>	END-PERFORM.
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T08:46:40+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACD7460.C6F335F9@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
>>> anonymous wrote:

Just in case your lecturer is watching ?

> fujitsu 4
> win 98
 
>  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>   GENERIC-PARAGRAPH.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> to quit the program AT END.

You haven't shown us '400-Exit-Prog04'.  From your error I would guess
that it is _NOT_ doing a STOP RUN and thus the PERFORM completes, it
executes this again from 'Generic-Paragraph' and fails to stop and drops
through back into '150-Read-File-Name' giving the error.

> 
> Now the problem occurs if I change things around a little bit.
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>         END-PERFORM.

The 'NOT AT END' is in column 100 or so and this may appear OK when
wrapped by your editor but may be ignored by the compiler.
```

```
net?' You are, after all, asking for 'free advice over the net.'

programming -as in most other endeavors - is good.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: strange occurence

- **From:** anonymous <mac3@slic.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T22:24:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACEA472.2DC6D90E@slic.com>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <5Vjz6.8700$Kr1.739747@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```


Jerry P wrote:
> 
> "anonymous" <mac3@slic.com> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> net?' You are, after all, asking for 'free advice over the net.'

But you helped me with one facet of the project.  Not every aspect
of it.  I don't expect someone to help me with every division and
every paragraph.  The problem ended up being something unrelated
to the advice offered.  I liked reading all the posts though because
they got me thinking.  

> 
> Programming is not a zero-sum activity and, unless you are graded 'on
> the curve,' there is no reason not to share. Cooperation in
> programming -as in most other endeavors - is good.

Interesting.  In lab we are assigned two or three randomly 
selected partners.  Our team receives the same grade and hands
in one project.  If my partner(s) don't do anything, I have to 
work extra hard to ensure everyone gets a good score.  It is
one version of collaborative learning.

I don't know if we are graded on a curve.  It seems to me if I 
pass out my code and someone struggling becomes enlightened suddenly,
I just helped them and they improved relative to my position in
the lab portion of the class.  Since I struggled on the hourly
written exam sometimes I feel I need the equalizer (lab). If I 
help someone in lab and they beat me on the test, I sort of lose.

I have helped people OUTSIDE my team on every project so far.  I'm 
talking a range of 9-12 hours of help per project.  Now I can only
help 1 or 2 groups besides my team.  Fifty minutes here or there doesnt
cut it.  Trying to help too many people in short sessions becomes
counter productive (unless your a professional teacher).  Also
sometimes if I help one person they turn around and help another.

Now what constitutes help??
It is a gray area.  I have often heard people in this group say do
your own work or write your own code.  

Since people here are helping me I will gladly extend my
usual help for the duration of the class. Other members of 
the class thank you for helping me.  They are seniors and 
wish to graduate.

Thanks
jim
```

```
John H Sleight Jr wrote:
> 
> anonymous wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Ok.  I will post the code.
Now you will see what foolishness I get myself into.

If you check out line 000206 you will see a problem.
I'm in a loop which hasn't finished and I jump to line
000155.  The problem is the first loop hasn't finished 
and I start another loop.  At some point the program 
tries to go back and jump from 000206 to 000214 and finish the 
loop that got interrupted.  When it gets to 000182 it tries
to read Number-Ordered but the Order file is closed.
The counter value reaches 15  (number of records in 
name-file) before it generates an error.

You will see the solution in the *working version *
lines 000206 - 000209.
The counter value reaches 13 (number of records
in name-file minus 2 records with no orders).
No errors are generated.

To summarize the error is in the 210-Verify-Order paragraph.

BTW one of the people I help outside my team just 
emailed me and said they didn't know what a subscript is.
I think that would be useful for this project.



Thanks.
Later
jim
```

```
000022  DATA DIVISION.
000023  FILE SECTION.
000024  FD INVENTORY-FILE
000025     RECORD CONTAINS 25 CHARACTERS
000026     DATA RECORD IS INVENTORY-RECORD.
000027
000028  01 INVENTORY-RECORD.
000029     05 ISBN                                              PIC
X(10).
000030     05 COST                                              PIC
99V99.
000031     05 MARKUP                                            PIC
X(2).
000032     05 R-CODE                                            PIC
X(2).
000033     05 C-INV                                             PIC
9(3).
000034     05 RPOINT                                            PIC 99.
000035     05 RCODE                                             PIC
X(2).
000036  
000037  FD ORDER-FILE
000038     RECORD CONTAINS 9 TO 69 CHARACTERS
000039     DATA RECORD IS ORDER-RECORD.
000040
000041  01 ORDER-RECORD.
000042     05 ID-NUMBER                                 PIC X(5).
000043     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1).
000044     05 NUMBER-ORDERED                            PIC 9(2).
000045     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1).
000046     05 ISBN-INFO OCCURS 0 TO 6 TIMES
000047          DEPENDING ON NUMBER-ORDERED.
000048          10 ISBN1                                PIC X(10).
000049
000050     
000051
000052  FD CODE-FILE
000053     RECORD CONTAINS 6 CHARACTERS
000054     DATA RECORD IS CODE-RECORD.
000055  
000056  01 CODE-RECORD.
000057     05 THE-CODE                                  PIC X(2).
000058     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1).
000059     05 CODE-VALUE                                PIC 999.
000060
000061  FD TITLE-FILE
000062     RECORD CONTAINS 90 CHARACTERS
000063     DATA RECORD IS TITLE-RECORD.
000064
000065  01 TITLE-RECORD.
000066     05 T-ISBN                                    PIC X(10).
000067     05 T-TITLE                                   PIC X(80).
000068
000069  FD NAME-FILE
000070     RECORD CONTAINS 30 CHARACTERS
000071     DATA RECORD IS NAME-RECORD.
000072
000073  01 NAME-RECORD.
000074     05 STUDENT-ID                                PIC X(5).
000075     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1).
000076     05 STUDENT-NAME                              PIC X(24).
000077
000078  FD PRINT-FILE
000079     RECORD CONTAINS 132 CHARACTERS .
000080  01 PRINT-LINE                                   PIC X(132).     
000081
000082  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000083  01 SWITCHES.
000084     05 ORDER-SWITCH                              PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000085     05 STUDENT-SWITCH                            PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.  
000086     05 NAME-SWITCH                               PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000087     05 TITLE-SWITCH                              PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000088     05 INVENTORY-SWITCH                          PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000089     05 CODE-SWITCH                               PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.  
000090     05 COUNTER                                   PIC 9(2) VALUE
ZEROS.   
000091   
000092
000093  01 SUBSCRIPTS.
000094     05 BOOK-SUB                                  PIC 99.
000095
000096  
000097  01 DETAIL1.
000098     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(10) VALUE
'STUDENT ID'.
000099     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000100     05 DET-ID                                    PIC X(5).
000101     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000102     05 DET-NAME                                  PIC X(23) .
000103     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(92) VALUE
SPACES.  
000104     
000105
000106  01 DETAIL2.
000107     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(5) VALUE
'TITLE'.
000108     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000109     05 DET-TITLE                                 PIC X(80) VALUE
SPACES.
000110     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000111     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(4) VALUE
'COST'.
000112     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000113     05 DET-COST                                  PIC 99.99 VALUE
ZEROS.
000114     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000115     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(7) VALUE
'MARK-UP'.
000116     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000117     05 DET-MARKUP                                PIC 9(2) VALUE
ZEROS.
000118     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
'%'.
000119     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000120     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(6) VALUE
'RETAIL'.
000121     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000122     05 DET-RETAIL                                PIC $Z99.99
VALUE ZEROS.
000123     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(9) VALUE
SPACES.
000124
000125  01 DETAILX.
000126     05 DET-NO-ORDER-MSG                          PIC X(27) VALUE
SPACES. 
000127     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(105) VALUE
SPACES.        
000128                           
000129
000130
000131  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000132  GENERIC-PARAGRAPH.
000133  
000134          OPEN INPUT  NAME-FILE
000135               OUTPUT  PRINT-FILE.
000136
000137      PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE .
000147          CLOSE NAME-FILE
000148                PRINT-FILE
000149          STOP RUN.    
000151                  
000155  150-READ-NAME-FILE.                                     
000156          PERFORM UNTIL NAME-SWITCH = 'NO'
000157      
000158                  READ NAME-FILE
000159                          AT END
000160                                  MOVE 'NO' TO NAME-SWITCH
000161                          NOT AT END
000163                                  PERFORM 200-PROCESS-BOOK-LIST
000164                  END-READ
000165          END-PERFORM     .
000166
000167      
000168  
000174  200-PROCESS-BOOK-LIST.
000175          
000176      COMPUTE COUNTER = COUNTER + 1
000177          DISPLAY COUNTER 
000178          PERFORM 210-VERIFY-ORDER
000179*         DISPLAY NUMBER-ORDERED
000180          PERFORM 215-GET-NAME-AND-ID
000181       
000182                          PERFORM 220-GET-EACH-BOOK-INFO
000183                VARYING BOOK-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
000184                          UNTIL BOOK-SUB > NUMBER-ORDERED
000185          CLOSE ORDER-FILE
000186          PERFORM 300-GET-TOTAL-COST.
000187
000188  210-VERIFY-ORDER.
000189     MOVE SPACES TO ORDER-SWITCH
000190     OPEN INPUT ORDER-FILE        
000191     PERFORM UNTIL ORDER-SWITCH = 'NO' OR
000192     STUDENT-ID = ID-NUMBER
000193     
000194                  READ ORDER-FILE
000195                          AT END
000196                          MOVE 'NO' TO ORDER-SWITCH
000197
000198                  END-READ
000199          END-PERFORM
000200
000201           IF STUDENT-ID NOT EQUAL ID-NUMBER OR
000202       NUMBER-ORDERED = 0  THEN
000203                  PERFORM 215-GET-NAME-AND-ID
000204                  PERFORM 216-NO-ORDERS
000205          CLOSE ORDER-FILE
000206                  PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE
000207                  
000208                                          
000210                                                            
000212          END-IF.
000213      
000214  215-GET-NAME-AND-ID.
000215      MOVE STUDENT-ID TO DET-ID
000216          MOVE STUDENT-NAME TO DET-NAME
000217      WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL1 .
000218
000219  216-NO-ORDERS.
000220      MOVE  'NO BOOKS IN ORDERS.DAT FILE' TO
000221          DET-NO-ORDER-MSG
000222          WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAILX
000223          INITIALIZE PRINT-LINE
000224          WRITE PRINT-LINE.
000225                   
000226
000227  220-GET-EACH-BOOK-INFO.
000228          PERFORM 230-GET-BOOK-TITLE
000229          PERFORM 240-GET-COST
000230          PERFORM 250-GET-MARKUP
000231          PERFORM 260-CALC-RETAIL-VALUE
000232          PERFORM 270-INCREMENT-RETAIL
000233          PERFORM 280-WRITE-DETAIL.
000234
000235                  
000236  230-GET-BOOK-TITLE.
000237          MOVE SPACES TO TITLE-SWITCH
000238      OPEN INPUT TITLE-FILE
000239       
000240          PERFORM UNTIL ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = T-ISBN  OR
000241      TITLE-SWITCH = 'NO'
000242
000243          READ TITLE-FILE
000244                  AT END MOVE 'NO' TO TITLE-SWITCH
000245          END-READ        
000246          END-PERFORM
000247
000248      IF ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = T-ISBN THEN
000249      MOVE T-TITLE TO DET-TITLE
000250          END-IF
000251
000252          CLOSE TITLE-FILE.
000253          
000254           
000255
000256  240-GET-COST.
000257          MOVE SPACES TO INVENTORY-SWITCH
000258      OPEN INPUT INVENTORY-FILE
000259          PERFORM UNTIL ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = ISBN
000260          READ INVENTORY-FILE
000261          AT END MOVE 'NO' TO INVENTORY-SWITCH
000262          END-READ
000263          END-PERFORM
000264
000265          IF ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = ISBN THEN
000266          MOVE COST TO DET-COST 
000267          END-IF.
000268          
000269          
000270
000271
000272  250-GET-MARKUP.
000273      MOVE SPACES TO CODE-SWITCH
000274          OPEN INPUT CODE-FILE
000275          PERFORM UNTIL MARKUP = THE-CODE
000276          READ CODE-FILE
000277          AT END MOVE 'NO' TO CODE-SWITCH
000278          END-READ
000279          END-PERFORM
000280
000281          IF MARKUP = THE-CODE THEN
000282          MOVE CODE-VALUE TO DET-MARKUP
000283          END-IF
000284
000285          CLOSE CODE-FILE.
000286 
000287  260-CALC-RETAIL-VALUE.
000288      CLOSE INVENTORY-FILE.
000289  270-INCREMENT-RETAIL.
000290
000291  280-WRITE-DETAIL.
000292          
000293      WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL2.
000294
000295  300-GET-TOTAL-COST.
000296          MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE
000297          WRITE PRINT-LINE .
000298
000299  400-EXIT-PROG04.
000300      CLOSE       NAME-FILE
000310                           PRINT-FILE.
000320
000330      STOP RUN.


000065  01 TITLE-RECORD.
000066     05 T-ISBN                                    PIC X(10).
000067     05 T-TITLE                                   PIC X(80).
000068
000069  FD NAME-FILE
000070     RECORD CONTAINS 30 CHARACTERS
000071     DATA RECORD IS NAME-RECORD.
000072
000073  01 NAME-RECORD.
000074     05 STUDENT-ID                                PIC X(5).
000075     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1).
000076     05 STUDENT-NAME                              PIC X(24).
000077
000078  FD PRINT-FILE
000079     RECORD CONTAINS 132 CHARACTERS .
000080  01 PRINT-LINE                                   PIC X(132).     
000081
000082  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000083  01 SWITCHES.
000084     05 ORDER-SWITCH                              PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000085     05 STUDENT-SWITCH                            PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.  
000086     05 NAME-SWITCH                               PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000087     05 TITLE-SWITCH                              PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000088     05 INVENTORY-SWITCH                          PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000089     05 CODE-SWITCH                               PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.  
000090     05 COUNTER                                   PIC 9(2) VALUE
ZEROS.   
000091   
000092
000093  01 SUBSCRIPTS.
000094     05 BOOK-SUB                                  PIC 99.
000095
000096  
000097  01 DETAIL1.

'STUDENT ID'.

SPACES.

000101     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000102     05 DET-NAME                                  PIC X(23) .
000103     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(92) VALUE
SPACES.  
000104     
000105

'TITLE'.

SPACES.

SPACES.
000110     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000111     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(4) VALUE
'COST'.
000112     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000113     05 DET-COST                                  PIC 99.99 VALUE
ZEROS.
000114     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.
000115     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(7) VALUE
'MARK-UP'.

SPACES.

ZEROS.

SPACES.
000122     05 DET-RETAIL                                PIC $Z99.99
VALUE ZEROS.

SPACES.
000124
```

```
000126     05 DET-NO-ORDER-MSG                          PIC X(27) VALUE
SPACES. 
000127     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(105) VALUE
SPACES.        
000128                           
000129
000130
000131  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000132  GENERIC-PARAGRAPH.
000133  
000134          OPEN INPUT  NAME-FILE
000135               OUTPUT  PRINT-FILE.
000136
000137      PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE .
000147          CLOSE NAME-FILE
000148                PRINT-FILE
000149          STOP RUN.    
000151                  
000155  150-READ-NAME-FILE.                                     
000156          PERFORM UNTIL NAME-SWITCH = 'NO'
000157      
000158                  READ NAME-FILE
000159                          AT END
000160                                  MOVE 'NO' TO NAME-SWITCH
000161                          NOT AT END
000163                                  PERFORM 200-PROCESS-BOOK-LIST
000164                  END-READ
000165          END-PERFORM     .
000166
000167      
000168  
000174  200-PROCESS-BOOK-LIST.
000175          
000176      COMPUTE COUNTER = COUNTER + 1
000177          DISPLAY COUNTER 
000178          PERFORM 210-VERIFY-ORDER
000179*         DISPLAY NUMBER-ORDERED
000180          PERFORM 215-GET-NAME-AND-ID
000181       
000182                          PERFORM 220-GET-EACH-BOOK-INFO
000183                VARYING BOOK-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
000184                          UNTIL BOOK-SUB > NUMBER-ORDERED
000185          CLOSE ORDER-FILE
000186          PERFORM 300-GET-TOTAL-COST.
000187
000188  210-VERIFY-ORDER.
000189     MOVE SPACES TO ORDER-SWITCH
000190     OPEN INPUT ORDER-FILE        
000191     PERFORM UNTIL ORDER-SWITCH = 'NO' OR
000192     STUDENT-ID = ID-NUMBER
000193     
000194                  READ ORDER-FILE
000195                          AT END
000196                          MOVE 'NO' TO ORDER-SWITCH
000197
000198                  END-READ
000199          END-PERFORM
000200
000201           IF STUDENT-ID NOT EQUAL ID-NUMBER OR
000202       NUMBER-ORDERED = 0  THEN
000203                  PERFORM 215-GET-NAME-AND-ID
000204                  PERFORM 216-NO-ORDERS
000205          CLOSE ORDER-FILE
000206                  PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE
000207                  
000208                                          
000210                                                            
000212          END-IF.
000213      
000214  215-GET-NAME-AND-ID.
000215      MOVE STUDENT-ID TO DET-ID
000216          MOVE STUDENT-NAME TO DET-NAME
000217      WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL1 .
000218
000219  216-NO-ORDERS.
000220      MOVE  'NO BOOKS IN ORDERS.DAT FILE' TO
000221          DET-NO-ORDER-MSG
000222          WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAILX
000223          INITIALIZE PRINT-LINE
000224          WRITE PRINT-LINE.
000225                   
000226
000227  220-GET-EACH-BOOK-INFO.
000228          PERFORM 230-GET-BOOK-TITLE
000229          PERFORM 240-GET-COST
000230          PERFORM 250-GET-MARKUP
000231          PERFORM 260-CALC-RETAIL-VALUE
000232          PERFORM 270-INCREMENT-RETAIL
000233          PERFORM 280-WRITE-DETAIL.
000234
000235                  
000236  230-GET-BOOK-TITLE.
000237          MOVE SPACES TO TITLE-SWITCH
000238      OPEN INPUT TITLE-FILE
000239       
000240          PERFORM UNTIL ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = T-ISBN  OR
000241      TITLE-SWITCH = 'NO'
000242
000243          READ TITLE-FILE
000244                  AT END MOVE 'NO' TO TITLE-SWITCH
000245          END-READ        
000246          END-PERFORM
000247
000248      IF ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = T-ISBN THEN
000249      MOVE T-TITLE TO DET-TITLE
000250          END-IF
000251
000252          CLOSE TITLE-FILE.
000253          
000254           
000255
000256  240-GET-COST.
000257          MOVE SPACES TO INVENTORY-SWITCH
000258      OPEN INPUT INVENTORY-FILE
000259          PERFORM UNTIL ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = ISBN
000260          READ INVENTORY-FILE
000261          AT END MOVE 'NO' TO INVENTORY-SWITCH
000262          END-READ
000263          END-PERFORM
000264
000265          IF ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = ISBN THEN
000266          MOVE COST TO DET-COST 
000267          END-IF.
000268          
000269          
000270
000271
000272  250-GET-MARKUP.
000273      MOVE SPACES TO CODE-SWITCH
000274          OPEN INPUT CODE-FILE
000275          PERFORM UNTIL MARKUP = THE-CODE
000276          READ CODE-FILE
000277          AT END MOVE 'NO' TO CODE-SWITCH
000278          END-READ
000279          END-PERFORM
000280
000281          IF MARKUP = THE-CODE THEN
000282          MOVE CODE-VALUE TO DET-MARKUP
000283          END-IF
000284
000285          CLOSE CODE-FILE.
000286 
000287  260-CALC-RETAIL-VALUE.
000288      CLOSE INVENTORY-FILE.
000289  270-INCREMENT-RETAIL.
000290
000291  280-WRITE-DETAIL.
000292          
000293      WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL2.
000294
000295  300-GET-TOTAL-COST.
000296          MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE
000297          WRITE PRINT-LINE .
000298
000299  400-EXIT-PROG04.
000300      CLOSE       NAME-FILE
000310                           PRINT-FILE.
000320
000330      STOP RUN.


* Code below is working version with minor flaw *
```

```
Ok.  I will post the code.

To summarize the error is in the 210-Verify-Order paragraph.

I left a flaw in the code so someone will get a minor
deduction if they try to scam it.  Please don't hint what
the flaw is.  I know about it.

Thanks.
Later
jim

000101     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.

000103     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(92) VALUE
SPACES.  
000104     
000105

'TITLE'.
000108     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.

SPACES.
000110     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1) VALUE
SPACES.

'COST'.

SPACES.

ZEROS.

SPACES.

'MARK-UP'.

VALUE ZEROS.

000124
000125  01 DETAILX.
000126     05 DET-NO-ORDER-MSG                          PIC X(27) VALUE
SPACES. 
000127     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(105) VALUE
SPACES.        
000128                           
000129
000130
000131  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000132  GENERIC-PARAGRAPH.
000133  
000134          OPEN INPUT  NAME-FILE
000135               OUTPUT  PRINT-FILE.
000136
000137      PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE .
000147          CLOSE NAME-FILE
000148                PRINT-FILE
000149          STOP RUN.    
000151                  
000155  150-READ-NAME-FILE.                                     
000156          PERFORM UNTIL NAME-SWITCH = 'NO'
000157      
000158                  READ NAME-FILE
000159                          AT END
000160                                  MOVE 'NO' TO NAME-SWITCH
000161                          NOT AT END
000163                                  PERFORM 200-PROCESS-BOOK-LIST
000164                  END-READ
000165          END-PERFORM     .
000166
000167      
000168  
000174  200-PROCESS-BOOK-LIST.
000175          
000176      COMPUTE COUNTER = COUNTER + 1
000177          DISPLAY COUNTER 
000178          PERFORM 210-VERIFY-ORDER
000179*         DISPLAY NUMBER-ORDERED
000180          PERFORM 215-GET-NAME-AND-ID
000181       
000182                          PERFORM 220-GET-EACH-BOOK-INFO
000183                VARYING BOOK-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
000184                          UNTIL BOOK-SUB > NUMBER-ORDERED
000185          CLOSE ORDER-FILE
000186          PERFORM 300-GET-TOTAL-COST.
000187
000188  210-VERIFY-ORDER.
000189     MOVE SPACES TO ORDER-SWITCH
000190     OPEN INPUT ORDER-FILE        
000191     PERFORM UNTIL ORDER-SWITCH = 'NO' OR
000192     STUDENT-ID = ID-NUMBER
000193     
000194                  READ ORDER-FILE
000195                          AT END
000196                          MOVE 'NO' TO ORDER-SWITCH
000197
000198                  END-READ
000199          END-PERFORM
000200
000201           IF STUDENT-ID NOT EQUAL ID-NUMBER OR
000202       NUMBER-ORDERED = 0  THEN
000203                  PERFORM 215-GET-NAME-AND-ID
000204                  PERFORM 216-NO-ORDERS
000205          CLOSE ORDER-FILE
000206                  PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE
000207                  
000208                                          
000210                                                            
000212          END-IF.
000213      
000214  215-GET-NAME-AND-ID.
000215      MOVE STUDENT-ID TO DET-ID
000216          MOVE STUDENT-NAME TO DET-NAME
000217      WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL1 .
000218
000219  216-NO-ORDERS.
000220      MOVE  'NO BOOKS IN ORDERS.DAT FILE' TO
000221          DET-NO-ORDER-MSG
000222          WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAILX
000223          INITIALIZE PRINT-LINE
000224          WRITE PRINT-LINE.
000225                   
000226
000227  220-GET-EACH-BOOK-INFO.
000228          PERFORM 230-GET-BOOK-TITLE
000229          PERFORM 240-GET-COST
000230          PERFORM 250-GET-MARKUP
000231          PERFORM 260-CALC-RETAIL-VALUE
000232          PERFORM 270-INCREMENT-RETAIL
000233          PERFORM 280-WRITE-DETAIL.
000234
000235                  
000236  230-GET-BOOK-TITLE.
000237          MOVE SPACES TO TITLE-SWITCH
000238      OPEN INPUT TITLE-FILE
000239       
000240          PERFORM UNTIL ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = T-ISBN  OR
000241      TITLE-SWITCH = 'NO'
000242
000243          READ TITLE-FILE
000244                  AT END MOVE 'NO' TO TITLE-SWITCH
000245          END-READ        
000246          END-PERFORM
000247
000248      IF ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = T-ISBN THEN
000249      MOVE T-TITLE TO DET-TITLE
000250          END-IF
000251
000252          CLOSE TITLE-FILE.
000253          
000254           
000255
000256  240-GET-COST.
000257          MOVE SPACES TO INVENTORY-SWITCH
000258      OPEN INPUT INVENTORY-FILE
000259          PERFORM UNTIL ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = ISBN
000260          READ INVENTORY-FILE
000261          AT END MOVE 'NO' TO INVENTORY-SWITCH
000262          END-READ
000263          END-PERFORM
000264
000265          IF ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = ISBN THEN
000266          MOVE COST TO DET-COST 
000267          END-IF.
000268          
000269          
000270
000271
000272  250-GET-MARKUP.
000273      MOVE SPACES TO CODE-SWITCH
000274          OPEN INPUT CODE-FILE
000275          PERFORM UNTIL MARKUP = THE-CODE
000276          READ CODE-FILE
000277          AT END MOVE 'NO' TO CODE-SWITCH
000278          END-READ
000279          END-PERFORM
000280
000281          IF MARKUP = THE-CODE THEN
000282          MOVE CODE-VALUE TO DET-MARKUP
000283          END-IF
000284
000285          CLOSE CODE-FILE.
000286 
000287  260-CALC-RETAIL-VALUE.
000288      CLOSE INVENTORY-FILE.
000289  270-INCREMENT-RETAIL.
000290
000291  280-WRITE-DETAIL.
000292          
000293      WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL2.
000294
000295  300-GET-TOTAL-COST.
000296          MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE
000297          WRITE PRINT-LINE .
000298
000299  400-EXIT-PROG04.
000300      CLOSE       NAME-FILE
000310                           PRINT-FILE.
000320
000330      STOP RUN.


* Code below is working version *

000001  IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000002  PROGRAM-ID.     PROG04.
000003  AUTHOR          DAVE ERIN JIM.
000004
000005  ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000006  INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000007  FILE-CONTROL.                        
000008
000009          SELECT INVENTORY-FILE ASSIGN TO SYSIN
000010          ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000011          SELECT ORDER-FILE ASSIGN TO OFILE
000012                  ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000013          SELECT CODE-FILE ASSIGN TO CFILE
000014                  ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000015          SELECT TITLE-FILE ASSIGN TO TFILE
000016                  ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000017          SELECT NAME-FILE ASSIGN TO NFILE
000018                  ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000019          SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO SYSOUT
000020                  ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000021
000022  DATA DIVISION.
000023  FILE SECTION.
000024  FD INVENTORY-FILE
000025     RECORD CONTAINS 25 CHARACTERS
000026     DATA RECORD IS INVENTORY-RECORD.
000027
000028  01 INVENTORY-RECORD.
000029     05 ISBN                                              PIC
X(10).
000030     05 COST                                              PIC
99V99.
000031     05 MARKUP                                            PIC
X(2).
000032     05 R-CODE                                            PIC
X(2).
000033     05 C-INV                                             PIC
9(3).
000034     05 RPOINT                                            PIC 99.
000035     05 RCODE                                             PIC
X(2).
000036  
000037  FD ORDER-FILE
000038     RECORD CONTAINS 9 TO 69 CHARACTERS
000039     DATA RECORD IS ORDER-RECORD.
000040
000041  01 ORDER-RECORD.
000042     05 ID-NUMBER                                 PIC X(5).
000043     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1).
000044     05 NUMBER-ORDERED                            PIC 9(2).
000045     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1).
000046     05 ISBN-INFO OCCURS 0 TO 6 TIMES
000047          DEPENDING ON NUMBER-ORDERED.
000048          10 ISBN1                                PIC X(10).
000049
000050     
000051
000052  FD CODE-FILE
000053     RECORD CONTAINS 6 CHARACTERS
000054     DATA RECORD IS CODE-RECORD.
000055  
000056  01 CODE-RECORD.
000057     05 THE-CODE                                  PIC X(2).
000058     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1).
000059     05 CODE-VALUE                                PIC 999.
000060
000061  FD TITLE-FILE
000062     RECORD CONTAINS 90 CHARACTERS
000063     DATA RECORD IS TITLE-RECORD.
000064
000065  01 TITLE-RECORD.
000066     05 T-ISBN                                    PIC X(10).
000067     05 T-TITLE                                   PIC X(80).
000068
000069  FD NAME-FILE
000070     RECORD CONTAINS 30 CHARACTERS
000071     DATA RECORD IS NAME-RECORD.
000072
000073  01 NAME-RECORD.
000074     05 STUDENT-ID                                PIC X(5).
000075     05 FILLER                                    PIC X(1).
000076     05 STUDENT-NAME                              PIC X(24).
000077
000078  FD PRINT-FILE
000079     RECORD CONTAINS 132 CHARACTERS .
000080  01 PRINT-LINE                                   PIC X(132).     
000081
000082  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000083  01 SWITCHES.
000084     05 ORDER-SWITCH                              PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000085     05 STUDENT-SWITCH                            PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.  
000086     05 NAME-SWITCH                               PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000087     05 TITLE-SWITCH                              PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000088     05 INVENTORY-SWITCH                          PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.
000089     05 CODE-SWITCH                               PIC X(2) VALUE
SPACES.  
000090     05 COUNTER                                   PIC 9(2) VALUE
ZEROS.   
000091   
000092
000093  01 SUBSCRIPTS.
000094     05 BOOK-SUB                                  PIC 99.
000095
000096

'STUDENT ID'.

SPACES.

000126     05 DET-NO-ORDER-MSG                          PIC X(27) VALUE
SPACES.

SPACES.
```

```
000130
000131  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000132  GENERIC-PARAGRAPH.
000133  
000134          OPEN INPUT  NAME-FILE
000135               OUTPUT  PRINT-FILE.
000136
000137      PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE .
000147          CLOSE NAME-FILE
000148                PRINT-FILE
000149          STOP RUN.    
000151                  
000155  150-READ-NAME-FILE.                                     
000156          PERFORM UNTIL NAME-SWITCH = 'NO'
000157      
000158                  READ NAME-FILE
000159                          AT END
000160                                  MOVE 'NO' TO NAME-SWITCH
000161                          NOT AT END
000163                                  PERFORM 200-PROCESS-BOOK-LIST
000164                  END-READ
000165          END-PERFORM     .
000166
000167      
000168  
000174  200-PROCESS-BOOK-LIST.
000175          
000176      COMPUTE COUNTER = COUNTER + 1
000177          DISPLAY COUNTER 
000178          PERFORM 210-VERIFY-ORDER
000179*         DISPLAY NUMBER-ORDERED
000180          PERFORM 215-GET-NAME-AND-ID
000181       
000182                          PERFORM 220-GET-EACH-BOOK-INFO
000183                VARYING BOOK-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
000184                          UNTIL BOOK-SUB > NUMBER-ORDERED
000185          CLOSE ORDER-FILE
000186          PERFORM 300-GET-TOTAL-COST.
000187
000188  210-VERIFY-ORDER.
000189     MOVE SPACES TO ORDER-SWITCH
000190     OPEN INPUT ORDER-FILE        
000191     PERFORM UNTIL ORDER-SWITCH = 'NO' OR
000192     STUDENT-ID = ID-NUMBER
000193     
000194                  READ ORDER-FILE
000195                          AT END
000196                          MOVE 'NO' TO ORDER-SWITCH
000197
000198                  END-READ
000199          END-PERFORM
000200
000201           IF STUDENT-ID NOT EQUAL ID-NUMBER OR
000202       NUMBER-ORDERED = 0  THEN
000203                  PERFORM 215-GET-NAME-AND-ID
000204                  PERFORM 216-NO-ORDERS
000205          CLOSE ORDER-FILE
000206                  PERFORM 150-READ-NAME-FILE
000207                  
000208                                          
000210                                                            
000212          END-IF.
000213      
000214  215-GET-NAME-AND-ID.
000215      MOVE STUDENT-ID TO DET-ID
000216          MOVE STUDENT-NAME TO DET-NAME
000217      WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL1 .
000218
000219  216-NO-ORDERS.
000220      MOVE  'NO BOOKS IN ORDERS.DAT FILE' TO
000221          DET-NO-ORDER-MSG
000222          WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAILX
000223          INITIALIZE PRINT-LINE
000224          WRITE PRINT-LINE.
000225                   
000226
000227  220-GET-EACH-BOOK-INFO.
000228          PERFORM 230-GET-BOOK-TITLE
000229          PERFORM 240-GET-COST
000230          PERFORM 250-GET-MARKUP
000231          PERFORM 260-CALC-RETAIL-VALUE
000232          PERFORM 270-INCREMENT-RETAIL
000233          PERFORM 280-WRITE-DETAIL.
000234
000235                  
000236  230-GET-BOOK-TITLE.
000237          MOVE SPACES TO TITLE-SWITCH
000238      OPEN INPUT TITLE-FILE
000239       
000240          PERFORM UNTIL ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = T-ISBN  OR
000241      TITLE-SWITCH = 'NO'
000242
000243          READ TITLE-FILE
000244                  AT END MOVE 'NO' TO TITLE-SWITCH
000245          END-READ        
000246          END-PERFORM
000247
000248      IF ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = T-ISBN THEN
000249      MOVE T-TITLE TO DET-TITLE
000250          END-IF
000251
000252          CLOSE TITLE-FILE.
000253          
000254           
000255
000256  240-GET-COST.
000257          MOVE SPACES TO INVENTORY-SWITCH
000258      OPEN INPUT INVENTORY-FILE
000259          PERFORM UNTIL ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = ISBN
000260          READ INVENTORY-FILE
000261          AT END MOVE 'NO' TO INVENTORY-SWITCH
000262          END-READ
000263          END-PERFORM
000264
000265          IF ISBN1(BOOK-SUB) = ISBN THEN
000266          MOVE COST TO DET-COST 
000267          END-IF.
000268          
000269          
000270
000271
000272  250-GET-MARKUP.
000273      MOVE SPACES TO CODE-SWITCH
000274          OPEN INPUT CODE-FILE
000275          PERFORM UNTIL MARKUP = THE-CODE
000276          READ CODE-FILE
000277          AT END MOVE 'NO' TO CODE-SWITCH
000278          END-READ
000279          END-PERFORM
000280
000281          IF MARKUP = THE-CODE THEN
000282          MOVE CODE-VALUE TO DET-MARKUP
000283          END-IF
000284
000285          CLOSE CODE-FILE.
000286 
000287  260-CALC-RETAIL-VALUE.
000288      CLOSE INVENTORY-FILE.
000289  270-INCREMENT-RETAIL.
000290
000291  280-WRITE-DETAIL.
000292          
000293      WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL2.
000294
000295  300-GET-TOTAL-COST.
000296          MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE
000297          WRITE PRINT-LINE .
000298
000299  400-EXIT-PROG04.
000300      CLOSE       NAME-FILE
000310                           PRINT-FILE.
000320
000330      STOP RUN.


000001  IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000002  PROGRAM-ID.     PROG04.
000003  AUTHOR          DAVE ERIN JIM.
000004
000005  ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000006  INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000007  FILE-CONTROL.                        
000008

000010          ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000011          SELECT ORDER-FILE ASSIGN TO OFILE

000013          SELECT CODE-FILE ASSIGN TO CFILE
000014                  ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

000016                  ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

000018                  ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

X(2).

X(2).

9(3).

X(2).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: strange occurence

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T04:32:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XXaA6.501$FY5.16573@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <5Vjz6.8700$Kr1.739747@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3ACEA472.2DC6D90E@slic.com> <wDEz6.11950$Kr1.1053874@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <KfGz6.16$F93.336@www.newsranger.com> <3ACFE947.9D64931@slic.com>`

```
Hi Mous,

Looking at your code in 210-, I noticed 2 things:
1) You open and close the order file for every student in the name file.
2) If a student doesn't have any open orders, you read the entire order file.

It looks like you do the same thing with the other search files. 
While these are not show stoppers in a homework environment, it can be a
disaster in a real world environment. See the thread entitled:
"Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?" starting with my post of 3/29
for a first hand account. 

You could correct these problems for the order file, since they both use the
same key in the same order (I assume both files are sorted by student id).
But since you've defined the other reference files as sequential and the
occurance of each key value is unpredicable, you can't use that approach. 
It sounds like these files should be indexed.

Regards, Jack






In article <3ACFE947.9D64931@slic.com>, anonymous says...
>
>
…[668 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
>Looking at your code in 210-, I noticed 2 things:

> of good luck.

240-Get-Cost

easier

code
because line numbers get changed often.  I just started numbering
paragraphs.
```

#### ↳ Re: strange occurence

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T18:18:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9asueg$2p8$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com>`

```
In article <3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com>, anonymous  <mac3@slic.com> writes:
>fujitsu 4
>win 98
>STUDENT QUESTION???
>
>Hello-
I have seen something similar happen before. In my case it was caused by
getting to the end of the program and failing to end the program with the 
"STOP RUN" command.  This can be caused by using a confusing entry in the
switch you check for if the program is at the end of the file.  You move 
'NO' to a field and you check to see if it says 'NO'.  I would assume it
sometimes is equal to 'YES'.  What it actually equals is 'NO '. This is
always confusing.  If you have a 3 character field use all 3 characters for
the move and test for all 3 characters as well. 

It is better to use 'Y' for yes and 'N' for no.  

One Idea:

When you test for a 'YES' or 'NO ' value use an if, else if, else structure. 
On the else it should cover anything that is not 'YES' or 'NO ', and display
what is in the field. 

Try putting a display in the test to tell yourself what the result of the test
is at the end of the program when you test for the end of the file.

It can also be helpful to display the file open and close information with
something similar to "DISPALY INPUT-FILE IS OPEN, FILE SATUS =" CHK-01.
This requires a field for the file Status like CHK-01 or something similar,
and a FILE-STATUS phrase in the INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION of the Environment
Division.

Use Displays to tell yourself what happens with the file. 

Another option is to use the 88 or condition field to set the condition up
ahead of time and give it a name like MORE-RECORDS, and NO-MORE-RECORDS. 
This makes a program readable.

01  END-OF-FILE PIC XXX VALUE = 'YES'.
    88 NO-MORE-RECORDS   VALUE 'NO '.

imagine the statement:

PERFORM MY-PROCESS UNTIL NO-MORE-RECORDS.

(READ PARAGRAPH)
  READ INPUT-FILE
     AT-END 
        SET NO-MORE-RECORDS TO TRUE.

In different programming books different examples are used. I think your
problem is that you have your program in a loop and after you close the files
you just keep going.  So when your read the file after you closed it the
program or the operating system complains.



>
>I was closing in on finishing lab project 04 when I noticed
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…
>	END-PERFORM.

  http://newsone.net/ -- Free reading and anonymous posting to 60,000+ groups
   NewsOne.Net prohibits users from posting spam.  If this or other posts
made through NewsOne.Net violate posting guidelines, email abuse@newsone.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: strange occurence

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T17:13:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD33F1E.993DC5D7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com> <9asueg$2p8$1@news.netmar.com>`

```


chammond@lc.cc.il.us wrote:

You offer him below several alternatives, and each has a flaw if you don't watch out for it :-

    - Did I code 'Y' or 'y' or 'N' or 'n'
    - Did I code "YES", "Yes", "yes", "NO^", "No^", "no^", "NO", "No" or 'no"

I know it is a preference of coding style - BUT -

01 MyFlag                                pic 9.
    88 ConditionMet                  value 1.
    88 ConditionNotMet            value 0.

set ConditionNotMet to true
perform  until ConditionMet
    blah blah blah........
    if a = b
        set ConditionMet to true
    End-if

Or : substitute for above IF as appropriate

    evaluate true
        when x    do this....
        when y    do this.....
        when z    set ConditionMet to true
        when other (Xtreme Programming) - I've got a boo-boo !
    End-evaluate

End-perform

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
In article <3ACCF8C3.D5DF81D1@slic.com>, anonymous  <mac3@slic.com> writes:
>fujitsu 4
>win 98
>STUDENT QUESTION???
>
We use to get together and discuss our programs all of the time.  I have seen
some classes where people would just copy each other's homework, but a good
instructor can tell when a program has been copied.  Usually no two programs
are exactly the same.

If you do copy something make sure to change the name where it says 
"AUTHOR."  It wouldnt be cool to turn in something with someone else's name on
it.

On my first Java programming test the instructor made us write a program on
the fly in about 20 minutes using OOP prrogramming techniques.  A lot of
people coudn't even figure out how to compile it.  Not only that but everyone
knew about it ahead of time.  Then he gave everyone 100% on the programming
part if their program compiled and ran resembling what it was suppose to do.
Some people couldnt even figure out how to format a disk or use the operating
system.
```

```
Although I can't take advantage of it (personally), I just received the
following note that I thought MIGHT be of interest to some who read this NG.

"The purpose of this email is to announce new the SHARE scholarship program
is now live on the web at http://www.share.org/scholarship.

SHARE is offering four annual scholarships to students who are pursuing
careers in the Information Technology (IT) industry.  The term "IT" refers
to a range of disciplines including, but not limited to:  Computer Science,
Management Information Systems, and Computer or Electrical Engineering.

Each SHARE scholarship includes:
 1. A cash award of $2,500 for use in defraying educational expenses
 2. Paid attendance at one SHARE conference during the year for which the
scholarship is awarded
 3. Airfare, hotel accommodations, and a food allowance for the conference
 4. An announcement of the winners posted on the SHARE web site and
displayed prominently at SHARE conferences

For additional details and scholarship application materials, please visit
    http://www.share.org/scholarship.

General information on SHARE is available at http://www.share.org.  If you
have any questions, do not hesitate to contact me at (312) 673-4793 or
    bkowalski@sba.com.

Thank you,

Brooke Kowalski
Program Coordinator

SHARE Headquarters
401 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL  60611-4267"
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol calling Pl/1 program

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T11:56:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010406075640.02162.00000558@ng-ci1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<9aj692$ruf$1@bob.news.rcn.net>`

```
SankarRavi writes ..

Guru,

>  I have a cobol program which has to call PL/1 program with some passing
> parms. When I tested Before calling PL/1 program Cobol displays the 
> correct values. But the PL/1 Programs Not taking it    and always shows 
> blanks, finally PL/1 abends because of No values being passed.

> My question  is there anything special I have to pass.

> Example:

>    C1 is cobol program


>    P1 PL/1 program

> In cobol

>    Call Pl using Address by (d2 )

> Where has d2 is character field in both programs.

> Help me please.

What the heck kind of call is that? I've never seen "Address by" in COBOL; and
why did you put the d2 in parentheses?

You didn't mention your compiler versions or operating system platform, but
here's the skinny for the latest compilers on the OS/390 platform:

In your COBOL program:

           CALL P1 using d2

In your PL/I program:

P1: proc (d2);

      dcl d2 char(10);


No big deal.

Regards,



Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol calling Pl/1 program

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethistoreply@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T10:54:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<th7tctsdsn5s1um7s9eh9e0i1bndv1a4iu@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9aj692$ruf$1@bob.news.rcn.net>`

```
"Ravi" <rsankar@erols.com> wrote:

>   I have a cobol program which has to call PL/1 program with some passing
>parms. When I tested
>Before calling PL/1 program Cobol displays the correct values. But the PL/1
>Programs Not taking it    and always shows blanks, finally PL/1 abends
>because of No values being passed.

I haven't done this in a very long time. But when I did this, it was
necessary to intialize the PL/1 environment by issuing a call to
PLISTART before the first call to the PL/1 program.  

Also, there are lots of gotchas in handling data types and arrays
between the two languages.  Make sure that everything matches up and
that you are not using compiler options that affect the field mapping.

Best place to start, the PL/1 Programmers Guide.  It will have a
section on doing this. 
                         
                         		Scott Peterson
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol calling Pl/1 program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T15:39:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9antv9$qcj$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9aj692$ruf$1@bob.news.rcn.net> <th7tctsdsn5s1um7s9eh9e0i1bndv1a4iu@4ax.com>`

```
Assuming the question is for the IBM mainframe environment, it depends on
whether you are using current/supported versions of the COBOL and PL/I
compilers.  If you are using the LE run-time and "supported" compilers, then
you should check out the ILC programming guide at:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/CEEA4100/CCONTENT
S

(There are "earlier" versions of this manual, but that is the most recent
one)
```

<a id="t-19b4242fa9"></a>
## Thread: LINE ADVANCING — 3 msgs

### LINE ADVANCING

- **From:** "Marty P" <martek@videotron.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T01:20:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<frcz6.1325$aF1.424233@weber.videotron.net>`

```
When I write a:

AFTER ADVANCING 3 LINES.

it seems to just advance 1 line in the Fujitsu Compiler v3 and is skipping
the 3 lines in MicroFocus Compiler.
What could be causing this?

thanks,

Marty
```

#### ↳ Re: LINE ADVANCING

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T02:34:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ajrjk$oal$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<frcz6.1325$aF1.424233@weber.videotron.net>`

```
Did you actually send the output to a printer or do you just see one "blank
line" when you look at the file?  Is it possible that it is using some
"unprintable" characters that you don't see and that if you actually
directed your output to a printer that it would advance the 3 lines?

I have heard of this as a COMMON problem on IBM mainframes, but don't
remember hearing of it on the PC - but I can imagine there being some "hex"
combinations that don't show up "correctly" when you view them - but would
on a printer.

The other question (sort-of related to above) is whether or not you have
defined your output as "line sequential" or "record sequential".  Again, how
the ADVANCING phrase is handled for those two types of files MAY be
different - and again different in a "text-editor" than an actual printer.
```

#### ↳ Re: LINE ADVANCING

- **From:** "Reason" <steveno@spamcfs.com.au>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T18:10:27+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3acd849c$1@nexus.comcen.com.au>`
- **References:** `<frcz6.1325$aF1.424233@weber.videotron.net>`

```
I have seen problems if you mix PRINT BEFORE and PRINT AFTER.
Stick to one or the other.

> When I write a:
>
> AFTER ADVANCING 3 LINES.
>
> it seems to just advance 1 line in the Fujitsu Compiler v3 and is
skipping
> the 3 lines in MicroFocus Compiler.
> What could be causing this?
```

```
It sounds to me as if the "common" problem that you are having is in the
"correct" use of the COBOL "areas" or "margins".  Although these may not be
REQUIRED with your (Fujitsu) compiler, they are certainly causing some
errors.

Let me "explain" the compiler messages to you - one by one - below.

NOTE: I think that most EXPERIENCED programmers would find these messages
"pretty clear" - but I do understand that they may seem like nothing but
"buzz-words" to the novice.
```

<a id="t-7ec2ef9350"></a>
## Thread: Vs: Vs: First cobol-program — 1 msgs

### Re: Vs: Vs: First cobol-program

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T08:20:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACDD0A3.B2B55F6E@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net> <GTez6.66$qw5.2787@read2.inet.fi>`

```


"Jarkko Kï¿½hkï¿½nen" wrote:

> Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Umm. Please explain more clearly.:)

  OPEN INPUT FRIENDS-FILE.
   PERFORM READ-MY-DATA-FILE UNTIL
END-OF-FRIENDS-FILE = 1
     DISPLAY OWNSTRING
   END-PERFORM

This performs READ-MY-DATA-FILE.   But it also has an END-PERFORM two lines
down.  If I were a compiler, I would be confused.   If you perform a paragraph,
that's what you perform, if you have an in-line perform, you perform from the
perform statement to the end-perform.


> > Some compilers give an error if they find a section without a paragraph
> name.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>     What's that paragraph name?

Come compilers require:
MY-SECTION SECTION.
MY-PARAGRAPH.
       CONTINUE.

instead of
MY-SECTION SECTION.
      CONTINUE.


> Best regards,
> Jake.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> assumed to be coded.

I suspect this may be a Fujitsu only message, but check your columns to make
sure your ID line is in the right place.


> *    1: JMN1019I-W    Indicator area must contain '-', '*', '/', 'D', or
> blank. A blank is assumed to be specified.
> .....

Ah, especially check to see what you have in column 7.  The above characters are
the only things allowed in column 7 and they all have special meanings.


> *    37:JMN1004I-W    Reserved word 'PERFORM' must start in area B. Assumed
> to start in area B.
> ...
> And so on.

More of the same.   Your program isn't aligned correctly.   While some compilers
have variations, the standard is to have 1-6 for sequence numbers (the compiler
doesn't look at these), column 7 for those symbols above, column 8-11 for the
start of commands which must start in area A (DIVISION, SECTION, FD, 01,
PARAGRAPHS, etc), 12-72 for everything else, and 73-80 as a redundant sequence
number place.

Lots of shops put change control comments in the sequence number columns.
```

#### ↳ Re: Oil and dick

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T13:56:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C0Fz6.12029$Kr1.1059975@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <3ACB7777.7ED67246@home.com> <1EF061510F4212DD.E26B3491FA8454C9.0433824481B06B70@lp.airnews.net> <ECdz6.34$qw5.1831@read2.inet.fi> <FA7376A874945331.B3BC8A6A7CC1CCB8.672B4906307E4CA0@lp.airnews.net>`

```

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam
>
> Dick Cheney is the Vice President of the United States.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not exactly right, but close enough for demonstrating extensive
knowledge of U.S. energy needs. For shaming with guilt-by-association,
it misses the mark.

Haliburton (Cheney's former employer) is a service company. They
provide products and services TO oil companies, mostly at the well
site. Much like a company that sells drill pipe, or paint, or radio
equipment/operators, or helicopter commuting.

Haliburton does not explore, drill, transport, refine, or market
petroleum products. From a purely business perspective, Haliburton is
indifferent to the U.S. oil industry (if oil is not produced here, it
will be produced elsewhere - and the need for Haliburton's services
moves with the well locations).

I used to work for Petroleum Information (a division,
believe-it-or-not, of A.C. Nielson). This company tabulated and
compiled the daily output of every oil and gas well in the world (as
best they could). The 'product' was a book, published daily, and
forwarded to some 3,000 subscribers (virtually all were oil
companies). (This was a big data-processing job in that data were
coming in from hundreds of sources - like state regulatory agencies -
in multiple formats and these data had to be massaged against a
massive database, a 2-300 page printout created, and sent to printing
so the book could make the evening FedEx planes).

Despite the name and the client list, Petroleum Information was no
more an oil company than Haliburton. But employees DID learn a lot
about oil, gas, and energy, just by osmosis.

P.S.
Another division of A.C. Nielson counts grocery coupons. The coupons
were shipped from a collection point (somewhere in Iowa, I think) to
Juarez. There, in a room the size of an airplane hanger, Mexican women
with eight-pound tongues sorted these little scraps of paper into
General Foods, Hunt, Carnation, etc. Boggles the mind. Nowadays, the
coupons have barcodes. The Mexican women have sensors mounted on their
tongue-tips.

A.C. Nielson is a division of Reed-Elsiver. Reed is a publishing
company that produces Books In Print, The Physician's Desk Reference,
Marquis' Who's Who, Martindale-Hubble Law Directory, etc. The common
thread, it seems to me, is that every one of this company's endeavors
has a massive cost of entry to a would-be competitor.
```

## Thread: question for everyone non-program, related to COBOL — 4 msgs

### question for everyone non-program, related to COBOL

- **From:** deek40@aol.com (Deek40)
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T18:11:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010406141136.27975.00000405@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```
I was thinking of starting a COBOL website for students taking COBOL.  A place
where students can help each other and learn new things in the process.  Many
people know of recources COBOL related, but either do not share them or get
sick of people asking for them.  Is this a viable idea?  Would people
contribute some information(ex: if u have a document on ur computer that
explains the intrisic function for dates etc....)  that could be posted on the
net and given full credit to the author.  I think this could be a good learning
resource.  Please give any advice. 

Deek
```

#### ↳ Re: question for everyone non-program, related to COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-06T19:27:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ACE1849.66645B70@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20010406141136.27975.00000405@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```


Deek40 wrote:

> I was thinking of starting a COBOL website for students taking COBOL.  A place
> where students can help each other and learn new things in the process.  Many
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Deek

Don't' want to stifle your enthusiasm Deek - but on and off I have been plugging
this one for about roughly two years.

What is truly required, is *somebody(bodies)* to co-ordinate, taking the existing
COBOL standard and the future COBOL 200X, and take every syntactical phrase and
illustrate it with numerous and varying examples. Then it becomes a case of getting
selective on 'traditional problem'. examples, e.g. :-

        - Write a program that reads in a file
        - produce totals for products
        - output a report or file etc....

Now to make the thing useful, it needs be, purely as an example :-

1    - Structured - Basics - the sort of examples you are looking for to help you
    getting acquainted with the language
2    - Structured - Advanced - the level I might be looking for to use a feature in

    COBOL that I haven't yet used, (e.g. the new VALIDATE verb to be introduced
    in COBOL 200X)
 3   - Structured - Performance - just as IMPORTANT - if you've been following
    some of the threads you will have seen messages from Judson about optimum
    coding  approaches for efficiency, like when you do/and do NOT open/close
files,
    and performance statistics resulting from different techniques
4 - Object Orientation - Basics - to get people off the ground
5 - Object Orientation - Advanced - melding different sub modules together,
    component technology, linking with other languages etc.
6 - etc...... (add you item......)

Now I have made the same sort of suggestion elsewhere and I did get a response from
two folks on J4 (that's 'our' committee that discusses and determines what COBOL
200X will be). My suggestion was it should be a supporting document to the standard
- their reaction, good idea but not produced by J4. One immediately got a glint in
his eye, "Here's a way to make a few bucks...."

I don't know the mechanics, but you'd have to have a heavy duty site - chances are
it will receive a lot of hits during the course of the day. Then there is the sheer
problem of selecting, co-ordinating and cataloguing finalized examples. Last but
not least, the money required to run the site.

Maybe it has to start with the academics (educators) - dunno.

Check the following site for some interesting examples - although I haven't
reviewed the quality :-

http://www.csis.ul.ie/COBOL/examples

PS: Picked up above reference here in the News group -  could have even been from
one of your messages <G>.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: Vs: First cobol-program — 15 msgs

### Re: Vs: First cobol-program

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T14:26:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<usFz6.12065$Kr1.1067948@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net> <GTez6.66$qw5.2787@read2.inet.fi>`

```
Others have commented, but you may want some background.

COBOL started in the days of punch cards, hence the 80-column standard
(that's where we get 80-column screens also) (And the original size of
the punch card was based on the size of U.S. currency - in 1890 -
because all kinds of equipment existed to count and stack and
transport currency) (And the holes in punch cards were based on the
size of card punching equipment used in the textile industry governing
the weaving of designs. So, card-size/whole-size = 80 columns.)

If you ever drop a source deck of 3000 punch cards, you'll know
immediately why the cards should be sequenced, hence the sequence
numbers in columns 1-6.

Early assembly language (also on punch cards) had its sequence numbers
in 73-80, and COBOL had, at one time, the construct to suddenly shift
to assembly ("BEGIN ASSEMBLY" or some-such). So, apparently, columns
73-80 were reserved for that.

Word-wrapping editors, terminals, and newer languages with more robust
parsing compilers, without the above constraints, yielded more
'free-form' coding techniques.

Somebody thought free-form would be a good idea for COBOL.

They forgot to tell you.

Here's the deal: Skip 1-6 and 73-80. Use 7 for comments (an asterisk).
Put Division and Paragraph names (and a few other things like FD and
01) in column 8. Put everything else in column 12+. Indent by 4 (or 2)
columns for successive outline clarity.

Your code will compile no matter what the compiler settings or
standards of your shop.

P.S.,
There is never any need for a "Continuation Mark (dash)" in column 7.
Nor is there any real use for a "Debug Mark ("D") in a PC environment
because of debuggers built into the compilers.

OK, OK, I know what you want:

 "D" allowed you to salt your code with all kinds of diagnostics -
like printouts and program halts - and have that imbedded
investigatory material ride along with the code. The compiler treated
debug statements as comments: until the program was re-compiled with
DEBUG=ON, then you'd get all the author's original debug code to
assist in solving a current problem.


"Jarkko K�hk�nen" <jarkko.kahkonen@softageneraattori.fi> wrote in
message news:GTez6.66$qw5.2787@read2.inet.fi...

have a

in-line

paragraph

other

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

Header

'D', or
```

```
Jerry P wrote:

> Others have commented, but you may want some background.

Agree with your history 'summation' and comments overall - but the
following ?

> P.S.,
> There is never any need for a "Continuation Mark (dash)" in column 7.
> Nor is there any real use for a "Debug Mark ("D") in a PC environment
> because of debuggers built into the compilers.
>

Sure don't need the "D" with an Animator, but the continuation 'dash' what
do you do instead. Typically when designing print lines, particularly
fixed literal headers, I'll 'draw' them in the source starting at column 1
with an "*" and then laying out the header info. (This gives me a mock-up
of several header lines followed by formats for data lines).

Now I set up my Level 01 print lines and move the above to pic x(??) value
".....
As I hit column 72,  then I move remainder to next line starting with the
'dash' in column 7 followed by the opening quote and the remaining part of
the literal; repeat this process until I don't reach Column 72.

Your approach ?

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net> <GTez6.66$qw5.2787@read2.inet.fi> <usFz6.12065$Kr1.1067948@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3ACF5D21.AE465280@home.com>`

```
news:3ACF5D21.AE465280@home.com...
>

7.

environment

'dash' what
> do you do instead. Typically when designing print lines,
particularly
```
