```
column 1
> with an "*" and then laying out the header info. (This gives me a
mock-up
> of several header lines followed by formats for data lines).
>
> Now I set up my Level 01 print lines and move the above to pic x(??)
value
> ".....
> As I hit column 72,  then I move remainder to next line starting
with the
> 'dash' in column 7 followed by the opening quote and the remaining
part of
> the literal; repeat this process until I don't reach Column 72.
>
> Your approach ?

In an attempt to keep the kid out of column seven, I just said there
is no 'need' to use the continuation character. I've been doing COBOL
for several decades and never used a continuation designation.

But I do like your idea of 'drawing' the page layout and moving pieces
around.

I accomplish the goal several ways - none perfect. One is to decompose
a 130-character output line into two PIC X(65) lines. The annotation
usually fits.

Another technique is to STRING the literal annotation at execution
time (no, it's not a waste of cycles - it's only done once).

On my next opportunity, I'll give your technique a try.

Thanks for sharing.
```

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:

> I think that you would find REPORT WRITER even easier <G>
>
> --
> Bill Klein

Only if it was standard enough in your shop that maintenance programmers will
be familiar with it.

I find that the old way of creating header lines which match the detail lines
is pretty easy and every CoBOL programmer knows how to do it.

The trouble with Report Writer is that it is only twice as easy as doing
things the way everything else is done in CoBOL.  That's not a sufficient
improvement to get it widely used.   And nowadays, I recommend that people
skip CoBOL altogether for reports, instead handle data for Crystal Reports to
use.
```

- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net> <GTez6.66$qw5.2787@read2.inet.fi> <usFz6.12065$Kr1.1067948@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3ACF5D21.AE465280@home.com>`

```
And another plug for "does your compiler vendor already support
'enhancements' from the draft Standard.

In the next COBOL Standard, you will be able to get rid of the "-" in column
7 for continuing alphanumeric literals.

Instead of coding

    Move        "abc...column 72
-      "continued stuff" to large-field

You will now be able to code

    Move        "abc...column 72"-
      "continued stuff" to large-field

You will also be able to use ">>D" in any column instead of "D" in column 7.

You will also be able to use ">>PAGE" instead of "/" in column 7

You will also be able to use ">*" instead of "*" in column 7

So *all* of the "column 7" (indicator stuff) will no longer be REQUIRED in
any program (whether it uses fixed-form reference format or free-form
reference format).  The "old" techniques will continue to be supported for
'fixed-form reference format' - but will NOT be included in 'free-form
reference format'

Check with your "vendor of choice" to see if they already support any/all of
these enhancements - and if not, when they will.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Vs: First cobol-program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T17:11:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ao3c2$4hb$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net> <GTez6.66$qw5.2787@read2.inet.fi> <usFz6.12065$Kr1.1067948@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3ACF5D21.AE465280@home.com> <3AD00FA5.3545C610@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
I am sort-of surprised that no one (so far) has mentioned ANOTHER way to get
around the "long alphanumeric literal" problem - in the '85 Standard.

Let's say that you currently have:

   Move           "ABC Column 72
-   " Some more stuff" to Long-Field

(where the '2' in '72' is in column 72 - no spaces following it)

then TODAY you can code:

   Move           "ABC Column 7"
           to Long-Field
   Move           "2  Some more stuff"
           to Long-Field (13:)

I am NOT saying that this is (necessarily) a "better" way of doing this than
defining "sub-fields" to "Long-Field" - but it does mean that if you have a
variety of "print-lines" with no COMMON LOGICAL breaking points, you don't
need to "create" artificial sub-fields.

(You can even use reference modification in conditional statements - if you
break up your "long literal" and do combined conditionals - such as
   If ((Long-Field (1:12) = "ABC Column 7")
           and
        (Long-Field (13:) = "2 Some more stuff"))  then ...
```

```
I use "D" quite a bit, not having a debugger which I like (especially for
DC-COBOL).   But I never use the dash.   That's what 05 (or 10 or 15 or
whatever) levels are for.

Typically I make print headings by copying the print detail lines and editing
them.   This makes the picture sizes match up.   When maintenance changes a
field, it is easy to change it both places.
```

## Thread: Calling AcuCOBOL from C++ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Calling AcuCOBOL from C++

- **From:** "Glenn A. Mitchell" <g.mitchell@computer.org>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T11:27:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QkGz6.6566$9%.927581@newsr1.maine.rr.com>`

```
I am in the early stages of designing an application for a client.  The
target platform is RS/6000 (AIX) with AcuCOBOL.  As part of the design, I
would like to have a daemon written in C++ invoke a COBOL program.
According to the AcuCOBOL manual, this is possible, but the manual is not
very clear about how this linkage occurs.

I don't have the manual (Appendix C) at hand at this moment, but basically
one simply calls "cobol" and passes it the name of the COBOL program to
invoke along with an argument list.  How is the reference to "cobol"
resolved??  Are there header files (.h) that need to be included?

TIA for any clarification you can provide.
```

```
Of those who develop software, how many of you enjoy paying per-user
COBOL runtime licensing fees to the COBOL vendor? Especially when the
initial cost for the development package isn't cheap.

the COBOL runtime licensing fees in the Net Express 3.0 product.

product.

Looks like it's time to switch COBOL vendors...
```

```
Keep your eye on TinyCobol. If development of that product continues at the
current pace, Merant will only have themselves to blame for destroying a
large part of their existing (and future) customer base.

While Fujitsu do not require runtime fees, the installation licensing
procedures for their latest version indicate they may be developing the same
short sighted attitudes which will ultimately drive their customers away as
well.

The result will be that open source products like TinyCobol and Tim
Josling's compiler will continue to be developed and ultimately thrive,
while companies like Merant and Fujitsu will end up effectively shelving
technically superior products.

That's the end of my soap box and free consulting session to Merant and
Fujitsu for today;-)

Regards, Robert Kovacic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol runtime licensing issues

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T06:26:39+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD0C97F.1E761564@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<3ACFAFBB.2C836B34@earthlink.net> <sWPz6.11380$45.65139@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <2BSz6.2186$ix4.1681813@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`

```
I think the free compilers will be a major challenge to the
closed source compilers, though the strong ones will survive.
They will survive where they add extra value - useful support,
productivity tools, etc.

The history of computing has shown many times that 'good enough'
at a lower price defeats soemthing better at a higher price,
especially if the lower price is 'free'.

Database is more difficult for free developers. To really test
databases, you need large machines, which are expensive. It is
also very complex technically to deliver good performance
combined with fast restart, robustness, data integrity,
transactions, row level locking, lock escalation etc.

Postgresql and mySQL are not really competitive with Oracle etc
at the high end IMHO, though they are very useful for the right
application.

Tim Josling
```

```
"Tim Josling" <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote:
> Database is more difficult for free developers. To really test
> databases, you need large machines, which are expensive. It is
> also very complex technically to deliver good performance
> combined with fast restart, robustness, data integrity,
> transactions, row level locking, lock escalation etc.

Tim, that's my point (apologies for the loaded question). your statement
applies with as much force to large COBOL applications (especially on the
higher-end Windows/UNIX boxes and the 'frames -- which coincidently is where
the larger $ deals are done). there is nothing better than COBOL for these
types of applications (tho' i think Java/J2EE will also [ultimately] be
successful in this space) -- that is what it was built for. the run-time
environments necessary to support execution of these applications in a
mission-critical manner are extremely complex ("run-time" is somewhat an
understatement -- "Application Server" as Micro Focus brands it is much
closer to the mark; and i don't think we'll see Weblogic or WebSphere going
"cheap" any time soon, if ever -- same "large systems" reasoning applies
here too).

now, an interesting point of view (and i'm not sure it's one i totally agree
with even if it might seem that way ;-) is that it is rather unfortunate
that COBOL is visible and popular outside of the "large systems" domain.
perhaps what the vendors need to do is recognise this as an opportunity and
provide a COBOL-oriented toolset that fits the "Visual [insert language
moniker]" paradigm.

years ago, Micro Focus attempted this with a product called "Visual Object
COBOL" that was priced at $499. in 6mths, over 3500 people registered &
downloaded the beta software. given the lack of "publicity" that was quite a
surprise (perhaps word of mouth spreads as fast as foot and mouth :-). but
the end of this "fairy tale" ended up being more like a "horror story"; at
least commercially. after release, relatively few people actually ponied up
and bought it. the successors: Net Express and it's UNIX counterpart Server
Express, were priced much higher; and have both done (and continue to do)
very well.

i don't think it's a coincidence that Fujitsu are now a good bit more
sensitive about protecting their revenue.

Gazaloo.
```

---

<a id="t-84fe744a96"></a>
## Thread: (US) COBOL Public Review period ends tomorrow, April 9th — 27 msgs

### (US) COBOL Public Review period ends tomorrow, April 9th

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-07T21:21:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9aohvt$72f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The US Public Review period on the "draft" COBOL Standard (FCD) ends
tomorrow (April 9, 2001 - at the "end of business" day).

As of my posting this note, there have been 38 (count them 38) public review
comments submitted.

Of these 5 (only 5 !!!) have been from people who are NOT "members" of the
COBOL Standards committees - either as "official observers," J4 members, or
WG4 delegates.

Of those 5 comments, none seems to be from "real programmers" who have even
read any of the "summary" lists (of enhancements, incompatibilities, etc) in
the FCD - but instead they have done "joint" comments on the reaffirmation
of the current Standard and for the next one "basically" saying that "COBOL
is important to us - please keep working on it".

My guesses (and they are only guesses) of WHY there have been so few "real
programmers" providing ANY input (good, bad, mixed, whatever) are as follows
(roughly in the order that I think they apply - although I suspect that it
is actually a combination of reasons).

1) Those who care about a "next" COBOL Standard are SO concerned about how
delayed this revision is, that they would agree to ANYTHING - just to get it
approved and implemented as soon as possible.

2) Many COBOL programmers/sites don't know when/if they will ever see an
implementation with all (most? any?) of these new enhancements.  They still
have '74 Standard COBOL programs in production; their programmers don't
"use" any of the new stuff - even when available; and/or it was ONLY Y2K
that got their shop to actually upgrade to an '85 Standard compiler - so
they don't expect to see a 200x compiler until 2010 or so.

3) COBOL users think that the standards group are made up of experts who
"know what is best for them" - and they MUST be putting stuff in that the
users will want to use - and that they will be defined in the BEST possible
way.  Therefore, there is no use for a "mere" programmer to look at this
draft because it MUST be the best possible way for COBOL to be in the
"future".

4) People have been hearing about the enhancements in the draft Standard for
years now.  From what they have seen, it is the "best thing since sliced
bread" - and they wouldn't change a THING about it - even if they could.

5) "Total Apathy" about revisions to COBOL - especially what is in a
"Standard" instead of in THEIR "implementation".  (This may actually be the
most likely reason - but I always HOPE that it isn't - no matter how many
times I am proved wrong.)

  ***

I did an article in CobolReport on how to "review" the draft Standard - that
pointed out how to tell what was KNOWN to be incompatible with the current
Standard (and many implementations) - as well as to "review" what has been
added as "enhancements" in this draft.  The fact that "real users" who are
not on/involved with the standards committees have NOT actually submitted
*any* substantive comments disturbs me and surprises me (a little - not too
much).

One thing that did come thru in the MANY comments that have come in
concerning the "reaffirmation" of the current ANSI (not ISO) COBOL Standard
is how important portability is for COBOL programmers.  Therefore, let me
point out just a couple of things that add to my questioning WHY people
aren't commenting on the FCD.

A) In the draft Standard, there is the following statement (my ABSOLUTE
least favorite thing about the FCD),

 "The decision of whether to claim support for a processor-dependent
language element is within an implementor's discretion. Factors that may be
considered include, but are not limited to, hardware capability, software
capability, and market positioning of the processor."

Note Well:
   I don't know which I hate more relying on "market positioning" in a COBOL
Standard *or* the fact that implementors don't even need to tell WHY they
aren't supporting these things.

Note Well-2:
 J4 and WG4 have *explicitly* rejected the thought of calling this
"optional" as the '85 Standard does - and instead have "mixed" optional and
hardware/software dependent items and made them ALL "optional" in all but
name.

B) Some interesting "statistics" comparing:
   - The "base" '85 Standard (X3.23-1985)
  - The '85 Standard with both amendments (X3.23-1986 + X3.23a-1989 +
X3.23b-1993)
  - The current draft of the next COBOL Standard (FCD)

I -  Implementor-Defined Language Element
    a)  Base '85 Standard - 80 items
    b) '85 Standard with Amendments - now 87 items
    c)  FCD - 218 items      *>> note this number particularly !!!

II - Undefined Language Element List
    a) Base '85 Standard - 28 items
    b) '85 Standard with Amendments -  now 36 items
    c) - FCD - 65 items

III -  Hardware Dependent Language Element List  & Optional Modules ('85
Standard vs Processor Dependent (FCD)
    a) Base '85 Standard: Hardware dependent - 20 items; 4 optional modules
    b) '85 Standard with Amendments: Hardware dependent -(still 20 items),
now 5 optional modules
    c) FCD - 30 items (includes both "individual" features and "module-type"
items)

For those who think that almost doubling the "undefined" list and MORE than
doubling the "implementor defined" list is "reasonable" because the new
Standard adds so many more "new" goodies, look at the following two
comparisons.

IV - Substantive changes potentially affecting (incompatible changes from
prior revision)
    a) Base '85 Standard - 42 items
    b) '85 Standard with Amendments - now  66 items
    c) FCD - 41 items

V - Substantive changes not affecting (incompatible changes from prior
revision)
    a) Base '85 Standard -  107 items
    b) '85 Standard with Amendments - now 113 items
    c) FCD - 147 items

The only good news (IMHO) is that the number of "incompatible" NEW changes
in the FCD is about the same as those in the '85 Standard (remember what it
was like moving from an '74 Standard compiler to an '85 Standard one - at
least this time it won't be much worse).  However, the fact that there were
24 ADDITIONAL "incompatible" changes in the amendments to the '85 Standard
might give users something to worry about as the new Standard gets
implemented - much less enhanced.

    ***

In "short":

 - If you live/work outside the US, check with your national body to see
when Public Review comments are due there.  You may still have "plenty" of
time to get your input in (again - it can be "good" "bad" or "detailed")

- If you live/work inside the US, then you CAN still get any comments that
you have in by the end of the day, Monday, April 9.

- If you live/work inside the US and cannot get your comments in by April
9 - but do have some, you can still send them in (especially if they get
there as "soon" after April 9 as possible).  Although ANSI (J4) are not
REQUIRED to process "late" comments, they often do.  The next J4 meeting is
scheduled to start April 22, 2001.  My *guess* is that any "detailed"
comments (suggestions, corrections, deletions - but NOT requests for
additions) that are received before that will be "looked at" - and if a
"valid" problem will actually be submitted by the US to ISO.

   ***

Finally, "once upon a time and long ago," - I was the project leader for the
GUIDE (now defunct IBM mainframe user group) COBOL project.  I had a "real"
reputation for raising issues/problems that none of the other project
members "understood" or took action on when I raised them.  The reason was
that I kept (trying) to look at the "ramifications" for things that were
"planned" or "previewed" - while most of the project was dealing with how to
get "today's" or "yesterday's" programs and compilers to work.  (A
"work-around" was better than a fix - if the "work-around" worked today -
and the "fix" would be available in a few weeks/months/releases).

There was no way then (nor is there any way now) that I can "force" COBOL
programmers to "look ahead" at what will happen to them, their programs, and
their compilers based on what is in a "draft Standard".  However, I can
"beg" you to at least be aware now - and spend as many resources on
"prevention of possible problems" as you do in "complaining about
implementations that you must live with."
```

#### ↳ Re: (US) COBOL Public Review period ends tomorrow, April 9th

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-04-08T20:49:45+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-B78A0B.20494508042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<9aohvt$72f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <9aohvt$72f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>,
 "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> The US Public Review period on the "draft" COBOL Standard (FCD) ends
> tomorrow (April 9, 2001 - at the "end of business" day).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> WG4 delegates.
<snip>
Well a couple of points.
1. Those of us outside the USA don't appear to have a voice in this. Do
you know who your national body is to send your comments to? Will they
listen?

2. I'm rapidly forming the opinion that all language standards bodies
should be abandonded in favour of the Open or GNU models. That is a
tight group of people who actively work on the software (that is writing
it) vote on useful feature as submitted by others (preferably the
submission includes the changes to the Open Source or GNU style code).

Why?
Well look at the history of the Cobol group.
They missed adding verbs for non-gui screens until GUI arrived.
They missed adding verbs for gui windows etc until ... well they still
haven't done anything.
They missed adding verbs for HTML and WEB interactions.
They missed adding verbs for including Java and Javascript.
They missed (and this is a biggie) adding verbs for relational databases
EVEN THOUGH SQL is a SETTLED AND STANDARD database language.
They missed adding verbs for transaction and process oriented tasks, and
this is another biggie, even though roughly 80% of the cobol code is
involved in online money transactions.
They added OO - which is now being used as an excuse to not add other
verbs because of so called standard OO libraries of function. As if we
haven't already got non-OO standard libraries.
Need I continue?

The only solution to this is to allow the language to evolve via
something like what Linux and it's various tools and programs are going
through now. A Massive increase in function - but still done in a
standard way by dint of all those concerned agreeing on the approach and
interfaces. (which is what a standard is isn't it?)

This is a far cry from the current situation where several competing
companies produce compilers that they make EVERY attempt to be different
from each other so that they can lock their users into their product.

These basic infrastructure items will never fulfill the requirement of
write once run anywhere until the programming languages and the
operating systems are removed from the arena of comercial competition.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: (US) COBOL Public Review period ends tomorrow, April 9th

```
Sorry for not including the reference in this note.  Most of my previous
notes gave the specific information on how to find out what your national
body is - and where to send comments.

For those OUTSIDE the US - you can/should contact your national standards
group by looking at:
 http://www.iso.ch/addresse/membodies.html

I believe that email ID's that end in "au" are from Australia.  If that is
where you are located, then the information from that page for you is:

Australia (SAI)
Address:
 Standards Australia International Ltd.
 286 Sussex Street (corner of Bathurst Street)
 AU-Sydney, NSW 2000

Postal address:
 GPO Box 5420
 AU-Sydney, NSW 2001

Telephone:+61 2 82 06 60 00
Telefax:+61 2 82 06 60 01
 E-mail: intsect@standards.com.au
 Web: http://www.standards.com.au/


(I cannot tell you when your deadline is for comments, but if you contact
them and ask about making comments on:

 Reference number of working document: ISO/IEC/JTC 1/SC 22/WG 4 N 0147
 Date: 2001-01-15
 Reference number of document: ISO/IEC FCD 1989:2001

They should be able to help you)
```

```
Joseph J Katnic wrote:
```

```
>  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

> all left out a usefull callable library of routines.

That's exactly why I asked Joe -  'In-house of course'.  In your case might be
OK, say a team of six, or even larger development units, 'sharing' their
experiences - just lean over your desk or poke your head around the cubicle wall,
"How did you do this buddy(cobber) ?"  This Joe Blow on his own, and for each one
like me, does it make sense that we should re-invent the wheel - and apart from
which it is time consuming.

One thing we do *know* from the other languages - this sharing through libraries
is a common feature - and not necessarily just those written by language vendors.
Either Structured or OO - our language could 'zing' if it had useful utility
libraries.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Well Jimmy, this is kind of "chicken and egg". You expressed the desire to
have a library but you can't say what should be in it until you see one...

I think a library is not a good idea. That's why I'm currently converting it
to components.

>Your translation to components obviously has interest to OO-users, but
outside
>of OO may well be easily useable by the 'PC flock', regardless of compiler.

Unfortunately, components really require an OO Facility in order to invoke
them properly. It is necessary to instantiate the component. You COULD do it
in structured COBOL (if you really know what you are doing) but it would
need to be a version of COBOL with OO Extensions in it.

>As I indicated to Deek - to be practical - this example or library stuff
has to
>be accessible in a constantly kept up to date site - set up represents a
lot of
>work, and then dedication, just like Bill with the FAQ, to keep it current.
>
>Any thoughts ?
>
Yes. I could easily set up such a facility but I'm not sure about its
usefulness and whether others would contribute to it. I believe Thane has
such a facility on his Web Site.

I am due to review my existing Web Site but nothing really significant is
likely to happen until I get my own server and become an ISP. (I need server
side code to do the things I want to do and to manage the necessary
databases). As I am travelling again in a few months (the holiday will be
over) there isn't much point in doing it right now.

I'll think some more about making the existing routines available on a free
server. I would need to know there is a demand for such (so far you are the
only one who has expressed interest) and in the meantime, why not take a
look at what Thane has and see if that is what you're looking for?

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: (US) COBOL Public Review period ends tomorrow, April 9th

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 

Bill I think the main reason is simply the effort needed to read
900 pages and comment sensibly on it. I must have spent 80-100
hours on this. In Australia I still have a couple of weeks to get
my comments in, which I will do.

The number of pages in the new document is not larger than the
old document, but the font is a lot smaller and a lot of the
cross reference tables are gone, so it is a lot bigger.

Many things make it extra difficult:

a) The fact so many terms are undefined (not in the list of
definitions) though seemingly used in a technical fashion. 

b) The list of definitions does not have xrefs to the detailed
discussion of the item

c) The constant contradictions in the document, presumably meant
to be resolved in favour of the more specific statement. You read
a statement, but somewhere else there is a qualification but
there is no cross reference.

d) The confusing use of terms eg 'IF a then S1 else S2 END-IF is
an 'imperative statement'???. Similarly the term 'identifier' is
a constant source of wonder.

e) The huge scope of the changes and additions. Eg garbage
collection is compulsory (in effect - it is the only way to free
objects) with huge implications on the performance and
responsiveness of programs (cf java).

I am doing my comments this weekend (Easter). All these issues
will be raised. However in Australia they will be vetted by an
expert before being passed on the the committee. So I don't know
what will come out the other side.

IMHO the committee is risking irrelevance by making everything
mandatory eg report writer, OO, screens (mandatory but hardware
dependent). A big part of the job of a committee is to say "NO"
and the COBOL group seems not to have done this. 

People will implement what they want to implement. 

Tim Josling
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: (US) COBOL Public Review period ends tomorrow, April 9th

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T01:24:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD10F19.5C02C88F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9aohvt$72f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3AD0CD48.E3724040@melbpc.org.au>`

```


Tim Josling wrote:

> "William M. Klein" wrote:
> >
>

All good points Tim makes. And Bill, you already know my thoughts on
this one - the bloody document is TOO technical, and by intent or
otherwise, makes it bloody difficult to comprehend, plus that cross
referencing Tim refers to.

What many others reading this may not realize, is that our J4 team
(COBOL Standards - approx. 13 'active' members), is SEVERELY constrained
by the ANSI/ISO mechanisms that lay down concrete rules how things will
be presented and expressed, e.g. use of word 'SHALL, the pair
'normative' and 'non normative' etc.

In terms of developer input - in one meal, too much, too late.

Only one I would disagree with Tim on is OO - make that optional, and
you can imagine how many vendors would implement - and look for COBOL
eventually being taught in fewer and fewer schools, to the point of
disappearance. I'm not suggesting OO is the 'saviour' - it's already too
late for that role - and the new 'product' wont happen until 2005.

Tim - just keep plugging at Tiny COBOL - we are rooting for you.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
I have to agree with Tim.

Iv'e got the document on my PC as a PDF.
I fell asleep reading the first time.
and the second time.

It appears to have been written by a bunch of lawers.
It is certainly the product of a "COMMITTEE".

Why does it all have to be released at once?
Could there not have been a release for comment once one small section 
was done? That way, it would be easier for people to respond and they 
would only have to respond to areas that concern them.

This whole process appears to be specifically designed to thwart any 
input from those upon whom it has a direct bearing - the COBOL 
programmers.
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<suiA6.537480$Pm2.8792352@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<9aohvt$72f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3AD0CD48.E3724040@melbpc.org.au> <josephk-E19FD5.20510509042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
"Joseph J Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:josephk-E19FD5.20510509042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au...

<snip>

> It appears to have been written by a bunch of lawers.
> It is certainly the product of a "COMMITTEE".
>

That is the fault of that standard standard.  The committee for aproving the
standard standard had to approve the words that could be used, and the
sentence structure that could be used first.  So it is not the commitee's
fault, but the committee committee that wrote the standard standard.

Keep in mind that the documentation standard for the standard documents had
to be standardized to the standard that documents the documentary style, in
order that the standard be standard.  That is what standards are for, after
all.  Once you understand that, the other 900 pages become much clearer.

;<(
```

```
Loved the post, Don...<G> Good stuff!

Sir Humphrey Appleby was a character in two very successful and extremely
funny UK television series called "Yes, Minister" and "Yes, Prime Minister".
He was a top level Civil Servant who spoke in Old Oxfordian Gobbledygook.
Usually in an attempt to prevent his Government Minister (Jim Hacker, who
later became Prime Minister and thus spawned the second series) from
achieving anything. Humphrey would lay down a smokescreen very close to what
you wrote...in an attempt (usually successful) to confuse and distract Jim
Hacker from the issue at hand.

The underlying theme was that Governments (Administrations, if you like)
come and go, but Civil Servants go on to Retirement and (in the UK)
Knighthoods or Lordships... The awful thing was that it was often close to
the truth. Margaret Thatcher is reputed to have been an avid fan and
apparently never missed an episode.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (US) COBOL Public Review period ends tomorrow, April 9th

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T10:06:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9asj48$514$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9aohvt$72f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3AD0CD48.E3724040@melbpc.org.au> <josephk-E19FD5.20510509042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
There are "legitimate" (IMHO) objects to the Standardization process, but
there HAVE been at least 10 working/committee draft documents available
during the development in the last 5 years.

I semi-frequently point people to the J4 "external" web page that includes
both the status and links to the most current "draft" document.

If you go back to my ORIGINAL article in www.cobolreport.com I gave some
clues about how to "review" the document - starting with the list of
"enhancement" - to the "Concepts" section and then (only if you were "strong
of heart" - or "strong of stomach" - going thru the detail.

By the way, that original article also pointed out HOW to contact individual
national standards bodies - outside the US.

The full article can still be seen at:
 http://www.cobolreport.com/columnists/bill/01222001.htm
```

#### ↳ Re: (US) COBOL Public Review period ends tomorrow, April 9th

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <dgallan1@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T03:01:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xC9A6.10632$wQ1.3276421@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<9aohvt$72f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
> 2) Many COBOL programmers/sites don't know when/if they will ever see an
> implementation with all (most? any?) of these new enhancements.  They
still
> have '74 Standard COBOL programs in production; their programmers don't
> "use" any of the new stuff

That's the biggest issue in our shop. Most programmers didn't even know
there was a 85 standard until I told them (in 1995 or 96) when wanting to
move to the 85 compiler. The bulk of code remains 74 and can't be moved to
our vendor's 85 compiler without significant modifications. (This is partly
a site-specific issue however.) I have encouraged people to use 85 when
possible and I've even forced its use for some new development but most of
the new code written would almost compile clean using a 74 compiler. When I
can't convince a lot of people that END-IF is useful (and I've seen their
code), you know you're in trouble.

> 5) "Total Apathy" about revisions to COBOL - especially what is in a
> "Standard" instead of in THEIR "implementation".
This is related to the above. We have no portability issues since the apps
are all in-house so the only thing that matters is what the vendor delivers.
Unfortunately, our vendor has chosen to implement the new draft standard
with a new compiler whose architecture (so far) makes it totally unusable in
our environment. So, even when there is a new standard, I can't use it
anyway, so absolutely why would I care. It may be a narrow vision but I
suspect most people are evaluating from that standpoint. I think those
requiring portable code should be much more aware though.

Additionally, I think other comments regarding the difficulty of even
evaluating what's in the draft because of the sheer size and complexity is
also valid. I'm not an OO guy so how could I even evaluate it rationally?
(refer to #2 above again)

Douglas Gallant

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9aohvt$72f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net...
> The US Public Review period on the "draft" COBOL Standard (FCD) ends
> tomorrow (April 9, 2001 - at the "end of business" day).
>
> As of my posting this note, there have been 38 (count them 38) public
review
> comments submitted.
>
> Of these 5 (only 5 !!!) have been from people who are NOT "members" of the
> COBOL Standards committees - either as "official observers," J4 members,
or
> WG4 delegates.
>
> Of those 5 comments, none seems to be from "real programmers" who have
even
> read any of the "summary" lists (of enhancements, incompatibilities, etc)
in
> the FCD - but instead they have done "joint" comments on the reaffirmation
> of the current Standard and for the next one "basically" saying that
"COBOL
> is important to us - please keep working on it".
>
> My guesses (and they are only guesses) of WHY there have been so few "real
> programmers" providing ANY input (good, bad, mixed, whatever) are as
follows
> (roughly in the order that I think they apply - although I suspect that it
> is actually a combination of reasons).
>
> 1) Those who care about a "next" COBOL Standard are SO concerned about how
> delayed this revision is, that they would agree to ANYTHING - just to get
it
> approved and implemented as soon as possible.
>
> 2) Many COBOL programmers/sites don't know when/if they will ever see an
> implementation with all (most? any?) of these new enhancements.  They
still
> have '74 Standard COBOL programs in production; their programmers don't
> "use" any of the new stuff - even when available; and/or it was ONLY Y2K
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> users will want to use - and that they will be defined in the BEST
possible
> way.  Therefore, there is no use for a "mere" programmer to look at this
> draft because it MUST be the best possible way for COBOL to be in the
> "future".
>
> 4) People have been hearing about the enhancements in the draft Standard
for
> years now.  From what they have seen, it is the "best thing since sliced
> bread" - and they wouldn't change a THING about it - even if they could.
>
> 5) "Total Apathy" about revisions to COBOL - especially what is in a
> "Standard" instead of in THEIR "implementation".  (This may actually be
the
> most likely reason - but I always HOPE that it isn't - no matter how many
> times I am proved wrong.)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

that
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: (US) COBOL Public Review period ends tomorrow, April 9th

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T20:43:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
There is a "pointer" to an place to down-load the current draft (in PDF
format) at:
  http://www.merant.com/Standards/

You can purchase (for $18 USD per document) for download (again in PDF form)
from:
 http://webstore.ansi.org/ansidocstore/dept.asp
```

## Thread: Anyone writing console apps using win32api ? — 38 this month / 42 total

### Anyone writing console apps using win32api ?

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-04-08T18:13:08+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Anyone out there got some examples of console usage using only the
win32api ? Ive found both Fujitsu and Merants compilers pretty lacking
in this respect. Please don't respond with just the CBL_ routines.

TIA
Alister
```

#### ↳ Re: Anyone writing console apps using win32api ?

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T12:05:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Just FYI so you don't go wasting a bunch of time, Fujitsu simply can't
in and of itself do this.   They have good support for the API with
ths singular exception that you can't register your entrypoint (No
Procedure Pointer yet).  I know that others have written C Stubs to
handle this, and call Fujitsu COBOL from this stub.  

In addition, very little documentation exists on interfacing with the
Windows API from COBOL.  Hopefully with .NET this might change, but
presently MS puts no emphasis on it.  

On Sun, 8 Apr 2001 18:13:08 +0100, "Alister Munro"
<alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>Anyone out there got some examples of console usage using only the
>win32api ? Ive found both Fujitsu and Merants compilers pretty lacking
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone writing console apps using win32api ?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-21T00:59:45+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ae03bdd_2@my.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane,

I use the Win32 API with Fujitsu COBOL regularly, although I have not tried
using the console with it, so was interested to read your post.

There is one line which I take issue with...

"They have good support for the API with this singular exception ..."

They have NO support for any of the Registry API calls because they do not
provide Import Libraries for the advapi32.dll. In fact, the ONLY API .dlls
which are supported are the Kernel and User.

As access to the Registry is a pretty fundamental requirement for serious
Windows apps, I consider this to be a regrettable omission which I hope will
be corrected in future versions of the compiler.

The official response is that if the API is invoked dynamically there is no
need for an Import Library.

However, some of these calls are so complex in their parameter passing that
I for one, have been unable to get them to work (notwithstanding they
compiled OK).  I am no slouch at this game, but despite spending many hours
with various Registry Opens, Searches,  and Updates, there are still some
Calls I have NEVER been able to get to work correctly when invoked
dynamically.

I believe that, compared to the API  support offered with Delphi
(f'instance) and VB, COBOL is VERY badly served (not just by Fujitsu, but
most COBOL vendors).

Pete.

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net>...
>Just FYI so you don't go wasting a bunch of time, Fujitsu simply can't
>in and of itself do this.   They have good support for the API with
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

```

```
display in an "About" box) which is - in other environments -a simple
API call. Ended up using a free Active-X control, and using the
control actually turned out better because the control provides more
information.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone writing console apps using win32api ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T15:33:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5FYD6.148$aC5.143337@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net> <3ae03bdd_2@my.newsfeeds.com> <5JXD6.232642$3J6.14391383@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Jerry P <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:5JXD6.232642$3J6.14391383@news2.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hmm, might it be possible to do this?

1. Get the actual EXPORTed name of the desired API call from the DLL using
something like the NT Quickview or a shareware product like PEBrowse (best
20 bucks you can spend if you need those "mangled" EXPORT names).

2. Use the LoadLibrary and GetProcAddress APIs to make the function
available to your process. Return GetProcAddress into a COBOL
PROCEDURE-POINTER datatype. Yes, you can use USER32.DLL, or GDI or KERNEL or
any other system DLL with LoadLibrary.

3.Call using whatever mechanism the particular compiler uses to CALL by
procedure-pointer (address). You may need to reverse the order of the passed
parameters for that C-language crap; I am not sure about that.

I don't know enough about the various Windows/COBOL compilers to know this
will always work, but this is a method I use for my Windows/BASIC language
programs and is probably worth a look for someone.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone writing console apps using win32api ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T22:03:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lm2E6.234781$3J6.14658297@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net> <3ae03bdd_2@my.newsfeeds.com> <5JXD6.232642$3J6.14391383@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <5FYD6.148$aC5.143337@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I opted for:

     MOVE 'MYPROG.DLL' TO 'FileName' OF MYCONTROL.

     MOVE 'ProductVersion' OF MYCONTROL TO DISPLAY-PVERSION.
     MOVE 'FileVersion' OF MYCONTROL TO DISPLAY-FVERSION.
     MOVE 'LastAccessDate' OF MYCONTROL TO DISPLAY-LADATE

     etc.

(Snap fingers here)
```

```
Jerry P <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:ekmE6.1955$8j.271466@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
> Dear 1900 (may I call you that)
>
> I don't think one can do a LoadLibrary API with Fujitsu.
>

Well, the I guess I'm down to handling myself and making the COBOL call as..

CALL 'DoTheRightThing' USING Parms

01  PARMS
     05 Libname        pic x(30).
     05 Procname      pic X(30)
     05  ParmsIn-count    PIC S9(04) USAGE BINARY.
     05 ParmsIn  Occurs 20.
          10   Parmsin-Type       PIC S9(04) USAGE BINARY (1= integer, 2=
string pointer, etc)
          10   ParmsIn-Length    PIC s9(04) USAGE BINARY (when needed)
          10   ParmsIn-Value-1   PIC S9(09) USAGE BINARY  (when integer or
pointer)
          10  ParmsIn-Value-2    REDEFINES ParmsIn-Value1
               (not sure I need this, since a think all API calls are 32-bit
values. Nah, some are 16-bit??)
    05  Parms-Result              PIC s9(09) USAGE BINARY    (what the API
function returns)
    05  Parms-Error-flag           PIC X(01).
    05  Parms-Error-code         PIC S9(04) COMP.
    05  Parms-Error-Text          PIC X(36).
    05  Parms-Out
           (repeat IN for the out). This might be redundant, but Some API
calls fill a text buffer and it might make it
           easier to use if the text comes back to the caller. But maybe
not.

Still not fully thought out yet, but you can see where this might go.

I "assume" Fujitsu CAN...

1. Call a DLL like this?  (Meaning an FJ program could call the WinAPI
itself unless you need a procedure-pointer)?
   (But even then , it would seem an FJ program could call the simple APIs
not requiring a procedure pointer, APIs  like the registry access).

(Oh, maybe... the FJ import libraries use the ordinals instead of procedure
names? Is that it?) (Nah, that's not it. Maybe there's no import library for
Loadlibrary?

Here's the header for LoadLibrary in BASIC:

DECLARE FUNCTION LoadLibrary LIB "KERNEL32.DLL" ALIAS "LoadLibraryA"
(lpLibFileName AS ASCIIZ) AS LONG
 (This is ANSI. A unicode version is implemented on NT/2000)

Can FJ use CALL xxx USING yyy RETURNING zzz  ( the return value is what is
needed here)

2. Actually obtain and pass an address (32-bit) for a working storage
dataname?
as in..
01  PARM-ADDRESS       USAGE POINTER.
01  PARM-ADDRESS-B    REDEFINES PARM-ADDRESS PIC 9(09) USAGE BINARY.

SET PARM-ADDRESS TO ADDRESS OF WS-ITEM
MOVE PARM-ADDRESS-B  TO  wherever

Comments invited...
```

```
On Sun, 22 Apr 2001 13:28:07 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
<michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:


... snipped all but the questions ...

>Still not fully thought out yet, but you can see where this might go.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Correct.  Fujitsu COBOL can do this.

>Can FJ use CALL xxx USING yyy RETURNING zzz  ( the return value is what is
>needed here)

Yes.

>
>2. Actually obtain and pass an address (32-bit) for a working storage
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>MOVE PARM-ADDRESS-B  TO  wherever

Yes.

You have to understand the API and whe it is asking for an address and
when it wants you to oass the actual pointer value.  You don't need to
do the redefines you can just reference the pointer.

There's really NOTHING that it can't call in the API EXCEPT things
requiring a procedure pointer.
```

```
Exactly.

Unless I have been confused, I thought the underlying question was:

" Using Fujitsu COBOL, how can I obtain a procedure pointer for a procedure
written using FJC, such procedure pointer being suitable for use as a
Windows callback function (e.g., various Enumxxx functions and procedures
used to subclass windows/controls), and in a form which can be passed to the
Windows API during the setup of that callback?"

The question may not have been phrased that way, but it is what I understood
to be the "basic science" required to solve the problem.

MCM
```

```
Thanks Michael.

This certainly looks feasible.

I guess what irritates is that, having spent money on the COBOL compiler, it
is then necessary to buy another product (Windows SDK or the software you
mention) in order to really use the API. This compares very unfavourably
with environments like VB and Delphi where full support for API calls is
included.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone writing console apps using win32api ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-21T13:39:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r4gE6.69$ag2.49768@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net> <3ae03bdd_2@my.newsfeeds.com> <5JXD6.232642$3J6.14391383@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <5FYD6.148$aC5.143337@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3ae0dbdb_3@my.newsfeeds.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> wrote in message
news:3ae0dbdb_3@my.newsfeeds.com...
> Thanks Michael.
>
> This certainly looks feasible.
>
> I guess what irritates is that, having spent money on the COBOL compiler,
it
> is then necessary to buy another product (Windows SDK or the software you
> mention) in order to really use the API. This compares very unfavourably
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

But you get what you pay for: When you use VB or Delphi or many of the
"easy-to-use" language products, a lot of "routine" programming becomes very
straightforward (read, "easy").  The cost is that doing something "other
than OUR WAY" becomes difficult. It appears Fujitsu has not supplied a real
good platform for extensive API calls, since a lot of the API calls require
a procedure-pointer.

For API reference, I have a "library" subscription to MSDN which cost me
$207 the first year and $114 for this current year. You get all the API
reference on CD. MS also make this available on-line for free, but with a
single phone line and a dialup connection, just browsing the list of
available calls is a pain.

MSDN offers two additional levels of subcription, but those include copies
of new operating systems, driver develpment kits and a whole bunch of stuff
I don't need, so I just went with the 'entry-level' subscription.

I am one of the most vocal people I know re praising the way MS has
implemented the Windows API. There are functions and functions to do damn
near anything, quickly and without bugs and the reference documentation is
well done. The MSDN includes a lot of code samples, but those are not of
much use to me, as they are all written in Microsoft C++, Microsoft Visual
BASIC, Microsoft FoxPro and  Microsoft whatever. (And all the Internet
examples assume you use Microsoft Internet Explorer as your browser).

MCM





MCM
```

```
Michael Mattias wrote:

> Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> wrote in message

Couple of comments Michael.

I appreciate your problem with just one phone. Same with me, sign up for about
$9.95 a month, but when you start downloading updates - suddenly you find your
plastic has a charge for $68  for the additional time ! It's you choice of
course, and subject to availability. I went direct cable connection - $40 per
month FIXED and I can spend 40 hours a day whistling around the Net if I so
choose. I don't do that, confining myself to topics 'COBOL'. It's another
approach.

Surprised where you listed Visual Basic as an 'alien' along with the others. Is
it really foreign to what you are used to do doing with basic BASIC ?

For Pete - SDK came as a freebie with Net Express, (can't remember, may have
been a freebie with VISOC). The other side of the coin, as you have mentioned,
you got CRYSTAL as a freebie with Fujitsu. My actual use of the SDK ? Looked to
find how to handle ENTER key instead of TAB key for dialogs. Three examples, all
in C, and as Michael says, you've then got to unravel the problem back into
COBOL syntax.

He's not messaging at the moment - but Ken Mullins in Atlanta, is certainly
using Win API calls from Net Express - although, as I recall, he avoids Win
printer calls, opting instead for PC_PRINT.

Jimmy

>
> I am one of the most vocal people I know re praising the way MS has
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
> hell why not just say 300 ! (Let compilers be 'developer friendly' NOT
> 'machine fireindly').

In what way is '200' or '300' more 'developer friendly' than '255' ?  If
you want 301 they are all unfriendly.  I can remember numbers that don't
have zeroes in them, or aren't denary, quite well, and denary numbers
with zeroes aren't always an appropriate size.

I can still remember 8,388,607 and I haven't even worked on those
machines for 25 years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone writing console apps using win32api ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-22T12:24:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<44AE6.64$Fv5.40037@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net> <3ae03bdd_2@my.newsfeeds.com> <5JXD6.232642$3J6.14391383@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <5FYD6.148$aC5.143337@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3ae0dbdb_3@my.newsfeeds.com> <r4gE6.69$ag2.49768@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3AE1C98F.B0B7AFF2@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3AE1C98F.B0B7AFF2@home.com...
>
> Surprised where you listed Visual Basic as an 'alien' along with the
others. Is
> it really foreign to what you are used to do doing with basic BASIC ?
>

The "basic" BASIC I use is, yes, very basic; but it was _designed_ to allow
the programmer full access to the Microsoft Win32 API (it provides code
pointers, thread-safe re-entrancy, etc.) whereas MSVB hides a lot of the API
interface and reserves things for its own use.

MSVB, for example, does not natively allow subclassing of controls without
the use of a third-party tool; and I think we all know that to run an MSVB
applicaiton you need about 1.4Mb of run-time support, a bunch of registry
entries, etc etc etc.

Given that, if you need to do a one-screen data entry with lots of fields,
you can do it a LOT faster in VB.

Just to show that eveything has a price: PowerBASIC recently added something
called "Dynamic Dialog Tools" to the PB/Windows Compiler. This is a set of
language verbs which allow the creation of dialog boxes from within the
program and no longer require a separate resource file. But... any dialog
created with DDT (and its controls) reserve the use of the "windowextra"
field (called "dialogextra" when its a dialog box) and so the programmer may
no longer use it.

MCM
```

- **References:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net> <3ae03bdd_2@my.newsfeeds.com> <5JXD6.232642$3J6.14391383@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <5FYD6.148$aC5.143337@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3ae0e222.45545357@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:3ae0e222.45545357@news1.attglobal.net...
> On Fri, 20 Apr 2001 15:33:22 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
> <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >PROCEDURE-POINTER datatype. Yes, you can use USER32.DLL, or GDI or KERNEL
or
> >any other system DLL with LoadLibrary.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, phooey. Oh, well,maybe someone got an idea from it anyway.

Can Fujitsu call a DLL which might be called like this?

01  Target-Address PIC 9(9) USAGE BINARY.
01  LibName          PIC X(20)
01  ProcName        PIC X(30)

CALL "CallByAddress"  USING   TargetAddress, parm-1, parm-2, parm-3....

or maybe something like

CALL "CallWin32API" using  LibName, ProcName, parm1, parm2..parm3.....

If FJ can do this, it might be fun for me to write a DLL which serves as a
"service layer" and my DLL would actually do the API call and return the
results.

I'd need to think a little bit more about the parameter list and how to
handle this, but I KNOW my compiler (PowerBASIC PB/DLL) can handle the
call-by-address and variable number of parameters part of it, and I know I
can handle the coding to pass and return the values. (Of course, I tend to
believe I can code anything).

If anyone is interested in this, post here for now. If there's enough
interest, I can take the discussion off-line.
```

```
With the new OOP facilities, we should be able to create a low level object,
compatable across Cobol product lines, with various attributes and methods.
For example, one attribute could be "OS-COMMAND-LINE", and a method
"EXECUTE-COMMAND-LINE.

The systex to use it would be:

    MOVE "MY-PROGRAM-NAME MY-FILE-1 MY-SWITCHES"             TO COMMAND-LINE
OF SYSTEM-OBJECT.
    INVOKE SYSTEM-OBJECT "Execute".

regardless of platform.
```

```
Yes, Fujitsu can to something LIKE this for AIPs where no .LIB is
provided.  It's two calls.  

The only thing it can't do is pass a procedure pointer to itself to
the API - as needed in the CreateWindow API call.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone writing console apps using win32api ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-28T14:09:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5aAG6.310$555.82410@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net> <3ae03bdd_2@my.newsfeeds.com> <5JXD6.232642$3J6.14391383@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <5FYD6.148$aC5.143337@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3ae0e222.45545357@news1.attglobal.net> <s4gE6.70$ag2.48414@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3aeabdbb.125593848@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Well,OK, so how about we think of this..

Supposin' you have an API you want to use which requires a procedure-pointer
to a procedure you create. So create your procedure such that it has its own
exported entry point (MAIN, ENTRY or nested/multiple program) .

Then call LoadLibrary to load the current module, Use GetProcAddress to get
the entry point address and go from there?  Since there is only one copy of
any executing module in a process space, the procedure pointer should be
valid regardless of the module handle used to get it, right?

You may need to find the "mangled" export name of your COBOL entry point,
but that's not all that hard using something like PEBrowse ($20.00 shareware
I recently discovered. A steal for my purposes). (Or maybe the format used
for exported names is documented by FJ?)

Does this sound possible? Or does FJ export all its entry points such that
everything enters the executing code run module on a path which passes
THROUGH a run-time module? Or if there is a runtime, does this screw up
everything? (Does FJ even use a runtime?)

This is one of those little challenges which fascinate me. If someone wants
to create an FJ executable with ENTRY and Nested/Multiple programs, and/or a
DLL with some exported functions, you can ZIP it up and send it to me and
I'll dissect it. Please include the PROGRAM-ID, ENTRY and any declarations
sections (is this where you you declare exports?) of the source code. (I do
not need the whole source code, but if you send it I'll extract what I'm
looking for).

I DO NOT HAVE FJ COBOL installed here, so you need to compile the
*.EXE/*.DLL files!
```

```
>but that's not all that hard using something like PEBrowse ($20.00 shareware
>I recently discovered. A steal for my purposes). (Or maybe the format used

Dunno - but it can be found looking in the .LIB file created. 

>Does this sound possible? Or does FJ export all its entry points such that
>everything enters the executing code run module on a path which passes
>THROUGH a run-time module? Or if there is a runtime, does this screw up
>everything? (Does FJ even use a runtime?)
>

Fujitsu uses a runtime, but it's so closely linked to the windows C++
runtime that this doesn't mess things up.

>This is one of those little challenges which fascinate me. If someone wants
>to create an FJ executable with ENTRY and Nested/Multiple programs, and/or a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You'll still need the runtime.  Also, if calling from C, you use a
special Link Library when creating the Fujitsu DLL.
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1B1H6.719$Rn1.420839@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net> <3ae03bdd_2@my.newsfeeds.com> <5JXD6.232642$3J6.14391383@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <5FYD6.148$aC5.143337@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3ae0e222.45545357@news1.attglobal.net> <s4gE6.70$ag2.48414@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3aeabdbb.125593848@news1.attglobal.net> <5aAG6.310$555.82410@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3aec1371.213008963@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:3aec1371.213008963@news1.attglobal.net...
> On Sat, 28 Apr 2001 14:09:05 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
> <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>

> >You may need to find the "mangled" export name of your COBOL entry point,
> >but that's not all that hard using something like PEBrowse ($20.00
shareware
> >I recently discovered. A steal for my purposes). (Or maybe the format
used
> >for exported names is documented by FJ?)
> >
>
> Dunno - but it can be found looking in the .LIB file created.

VALUABLE INFO!!!

>
> >Does this sound possible? Or does FJ export all its entry points such
that
> >everything enters the executing code run module on a path which passes
> >THROUGH a run-time module? Or if there is a runtime, does this screw up
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> runtime that this doesn't mess things up.

Windows does not use a "C++" run-time. The Windows API is a fully-documented
programming interface which is language-neutral. The C++ runtimes (files
"MFCnn.DLL" ) are the run-time(*) for applications written using Microsoft
Visual C++. (*. When first typed this, I made a mistake and typed
"ruin-time." Maybe I should have left it that way?).

> >I DO NOT HAVE FJ COBOL installed here, so you need to compile the
> >*.EXE/*.DLL files!
> >
>
> You'll still need the runtime.


No, I don't - not yet, anyway, because I am not running anything. All I am
doing is finding the entry point for the FJ procedures which the programmer
wants to use for a callback. (See reply to Mr Sunley).

> Also, if calling from C, you use a special Link Library when creating the
Fujitsu DLL.

I don't write in C.

Besides, only wimps use a LIB file and static linking. Real Men use
LoadLibrary/GetProcAddress and do everything dynamically.
```

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca

```
Thanks all for coming back with answers ?.

I am looking to convert my DOS/UNIX code into Win32. I written my
own accept, display, window, and screen panel routines in the hope that
this would allow me to swap to other compilers / languages. I wish to
keep writing character apps as end users (the people who key in the
data) prefer these to the gui's and as such now need to fully control the
console windows via the win32api's. If anyone could suggest some
decent reference material (not MSDN) I would be willing to share code
with this group on my findings. I need some examples to get me started :)

Oh well, better get my VAT return finshed....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T22:24:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ad23520.22891416@news>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-5z3YZnBhjjd4@tcpserver> <EE7EDD49828C5E0E.5D57EC5C8537D0B1.5E539EB0FCEF84D5@lp.airnews.net> <yToA6.1179$Bz.44585@news2.atl> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
On Mon, 09 Apr 2001 21:52:49 GMT, "Donald Tees"
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>We are going to run out of oil eventually, regardless of whether we destroy
>the planet first. 
==========================
Technically, yes, but in reality we won't run out of oil, it'll simply
become too expensive, relative to alternative sources of energy.
That's what happened around a century ago when petroleum became less
expensive than whale oil for lighting (because the whalers pretty well
wiped out the whales.)

That's also why we upholster with vinyl instead of leather; it's
cheaper.  It's true of all kinds of stuff.

The real problem (I guess) is that petroleum is the cheapest source of
energy around.  One of these days, when there isn't so much around,
something else solar/wind/whatever will supersede it.  

Human nature being what it is, we're gonna keep burning oil until it
becomes too expensive.  Regardless of the good intentions of those who
can see what's probably going to happen, politicians - and ultimately
those that elect 'em - will never, ever have the resolve to conserve
if it crimps their lifestyle.  

Shucks, even Al Gore burned a couple zillion gallons of fuel tooling
around in Air Force Two while he was Veep.  I know lots of greenies
who fly all over the world (burning oil) to enjoy nature whilst
wearing synthetic clothing and paddling plastic kayaks.  Conservation
is fine as long as it's the other guy making the sacrifices..

On a related subject, a friend once told me what the REAL difference
was between an environmentalist and a developer.

"The environmentalist already HAS his/her home in the woods."

Cynical, perhaps, but often too true...

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T08:43:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-5z3YZnBhjjd4@tcpserver> <EE7EDD49828C5E0E.5D57EC5C8537D0B1.5E539EB0FCEF84D5@lp.airnews.net> <yToA6.1179$Bz.44585@news2.atl> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news>`

```
"Kevin J. Hansen" <Support@ScreenIO.com> wrote:
>
> On a related subject, a friend once told me what the REAL difference
> was between an environmentalist and a developer.
>
> "The environmentalist already HAS his/her home in the woods."

The first step to understanding rabid environmentalism is to realize
that, at heart, it is not about conservation, but about elitism.  My
daughter and her family took a scenic tour a couple of weeks ago.
Before they left, they checked the official web site for Yellowstone
Park, which said the park was open, though some roads were closed.
After driving 2700 miles, they were told at the gate "The bears are
coming out of hibernation and you can't drive through; it's too
dangerous.  But if you were bicycling, we would let you through."
Think about it.  Dangerous for whom?  Wonder how many bears have been
attacked by tourists in Yellowstone? ;-)

In a country that was founded on the principle of private ownership
of real property, the government controls over 40% of the land.  The
average amount of land that has been set aside for wilderness in each
state is over 25%.  Any moron should be able to see that nature has
hardly been squeezed out in the U.S.  If you think so, just take a
tour through Colorado, Utah, New Mexico, Arizona, Montana, Wyoming,
Nevada, etc.  There are valleys out there where you could lose New
York city.  There are places where it is over 100 miles between
service stations.  On I-70 west from Colorado through Utah, you get
gas in the last town in Colorado, then drive 100 miles to the first
service station, then drive another 100 miles to the next service
station near I-15.  Or get in an airplane and fly over anywhere in
the Southeast U.S.  From the air, it is difficult to spot evidence of
man, other than a few cities and major highways.  For the most part,
it looks like you're flying over a forest.  I live just a few miles
(2 or 3) outside the Birmingham, Alabama city limit, and my wife ran
into a deer a couple of weeks ago that jumped in front of her car
less than a quarter mile from our house.  Fortunately, my wife was
not going very fast, and she was okay, but it broke the poor deer's
back legs, and we had to put it down (not a pleasant job).  My aunt,
who lives in south Alabama, has hit two in the last couple of years.
We have an over-population of deer in Alabama right now.  Yep, we're
just crowding the 'natural' world right out of existence! ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T08:12:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bLYA6.2259$6R5.141914@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl> <MaEA6.346$DW1.22421@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
" NA" <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> mere rhetoric.

This is true.  I was only trying to differentiate between reasonable
environmentalists and the extremists.  I consider myself a reasonable
environmentalist.  For example, I don't want to see those 2,000 year
old sequoia and redwood trees cut down, but I don't weep over a dead
tree as if it were a human being.  I want to see companies stop
dumping toxic waste into our water and air, but I don't want to see
industry destroyed in doing so.  I want to see natural resources used
as we need them, but strip mining operations (all of them) should
have to clean up their messes.  Life is not without risks, it never
was without risks, and it never will be without risks.  Just look at
what 'rabid' ('extreme', if you prefer) environmentalists have done
to California's power system.  Do the morons think we can continue
forever without building any new power plants?  Do they sit in a dark
stone hut every night, with no TV or computer or telephone?  Though
impossible, of course, what I would love to see, and what would be
just, is for every person in California who has opposed building new
power plants to have their power shut off, so they could see what it
means not to build power plants.  Obviously, they are too obtuse to
make the logical connection any other way.  And the problem certainly
isn't deregulation, because other states have deregulated with great
success, but they have built new power plants.  Fortunately for
California, because their excess capacity is coming from states who
have had the common sense to build more power plants.

Everybody shudders at the possible consequences of Three Mile Island,
while ignoring the vast number of people who have died in the oil and
coal industry.  There have been a large number of oil field disasters
resulting in thousands upon thousands of deaths, human deaths, not
fish or birds.  Not to mention the thousands of coal miners who have
died from Black Lung disease.  Earlier this century, a ship full of
fertilizer blew up in (Galveston, TX, IIIRC) killing thousands.  We
learn our hard-won lessons and move on.  But now, all of a sudden, we
seem to have become a society of outright cowards, so afraid of risk
that we want to stick our heads in the sand and act as if risk will
go away if we just don't do anything.  Where the #$%&*#% do you think
we would be now, if our ancestors had taken that attitude?  Sitting
in dark caves eating raw meat?  Probably.  Have you ever stopped to
calculate how many people have died from hurricanes, simply because
they wanted a nice ocean view?  Or drowned in floods because they
wanted to live near a river?  If you want to protect people from
needless risk, make it illegal to build a house near the ocean or in
a river floodplain.  Having power and raw materials for a comfortable
technological society is far more important to society than having a
nice view, is it not? ;-)
```

```
>I consider myself a reasonable

Most folks I know consider themselves to be 'reasonable'... even the

>Just look at
>what 'rabid' ('extreme', if you prefer) environmentalists have done
>to California's power system.  Do the morons think we can continue
>forever without building any new power plants?

by the power companies themselves, don't you?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** torek@elf.bsdi.com (Chris Torek)
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T21:09:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<9br15e$2cr$1@elf.bsdi.com>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <3AD46C61.7BDF4442@brazee.net> <JJ_A6.543$DW1.30000@iad-read.news.verio.net> <66011AD679519BB3.7076B8EDDA97316A.C42595EDCBD4A13F@lp.airnews.net>`

```
>On Wed, 11 Apr 2001 15:27:37 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net (  NA)
>enlightened us:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>DD

In article <66011AD679519BB3.7076B8EDDA97316A.C42595EDCBD4A13F@lp.airnews.net>
SkippyPB  <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote:
>The best write up I've seen that gives a good explanation of how
>California has arrived at its current energy crisis can be found at:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>brokers in California.

I am not sure who you are calling "the 3 major power brokers in
California" -- generating plants are owned by a rather diverse set
of companies, such as Duke, Calpine, Southern Energy LLC, Reliant,
Dynergy, and so on.  The only "3" I can think of are the utility
distribution companies: PG&E, SCE, and SDG&E.  The UDCs' parent
companies do still own some generation, often in the other UDCs'
territory.

As for power companies opposing other power companies, see, e.g.,
http://dailynews.yahoo.com/h/ap/20010419/us/power_new_plant_3.html
which mentions -- although not by name -- that Sempra opposed PG&E
Generating's bid to build a natural-gas fired plant in Otay Mesa;
(this plant has finally been approved).

>If so, then I could understand they would not
>want to see their profits eroded.  Otherwise, I'm at a loss.

I have a some writeups on some of the issues that I plan to put up
on my web pages eventually.

Basically, the trick amounts to this: if supply is tight, everyone
who has some will have theirs bought at virtually any price.  If
you build a modern, efficient plant, you can sell its output at
the same price -- or on lower-load days, just below the price --
commanded by an old, inefficient plant.  The modern, efficient
plant costs less than half as much to run as the old one.  The old
one is of course fully justified in charging exorbitant rates,
because they have to pay a great deal just for the fuel to run that
plant.  With wholesale rates like $300/MWh (.30/kWh), a 500 MW
plant run for 10 hours produces $1.5 million in revenue.  Subtracting
operating costs leaves the modern plant with a profit of over
$750000 every ten hours (vs some tiny profit at the old plant).

As soon as the new plant goes up, of course, the old plant stops
being able to sell at $300/MWh on low-load days.  But it costs
them almost $300/MWh just to run, so they stop being able to make
any money at all.  Hence, the old-plant-owner is very much against
having the new plant go up (and put them out of business).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-23T22:52:04-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fhq9et4enpaqmdfk1apiqc4ppiv8f469ti@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <3AD46C61.7BDF4442@brazee.net> <JJ_A6.543$DW1.30000@iad-read.news.verio.net> <66011AD679519BB3.7076B8EDDA97316A.C42595EDCBD4A13F@lp.airnews.net> <9br15e$2cr$1@elf.bsdi.com>`

```
torek@elf.bsdi.com (Chris Torek) wrote:


>As soon as the new plant goes up, of course, the old plant stops
>being able to sell at $300/MWh on low-load days.  But it costs
>them almost $300/MWh just to run, so they stop being able to make
>any money at all.  Hence, the old-plant-owner is very much against
>having the new plant go up (and put them out of business).

since this is economics, there are lots of ways of looking at it. the
older plant, if it gets more competition, might be put out of
business. on the other hand, if the new plant costs more to run
because of lack of experience, the investor loses their shirt. now,
add in that there might be a fixed demand for electricity. what i mean
by fixed demand is, even if a consumer could buy electricity 100 times
cheaper, the related costs in society may keep them from doing so.
they may have the ability to pay for the electricity for a search
light instead of a 100 watt light bulb, but the search light might be
$1000, where the light bulb is $1. even if you make the electric bill
$100 cheaper, the $900 doesn't appear.

also, if there is a fixed demand, a startup business would only be
able to make less per watt than the current suppliers. now, if the
technicians want to switch plants, they would want more money, not
less. and you can't exactly hire inexperienced techs to build a new
plant.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-25T03:24:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KrrF6.283288$3J6.19131595@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <bLYA6.2259$6R5.141914@news1.atl> <c3ZA6.509$DW1.29477@iad-read.news.verio.net> <cvZA6.2286$6R5.145401@news1.atl> <zLZA6.523$DW1.29507@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

" NA" <docdwarf@clark.net>
>
> I also think that by the way you addressed my question your answer
is 'no,
> I was unaware of the fact that building new plants in Claifornia has
been
> opposed by the power companies themselves'; now that you have been
made
> aware of this it might be that your opinion will change.

And he is still unaware that the reason no new power plants have been
built in California in the last ten years was because the power
companies opposed the construction. As am I. As is almost everyone
else on the planet including the power companies, the regulatory
agencies, the consuming public, the environmental groups, the
executive office of the state, and the legislature.

This absurd principle defies logic, common sense, facts on the ground,
and basic business practice.

No business would cut itself off from a supply of raw materials; if it
has nothing to sell, it can't make a profit. Second, the power
companies in California do not own power the power generating stations
(with a few exceptions for hydro-electric facilities). It would be to
their interest to encourage power plant construction - more
competition means lower prices. Further, power in San Diego comes from
a municipally-owned power company; they surely cannot be lobbying
against themselves.

No, the notion that the lack of new power plants is the goal of the
power companies does not pass the giggle test.
```
