###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T07:44:14-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD45FAD.ED351921@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl> <MaEA6.346$DW1.22421@iad-read.news.verio.net> <bLYA6.2259$6R5.141914@news1.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> Everybody shudders at the possible consequences of Three Mile Island,
> while ignoring the vast number of people who have died in the oil and
> coal industry.

The biggest disasters have been from hydroelectric power.  This can be measured
by human lives, or by permanent ecological change.

But advocates of a single source of power are taking gambles too.  Right now,
gas fired power plants are the easiest to get built.  But if everybody uses gas,
then everybody is dependent on the supply of that one resource.  And will pay as
gas prices rise.   National security should encourage diversity, and discourage
dependence upon one commodity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T09:05:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5xZA6.2289$6R5.145430@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl> <MaEA6.346$DW1.22421@iad-read.news.verio.net> <bLYA6.2259$6R5.141914@news1.atl> <3AD45FAD.ED351921@brazee.net>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> But advocates of a single source of power are taking gambles too.  Right now,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> dependence upon one commodity.

I completely agree.  For a number of practical and strategic reasons,
it is not a good idea to put all your eggs in one basket.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-12T02:23:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD5115B.915F170F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl> <MaEA6.346$DW1.22421@iad-read.news.verio.net> <bLYA6.2259$6R5.141914@news1.atl> <3AD48697.2C881EEB@home.com> <yJ%A6.2416$6R5.152429@news1.atl> <3AD48FED.1D01C37E@home.com> <mE1B6.3412$FY5.231319@www.newsranger.com>`

```


John H Sleight Jr wrote:

> This is the 4th thread "contaminated by environmental BS, and they seem to go on
> forever. Why don't you start an ENVIRONMENTAL WHACKO forum and leave this one
> for <gasp> COBOL.
>

Always pleased to oblige - and wont be quite as direct as Don <G>. So back to "Is COBOL
Dying ....?"  Answer - YES - and it DEPRESSES the hell out of me !  So forgive any
levity, but depression results from thinking about something seriously,  and a medicinal
cure to 'down in the dumps' is the occasional foray into levity.

Watch though : There's a homework assignment for you at the end of this !

Why do I think it's dying. A combination of factors, paraphrased :-

1 - Horrendous prices of compilers whether or not you are talking mainframes or PCs
(Mainframe example - knew of one outfit that used DEC DIBOL because they couldn't
afford/justify buying DEC COBOL. Others here have referred to using earlier versions
because they couldn't justify the cost of upgrades).

2 - Additional irritant of release keys and runtime fees for certain of the PC
compilers.

3 - Tech support can be a real 'come  on'; nicely 'graded' to bring in additional
dollars

4 - Portability - Three Java vendors, Sun, Microsoft and HP. Apparently you *can* do it
Java 'vanilla' if you watch out for the 'gotchas' in Microsoft and HP. (Oh yes - and I
have a copy of Sun, not yet out of the sleeve - but it's a freebie !).

So to COBOL, quick count :-

 IBM, Unisys, DEC (or are they now defunct ?), Liant (Ryan McFarland), Merant (Micro
Focus), AcuCOBOL, Fujitsu, Hitachi, Siemens (not the Fujitsu bit), ICL (or is that now
defunct as part of Fujitsu family) - there's obviously a few more but they don't 'jump'
at me right now.

They are all portable. Right ! If perhaps you use COBOL 74 - no big deal  it is only
TWENTY SEVEN years outmoded, even if it does the job)

5 - Certainly PC-wise the vendors can't make a decent buck, and that was said over a
year ago by Bill Klein.. (Check Merant's third quarter results)

6 - Again, over a year ago Bill speculated that not all vendors will implement all
aspects of the new standard - he has *his* contacts

7 - It aint being taught. Yes, I know people can give me quotes it's happening at the
college level, but without question, for very wrong reasons, there are a large body of
academics who see that they should drop COBOL in favour of the new stuff.

7 - A new standard document that Pete points out is some 900 pages - unreadable,
unintelligible, un- about everything...

8 - The above document is written in gobbledygook - Bill points out - it is a
'technical' document - not something really intended for us serfs to read/understand.

9. Although a mixture of independent developers and vendors - the Standards Committee is
woefully lacking in support from ALL COBOL vendors - some appear on the list as
'observers'. As an individual it is bloody expensive to be a member.

Chuck - Now I'm playing dirty pool - one of the venerable names from COBOL's early days
Univac aka Unisys have only just returned to the Standards Committee after an absence of
??????  I'd love to know why they quit in the first place.

10 - The standard has an obsession with staying neutral flavoured, don't even allow a
hint at any operating system - hell we're talking about the guys on the big machines,
right ? Bear in mind the writer of the article, that this thread refers to, makes the
point that of of those (Gartner survey), 3 million programmers (OK let's be conservative
- call it 2 million ), 90% is done on mainframes anyway.

I'd query that one slightly - possibly 80% mainframe the other Unix/PCs. Question -
should us PC'ers go it alone and push what Tim Josling is doing with Tiny COBOL ? And as
regards you mainframers sing, "Balls to Mr. Banglestein....."

11 - Back to portability - the PC versions certainly have 'extensions', making it
difficult to switch from one to another. You don't on mainframes (ha, ha), you just use
nice names like JCL control, LE and God knows what else. As Bill pointed out recently in
the thread "COBOL files v DBs" - he got 'culture shock' when he switched from mainframes
to PCs. (I haven't programmed for mainframes - but get the firm impression much of your
activity is controlled via JCL. That is not the case with PCs - almost everything is
inter-active and must flow automatically).

12. Mandatory Standards being introduced - logic, if you make it optional, it wont
happen. (But back to # 6 above - will all vendors implement even if the Committee still
says 'Mandatory'). Currently mandatory is Screen Section, (sorry Judson, wait for it).
This is Stoneage stuff in the Twenty-First century. Makes a useful learning tool for
newbies - but really has no relevance to the inter-activity required with GUIs - to
COMPETE with the other languages.

Also Report Writer is now mandatory. Guess how long this jazz has been going on ? 10
years, coupled with the new VALIDATE verb and the Japanese getting shafted by still no
firming up on using their character sets. This is an inter-twined trio.

13. If readers are really observant - they will note I haven't even made any reference
to OO. (Just let's say it started 10 years ago - and it still aint finished).

14. The standards machinery or 'machinations' would be a more appropriate word - a
mechanism designed to ensure things crawl at a snail's pace. OK so we had COBOL 97
giving us Intrinsic Functions - but it really will be a timescale from 1985 - 2005.
Nothing to do with COBOLers, but a bureaucratic SNAFU 'technically' makes our current
COBOL 'dead' - see Reaffirmation for details.

15 - Last and perhaps most IMPORTANT. Of those 2-3 million COBOL programmers maybe
there's 500 who care. The rest couldn't give a s-----, showing absolute indifference to
what's coming down the pipe - on something which affects their livelihood.

I could go on, and on. Howz those apples grab you ?.

Now to your homework assignment :-
-------------------------------------
1 - Are you familiar with the Standards organization, ANSI, ISO, J4, NCITS, WG4 and
where each fits into the puzzle.

2 - Do you know the procedure for making submissions - Judson does - and the result left
him with a very bad taste.

3 - Have you kept yourself briefed on what's going down by visiting the Standard's sites
- no clues - you go find them

4 - Have you read the latest Standard - if not, why not ?

5 - Everything in the future standard OK by you. If not - back to # 4

6 - When will your vendor of choice embrace/release a new compiler that complies with
the standard, whole or bits,  and what possible vendor allowed 'options/implementations'
will they inroduce as 'gotchas' to tie your code into their very latest.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Judson,

This guy's so touchy I didn't submit my comments in response to you writing  the East
Indians should be thankful to their British masters. Having penned a thesis - to hell
with it - publish and be damned - look under thread 'OT: British and India' when I send
<G>

Rick's lurking there in the background so I'll title it "OT - British and India' <G>
```

```
It makes us appreciate Sun's arguments to own Java.  Design a language upgrade by committee
and you will come up with something which looks as though it was designed by committee.
There are good ideas - but the focus or direction is gone.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-12T15:34:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9b5ah9$ig$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl> <MaEA6.346$DW1.22421@iad-read.news.verio.net> <bLYA6.2259$6R5.141914@news1.atl> <3AD48697.2C881EEB@home.com> <yJ%A6.2416$6R5.152429@news1.atl> <3AD48FED.1D01C37E@home.com> <mE1B6.3412$FY5.231319@www.newsranger.com> <3AD5115B.915F170F@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3AD5115B.915F170F@home.com...

<<Chuck - Now I'm playing dirty pool - one of the venerable names from
COBOL's early days Univac aka Unisys have only just returned to the
Standards Committee after an absence of ??????  I'd love to know why they
quit in the first place.>>

I have responded privately to Mr. Gavan with a more extensive history of the
events of May through October 2000.  Herewith a shorter version, *speaking
only for myself based on my observations*:

Unisys did not "quit".

Unisys' membership in J4 was placed in jeopardy for spotty attendance
effective at the end of Meeting #224 (30 March 2000).   The role within the
company of our primary representative on the committee had changed, and he
was unable to attend meetings as regularly as he once did.   In addition,
our designated alternate was unable to attend either of the previous
meetings.

Neither Unisys representative was able to attend Meeting #225 in England
(17-25 May 2000), at the end of which meeting Unisys' membership was lost.

Unisys petitioned for reinstatement at the beginning of Meeting #226 (23
July 2000).

Unisys was reinstated effective at the beginning of Meeting #227 (2 October
2000).   I personally attended Meetings #228 and 229, and am preparing to
attend #230.  Barring any illness or other unforeseen circumstance I expect
to attending *all* of *every* J4 and J4-TAG meeting for the foreseeable
future as the primary J4 representative (unless the Unisys alternate has a
particular desire to do so, in which event I will accede to his wishes!).

I would also like to call attention to the fact that Burroughs Corporation
had at least as strong a committment to the COBOL community, to the
standards, and to the ongoing development of COBOL over the decades as
Sperry Univac did.   It is not appropriate to ignore that contribution and
presume that Unisys is really, and only, Univac with a different name.
Just ain't so.

Yes, for around four months of last year, out of several decades of
continuous and dedicated work on the part of numerous Sperry Univac,
Burroughs and Unisys employees representing the company on J4 and its
predecessors, Unisys was unable to participate as an active member.   But
during those four months considerable work was done to ensure that Unisys
would be able not only to return to participation as quickly as possible but
also to *continue* to participate.

The fact that the company petitioned for reinstatement at the meeting
immediately following the loss of its membership should, I believe, indicate
that Unisys did not "quit" as you assert.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```


Chuck Stevens wrote:

> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
> news:3AD5115B.915F170F@home.com...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Unisys did not "quit".

Touched a raw nerve have I ? From recollection I note some of your other
responses have tended to be in the same category. Perhaps if I had written :-

"......COBOL's early days Univac aka Unisys have only just returned to the
Standards Committee after an absence of ??????  I'd love to know why they
WERE ABSENTEES  in the first place"

it wouldn't have got your dander up.

As you say, you wrote to me privately, essentially the same as your message here
- to which I replied, and said I was satisfied. Now I jump into clc and see a
repeat. - ONE message would have sufficed !

A simple, 'You misconstrued our absence. It was due to an administrative SNAFU.
We are again participants and we are 100% behind COBOL". Topic finished.

As you felt *compelled* to reply here in great detail, how about something more
constructive with regard to the various points I raised, e.g.  :-

(a) You are unduly pessimistic, COBOL is on the right course and will thrive

(b) You are way off the ball on the following observations :-
            (i)...........
            (ii)......

(c) J4 CD 1.10 will definitely make it to the best seller list, and is perfectly
readable

(d) Vendors WILL implement the whole standard, including OO (well, at least
Unisys will....)

(e) There is absolutely nothing, but nothing, wrong with the ANSI/ISO machinery

(f) Be assured, in future we wont have 15- 20 years between each new COBOL
version.

Take your pick - and give some assurances where you can. Please don't wrap
answers in Orwellian double-speak. I'm not interested. Plain words please.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-13T15:10:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9b7tge$lk4$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl> <MaEA6.346$DW1.22421@iad-read.news.verio.net> <bLYA6.2259$6R5.141914@news1.atl> <3AD48697.2C881EEB@home.com> <yJ%A6.2416$6R5.152429@news1.atl> <3AD48FED.1D01C37E@home.com> <mE1B6.3412$FY5.231319@www.newsranger.com> <3AD5115B.915F170F@home.com> <9b5ah9$ig$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AD643CA.7FD1360C@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3AD643CA.7FD1360C@home.com...

<<Touched a raw nerve have I ? From recollection I note some of your other
responses have tended to be in the same category.>>

I'm not fond of questions like "when did you stop molesting your next-door
neighbor's child?" and I react accordingly.   Are you prepared to answer
that question with the only permissible answer -- a date and a time?

If you are deliberately attempting to touch raw nerves in a public forum, I
believe that's called "trolling", and it's behavior that strikes me
unbecoming of a professional.

<<Perhaps if I had written :-  "......COBOL's early days Univac aka Unisys
have only just returned to the
Standards Committee after an absence of ??????  I'd love to know why they
WERE ABSENTEES  in the first place"   it wouldn't have got your dander up.>>

Well, it's not clear to me that it was any of your business what personal
reasons the Unisys representatives might have had for their absences from J4
meetings to start with, or for that matter that it would be appropriate for
me to discuss those reasons in a *public* forum.

<<As you say, you wrote to me privately, essentially the same as your
message here - to which I replied, and said I was satisfied. Now I jump into
clc and see a repeat. - ONE message would have sufficed !>>

I felt there was more detail in the private message that would help you as
an individual understand the conflicts and issues that were involved.  I
thought I was offeringyou the courtesy of a more comprehensive and detailed
reply than that which was appropriate for the forum; I regret that you found
it offensive for me to do so and will strive to avoid extending such
courtesies in future!   I also felt that the readership of the forum
deserved a response.

<<A simple, 'You misconstrued our absence. It was due to an administrative
SNAFU. We are again participants and we are 100% behind COBOL". Topic
finished.>>

OK, clearly my response (private and public) was inadequate in your judgment
in some way.  I'm beginning to smell some trolls here.


My experience does *not* lead me to believe that, had I responded exactly
that way, the topic would indeed have been finished!  I'm pretty certain
*somebody* -- whether you or not,  don't know,
would have challenged it or asked for specifics!

<< As you felt *compelled* to reply here in great detail, how about
something more constructive with regard to the various points I raised, e.g.
:->>

I quoted the one point that I felt bore directly on the good name of my
company.  I only quoted the point I was responding to.  But I'll take the
bait; as to your challenges herein:

<<(a) You are unduly pessimistic, COBOL is on the right course and will
thrive>>

I think J4 is on the right course with respect to COBOL.  I do not know
whether *users* will migrate towards, or away from, COBOL.  But I do believe
COBOL doesn't stand a chance if the standards process is changed before the
next standard is adopted, so at least through the adoption of the next
standard by ISO:  Yes, I believe J4 is on the right course.   Otherwise I
wouldn't be on the committee.

<<(b)  You are way off the ball on the following observations ...>>

See a previous entry responding to your observations about Unisys' quitting
J4 ...

<<(c) J4 CD 1.10 will definitely make it to the best seller list, and is
perfectly readable>>

Dunno about either point here.  I wouldn't expect it to sell as well as
*draft* as it would as an *approved standard*.   I daresay most *users*
would be far more interested in the reference manuals provided by the
*implementors* of the compliant compilers than in the contents of the
standard itself.

Personally I don't care how well the adopted standard sells; I would be
comfortable if the handful of people who are actually implementing compilers
purporting to be conformant with it worldwide are aware of its contents.

As far as readability goes, it takes a while to understand the particular
jargon, but I believe it's *precise*, and I'm more concerned about
*precision* than anything else.   I have no objection spending time parsing
long sentences (after all, I am certainly used to producing them!
:-)     ).

<<(d)  Vendors WILL implement the whole standard, including OO (well, at
least Unisys will ...)>>

I can't answer that question for Unisys, regardless of whether or not I was
privy to the answer to the question in the first place, which I'm not;
because I'm not in product management.   You'll have to take up that
question, as it applies to Unisys, with the marketing folk.   I'd be in deep
doo-doo if I ventured a direct response here in any case.    I can't talk
about products we haven't  announced, whether or not such products exist now
or will exist in future.  Not my place.

My personal opinion is that *some* vendors will provide features in their
current compilers that map in syntax and behavior exactly onto the features
of the FCD, without necessarily  producing 100%-compliant compilers, and I
for one believe the feature content of such compilers would be a sufficient
improvement for vendors to take that approach, whether or not they also
produce fully-compliant versions at some point.   Oh, yeah.  Some vendors
have already done that.

Whether other vendors will indeed provide a 100%-compliant compiler I can't
answer as a fact, but I am reasonably certain some will.

Fundamentally my personal opinion is that the FCD, as for each of the
standards before it, is of enormous value, regardless of how many vendors
actually implement compilers 100% compliant with it.  For example, "In-line
comments" and "compiler directives" would be welcome additions to *any*
COBOL compiler  as an extension, regardless of the standard to which that
compiler (unextended) complied.  Should a '74 compiler "flag" inline
comments?  Well, yeah, arguably, but how many users of that feature would
actually *object* to its very presence in the compiler?    The vital,
critical importance of the standard is not how many *vendors* produce
compilers 100% compliant with it but rather how many *users* make use of
some of its features in production code.  The latter does *not* require a
100%-compliant compiler, only one that is compliant for the subset of syntax
the programmer actually used in the program.

<<(e)  There is absolutely nothing, but nothing, wrong with the ANSI/ISO
machinery>>

I would argue that there is *no* human endeavor which there is "absolutely
nothing, but nothing, wrong."  The point of your question is ... ?   Is
there another troll lurking under this particular bridge?      ;-)

<<(f)  Be assured, in future we won't have 15-20 years between each new
COBOL version.>>

I am not in a position to assure one way or another; I don't have either a
crystal ball or authority plenipotentiary to affect it!  I do have an
*opinion*, though:

One way or another I do *not* believe we will have 15-20 years between new
COBOL versions in the future.   Presuming the FCD is adopted pretty much as
is, It strikes me far more likely that either (1) the process will be
changed so that amendments can be processed more quickly (and as part of
that, J4 will do a better job of balancing the size of the changes in each
revision!), or (2) interest in further formal development of COBOL as a
language will ebb to the point that nothing more than interpretations will
follow.   I hope the first is what happens, but the second is a real
possibility.   They may not be the only two, but I believe they're the most
likely.

<<Take your pick - and give some assurances where you can.>>

I think I've covered 'em all from *this* message.

<<Please don't wrap answers in Orwellian double-speak. I'm not interested.
Plain words please.>>

Dunno what that's about.  I strive to be precise, and sometimes that ends up
being perceived as wordy.   What demonstrably constitutes "Orwellian
double-speak" in my entries?

As to your numbered entries:  I have no input on 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7a, 7b, 11,
13.

6:  As I indicated above, I think there will be some vendors that provide
compilers with *some*, but not *all* of the new features of the FCD.  They
already have.  Some vendors may not go beyond that.  The point being ...?

8.   I believe the standard is written for compiler implementors; the
expectation that it be immediately understood by a novice programmer in the
same way and to the same degree as a basic tutorial in COBOL would be
strikes me as unreasonable.  The standard is first and foremost a reference
manual for *compiler implementors*, I believe.

9.   I'm not convinced it's necessary for all compiler *implementors* to
have a say in the feature content of the compilers; all they have to do is
*implement* according to that feature content.  Or not (see 6).    And the
"dirty pool" note I've already addressed.

10.  I agree wholeheartedly that the standard should be "neutral flavored".
If "TinyCobol" outcompetes and ultimately supplants "COBOL2002" in the
marketplace, well, the new standard will be irrelevant.  I wouldn't expect
that to happen, but it's a real possibility.  Not sure whether "pushing for
it" either way is the right thing to do.

11.  That's a hardware-specific issue, not necessarily characteristic of the
"PC-vs.-mainframe" question.  MCP/AS programs can initiate tasks in a manner
similar to WFL (analogous to JCL).

12.   Unisys MCP/AS COBOL85 implements to Level 2 in all modules.  "If you
make it optional, it won't happen."   Not so.

14.   I believe a standard should exist, and that J4/NCITS/ANSI/WG4/ISO is
currently the *appropriate* mechanism, whatever its procedural flaws might
be.  My personal opinion is that the reason the FCD took so long to produce
is that J4 took on more features *requested by users* than it could handle.

If the current draft is adopted, 2002 strikes me as more likely than 2005,
but otherwise your point is acknowledged.

15.  How many FORTRAN programmers actually worried about the *standards*
differences that underlay the various generations in that language?  Seems
to me as a group they were far more interested in the reference materials
for the various *implementations*!   I know I was!  A programmer converting
a FORTRAN (66) program to FORTRAN77 isn't going to be looking at the
standards for guidance, he's going to be looking at the two reference
manuals for his implementation.  I'm thus unsure why the fact that there are
probably 500 programmers worldwide who care about the specific wording of
the standard should be a big surprise to you, or in any way remarkable!

       -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:bLYA6.2259$6R5.141914@news1.atl...

<<I was only trying to differentiate between reasonable environmentalists
and the extremists.  I consider myself a reasonable environmentalist.  For
example, ...>>

*Everyone* considers himself reasonable, no matter how extreme his views are
*to others*, because he's defining what is reasonable for himself to begin
with; all the examples do is provide clues to the definitions, not a factual
base.

Quixote's quest made perfect sense *to him*.

From the perspective of this observer, the protestations of reasonableness
seem to be being made in the form of wave upon wave of examples of rhetoric.
While the content might  be regarded as  speaking to reasonableness, the
quantity seems to me extreme ...     ;-)

(writing as one whose essays tend toward the verbose...)

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Hey Judson, "rabid" ANYTHING is illogical, and tends to be elitism. That
does not mean the nuts do not have a point.

I tend to view the crazies as the avante garde of the social gestault.  Some
are proven to be correct after twenty years, and become mainstream. Others
prove to be wrong.  While some judgement is required, though, it is never
really a smart thing to completely ignore them.  There is just about always
a few valid points, and often some very important ones.

I am sure those against slavery in the 18th century, and those against
punishing the insane or jailing the destitute in the 19th were considered
complete nutcakes in their time.  I can even remember when anybody against
the war in Vietnam was a complete idiot.
```

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote:
>
> Yellowstone IS primarily for the wildlife.

Ahem.  Yellowstone belongs to the *people* of the United States, not
the bears or the buffalo.  Remember that line about "government of
the people by the people FOR the people?"  Animals are property, not
citizens.  Do you think Teddy Roosevelt felt differently?  He wanted
Yellowstone saved *for the people*.  He hunted animals, remember? ;-)

> What is dangerous is the
> cars being driven through, and the noise they make.  Add to that the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> certainly by humans on purpose in Yellowstone.

Yes, notably Teddy Roosevelt.  Remember, Teddy didn't want to shoot the
bear that was tied up (not sporting), but he had no qualms about shooting
animals running around lose. :-)  Now, considering that TR was the man
who made Yellowstone a National Park in the first place, want to revisit
that nonsense about who Yellowstone is for? ;-)

> I've lived in Colorado and in Arizona and man has decimated so much of
> the mountains and the desert it is truly sad.  What you see flying
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> that desert has been destroyed.

So much that, in flying over or driving through, it is completely
unnoticable.  Wow.  Now, you are worried not only about the bears,
you are worried about the rocks and dirt, too.  I assume your home
was built on a piece of land that didn't exist anywhere before your
house was built on it?  Don't tell me you actually ruined a piece
of desert for the selfish purpose of living on it? ;-)  So it was
okay for you to use your piece of desert for a home, but it is very
wrong for other people to use their pieces of desert, hum? :-)

> You know why you have an over population of deer?  Because all the
> wolves have been killed off many years ago by ranchers and farmers.

Yea, let's bring the wolves back so they can thin out the children,
too.  After all, that's the goal isn't it?  Get rid of all the nasty,
greedy people so the nice wild animals can have it all? :-)  How
would you like a hungry wolf prowling around the back yard with your
2 year old children, hum?  How many animals does it take to equal
the life of one of your immediate family?  Children are eaten by
alligators in Florida all the time (as well as pets), and wolves were
essentially wiped out around here because they killed people and
cattle.  A hungry wolf is just as happy to eat a child as a sheep.
Actually, a small child is easier to kill and not as tough.

> The deer have no natural enemies other than disease and cars.  That
> is what is most upsetting about man's encroaching on wildlife areas.

What do you think the natural land is *for*, anyway?  It is for man
to use.  God did not make the Earth for the animals.  He made it, and
the animals, for man.  Unless you believe the universe made itself,
and dirt spontaneously formed itself into all these living organisms,
all without God. ;-)

> It is the way they upset the balance of nature.

Man upsets the balance of nature by being stupid, not by using it.  Environmentalists have done some of the stupidest things in
history
to the environment, such as introducing foreign organisms "to balance
things out."  Man is not even remotely as smart as he thinks he is.
I can't wait until man's tinkering with genetics runs wild somewhere.
It has the potential to make every disaster man has ever caused look
like a picnic. :-(
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T08:46:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NeZA6.2276$6R5.144402@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-5z3YZnBhjjd4@tcpserver> <EE7EDD49828C5E0E.5D57EC5C8537D0B1.5E539EB0FCEF84D5@lp.airnews.net> <yToA6.1179$Bz.44585@news2.atl> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl> <A85EA504170C25CF.DD61B07A8B7F5A09.0F573E3EDA41AB1A@lp.airnews.net> <NbLA6.1647$6R5.89444@news1.atl> <BbPA6.549223$f36.16589381@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
>
> YOU CHAUVANISTIC PIG !!!

Oink, oink. ;-)

> There.  Having calmly stated my case, and also saying "of course, they were
> not very enlightened 200 and some-odd years ago", do you really think that
> the human being is the only living thing in the unniverse with a right to
> life?  I simply cannot believe that of you Judson.  I know you too well.

We do disagree on this subject, Donald.  Remember, my viewpoint is
based on the Bible viewpoint.  I don't want to start a war over God,
Bible, etc., so I'll simply state, for the purpose of explaining my
viewpoint, that the Bible makes it clear that dominion over earth,
animals and plants was given to man.  Yes, the Bible says that animals
are not to be abused.  But owning them, eating them, or using them for
man's purpose is definitely sanctioned, to the point of destroying
animals that are annoying or in the way.  Animals, unlike man, were
not made 'in the image of God', and unlike man, they have no eternal
spirit.  I'm not trying to convince anyone, just explaining why I
believe as I do.  Of course, I have Scripture references to support
what I said above, for anyone who doesn't believe the Bible supports
it. :-)

I do agree, of course, that every human being is equal before God.
But the Bible doesn't speak of our 'rights', Donald.  It speaks of
our responsibilities toward God, and the priviledges God has given
us, particularly the priveledge of forgiveness in Jesus.  But I
think you already know this. :-)

> I can also tell you for fact (I have a cat as a room-mate), that some
> animals cannot be owned.  You, in your ego, may think you own them, but
> every living being ultimately owns itself.  Each and every one of us.

Just because the animal does not acknowledge the ownership doesn't
mean the ownership doesn't exist.  You have the authority, sanctioned
by both man and God, to have your cat destroyed, if you wished it.  No
authority in heaven or earth would stop or prosecute you for doing so,
if you did it humanely.  However, you cannot destroy the land you are
living on, or burn down your house, without permission.  You can't
even try to commit suicide without breaking the law (man's and God's).
If your cat were able, and killed you or anyone else, it would be
destroyed by law.  So who owns what Donald? ;-)
```

- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-5z3YZnBhjjd4@tcpserver> <EE7EDD49828C5E0E.5D57EC5C8537D0B1.5E539EB0FCEF84D5@lp.airnews.net> <yToA6.1179$Bz.44585@news2.atl> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl> <A85EA504170C25CF.DD61B07A8B7F5A09.0F573E3EDA41AB1A@lp.airnews.net> <NbLA6.1647$6R5.89444@news1.atl> <BbPA6.549223$f36.16589381@news20.bellglobal.com> <NeZA6.2276$6R5.144402@news1.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> > There.  Having calmly stated my case, and also saying "of course, they were
> > not very enlightened 200 and some-odd years ago", do you really think that
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> animals and plants was given to man.

Which really doesn't change anything.  If enough people want to have animals
around to enjoy, then keeping them around is serving the people.  And certainly,
having dominion over the plants and animals is a responsibility - if you believe
they were put here for a purpose, then it probably is a good idea to make sure
they stay around in their environment - for future generations who will also be
responsible for them.   Being granted dominion over, say white tailed ferrets,
doesn't mean we should allow them to die out.
```

```
departed drastically from the Biblical viewpoint, which is the point
```

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca

> Judson, I do not understand.  You are absolutely correct in what the
old
> testament says.  I thought, though, that you were Christian?
>
> Donald. (the pot smoking anarchist hippie athiest believer in love
that
> programs in Cobol)

It's Old Testament or, more properly, The Hebrew Scriptures.

Here's a subtle distinction that an atheist may not understand: The
Christian belief does not in any way detract from the accuracy or
validity of the Hebrew Scriptures. The Old Testament is many things,
including a book of history and a list of commandments. The New
Testament affects only the old commandments, not the history nor God's
rationale.

I once tried to fix a program last modified by a dope-smoking hippie.
It was full of variable names like "CHILL-OUT," and "MOSEY," and
"COOL-IT." It had statements like

MOVE X.

I think that guy moved on and is now a school superintendent in a
large city.
```

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bDrF6.283361$3J6.19138529@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-5z3YZnBhjjd4@tcpserver> <EE7EDD49828C5E0E.5D57EC5C8537D0B1.5E539EB0FCEF84D5@lp.airnews.net> <yToA6.1179$Bz.44585@news2.atl> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl> <A85EA504170C25CF.DD61B07A8B7F5A09.0F573E3EDA41AB1A@lp.airnews.net> <NbLA6.1647$6R5.89444@news1.atl>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>

A well-reasoned and articulated reply. One or two small corrections,
though:

> Children are eaten by
> alligators in Florida all the time (as well as pets), and wolves
were
> essentially wiped out around here because they killed people and
> cattle.  A hungry wolf is just as happy to eat a child as a sheep.
> Actually, a small child is easier to kill and not as tough.

Children (or adults) are not eaten by alligators in Florida. An
alligator attacking a human is extremely rare, maybe one a year. None
are eaten. Kids often toss Twinkies to their favorite reptile on the
way to school.

And it's not 'pets.' In the main, it's loud, yappy lap-dogs who insist
on getting in a 'gators face and dissin' him. No cats. No canaries.
Just yappy lap-dogs.

I once heard there was not a single documented case of wolves
attacking humans. I may have heard wrong. Might be worth researching.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-25T11:02:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MxCF6.2602$5b1.150723@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-5z3YZnBhjjd4@tcpserver> <EE7EDD49828C5E0E.5D57EC5C8537D0B1.5E539EB0FCEF84D5@lp.airnews.net> <yToA6.1179$Bz.44585@news2.atl> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl> <A85EA504170C25CF.DD61B07A8B7F5A09.0F573E3EDA41AB1A@lp.airnews.net> <NbLA6.1647$6R5.89444@news1.atl> <bDrF6.283361$3J6.19138529@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
"Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote:
>
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just yappy lap-dogs.

I don't live in Florida, but I have friends who do, and what they tell
me contradicts what you say.  I have seen news reports of children
partially eaten by alligators.  If they're entirely eaten, you don't
know if an alligator ate them or not.  How many missing children are
eaten by alligators in Florida and Lousiana?  There is no way to know,
and saying it doesn't happen defies logic.  Alligators are predators
and will eat anything.  And how many dogs do you know of that would not
become very upset if an alligator came into 'their' yard?  More dogs
and cats aren't eaten simply because they are generally too wary and
quick for an alligator to catch.  Canaries, living in cages inside
homes, are an even more difficult prey. ;-)

Alligator attacks 'rare', hum?  Check this out:
    http://seattlep-i.nwsource.com/getaways/022097/wild20.html

> I once heard there was not a single documented case of wolves
> attacking humans. I may have heard wrong. Might be worth researching.

You should talk with people who have lived in Siberia where wolves are
relatively abundant.  My grandparents were old enough to remember when
wolves and bears roamed these parts, and wolves *did* kill people.  Not
every day, of course, but even adults would sometimes be killed.  People
knew about the predators and took precautions, but children were still
lost to wolves.  Even dogs that are pets kill people sometimes, and
wolves are simply 'generic' wild dogs, as conclusively proven a few
years ago by an extensive worldwide canine DNA survey.  I personally
know of a number of people who have had their children attacked by the
family dog, even dogs that had never shown any signs of aggression
before.  Certain breeds are well known for this, including the Chow,
German Shepard, Doberman and, of course, pit bull.  When I was a teen
living in the country, a neighbor's large dog attacked a relative's
baby in its stroller, almost literally ripping the baby's face off,
with the family watching.  The idiots didn't even destroy the dog, but
somebody else took care of that for them a few weeks later.  I had
thought about doing it myself, but somebody else beat me to it. :-)
So if a pet dog that has been around people all its life can suddenly
attack them with no provocation, it defies reason to the point of
absurdity to claim that wild wolves would never attack people.  Wild
wolves tend to avoid people usually, but when living in close proximity
attacks are bound to happen.  Check out the links below, a few of those
that my son-in-law Mark found as I have been writing this.  Note that
the reasons given for these attacks are usually closer contact between
humans and wild animals.  This was my original point, that wolves were
killed off in populated areas because of these attacks, and bringing
wild predators such as wolves back into populated areas will result in
even more attacks.

    http://www.wolfpark.org/Articles/Wyman.html
    http://www.gilawilderness.com/local/wolfboyattacked.htm
    http://www.king5.com/localnews/storydetail.html?StoryID=3125
    http://home.neo.rr.com/springtwp/newpage1.htm

Reasonably balanced article by a wolf lover:
    http://www.wolf.org/GH/Preview/Spring98F2.html

Just so nobody will think I am a canine hater, I love dogs, I had dogs
as a boy, and we have a dog now, a black and white Siberian Husky named
'Dixie'.  She was born on my birthday last year.  But even pet dogs,
when they start running in packs, can be dangerous.  Wolves are far more
so.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T16:57:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ad3340f.88154429@news>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-5z3YZnBhjjd4@tcpserver> <EE7EDD49828C5E0E.5D57EC5C8537D0B1.5E539EB0FCEF84D5@lp.airnews.net> <yToA6.1179$Bz.44585@news2.atl> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl>`

```
On Tue, 10 Apr 2001 08:43:02 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>In a country that was founded on the principle of private ownership
>of real property, the government controls over 40% of the land.  The
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>man, other than a few cities and major highways.  

=============
Judson,

My wilderness is bigger than your wilderness.    ;- )

We're based in Alaska; I arrived over 25 years ago.

There's considerably more (locked up) Federal land in Alaska than in
all the Lower 48 combined, I'd imagine.  Only a couple percent, if
that,  is in private hands.  As a result, land is outrageously
expensive, which really makes little sense.

Juneau, the capital, like many Alaskan communities, is landlocked (no
road access) and has a population in the greater area of < 35K.  The
only other two cities of any size in the whole state are Anchorage
(600 miles away, ~300K population) and Fairbanks (900 miles away,
<100K population).

The entire state, about 1/3 the landmass of the continental US, has a
population of about 600K.  Think about it.  That's less than a small
city anywhere else in the world - occupying the entire state!

While I'm no fan of raping the planet, you need to keep things in
perspective (particularly if people insist of procreating like rabbits
and consuming energy at the current rate). I'd venture to guess that
the vast majority of Americans have absolutely -no- idea how big it
really is.   ANWR is, well,  mind-bogglingly huge.  Just to get there
from Juneau - by jet - takes about as long as it does to fly most of
the way across the Lower 48.  It would take hours to fly from one side
to the other by jet.  It's easily large enough to comfortably hold any
of a number of middlin' sized states within its borders.

Drilling in ANWR?  I've got mixed feelings.  On the one hand
conservation is a Good Thing, but on the other - why can't Alaskans
have the right to develop their own state if they wish?  All the other
states got to develop their resources.  Why not Alaska?  It's an issue
that involves the livelihoods of real people who have a real stake in
it - jobs, homes, lives - it is NOT an abstraction if you live in
Alaska.  

As I observed before, conservation is fine as long as it's the other
guy who's making the sacrifices.  There isn't much controversy about
developing ANWR within Alaska.  It's all from outsiders who have no
real stake in it.

So, what's this got to do with COBOL, anyway?

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

```


"Kevin J. Hansen" wrote:

>
> My wilderness is bigger than your wilderness.    ;- )
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yep it is B-I-G.  Recall first time I flew from UK to Canada. We hit lots
of ice and snow. 'Ah Canada', I thought. Well Yes and No. I *was* over
Canada, but we still had some 3 or 4 more hours to fly before I made it to
Calgary.

As you say, we have to make compromises - unlike COBOL it is more than
just a Boolean true of false, there's a 'maybe' or a 'perhaps'  tucked in
there as well.

But watch it when you talk about Alaskans doing their own thing. That's
not dissimilar to slightly further south down here. The 'get up and go
Westerners', (i.e. the oil barons), see absolutely no reason why they
shouldn't do their own thing - they completely dismiss Kyoto, (bunch of
cranks, no proof, hidden agenda, ulterior motives  etc., which is another
way of saying 'Hi Mom. Everybody is out of step except me'), and the
cheeky buggers even question our Federal government speaking on behalf of
us 'nationally'.

Just like papa knows best - they just want to get the oil/gas out of the
sucker, locally. Lil ole me, I just don't fit into their equation.

>
> So, what's this got to do with COBOL, anyway?

Absolutely bugger all ! But it's fascinating isn't it. I'm tempted, maybe
I'll give the topic a different twist and counter Judson's comments about
India, (I   L-U-V History) <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T19:19:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ad35afd.98120409@news>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-5z3YZnBhjjd4@tcpserver> <EE7EDD49828C5E0E.5D57EC5C8537D0B1.5E539EB0FCEF84D5@lp.airnews.net> <yToA6.1179$Bz.44585@news2.atl> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <3ad23520.22891416@news> <y5EA6.1376$6R5.77834@news1.atl> <3ad3340f.88154429@news> <3AD350E1.B3A7052@home.com>`

```
On Tue, 10 Apr 2001 18:29:33 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:


>As you say, we have to make compromises - unlike COBOL it is more than
>just a Boolean true of false, there's a 'maybe' or a 'perhaps'  tucked in
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>sucker, locally. Lil ole me, I just don't fit into their equation.
===============
Hey, Jimmy,

Oh, I hear you.  Big Oil is drooling all over themselves about ANWR.
I really am ambivalent about it - in the literal sense.  There are
strong arguments for both sides.

It all boils down to economics as I mentioned earlier.  A few will
bite the bullet and drive electric or compact cars, or ride bike (like
I do) but most people - including so-called "environmentalists" will
keep burning oil until it becomes so scarce they can't afford it.
THAT's when we'll get serious about alternative energy.

Reminds me of a greenie friend who was ranting over the cost of lumber
for his (large) house remodel, complete with lots of hardwood floors.

"Gosh, Jeff, did it ever occur to you that if you guys allowed logging
here, that lumber might be cheaper?"

"Nonsense!" he said indignantly, "All this timber gets exported to
Japan!"

...which reduces the supply, which drives up the cost.  Economics 101.


He never did get it.

Funny thing, he had NO problems with chopping a bunch of trees down to
put in a (taxpayer subsidized) downhill ski area.  He owns two cars,
too, and thinks nothing of flying a bunch of tourists all over the
place in the summer for his guiding business.

It all depends on your personal frame of reference, doesn't it?

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

```
"Kevin J. Hansen" <Support@ScreenIO.com>

> While I'm no fan of raping the planet, you need to keep things in

rabbits
> and consuming energy at the current rate). I'd venture to guess that
> the vast majority of Americans have absolutely -no- idea how big it

I ran a couple of numbers. If ANWR covered the same area as a large
house, say 3000 sq.ft., the area proposed for exploration and drilling
would be slightly larger than a saucer (about 28 sq. in.).

And you're right about people not comprehending distance. Most can't
imagine, for example, that Moscow is closer to New York than it is to
Vladivostok, or that El Paso is closer to the Pacific Ocean than it is
to Houston.
```

```
On Wed, 11 Apr 2001 20:00:11 GMT, "Donald Tees"
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> enlightened us:

>
>"Liam Devlin" <LiammD@optonline.net> wrote in message
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Some would argue we should all be vegetarians any way so we don't need
animals.  Of course those same people don't understand the balance
between plant and animal.  And they are also the same ones who don't
realize that man has drove into extinction hundreds of types of plants
from the toxins we put into the air and water.  It has been speculated
that the ancient Egyptians may have had a cure for cancer.  We don't
know (yet) what is was, but we've probably already destroyed the herbs
they used to make the medicine anyway.

Regards,

P.S.  In case anyone is really wondering, I am not a greenie.  I do
belong to several environmentalist groups (NRDC, Sierra Club, World
Wildlife Fund) but I believe in a reasonable environmental policy.  I
support that which retains or regains the balances in nature that man
has disturbed.  I support anything which makes us less dependant on
fossil fuels and which makes air and water clean and safe.




          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I have a twin brother; he's identical, but I'm not.


DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge
Read the facts and sign a petition at:

http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote:
>
> So tell me, Judson, how many whales has your aunt run into lately?

None that I am aware of.  How about your aunt? ;-)

> We,
> humans in general, are also decimating the various shark populations. By
> & large they are apex predators, so with them gone what will happen to
> the populations they now hold in check?

Much better catches for fishermen, and no eaten swimmers? :-)

> I think you'll agree your post oversimplifies things a bit.

It was only an attempt to counter what I consider a very overbalanced
attitude common today that would see humans starve to save animals.
Stopping a $ millions housing development to save rats, for example.
If common sense prevailed, such a suit would have been laughed out of
court.  It *would* have been, any time in U.S. history, other than
the last couple of decades.
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> "SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> None that I am aware of.  How about your aunt? ;-)

Also, none. Could it have anything to do with the scarcity of whales?

> > We,
> > humans in general, are also decimating the various shark populations. By
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Much better catches for fishermen, and no eaten swimmers? :-)

Hoo, boy, that's a good one:-) But the catches aren't going to improve
because the fishermen have overfished the sharks as well as the prey, at
least not for a while. Then the prey populations will explode,
overrunning their habitats & crashing. Might provide plenty of sick fish
for human consumption, which might compensate for the humans not
consumed by sharks:-) 

> > I think you'll agree your post oversimplifies things a bit.
> 
> It was only an attempt to counter what I consider a very overbalanced
> attitude common today that would see humans starve to save animals.

I've seen plenty of nonsense concerning animal rights, the wearing of
fur and eating of meat; but I haven't seen this this particular
attitude. Animals can't have rights, they have no money to contribute to
politicians.

> Stopping a $ millions housing development to save rats, for example.
> If common sense prevailed, such a suit would have been laughed out of
> court.  It *would* have been, any time in U.S. history, other than
> the last couple of decades.

I have to confess that I've never heard of any construction projects
being held up because of rats. Was this some rare strain whose habitat
was going to be destroyed? I can't imagine anyone arguing seriously to
preserve rattus rattus.

I agree with your point about frivolous suits. I wish our judges took a
more active role in tossing out the completely ridiculous ones so
parties with deep pockets can't screw over defendants whose only crime
is having less dough. 

Anyway, if you live near the coast keep an eye out for the landsharks
<g>

LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T09:44:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I_EA6.1409$6R5.79528@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-5z3YZnBhjjd4@tcpserver> <EE7EDD49828C5E0E.5D57EC5C8537D0B1.5E539EB0FCEF84D5@lp.airnews.net> <yToA6.1179$Bz.44585@news2.atl> <RaqA6.538380$Pm2.8827097@news20.bellglobal.com> <josephk-A99046.21474410042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
"Joseph J Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:
>
> WHO says the earth is warming?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> WHY should we believe them? WHATs their agenda?

Good points.  I used to spend time reading weather related newsgroups,
where a number of professional meteorologists posted.  After a while
it became clear that the 'yes warming' and the 'not warming' camps
were divided in a well defined way.  The 'not warming' camp was basing
all their arguments on actual measurements and observations, such as
30 years of accurate (to .1 degree) satellite data.  But the 'yes
warming' camp based all their arguments on models and theories, which
they 'assumed' to be accurate.  I don't know if global warming is a
fact, but it is pretty clear to me that the actual hard data says no.
Since when in science does unproven theory take precedence over hard
data that contradicts?
```

```
"G Moore" <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> hehe. so you basically trusted *scientists* who predicte the weather
> 100 years in advance, but couldn't more than 3 weeks.

Remember when we were told "When we put up weather satellites, we
will be able to reliably predict the weather 18 months in advance?"

Ha. Ha. Ha.  I would be happy with 18 days. ;-)  Not saying weather
satellites aren't a good idea, just that man is always thinking he
knows far more than he does, yet man's record of figuring things out
correctly before the event is dismal.  Even with all the materials
present for experimentation, look how many years it took to 'perfect'
liquid fuel rockets.  Yet 'science' speaks with total confidence
about subjects such as evolution, when nobody but God was there to
see, and experimentation is impossible.  Nobody on earth can give
a single reasonable hypothesis on how dirt could form itself into
a living organism without intelligent intervention, yet we're told
with absolute confidence that it *must* have been so.  The arrogance
of mankind apparently knows no bounds. :-)
```

```
>  Liam Devlin <LiammD@optonline.net> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > Funny how we can build safe atomic bombs, but not a safe nuclear
reactor.
> > > > We are going to run out of oil eventually, regardless of whether we
> > > > destroy
> > > > the planet first.  We may as well leave something for the insects,
as
> > > > they
> > > > will inherit the earth.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Joe Katnic

Well its solved for us.   We've just passed the problem down to our
descendants!!

As it happens I have been (and probably still am when I think of the
alternatives) a supporter of nuclear power.  I believe in science and its
power to improve things.  It's some scientists/engineers who are the
problem.  When we see nuclear leaks that are covered up, meltdowns taht
couldn't happen in the best of all possible worlds, and mistakes about
environmental hazards then you figure that either scientists/engineers don't
give a damn, don't really know what they are doing, or can be persuaded to
lie about facts in order to keep their jobs.  This confuses ordinary people
about risks.

In this country (UK), people were worried abou BSE crossing the species
barrier but were told that it wasn't a reasonable risk.  The scientists
actually didn't know and made an assumption.  They said that people weren't
very sensible about understanding risk.

Now, here, people basically don't trust what scientists say.  I find this
understandable but very worrying.  I think that the only thing that can save
us/keep us going as a civilization is science.

Unfortunately science seem to believe in the absolute results of risk
analysis (which could be reasonable) but don't take into account what the
effect could be if their assumptions are totally wrong.
```

```
Yep a fair question, and I don't think it will get his dander up. But
accepting the pros and cons, I love nature, but am no 'tree-hugger'. We
'us', our generation(s) have to accept that there must be compromises -

world's population generates 25% of emissions. Although a lower figure,
we don't do so great up here in the Great White North either.

have passed on....' <G>).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying...or Thriving?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T09:34:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GREA6.1401$6R5.79210@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<20010408133734.25450.00002014@ng-mf1.aol.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-5z3YZnBhjjd4@tcpserver> <EE7EDD49828C5E0E.5D57EC5C8537D0B1.5E539EB0FCEF84D5@lp.airnews.net> <yToA6.1179$Bz.44585@news2.atl> <3AD23691.C5F9958F@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>
> Can't turn up that Time Magazine graph - but what was it - some 4% of
> world's population generates 25% of emissions.

And the rest of the world is working like bees to do the same thing. ;-)
It reminds me of the resentment India has for the British, all the
while knowing that their entire economy would collapse overnight, if not
for the roads and trains the British put there 150 years ago.  If it
were not for the technological progress and prosperity in the U.S.,
where do you think the rest of the world would be right now?  The whole
world has profited from the success of the U.S., both in goods sold to
us and bought from us.  This prosperity was managed through the use of
fossil/nuclear energy to replace man and animal power.  And the U.S.
has a far better pollution record than many other nations, such as
Japan.  You can smell sewage in the streets of Tokyo, for goodness sake!
And did it ever occur to anyone that the reason everybody knows how
much pollution the U.S puts out is because we carefully track it, and
report it?  How accurately do you think Russia, China, India, Japan and
North Korea track theirs, hum?  And do you think they openly admit what
they do know?  The U.S. has always been more willing (for good or ill)
to air our dirty laundry in public than most countries.  My guess is
that the 25% is much overblown, because of under-reporting elsewhere,
and because U.S. bashing has become a favorite sport in some circles. :-)

And if you add up *all* of mankind's emissions, it amounts to a drop
in the ocean against the emissions put out by a single active volcano,
of which there are hundreds.  Do you know where the most of the methane
in the atmosphere comes from?  Volcanoes and termites.  NOAA, the parent
organization of the U.S. Weather Service, did a thorough study a few
years ago to find out why there is acid rain 'way down in South America,
over 1500 miles from any industry.  They found that there are so many
termites in the rain forests (and their little digestive systems produce
methane when digesting cellulose, just like cows) that they were causing
it. But they also found that one single volcano in the southern hemisphere
was putting out so much methane and other gasses, that it dwarfed even
the termites, and certainly all of man's output.  Did you know that two
years after Exxon Valdez, they brought in the largest fishing crop in
history?  Large oil spills, unlike the environmentalists want us to
believe, are not an unmitigated disaster.  They act more like forest
fires.  For many years forest fires were thought to be a scourge.  But
after long study, it was found that it is actually needful for a forest
to burn over once every hundred years or so.  It results in a renewal
of growth which is necessary for certain species of plants and animals.
Oil spills, oddly enough, have a similar long-term effect on the ocean.
The Earth is not nearly so fragile as we are led to believe.

Understand, I believe in conservation.  We should be good stewards of
what we have, including natural resources.  But there must be balance,
and we don't see that from the environmentalists.  Resources are to be
used, not wasted, and not hoarded, never to be used.  And we should
not be spewing pollution everywhere, either.  But the EPA has gone way,
way overboard in many cases, such as asbestos.  Even they admitted that
a lot more asbestos was released into the air by their drastic 'cleanup'
efforts than if it had simply been left alone.  And stopping a multi-
million dollar home development project because someone found rats
living there is beyond absurd and borders on the utterly unbelievable.
```

## Thread: AVGREC caculation — 1 msgs

### AVGREC caculation

- **From:** "dwilson7" <dwilson7@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-08T17:49:19-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9aqmgk$6es6$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I'm wondering if any of you fellow mainframers know if there is a
program/tool for calculating the JCL Space requirements using "AVGREC",
SPACE=(80,(1500,334),RLSE),AVGREC=K,
I know its not that difficult manually, but when you have a 20 step Proc it
saves time to have a program/tool to just plug in the LRECL and record
counts, then cut and paste from that.  We used to have one but it was
written in REXX, but took it away since we have no REXX resource in our
shop.  Any help would be appreciated.
Thanks!
```

## Thread: compiler options for COBOL under os390 — 13 msgs

### compiler options for COBOL under os390

- **From:** "G���nther Ruf" <guenther.ruf@aon.at>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T10:56:03+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at>`

```
Hi!

Is there a way to write the COBOL options into a member and read it from
there instead of the
//     PARM='....' parameter in the JCL?

Thank you very much!

Greetings from Austria!

G�nther
```

#### ↳ Re: compiler options for COBOL under os390

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T20:57:36+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-289767.20573609042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at>`

```
In article <3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at>,
 "Gï¿½nther Ruf" <guenther.ruf@aon.at> wrote:

> Hi!
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
At the very beginning of your program (before IDENTIFICATION DIVISION) 
put

PROCESS option,option,....
```

#### ↳ Re: compiler options for COBOL under os390

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T13:00:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010409090006.22493.00003858@ng-xc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at>`

```
Gï¿½nther writes...

>Hi!
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thank you very much!

Well, you can have the options as part of the source code itself: PROCESS lines
at the beginning of the source module ensure the compiler options go with the
code and are not dependent on various procs, JCL, or workbenches.

Don't think there's a way to put them in a separate file to be merged at
compile time, unless you write your own pre-process step (which wouldn't be too
hard; could use Dialog Manager file tailoring, for example).

Hope this helps,

Regards,

Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: compiler options for COBOL under os390

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T14:06:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BljA6.512$y32.61858@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at>`

```
G�nther Ruf <guenther.ruf@aon.at> wrote in message
news:3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at...
> Hi!
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Assuming your options are a bunch of PROCESS statements, it be easier in
your compile JCL to just concatenate the options member to precede the real
source...


SYSIN DD  DSN=PDS.PROCESS.STATEMENTS(FAVORITE),DISP=SHR
           DD  DSN=THE.REAL.SOURCE.CODE,DISP=OLD

result to compiler...

  PROCESS TRUNC(BIN)
  PROCESS NOSSRANGE
  etc
  ID DIVISION.
  PROGRAM-ID. GREAT.
....
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: compiler options for COBOL under os390

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T15:39:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QIkA6.872$FY5.51368@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at> <BljA6.512$y32.61858@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Hi Gunther,

You didn't say why you wanted to do this. It could eliminate some of the
possibilities. Well, anyway:

You could use the JCL INCLUDE statement and code your EXEC statement in 
an INCLUDE member.

Jack
```

#### ↳ Re: compiler options for COBOL under os390

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T10:35:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD1E4C7.A2348E91@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at>`

```
"Gï¿½nther Ruf" wrote:

> Hi!
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you very much!

You can put them in the front of your CoBOL source, which means you can
concatenate it to your source.   But I can't because Endeavor will cough
when it tries to migrate them to production.

Of course, you can have your JCL call a proc which has these parameters in
the proc.

It might help if we knew why you want to do this.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: compiler options for COBOL under os390

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T06:06:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD2A2B7.643846E2@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at> <3AD1E4C7.A2348E91@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> "Gï¿½nther Ruf" wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> when it tries to migrate them to production.

If I recall correctly, you have mentioned this before as a
customization of Endevor (tm) at your site.  My shop uses Endevor and
we have no problems embedding PROCESS or CBL statements in our
programs.  I tend to do it because it will always be saved with the
source code.

> 
> Of course, you can have your JCL call a proc which has these parameters in
> the proc.
> 
> It might help if we knew why you want to do this.

I can think of several reasons why one would want to do this.  I once
had an large and ancient program blow up with a S0C7 after conversion
to COBOL II.  It wouldn't work with the NUMPROC(MIG) option which was
then our default, but would produce correct results with PROCESS
NUMPROC(NOPFD).

Recently, I was working with a large group of older programs that were
being converted to COBOL for MVS & VM solely to make them 31-bit
compatible.  A couple of those programs called ancient assembler
subprograms that were AMODE(24),RMODE(24) and could not be changed
(political reasons).  So, I needed to add RMODE(24) in the PROCESS
statement to keep them from blowing up.

Your default options probably include NORENT for batch COBOL.  In the
unlikely event that one might need to build a re-entrant batch COBOL
program, it would be better if the RENT option could be embedded in
the source code.

DYNAM is the default option for batch CONOL in my shop.  I might need
NODYNAM for batch EXCI programs.

I like the idea of concatenating a PROCESS member in front of the
source code, as suggested by Michael Mattias, but it probably would
cause problems for my Endevor administrators.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compiler options for COBOL under os390

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T08:21:11-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD316D6.E56F2036@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at> <3AD1E4C7.A2348E91@brazee.net> <3AD2A2B7.643846E2@worldnet.att.net>`

```


Arnold Trembley wrote:

> Howard Brazee wrote:
> >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> source code.

yep.


> > Of course, you can have your JCL call a proc which has these parameters in
> > the proc.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> NUMPROC(NOPFD).

...

The thing which wasn't clear to me was in his original question:

> Is there a way to write the COBOL options into a
> member and read it from
> there instead of the
> //     PARM='....' parameter in the JCL?
>

It sounds as though this is a standard member which all programs use.  I don't
quite get why this would be desirable though.  If it was a program by program
basis, why use a member?  I don't understand his needs, and without such
understanding, I can answer his question but it may be the wrong question.   I'm
sure we have all seen this in applications development - sure I can do what you
asked, but is what you asked really the problem?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compiler options for COBOL under os390

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** JD <dragonslayer@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T22:05:50-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD3BBFD.C673D93@bigfoot.com>`
- **References:** `<3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at> <3AD1E4C7.A2348E91@brazee.net> <3AD2A2B7.643846E2@worldnet.att.net> <3AD316D6.E56F2036@brazee.net>`

```
One reason is that the systems programmers do not have the compiler
installed with all the appropriate defaults, so having a common member
with all the appropriate options would simplify standardization.

Another is they may simply have a specific system which needs to be
compiled with specialized options, etc., etc.

Howard Brazee wrote:

>
>
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
> you asked really the problem?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compiler options for COBOL under os390

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T22:12:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9b0i3t$cel$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at> <3AD1E4C7.A2348E91@brazee.net> <3AD2A2B7.643846E2@worldnet.att.net> <3AD316D6.E56F2036@brazee.net> <3AD3BBFD.C673D93@bigfoot.com>`

```
The method of creating a "customized" default compiler option module is
documented in the IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM "customization" manual.  It is
entirely possible for an application group to create their own "default"
compiler module - place it in a load module and put that in the "steplib"
for their compile PROC.

My guess is that this is "fairly obscure" for most of the readers of this
NG, but I would be willing to explain the "details" - if others are
interested (and haven't read the customization guide for themselves)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compiler options for COBOL under os390

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T07:28:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD45C01.DE550721@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3ad17920$0$18874@SSP1NO25.highway.telekom.at> <3AD1E4C7.A2348E91@brazee.net> <3AD2A2B7.643846E2@worldnet.att.net> <3AD316D6.E56F2036@brazee.net> <3AD3BBFD.C673D93@bigfoot.com>`

```
In that case, my solution is to create a proc with the standard defaults, and
have everybody run JCL which calls the proc.  They can still do JCL
overrides.   I think this would work better and easier than having the
defaults in a member.

I do this with my JCL:
//SGPF       EXEC SGPF,PARM.SGPF#2=(MAP,XREF,
//     SSRANGE,FLAGSTD(H),OUTDD(SYSOUX),DYNAM,BUFSIZE(69900)),

The Proc doesn't have MAP, XREF, nor SSRANGE, but I like them in testing, so
I override it.

JD wrote:

> One reason is that the systems programmers do not have the compiler
> installed with all the appropriate defaults, so having a common member
…[71 more quoted lines elided]…
> > you asked really the problem?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: compiler options for COBOL under os390

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T13:29:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010411092927.00621.00001032@nso-fe.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<NPQA6.2818$FY5.200311@www.newsranger.com>`

```
In article <NPQA6.2818$FY5.200311@www.newsranger.com>, John H Sleight Jr
<John_member@newsranger.com> writes:

>
>A plea to Gunther REf: PLEASE! Tell us, what problem were you trying to solve
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I would venture to guess that PARM= does not work as there is a 100-char
limit to what can be passed via PARM=.
I ran into the same situation when experimenting with enhancing our LE 
implementation and doing some 'tuning' tests.
Both options of 1) include PROCESS records in the source file and 
2)  DD concatenation were used in my case.  Initial testing used a 
single source with the PROCESS records embedded in the source.
When the options were defined, I copied the records into a separate 
PDS member to be included as the first SYSIN DD for the source to compile.
Later, when the Systems Programmer found the time to set the options
as defaults, we were able to phase out use of the Options Pds Member.
```

## Thread: Check new one and please correct it and send to me, please!=) — 1 msgs

### Re: Check new one and please correct it and send to me, please!=)

- **From:** "Kevin Solomon" <kjsolo@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T10:20:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w1gA6.186866$A6.42171561@news4.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net> <GTez6.66$qw5.2787@read2.inet.fi> <usFz6.12065$Kr1.1067948@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <uIdA6.39$P22.911@read2.inet.fi>`

```
Here's your completely debugged source.  Compare it with what you had.  Note
especially that the Environment Division comes before the Data Division.
Read up on the use of Section and scope terminators.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. FRIENDS2NUM.
       AUTHOR.     JARKKO

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

       SELECT FRIENDS-FILE ASSIGN TO "C:\FRIENDS.DAT"
       ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.

       FILE SECTION.

       FD FRIENDS-FILE

       RECORD CONTAINS 82 CHARACTERS.

       01 FF-FRIENDS-FILE.
          05 FRIENDS-NAME PIC X(17).
          05 FRIENDS-EMAIL-ADDRESS PIC X(39).
          05 FRIENDS-GSM-NUMBER PIC X(14).
          05 FRIENDS-ADDRESS PIC X(12).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01   OWNSTRING               PIC X(82).
       01   END-OF-FRIENDS-FILE     PIC 9       VALUE ZERO.


       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       000-PROGRAM-BODY.

       DISPLAY "!Reading friends.dat line by line!"
       OPEN INPUT FRIENDS-FILE.
       PERFORM 100-READ-MY-DATA-FILE UNTIL END-OF-FRIENDS-FILE = 1
       DISPLAY OWNSTRING

       CLOSE FRIENDS-FILE.
       DISPLAY "cool, eh?=)"

       STOP RUN.

       100-READ-MY-DATA-FILE.

       READ FRIENDS-FILE NEXT INTO OWNSTRING
       AT END MOVE 1 TO END-OF-FRIENDS-FILE
       END-READ.
```
