### Re: Check new one and please correct it and send to me, please!=)

- **From:** "Kevin Solomon" <kjsolo@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T10:33:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eegA6.187031$A6.42180473@news4.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net> <GTez6.66$qw5.2787@read2.inet.fi> <usFz6.12065$Kr1.1067948@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <uIdA6.39$P22.911@read2.inet.fi>`

```
Here's your completely debugged source.  Compare it very carefully with what
you had.  Note especially the following three things -- the Environment
Division comes before the Data Division, you cannot have a data name
identical to a file name and the display of OWNSTRING in the NOT AT END
clause of the read.
Also, read up on the use of Section and scope terminators.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. FRIENDS2NUM.
       AUTHOR.     JARKKO

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

       SELECT FRIENDS-FILE
       ASSIGN TO "C:\FRIENDS.DAT"
       ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.

       FD FRIENDS-FILE

       RECORD CONTAINS 82 CHARACTERS.

       01 FF-FRIENDS-FILE.
          05 FRIENDS-NAME PIC X(17).
          05 FRIENDS-EMAIL-ADDRESS PIC X(39).
          05 FRIENDS-GSM-NUMBER PIC X(14).
          05 FRIENDS-ADDRESS PIC X(12).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01   OWNSTRING                         PIC X(82).
       01   END-OF-FRIENDS-FILE  PIC 9                 VALUE ZERO.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       000-PROGRAM-BODY.

       DISPLAY "!Reading friends.dat line by line!"

       OPEN INPUT FRIENDS-FILE.
       PERFORM 100-READ-MY-DATA-FILE
            UNTIL END-OF-FRIENDS-FILE = 1

       CLOSE FRIENDS-FILE.
       DISPLAY "cool, eh?=)"

       STOP RUN.

       100-READ-MY-DATA-FILE.

       READ FRIENDS-FILE NEXT INTO       OWNSTRING
       AT END MOVE 1 TO END-OF-FRIENDS-FILE
NOT AT END
      DISPLAY OWNSTRING
       END-READ.
```

- **Date:** 2001-04-09T12:15:59+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3KhA6.618413$JT5.17067036@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net> <GTez6.66$qw5.2787@read2.inet.fi> <usFz6.12065$Kr1.1067948@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <uIdA6.39$P22.911@read2.inet.fi>`

```
The stars are for comment lines, though you do not need them if the rest of
the line is blank.

The real key to writing this stuff, (which you seem to be discovering), is
to only write a bit a time.   Get a loop working with a simple display in
it, so you can test the loop mechanism.  When that works, insert some logic
in the loop, then test again with a display.  When that works, use the
result in the next step.  And so on.

news:uIdA6.39$P22.911@read2.inet.fi...

## Thread: Check new one and please correct it and send to me, please!=) — 1 msgs

### Re: Check new one and please correct it and send to me, please!=)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T10:22:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD1E1C2.BA210ECE@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<0Yyy6.15$AX2.990@read2.inet.fi> <3acb0f45_1@my.newsfeeds.com> <2D713F5028C5113F.C640D91E22085D00.E58BDCCEB6256DD0@lp.airnews.net> <SyWy6.75$Td4.3755@read2.inet.fi> <3ACC730C.15742143@brazee.net> <GTez6.66$qw5.2787@read2.inet.fi> <usFz6.12065$Kr1.1067948@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <uIdA6.39$P22.911@read2.inet.fi>`

```


"Jarkko Kï¿½hkï¿½nen" wrote:

> Jerry P <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote
> -CUT-
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> 001400        05 FRIENDS-ADDRESS PIC X(12).

Only 01 levels and FDs (as well as PARAGRAPH SECTION & DIVISION names) can be
in columns 8-11.  It looks as though you have 05 levels there


>
> 001500
> 001600  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
> 001700    01   OWNSTRING               PIC X(82).
> 001800    01   END-OF-FRIENDS-FILE     PIC 9           VALUE ZERO.

This is in the wrong place.   What did your error messages say?

> 001850
> 001900 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 002500    ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

This is the wrong place.

>
> 002600*
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 003000   THIS-MUST-BE-SOME-KIND-OF-PARAGRAPH-NAME-EH SECTION.

No.  This is an empty section followed by another section.  In the procedure
division, sections are groups of paragraphs.  The word section follows their
name.  Paragraphs are names without the word SECTION following them.   CoBOL
words must be 32 characters or less.

I don't use SECTIONs in my PROCEDURE division, but it is popular in Europe.
But they hold paragraphs.  There is no longer any reason to have sections
except to perform several paragraphs with one perform.  (except for a type of
error handling you're not ready for)

> 003050
> 003100   DISPLAY '!Reading friends.dat line by line!'

Looks as though you still don't have your columns straight.  Read what I said
above.

>
> 003200
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 003600    END-PERFORM

You still have in-line performs mixed up with external performs.  We told you
about that.  Do you have any manuals at all showing syntax?


>
> 003700
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Stars are - er - I don't know. Why they art there?

I see lots of programs which comment out blank lines.   I have no idea why
they are there though.  I tend to leave blank lines blank, and only comment
out lines with text on them.

Learn to look up things in your reference manual.
```

<a id="t-bc1a822bb1"></a>
## Thread: SORT-RETURN values — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Re: SORT-RETURN values

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T13:38:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9asviv$rd1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3AD1DE83.CC9CC17F@brazee.net>`

```
Short answer - NOT VERY EASILY.

Medium answer - if all you want to know is whether it succeeded or not,
check out:
 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igypg205/1.11.12

Long Answer (and I have *not* tried this).
Use the SORT-MESSAGE special register to set the output DDname for your sort
messages to a "known" file - that you allocate in your run-time JCL. *also*
allocate that same file with a Select/Assign statement, an FD - but do NOT
open it before your SORT.

In the example (pointed to above), after you have determined that the
SORT-RETURN special register is not zero, THEN open the file with the sort
diagnostics (pointed to by your Select/Assign - FD) and "read" those
messages and DISPLAY them on the console.

  ***

Probably the BEST way (IMHO) is to simply test for SORT-RETURN not being
zero.  In that case send a message to your console operator to read the
diagnostics in the SORT sysout file (which can be "modified" by the use of
the SORT-MESSAGE special register).
```

#### ↳ Re: PrintPreview

- **From:** Jeff Campbell <jcampbell@ins-msi.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-09T22:29:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD231EC.4C38F6F4@ins-msi.com>`
- **References:** `<3AD20AAB.A2A54592@attglobal.net>`

```
Carlos Lages wrote:
> 
> Hi, Some Weeks ago somebody said
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> ps. Clages@attglobal.net

I am using VSView7 in one of my projects. The VSPrinter control
must be added to the form first.

Here are some lines out of my subroutine that displays the contents
of a file:

         INVOKE vsprinter1 "Clear"
         INVOKE vsprinter1 "StartDoc"
					
         move "0.25in" to "MarginLeft" OF VSPRINTER1

         move ifr-data(1:ws-rec-siz - 11) to "Paragraph" OF vsprinter1

         INVOKE vsprinter1 "NewPage"
         move space to "NavBarText" OF vsprinter1

         INVOKE vsprinter1 "EndDoc"


Most of the VARIANT arguments can be handled as strings. Numbers should
be COMP-5.

A couple of things learned the hard way (8-): The NavBarText property
must be written to force the page count displayed with the NavBar to
update. The NewPage method does not interact with it. Two, the Paragraph
property is the preferred way to write a single line of text.

HTH,

Jeff

Jeff Campbell
n8wxs@arrl.net
```

Got trained in JSP, when I started programming in the '80's.
Trouble was that everyone used some form of procedural
decomposition, and I hardly ever used it after that.

But it does tick me off, that the "new breed" talk about OO
programming like tradional programmers never did anything
non-procedural.

I even did some Modula-2 back in 1990-ish, which was
modularised  Pascal. So all it seems the new generation has
really done is to rehash/combine existing methods in a "new"
language and - encapsulate the code & data together.

And I swear - I'm sure they thought up slightly different
definitions for terms like "class", " method" & "object"
just to bug existing proigrammers.

## Thread: OT: ANWR, Energy, Nuclear fuel, Greenies, tree huggers, NRA, etc...... — 32 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: ANWR, Energy, Nuclear fuel, Greenies, tree huggers, NRA, etc......

- **From:** Anonymous <anonymous@anonymous.anonymous>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T17:10:41+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ad29861_2@anonymous>`

```
Judson made a good point...

And, obviously many people feel very strongly about both sides of the
argument.

Here's my two cents on this...

Whether a Wildlife reserve is mud or lush forest is irrelevant. It is
designated as a reserve.

If you want to develop it, change the designation.

If you are unable to do that, accept that you live in a democracy and try
and persuade other people to your point of view. Then try again to get the
designation changed.

If you still can't do it, you have limited options:

1. Accept that yours is a minority viewpoint and get on with your life.

2. Start a terrorist enclave and resolve to bomb the majority until they
accept your view. A high risk strategy which has been seen to be
ineffective, (I lived in London during the IRA bombing campaign), and often
has the opposite effect than that which you intended; it is amazing how a
few dismemberments and deaths of innocent civilians will steel the resolve
of even the most apathetic Citizens and turn them into implacable enemies of
this particular form of persuasion...)

3. Go and live somewhere else. (possibly under a different form of
Government).

Bottom line: Whether the Polar Bears want it or not, whether you like Polar
Bears or not, it is designated as a reserve. Until it isn't, you must
reserve it.

Now, looking at Judson's points...

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
>
> DON'T DRILL in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge
> Read the facts and sign a petition at:

>>>
Just curious.  If we stop using nuclear power and drilling for oil,
we will eventually run out of energy.
<<<
No we won't.  Very large amounts of it arrive every day from a nearby star
(certainly more than we actually need on a daily basis). Now, if the said
star becomes tired and stops radiating it, THEN we will run out of energy.
(this event is due to happen around 6 billion years from now, so there is no
immediate cause for concern...<G>)

>>>
 What do you propose we use to
heat our homes and offices, power our society, and use for all the
multitude of products and drugs we derive from petroleum?
<<<
OK, how about this...

Use solar power to generate electricity. Use the electricity so generated,
to electrolyse sea-water and derive Hydrogen and Oxygen (twice as much
Hydrogen as Oxygen due to the curious affinity of electrons for each other
(valency)).

Liquefy the hydrogen and release the oxygen into the atmosphere as a tiny
compensation for the destruction of the natural atmosphere factory we call
the Rain Forest. Power our internal combustion engines with hydrogen, until
we can get 'em converted to electric motors running on hydrogen fuel cells.
(cars capable of 130 MPH have been tested with this technology so all you
boy racers out there needn't get despondent...)

No pollution. No by-products and the hydrogen gets oxidised in the process
to fall as rainwater back in the Ocean from whence it came. So the cycle
would repeat indefinitely.

(It does not violate the laws of entropy  and so achieve "perpetual motion"
because large amounts of energy are being added to the system in the form of
solar power)

Lubrication? Synthetic oil.

Drugs and pharmaceuticals derived from Petroleum? Find alternatives and
synthesize them if we can't.

Viable? Definitely. (At least, no one has been able to show me that it
isn't, and I have been advocating this since I first electrolysed water  for
fun, as a teenager...)

Why haven't we done it? Think about it. Who stands to lose? Who has
virtually unlimited wealth and power? Why would anybody NOT want it to
happen... If a teenage kid in New Zealand can see this as a feasible
resolution of the energy crisis, you can't tell me that the best brains on
the Planet haven't thought of it. So, if it isn't happening it has to be
because of a commercial conflict of interest. (Or maybe there's a flaw in my
reasoning...any offers?)

>>>
Obviously,
you didn't post the above message without using electrical power.
<<<
No, and no Polar Bears were harmed in the production of the message, either.
What's the point? The fact that electricity was used is irrelevant. If the
implication is, that in order to continue using electricity we need to
destroy Wildlife Reserves, do you really want to pursue or maintain that
position?

>>>
Are you willing to give up technology to "save the polar bears?"
<<<
This is really "dirty pool", Judson and, frankly, I expected better from
you...<G>

The choice does NOT come down to renouncing technology in order to save the
Polar Bears; we can certainly enjoy BOTH.

We are being cynically manipulated into BELIEVING that this is the choice,
by vested interests who don't want us to explore any other avenues.

If we were to change the base rules a bit, we would probably find that the
resources of Planet Earth are very adequate to support both bears and
people, and even other forms of endangered wildlife (like COBOL Programmers,
f'rinstance...<G>).

What are we going to do when everyone in China drives a car?
Say, "No, sorry, you can't have a car." When we have a double garage at
home? Can you imagine what the air will be like with around 4 billion
internal combustion engines all pumping Petroleum by-products into it?

The point here is that sooner or later we will HAVE to review the way we
power vehicles. Or forego the freedom of movement we now enjoy. If they
persuade us that THAT is necessary then we really are seeing  the overthrow
of democracy in the form of Conservation....ironic.

Oil Companies would rather it was later...Using their lobby, they will
arrange it so that wealthy people can drive and others can't (Tax on
gasoline, roads, cars, tolls, etc.).
(There is already a sadly emerging tendency for people to say "It's OK, I
can AFFORD to pollute..." so the Have's needn't worry. This short-sighted
view will be hastily revised when they find that their children are
suffering from asthma and lead pollution and a host of other
"atmosphere-related" illnesses, just like the Have Not's kids are...)


Oil Companies make very little return on providing fuel to the average
motorist anyway (distillation is costly and so is distribution); the real
profits are in industrial use...

They are required by Law to produce gasoline, and Governments love it as a
revenue raiser. So, they can get to an ideal solution where they can reduce
the number of cars on the road and  correspondingly, the gas they must
produce, and still meet their legal obligations, so long as no new
technology threatens to replace the internal combustion engine...

Nuclear fission is too inefficient, even if the dangers could be dealt with
(and I believe they can). Less than 5% of the material in a nuclear bomb is
directly converted to energy. Fusion is a much better solution (it works for
the Sun...fusion in the Sun's "core" produces many times more energy than
the fission nearer the surface). We will never rid ourselves of the spectres
of Three Mile Island and Chernobyl (I was living in Dusseldorf, around 400
miles away, when that happened...my pants still glow in the dark <G>) and
nobody really wants a nuclear reactor of any description in their back yard.

As for Greenies, Tree huggers and the NRA...

I was involved with attempts to stop French Nuclear testing in the Pacific
before Greenpeace was formed. Although I endorse the aims of the
Organisation, I am always wary when people become extreme over an ideal.
This same wariness extends to the NRA.

We are not a gun crazy society here, but I have friends who enjoy hunting
(and I am guilty by association because I have never refused the odd haunch
of venison or wild pork when offered...)

One of them does it with a Bow and Arrow. (You have to get within 18 yards
of a deer to bring it down with a hunting bow; this requires considerable
skill in dense bush). None of the people I know kill animals just for the
Hell of it. Most, if not all, of what is killed is eaten. The same with
Fishing here. Eat 'em, or chuck 'em back...

I see nothing wrong with people who are gun enthusiasts forming a society to
protect their interests. I have a problem when it endangers my interests
(like, my kids need to wear Teflon  body armour to school in the
morning...).

I don't know what the answer is for America. You can't legislate it. Minds
have to be changed. There is currently little indication of this, but it is
wrong to lay the responsibility on the NRA. It has to come from everybody.

Pete.





  --------== Posted Anonymously via Newsfeeds.Com ==-------
     Featuring the worlds only Anonymous Usenet Server
    -----------== http://www.newsfeeds.com ==----------
```

#### ↳ Re: ANWR, Energy, Nuclear fuel, Greenies, tree huggers, NRA, etc......

"Anonymous" <anonymous@anonymous.anonymous> wrote in message news:3ad29861_2@anonymous...
<<major snippage>> 
> Use solar power to generate electricity. Use the electricity so generated,
> to electrolyse sea-water and derive Hydrogen and Oxygen (twice as much
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> would repeat indefinitely.

... and an incomplete equation. The production of solar panels is not free of pollutants and byproducts. The production of semiconductors and other materials needed to collect, convert and transmit that "clean" power is not free of pollutants or byproducts. If you introduce any kind of storage batteries into the loop then the amount of toxic substances can become quite significant.

The other piece of the equation that's missing is efficiencies. I'd like to see the math on exactly how much water you need to electrolyse to produce the equivalent usable energy  extracted from a single barrel of crude oil. I think you might find that you'd have to convert some significant portion of the earth's water supply to meet the world demand for energy.

For every problem there is a solution that is simple, elegant, and wrong. This may be one of those solutions.

- Karl

<<more massive snippage>>

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%ILA6.1662$6R5.90294@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<3ad29861_2@anonymous> <H9EA6.73232$XV.18369783@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <8DF0342735ED6B5A.4217AD6745DAC95B.3F5AF3C839A642F0@lp.airnews.net> <3AD349F9.9BAAF5BD@home.com>`

```
My educational background is in physics and math, so I'm not totally
unfamiliar with the ideas discussed here.  Unfortunately, there simply
isn't enough sunlight falling on any reasonable amount of area that
could be practically utilized that would produce the needed energy.
Though the sum total of solar energy falling on the earth is awesome,
we can't cover a significant percent of the earth's surface with solar
collectors.  Wouldn't the greenies love that!  Perhaps some future
method to extract heat from the ocean, or some such, would allow us
to harvest energy of that magnitude, but that's only hypothesis.  The
only practical available energy source we have today, other than
fossil fuels, is nuclear.  Geothermal is too localized, and has only
been used successfully in a very few places.  Wind power, a variant
of solar power, is great, but like direct solar power, can only be
used successfully in certain places.  We have already utilized most
of the potential hydroelectric power available.  About 20% of the
power in the U.S. is generated by nuclear plants.  In my opinion,
the only other possible source of that much power, besides fossil
fuels and nuclear, is if thermonuclear power is made practical.  It
is not clear yet if fusion reactors can be made practical.

Unfortunately, many people have a knee-jerk reaction at the mention
of "thermonuclear" (Wow! "Nuclear is bad, "thermonuclear" must be
much worse!).  However, the truth is actually the reverse.  Fission
(nuclear) fuels are radioactive, fusion (thermonuclear) fuels are
not.  Fission reactions are inherently dangerous, because you must
keep the reactor core cool and contained at all costs.  If the
cooling system fails for any number of reasons, you could have a
meltdown.  But the difficulty with fusion reactions is keeping them
going in the first place.  If anything were to go wrong, it would
shut down.  The byproducts of a fission reaction are naturally
radioactive for thousands of years.  The byproducts of a fusion
reaction are not naturally radioactive, and 'cool down' in a short
period of time.  In fact, they are useful substances that could be
safely used for other purposes once the residual radioactivity was
gone.  All of the fission fuel is present in the reactor core, but
in a fusion reaction, fuel is fed to it a tiny bit at time.  Even
if there was a catastrophic failure, such as loss of containment,
there would be an explosion (heat, not nuclear), but there would be
only a relatively small amount of radiation released, and it would
shut down.  A fusion reaction could not 'run away', as can fission
reactions.  I don't know if they will ever make fusion reactors
work, but we should all be hoping and praying that they do.  For it
would truly mean virtually limitless power, with *no* pollution,
and safety risks comparable to conventional coal or oil fired power
plants, with no radioactive fuels, no radioactive byproducts to
store forever, no danger of meltdown, and fuel from seawater that
would last virtually forever. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ANWR, Energy, Nuclear fuel, Greenies, tree huggers, NRA, etc......

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T22:55:00+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-28D2E7.22550011042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<3ad29861_2@anonymous> <H9EA6.73232$XV.18369783@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <8DF0342735ED6B5A.4217AD6745DAC95B.3F5AF3C839A642F0@lp.airnews.net> <3AD349F9.9BAAF5BD@home.com> <%ILA6.1662$6R5.90294@news1.atl>`

```
In article <%ILA6.1662$6R5.90294@news1.atl>,
 "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

> ..... The byproducts of a fusion
> reaction are not naturally radioactive, and 'cool down' in a short
> period of time.
Actually, the byproducts of a fusion reaction are an awful lot of very 
fast neutrons. It may be possible to harvest them for their energy, but 
the byproduct will be radioactive by definition. (Neutrons smashing into 
atoms generally create an unstable radioactive isotope).

So Thermonuclear is not the total answer. But I agree it's much better 
than fission, and well worth researching.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ANWR, Energy, Nuclear fuel, Greenies, tree huggers, NRA, etc......

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T10:36:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UR_A6.2328$6R5.150017@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<3ad29861_2@anonymous> <H9EA6.73232$XV.18369783@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <8DF0342735ED6B5A.4217AD6745DAC95B.3F5AF3C839A642F0@lp.airnews.net> <3AD349F9.9BAAF5BD@home.com> <%ILA6.1662$6R5.90294@news1.atl> <josephk-28D2E7.22550011042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
"Joseph J Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:
>
>"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> atoms generally create an unstable radioactive isotope).

The byproducts *include* a lot of neutrons. ;-)  As you (should) know,
fusion is the combining of two light atoms at the lower end of the
binding energy curve, resulting in the formation of a heaver atom, plus
various subatomic particles, plus energy.  In hydrogen fusion, the
primary byproduct is helium-3 and neutrons.  For the very light elements,
particularly hydrogen, fusion releases enormously more energy than it
takes to initiate the fusion.  For each heaver element, the energy
released is less and the energy required to initiate fusion is greater,
until you soon reach a point where more energy is required to initiate
fusion than is released by the reaction.  In practice, the energy to
maintain the containment field must also be considered (see the Lawson
criterion).  But, back to my earlier statements.  The quote below is
from Microsoft Encarta 2001:

  "If fusion energy does become practical, it offers the following
  advantages: (1) a limitless source of fuel, deuterium from the ocean;
  (2) no possibility of a reactor accident, as the amount of fuel in
  the system is very small; and (3) waste products much less radioactive
  and simpler to handle than those from fission systems."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ANWR, Energy, Nuclear fuel, Greenies, tree huggers, NRA, etc......

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T21:25:34+00:00

```

"Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>


The other piece of the equation that's missing is efficiencies. I'd
like to see the math on exactly how much water you need to electrolyse
to produce the equivalent usable energy  extracted from a single
barrel of crude oil. I think you might find that you'd have to convert
some significant portion of the earth's water supply to meet the world
demand for energy.

### The amount of solar energy falling on the surface of the earth at
the equator is about 500 watts / sq meter. Assuming solar collectors
of 10% efficiency, you get 50w/m2. To produce the equivalent power of
a 500 megawatt generating station, you'll need 10 million square
meters of collectors. or 125,000 acres (200 square miles). That's with
no clouds. During daylight. At the equator.

### Available solar energy can, however, be increased by moving the
earth closer to the sun.

For every problem there is a solution that is simple, elegant, and
wrong. This may be one of those solutions.

- Karl

<<more massive snippage>>
```

In article <H9EA6.73232$XV.18369783@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>,
 "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com> wrote:

> "Anonymous" <anonymous@anonymous.anonymous> wrote in message news:3ad29861 
> 2@anonymous...
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I'll have a go, but it may be wrong.

Let us assume that oil is all methane (I know it's not - this is just 
hand waving). Methane is CH4 that is 4 Hydrogen to 1 Carbon.
Oxygen diHydride or OH2 (H2O to you non chemists) has obviously 2 
hydrogen atoms. So by simple comparison you would need at least twice as 
much water for the same amount of hydrogen. Now, Oil is much more 
complex and in fact you would need much more than twice the amount of 
water.

But this is all immaterial, with sufficient power anything can be 
achieved. But we need the power to extract the power. Hmmm, something 
not quite right here. Hydrogen, Oil etc, should be seen as a clean 
efficient method of power STORAGE. Power collection, is something we 
don't really do very well at the moment.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: ANWR, Energy, Nuclear fuel, Greenies, tree huggers, NRA, etc......

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T12:38:48-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<90C360AEF8FCFEB9.3491C7A3E4811D16.5304EC6A7F62A9D6@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<3ad29861_2@anonymous> <josephk-5C5BDC.22015410042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
On Tue, 10 Apr 2001 22:01:54 +0800, Joseph J Katnic
<josephk@iinet.net.au> enlightened us:

>In article <3ad29861_2@anonymous>,
> Anonymous <anonymous@anonymous.anonymous> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Solar power, wind power, geothermal power and hydrogen based systems
are all very good alternatives to using fossil fuels.  The problem is
there hasn't been enough money put into doing the research to make
these alternatives viable.  There is a lot at stake here.  So much of,
not only the U.S. but other countries' economy is based either
directly or indirectly on oil.  What happens if hydrogen production
becomes larger than oil?  Folks like Shell Oil and Chevron and many
others do not want to see that day come.  Even the Big 3 car makers
have been reluctant to make their vehicles fuel efficient.  If not for
EPA rules, current day cars may only get 10 miles to the gallon
because there was no incentive to the auto makers to make them
otherwise.  

>People will not pay for it. PERIOD.
>Good lord, down here in Australia, we're about to throw out the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You say that, but people will pay.  They always do.  They'll just
complain more.  The higher prices are supposed to encourage
conservation.  It's too bad that people can't conserve on their own
without having to be forced to by economics.  The sad part is, those
in the middle to lower income brackets, who probably use less fuel
than those in the higher brackets, are the ones hurt the worst.  Those
in the higher brackets just whine more but keep on burning and paying
because they can.

>> Viable? Definitely. (At least, no one has been able to show me that it
>> isn't, and I have been advocating this since I first electrolysed water  for
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>reduce pollutants to almost nothing.

My only problem with nuclear is what to do with the waste and the
amount of time it takes to get a plant on-line.  I'm not against
nuclear power, but I'm not parading for its use either.  

My whole approach is that we need to take care of this planet we call
home and all of its denizens.  We are the Earth's stewards.  And we
will be so until some other more intelligent being comes along and
treats us the way we do our wildlife.

We can live in harmony with nature and wildlife without giving up our
style of living if we really want to.  Like anything, it takes a
willingness on the part of the politicians and the citizens and the
scientists and the business men to make it happen.  What happens way
too often is that greed, ambition at all costs and the need to do
things the easy way take precedence over doing the things the right
way.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I have a twin brother; he's identical, but I'm not.


DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge
Read the facts and sign a petition at:

http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: ANWR, Energy, Nuclear fuel, Greenies, tree huggers, NRA, etc......

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T21:50:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AeLA6.750$c53.60627@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3ad29861_2@anonymous> <josephk-5C5BDC.22015410042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <90C360AEF8FCFEB9.3491C7A3E4811D16.5304EC6A7F62A9D6@lp.airnews.net>`

```

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam
>
> Solar power, wind power, geothermal power and hydrogen based systems
> are all very good alternatives to using fossil fuels.

No they are not. You cannot get significant amounts of power from
solar, wind, or tides. Geothermal is limited by location. Hydrogen
based systems are like batteries - it take more energy to produce the
source than is gained by its portability. Interestingly, the most
hazardous form of energy production is hydroelectric; dams don't fail
often, but when they do, boy, watch out.

> The problem is
> there hasn't been enough money put into doing the research to make
> these alternatives viable.

No. First, research has been going on in some areas (think windmills)
for hundreds of years. Currently, more research goes into these areas
as into perpetual motion or the search for the Holy Grail, with a
corresponding chance of success.
```

Which simply proves (if proof were needed) that what INTERESTS people is
what motivates them...

It is MUCH more fun to discuss Conservation and the Environment than it is
to examine the current COBOL Draft Standard.

Come to think of it, it is MUCH more fun to watch paint dry, or listen to
Vogon poetry (where people have been known to gnaw their legs off from
boredom), than it is to tackle a 900 page humourless, exampleless,
drawingless document, prepared by a wet fish committee of frustrated Legal
Secretaries, in English that is painful to read (and yes, Thane, I have
looked at it now...) and yet could affect the lives of many of the people in
this forum.

There is one word for it: Disaster! (there are others, but kids may be
watching...)

This document totally fails to communicate at any but the most pedantic
level. It certainly won't make the Best Seller list.

And that's a pity, because it should...

Pete.

explored here.

to do it....<G>

As for insufficient knowledge of Physics, well I'm working on it...<G>

While I wasn't "kidding", (there is usually a "Grin" <G> in sentences where

it; life is not JUST about work...

I have found the posts in response to my suggestion to be extremely

Unless we do SOMETHING about an alternative (whether my suggestion is viable
or not) there will be nothing for us to breathe in 20 years.

Here are some scenarios...

1. We pollute ourselves to death in the next 200 years and the cockroaches
inherit the Earth. (I know Don likes this one...)

2. We launch a pre-emptive Nuclear Attack on China and destroy the place so
they can't drive cars. Hey, their record on Human Rights is pretty bad and
they're holding American airmen... that's reason enough...<G> The
cockroaches bide their time and bask in the radiation...

3. The Chinese launch a pre-emptive Nuclear Attack on the United States so
they CAN drive cars and become "Top Nation" as well. Then their record on
Human Rights is unimportant...
The cockroaches bide their time and bask in the radiation.

4. 30,000,000 years from now a six foot cockroach, wearing a 3 piece suit
and driving a BMW turns up for work at his laboratory, puts on a white coat
and splits the atom.
(Some little shrew mice, the last of the mammals, cowering in a burrow, bide
their time...)

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ANWR, Energy, Nuclear fuel, Greenies, tree huggers, NRA, etc......

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T22:33:48+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-4C7958.22334811042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<3ad29861_2@anonymous> <83LA6.732$c53.57717@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3ad3a1a9_3@my.newsfeeds.com>`

```
In article <3ad3a1a9_3@my.newsfeeds.com>,
 "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> wrote:

<snip re: solar power collection>
> Ah, that's a good point. I dispute (but cannot refute) it thus: It should
> say "...than can be captured WITH THE TECHNOLOGY WE HAVE TODAY."
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> knows what could be achieved?
Whilst this is true. The problem is not in the amount of energy 
available to be collected, or necessarily the efficiency of the 
collector. The problem is in the amortization cost of the produced 
power. Roughly put, the cost per kilowatt hour of these systems cannot 
compete with existing systems. Two ways to collect the huge amounts of 
energy required are "Orbiting Solar Power Stations" and "Ocean 
Hydrothermal Systems" See "A Step Farther Out" by Jerry Pournelle.

The problems are that the capital expenses required to create and 
maintain these power sources force the price of power provided to be 
uncompetative.

> >Available solar power is insufficient.
> 
> 
> No it isn't. We just may not be able to convert it to a useful form...yet.
> 
Yes and no.

We can convert it to hydrogen. However, hydrogen is a gas and is not 
easily transportable, Furthermore, only as a liquid does it have the 
energy density required for urban travel. That is, it's too bulky in gas 
form. But in liquid form it's diabolically corrosive and prone to leaks.

Another form of hydrogen storage is via combination with other elements. 
Here you want a compound that is high in hydrogen and low in the glue 
elements. One obvious candidate springs to mind. Hydrocarbons (ethanol, 
methanol etc) or better yet, ethane, methane etc, have large numbers of 
hydrogen atoms in their structure.

Now we are back to the original problem - if you believe it is a problem 
of what to do with the CO2 produced in combustion.

Catch 22 isn't it!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ANWR, Energy, Nuclear fuel, Greenies, tree huggers, NRA, etc......

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T23:23:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FH5B6.2772$cb6.247238@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3ad29861_2@anonymous> <83LA6.732$c53.57717@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3ad3a1a9_3@my.newsfeeds.com> <josephk-4C7958.22334811042001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```

"Joseph J Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au

> Whilst this is true. The problem is not in the amount of energy
> available to be collected, or necessarily the efficiency of the
> collector.

It is! It is!

To repeat:

### The amount of solar energy falling on the surface of the earth at
the equator is about 500 watts / sq meter. Assuming solar collectors
of 10% efficiency, you get 50w/m2. To produce the equivalent power of
a 500 megawatt generating station, you'll need 10 million square
meters of collectors. or 125,000 acres (200 square miles). That's with
no clouds. During daylight. At the equator.

Assume ONE HUNDRED PERCENT efficiency of the solar energy collection
system, you need 20 SQUARE MILES of solar collectors to equal the
output of a 500 megawatt generating stations.

California energy demand will peak, this summer, at around 56,000
megawatts. that's over 2,000 square miles of solar collectors -
roughly the size of the Los Angeles valley (if they were at the
equator and there were no clouds). Convert Los Angeles to a giant
solar collector? Hmmm.

As much as they would like to, Californians can't run their community
off of sunbeams.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT, Long, about Roaches, nuclear devices, chicken drool, and ANWR. No COBOL.

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T23:36:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sU5B6.2816$cb6.265838@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3ad29861_2@anonymous> <83LA6.732$c53.57717@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3ad3a1a9_3@my.newsfeeds.com>`

```

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <
>
> 4. 30,000,000 years from now a six foot cockroach, wearing a 3 piece
suit
> and driving a BMW turns up for work at his laboratory, puts on a
white coat
> and splits the atom.
> (Some little shrew mice, the last of the mammals, cowering in a
burrow, bide
> their time...)

You may chuckle, but I addressed this very problem over 20 years ago:

Roach research results:


I do have a story about small brown bugs (Small Brown Bugs are very
numerous and consist of over 2500 distinct families.  The rarest of
these is the Banana Beetle of Batswanaland.  (Since there are no
bananas in Batswanaland and the Banana Beetle is known to eat nothing
but, there is really no mystery as to why it is rare.)  The
small-brown-bug to which I allude is, of course, the roach.  (There
are at least 138 distinct varieties of insect colloquially known as
"roach," but I am speaking generically of course.)  The whole idea
makes me somewhat queasy; perhaps I should explain.

You surely know how disconcerting the wee beasties are.  They are
nasty looking, nasty acting, and (probably) nasty thinking.  But did
it occur to you that matters could be worse?

Science tells us that these creepy creatures have remained unchanged
for millions of years.  Not much else has.  What might have happened,
I wonder, if the repulsive roach had evolved?  For example, what if
the rapacious rascals had developed eyes on the ends of stalks, like
snails.  How unsplendid.  To have those little eyes following you
around the room.  Perhaps, instead, they could have grown in size much
like the primitive horse.  If the roaches (the French have a word for
roaches, but I don't know what it is) had increased in bulk in
proportion to the horse, I compute the contemporary critter would be
about two and a half feet tall and weigh about eighty pounds.  Just
think about meeting one of those in a darkened room!  Or what if they
had developed vocal cords instead?

Consider, you are sound asleep in your little bed.  One of the buggers
crawls stealthily to your ear and screams:


  B O O G I E W A H H H ! ! !


Anxiety attack!

On the other hand, what if these fey fellows had gotten smarter
instead of physically changing?  Imagine, you awaken one morning to
find, once your vision clears, thousands of them spelling out "YOUR
DOOM IS NEAR" in college-band script on the ceiling.  Unnerving, to
say the least.  Or what if one peeks out from beneath a chair daring
you to chase him.  You do.  He runs under the lamp where, unbeknownst
to you, he has a confederate in waiting.  So, out from under the lamp,
two roaches.  You, somewhat befuddled, continue the pursuit.  Over to
the rug.  From under the rug, Four Roaches!  Zip to the picture of
Aunt Hissey on the wall.  From behind the picture, EIGHT roaches.  You
would be a incoherent before they reached the TV.

The mind boggles at the though of it all!

What got me thinking on this was a call from a customer in Austin.
This call in turn engendered certain reflections about my college days
and the "War to End All Wars" waged with winged waifs.  As I think
back, one of my biggest concerns was the safety of goodies from home.

From time to time my mom would send me a CARE package and I learned,
from experience (experience being a great teacher, although,
admittedly, not nearly as neat as a detailed instruction manual), to
stuff myself immediately for that which remained uneaten would, in the
morning, be either eaten or inedible.  But I devised a plan: I built a
ROACH-MOAT.  Oh, the idea was simple enough, a number-two washtub
filled with water, a pedestal in the center and, on this artificial
atoll, the leftovers from the day's mail-sent manna.  I still remember
going to bed that night, a gleeful grin on my face, certain beyond
certain that all was well in the world.  Now I don't know exactly what
happened while I slept, but I can imagine.  In my mind's eye, I see
hundreds of little roach-faces gathered around the rim of the tub.  I
can see the tears in their little roach-eyes and can almost hear a
rending roach-sob or two.  Then, it happens.

One roach, perhaps younger than the rest or perhaps imbued with an
attenuated sense of familial loyalty, stands up on his four hind legs,
screeches in roach-talk "One small step for roachkind!" and throws
himself into the murky moat.  After the initial shock, others take up
the cry and before you know it, there is a ROACH-BRIDGE to the
victuals thought to be safe beyond their grasp.

They were still at their rapacious roach-repast when I casually came
into the kitchen the next morning.  I hope you never have to
experience the profound sense of loss I felt at seeing my dreams
destroyed, my logic exposed as ludicrous, and my goodies gone.  I can
still remember the little roach-chuckles as they waddled their obese
brown bodies off to their roach-lairs.

In a perfect frenzy of loathing (there have been only four perfect
frenzies since records have been kept of that sort of thing), I sped
forthwith to the corner store and garnered a gallon of "Granny
Goshen's Roach Rectifier" (not to be used in an aluminum utensil -
rubber gloves recommended) and, returning to the apartment, doused
everything.  All this frenzying, speeding, and dousing  rendered the
place uninhabitable to everything BUT roaches for almost two weeks.
The roaches, in a frenzy and with speed, but without dousing, bred
like crazy, way beyond, I might add, the Malthusian projections.

At least the Aggies were happy.  We had to swallow our pride and ask
for help (beg, actually) which was gleefully provided in the form of a
small, tactical nuclear device detonated right in the middle of all
the frenzying and speeding and asking and begging. Three square miles
of Austin, just southeast of the campus, remains uninhabited to this
day.  Well, almost uninhabited.  It is rumored that in the rubble
lurks an eighty pound, knee-high intelligent roach that not only
screams when the moon is full, but can actually swim.

For reasons that should now be apparent, I no longer live in Austin.
Watch for the next report on how, exactly, chickens spit.
```

store anything that is writen on the screen, because i want to put a little

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: screen

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-13T08:20:15+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD6A8AE.8E4DD712@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9av92j$pe1$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <wRNA6.18330$rk4.1294439@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9b43m6$1pl6$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
Apollo Hoogland wrote:
> 
> Microsoft (R) COBOL Optimizing Compiler Version 3.00
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > help me with the commands.

MS Cobol 3.0 appears to be a version of MF Cobol/2 version 2.1.  In
order to use the product beyond simple play and tests you should have
the proper documentation.  Language reference and User Guide for that
particular compiler would be minimum requirement.

MF Cobol 2.1 (and thus MS 3) did not have the CBL_ routines that would
read and write characters and attributes from the screen that would make
it relatively easy.

You could use SCREEN SECTION to define your screens with all atrributes
and fields and then redisplay the whole screen after the pop-up box.  In
general I would reccomend reserving a 'message area' in each screen,
such as line 24, where all errors and non-standard requests are made.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: screen

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

- **References:** `<9av92j$pe1$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <wRNA6.18330$rk4.1294439@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9b43m6$1pl6$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <3AD6A8AE.8E4DD712@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
We are talking about a "memory tester" here, but I think that the Micro
Focus product of that date (and PROBABLY the Microsoft one - but I am not
certain) had callable "X-routines" that could read character strokes.

As Richard indicated, the best place to find out - would be in the
documentation for that VERY old product.
```

<a id="t-6dbf13765b"></a>
## Thread: Function NUMVAL - Situation Solved — 1 msgs

### Function NUMVAL - Situation Solved

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T02:42:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010410224250.26397.00004160@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
A while back I had posted about strange happenings using FUNCTION NUMVAL. I was
asked for some code and didn't get to it until last Friday (the usual panics at
month-end and then other stuff came up).

While merrily cutting and pasting from the Xpediter session I finally saw what
was actually happening instead of what I thought was there.

The caculation was taking place after the WRITE statement (this was a CICS
program for those that don't remember).  The WRITE torqued over the first 8
bytes of the record which of course included one of the fields I was using in
the calculation.  Moved the calc to before the WRITE and all is well.

Program went production today :).

Thanks for making me take another good look!

Eileen
```

---

<a id="t-db5ea614b3"></a>
## Thread: Ugly IF Statement — 27 this month / 38 total

### Ugly IF Statement

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T02:54:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
We get a lot of students either posting or lurking here so the other day while
working on a particularly nasty program (8000 some lines of spaghetti code with
polish logic) I came across this particular IF statement no less than 9 times
in the program and thought they would like to see what's in store for them.

This is what you get to see in the real world :)

IF (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 11 OR 34 OR 61) OR                  
    ((AIHD-SALES-CLS = 14 OR  35) AND                     
     (WS-DEST-CD-1 = 'AP' OR 'WE'))                       
  OR SPECIAL-CHRY-CLS                                     
  OR ((AIHD-SALES-CLS = 14) AND                           
     (WS-DEST-CD-1 = 'EE' OR 'EF' OR 'FD' OR 'PP' OR 'CC' 
                          OR 'IE'))                       
  OR (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 35)                                
  OR ((AIHD-SALES-CLS = 30) AND                           
      (AIHD-CUST-NO                                       
                   = 09937 OR 10100 OR 10181 OR 10190     
                           OR 11100 OR 09941 OR 09949     
                           OR 10222 OR 01552 OR 11109     
                           OR 10108))                     
  OR ((AIHD-SALES-CLS = 14 OR 35) AND                     
      (WS-DEST-CD     = 'TC04A' OR 'TC11A' OR '0100A'))   
  OR ((AIHD-SALES-CLS = 18) AND                           
      (WS-DEST-CD = '48063' OR '17576'))
OR ((AIHD-SALES-CLS = 30) AND                               
      (WS-DEST-CD = '04179' OR '12014' OR 'LSC07' OR '31   '))
  OR (AIHD-CUST-NO = 08221 OR 05659 OR 00879 OR               
                     08228 OR 10418 OR 11010 OR               
                     08288)                                   
      PERFORM xxxxxx
END-IF

There are lower case x's at the PERFORM to indicate that different paragraphs
are performed each time the IF appeared in the program.  The paragraphs were
identical except for one or two variables from the map (this is a CICS
program).  These variables are actually an occurance but the map is not
redefined so each time the field in question is used such as QTY1 or QTY2 the
paragraph is duplicated.  Makes maintenance a real nightmare.

You will also notice that there are a few ORs that preclude the execution of
other ORs (that is it doesn't matter as something before it takes precedent -
assignment: see if you can find it).

The fields being tested are in the header record which is read at the beginning
of the program.  The IF statement is now reworked and a switch set if
everything makes it thru the tangle and the switch checked where each of the
original IFs were.  Just my part of making this nightmare a bit more managable.

Eileen
```

#### ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T22:33:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9b0jc8$3o2u$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
That compound IF statement looks like a prime example of someone's
attempting to avoid GO TO statements and by so doing, making the situation
even worse.  I sympathize with you.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-12T13:18:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD59D32.2D917112@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com> <9b0jc8$3o2u$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
> "YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
> >
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> > END-IF

It seems to me that this has been a 'special case' that has grown over
the years.

There area a number of Cust-No, a few Sales-Cls (Sales Class?).  It
seems to me that this is as inappropriate to be hard coded as would the
prices of products.  Without details of what this is actually trying to
solve I would suggest that it would be done better by a field held in
the Customer master record, one in the Sales Class Master (or some
similar) and possibly another in the Destination master record, or maybe
a Class/Destination,Class/Customer file built for the task.

Then the appropriate people can set/reset the factors controlling this
without having to reprogram the system each time.  You may also find
that many of the special cases actually are no longer required but the
reports that result from the selection are just ignored now for those
cases.

It is not so much an 'ugly if statement', but an 'ugly solution' to
whatever requirement drove it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

```

<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> enlightened us:

>whatever requirement drove it.

I've seen uglier IFs than that one, but that one would give you a
headache trying to envision the decision tree.

To add to Richard's thought, I'll bet that IF started out very simple.
Then, for whatever reason, more conditions were added as time went
along.  Finally, it has reached the point where the requirements need
to be totally defined so that the solution is apparent.

Regards,

Steve

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

- **From:** Jeff Baynard <jeffbaynard@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-13T02:23:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B6FBDB78.8BF%jeffbaynard@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com> <9b0jc8$3o2u$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <3AD59D32.2D917112@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
yeah, you just can't write good and clean with this much hardcode.  hardcode
makes me sick pretty much, although it is good job security and OK for older
jobs or systems.  This *could* have been done better with external tables
either from files or a database.

I see NEW programs being written this way, typically to interface one system
to another.  It is pathetic to see NEW programs written that look like this,
but trust me, these people 'round here' can churn this monkey code out in a
fairly unremarkable period of time.  THERE ARE VERY LARGE COMPANIES MAKING
VERY POOR DECISIONS THESE DAYS.   IT CONTINUES TO AMAZE AND BEWILDER ME.



Jeff

#### ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-11T03:59:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD3D64A.A40007C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```


YukonMama wrote:

> We get a lot of students either posting or lurking here so the other day while
> working on a particularly nasty program (8000 some lines of spaghetti code with
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> Eileen

Ugly aint the right word for it. I was right, you are still living your life on
this earth maintaining those gawdawful systems <G>. I just love applications where
some jerk throws in account numbers to create exception tests.

Smaller scale, but inspection points being tested look like this :-

        01 Top
        02 Bottom
        03 North
        04 South
        05 .....
        32 After Weld
        ....
        35 Lamination
        .......
        42 Temperature Loss
        43 etc....

In my application last three codes, plus more, create exception conditions for
different calculations - necessitated storing a 'table'. Problem was it was an
afterthought by the user. For the new version of the application I gave the above
two-digit numeric codes four-character mnemonics - easier for user to remember -
TOP - Top, BTM - Bottom, AW - After Weld, LAM - Lamination, TEMP - Temperature etc.

Still concerned about exception testing I took those last three and their
'brethren' and called them XAW, XLAM, XTMP - the "X" gives me one conditional test
- and then I go from there.

What my solution says - it starts at the design level before you hit programming.
In your case that may be a luxury you don't have. But you may have a solution if
you push really hard. I note the account numbers you quote are 5-digit. Can you not
make a case that exception account #s should be re-allocated to start with a  "9"
for example. (That's purely a wild suggestion, not knowing how your A/c #s are
structured). I *know* it's a change of account numbers - but seriously, nobody
should go histrionic over such a suggestion from you, if the justification is to
keep the programming simple and effective. Give them (the bean counters) some BS
that the program currently takes 30 minutes longer to run than it would without an
account number revision.

One other possible solution is what I call analysis codes, e.g. just like in a
Chart of Accounts - completely ignoring the coding system, (which inevitably breaks
down from the originator's original conception), you have an Account-Type field,
telling you it's Current Assets, Fixed Assets etc... Given space in the appropriate
records - say Customer Names, have an analysis flag, (Type of Customer)  that
triggers an exception.

Three questions :-

(1) Not sure when you say "The fields being tested are in the header record which
is read at the beginning of the program". Are these (a) run parameters or (b) a
fixed table complying with Level 88 rules ? Even if (a) they could still be fed
into a table.
.
(1) - Do you favour IFs over EVALUATEs - If it was me I'd go for the latter, even
with the confusion caused in the existing code.

(2) Howz about :-

01 GawdawfulConstants                pic 9(02).
    88 SalesGroup1                        value 11, 34, 61.
    88 SalesGroup2                        value 14, 35.

IF (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 11 OR 34 OR 61) OR
    ((AIHD-SALES-CLS = 14 OR  35) AND

Evaluate true
    when SalesGroup1.......
    when SalesGroup2 AND .........
    when ........

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-10T23:12:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9b0lj9$a9$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
Be glad it written in COBOL. At least you can read it.
Think what unintelligible, cryptic, gobbledegook this
would be in C!

IF (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 35) overrides the check further on down
looking for values in WS-DEST-CD. The perform will occur no
matter what is in WS-DEST-CD.

Years ago I worked at an insurance company. They had a claims
processing program (I wish I had a copy of it) that had 1 nested
IF statement 5 pages long. No kidding. An ELSE on page 4 paired
with an IF on page 1 for example. And a whole bunch of
intervening IF - ELSE scattered all throughout.
The only way to do anything with this mess was to print the whole
thing, tack it up on the wall and draw lines to line up the
IF's with the ELSE's, which of course didn't align on the page
at all. This was in the days before END-IF too. It was just dreadful.

=================================================================


"YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message news:20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com...
> We get a lot of students either posting or lurking here so the other day while
> working on a particularly nasty program (8000 some lines of spaghetti code with
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-04-14T02:42:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010413224213.00708.00000108@ng-ce1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<9b0lj9$a9$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
>From: "Ron" NoSoy@swbell.net 
>Date: 4/10/01 9:12 PM Pacific Daylight Time

>Years ago I worked at an insurance company. They had a claims
>processing program (I wish I had a copy of it) that had 1 nested
>IF statement 5 pages long.


Was the company 'blue' by chance? :D  I've also worked in insurance and I think
I may have seen that IF statement!

As for this particular IF - I can tell everyone that yes - those are sales
classes - tells us if the product is production, service, or prototype.  Each
of our customers is assigned a set of these (well at least the big 3 are), and
the DEST are destination codes indicating the assembly plant/dock the product
is to be delivered to.  I have checked and all the customer numbers are still
in business - in fact it is one customer but we assign a customer number to
each ship to rather than sold to thus the multiple numbers (there is another
master that uses Duns numbers for the sold to).  And yes - I hate the hard
coding as much as anyone else.

I can't remember who saw the AIHD-SALES-CLS = 35 but yes - that's it for some
of the other compound parts of the IF.  I've already move it up in the IF and
taken it out of the '14 or 35' statements.

What this is trying to do is determine if another file has to be accessed
and/or updated (which is determined further along in the program).  

Sales classes 11, 34, and 61 belong to one customer and only those particular
destinations need the file updated.  14 and 35 belong to another which could
have destinations starting the same as the other so that's why the compounds
are the way they are (I would have done as was shown by someone else combining
things had this not been the case - and they did not know this so I agree with
the 'solution' as it were). 

Came across another goodie in that program - would you like to tear that one
apart too?  I think it only appears 3 times (maybe 4) in the program.

Eileen
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-14T14:13:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hWYB6.1064$dW5.132936@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9b0lj9$a9$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <20010413224213.00708.00000108@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010413224213.00708.00000108@ng-ce1.aol.com...
> >From: "Ron" NoSoy@swbell.net
> >Date: 4/10/01 9:12 PM Pacific Daylight Time
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> classes - tells us if the product is production, service, or prototype....

This whole thing is is really the slam-dunk case for an external call to a
separate (or nested, see below) program.

Instead of inlining the call 4-5-times across several separate programs,
this really should be re-engineered as...


ID DIVISION.
PROGAM-ID. UPSALES.
...
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 SALES-CLASS          PIC X(02).
01 CUSTOMER-NUMBER  PIC X(12),
01 DESTINATION_CODE   PIC X(02).
01 YES-OR-NO                PIC X(01)

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING SALES-CLASS, CUSTOMER-NUMBER, DESTINATION-CODE,
YES-OR-NO

IF
  as ugly as you want it

END-IF
GOBACK
END PROGRAM UPSALES


Then in all using programs at any point,

CALL UPSALES-PROGRAM USING .....    <<< dynamic call
IF YES-OR-NO = "Y'
   PERFORM xxx
END-IF


Now, so what if it's ugly? The using programmer doesn't need to know squat,
and any change in the business logic automatically propogates to all using
programs without recompilation or re-linking. (And you can make the changes
NOW without the need to wait for the business people to decide what the rule
is going to be today).

I did a bunch of these for a "Blue" healthcare insurance firm. I, too, got
tired of the same damn code in four or five different programs and four or
five repeats within each program - changes in the rules were just too big a
PITA to implement across the system.

In this case (a single program with multiple repititions of the same ugly
logic) a nested or conatined program - not even a separate run unit - could
have relocated all the long, ugly IF statements to one common point for
future maintenance.

No, I have not solved the problem of "Ugly IF" but at least this ways
isolates it and prepares the system for a change in the business rules.

Maybe the "Ugly IF" is the result of poor business rules, but it seems to me
the programmer is responsible for engineering the implementation of those
rules, and should have selected a method similar to this.


MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-14T11:19:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tdgtqrtdka71d8@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<9b0lj9$a9$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <20010413224213.00708.00000108@ng-ce1.aol.com> <hWYB6.1064$dW5.132936@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <OtZB6.7996$Pj2.725292@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
If multiple copies of this "cloned" code (or variants) exist across
multiple files, than you indeed have a serious problem keeping
them up to date.

A tool that can find these copies and factor them out into single
abstractions (e.g., a copybook) can be seen at
http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/Clone/index.html

Another poster suggested that carrying out symbolic simplification
of the ugly IF statements would at least help readability.
The DMS engine underneath the Clone remover can actually
do such symbolic simplification.
```

> Was the company 'blue' by chance? :D  I've also worked in insurance and I think
> I may have seen that IF statement!

Just getting back from Easter vacation....
You got it -  It was in fact 'Blue'! Pretty disgusting code wasn't it?

Another little gem from the same place in a different program was
a PERFORM A THRU A-Exit. Paragraph A contained various GO TO statements
that sent control all over the program no-where within the paragragh A
range. Then from a half dozen different places you would find (in
paragraph R, for example) 'GO TO A-EXIT'. YIKES! A huge mess.

One interesting thing in yet another program was something someone
did with data names by using more than one dash, something like:

05 WS01---CUSTOMER--FIRST--NAME   PIC X(30).
05 WS01---ZIP--CODE               PIC X(5).

Not illegal but I've never that done before or since. Very weird.

Ron.

In a new version of our compiler (Compaq/Tandem) I had to redo the method of
handling PERFORM exits and had to put a finite limit on the number of nested
performs.  I made it around 30.  The first customer that hit it had exactly what you
describe and found that the program was not doing what they thought it was doing.
The next one said they needed a bigger limit, so I changed it to 50.  Can you
imagine 50 levels of nested performs?  Or, is this legitimate?

Don Nelson

#### ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

- **From:** Anonymous <anonymous@anonymous.anonymous>
- **Date:** 2001-04-12T03:11:09+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ad47463_1@anonymous>`
- **References:** `<20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
Eileen,

I thought it was a beautiful IF statement...<G>

It was certainly jolly interesting...

Instead of doing the daily crossword today I resolved to see if it could be
simplified and how much would be gained.

I converted it to symbolic notation, did a Boolean simplification on it (it
would've been MUCH more "interesting" if we had some NOTs in it...<G>) and
arrived at the following, which purports to be logically equivalent (unless
I made a mistake in the simplification or symbol transitions...not
unknown...I haven't time or inclination to check it on my computer so I'll
just risk the red face if it is wrong...)

Anyway, although there are some savings, there are not as many as you might
think when first looking at it...

if AIHD-SALES-CLS = 11 OR 34 OR 61 OR 35
                         OR
    SPECIAL-CHRY-CLS
                          OR
    AIHD-CUST-NO = 08221 OR 05659 OR 00879 OR
                     08228 OR 10418 OR 11010 OR
                     08288
                          OR
    (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 14 AND
    (WS-DEST-CD-1 = 'AP' OR 'WE' OR 'EE' OR 'EF' OR 'FD' OR
                                       'PP' OR 'CC' OR 'IE'  OR
    WS-DEST-CD     = 'TC04A' OR 'TC11A' OR '0100A'  ))
                          OR
    (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 30 AND
    (AIHD-CUST-NO  = 09937 OR 10100 OR 10181 OR 10190  OR
                                       11100 OR 09941 OR 09949 OR
                                       10222 OR 01552 OR 11109  OR
                                       10108 OR
     WS-DEST-CD = '04179' OR '12014' OR 'LSC07' OR '31   '))
                            OR
     (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 18 AND
      (WS-DEST-CD = '48063' OR '17576'))
        perform XXXX
end-if

I am NOT proposing that the above is "elegant" code, but at least it has
removed the redundant tests (or it should have, if I got my sums
right...<G>). This in turn removes the "precedence" which someone else in
the thread has already identified.

In an on-line environment the tests could be prioritised and shuffled around
the "OR"s so that the most likely events were tested for first; in Batch, it
really doesn't matter...

I agree that a better approach would be to break it down and derive "logic
values" which could be tested.

Thanks for an interesting 20 minutes...<G>

Pete.

YukonMama wrote in message <20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com>...
>We get a lot of students either posting or lurking here so the other day
while
>working on a particularly nasty program (8000 some lines of spaghetti code
with
>polish logic) I came across this particular IF statement no less than 9
times
>in the program and thought they would like to see what's in store for them.
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>There are lower case x's at the PERFORM to indicate that different
paragraphs
>are performed each time the IF appeared in the program.  The paragraphs
were
>identical except for one or two variables from the map (this is a CICS
>program).  These variables are actually an occurance but the map is not
>redefined so each time the field in question is used such as QTY1 or QTY2
the
>paragraph is duplicated.  Makes maintenance a real nightmare.
>
>You will also notice that there are a few ORs that preclude the execution
of
>other ORs (that is it doesn't matter as something before it takes
precedent -
>assignment: see if you can find it).
>
>The fields being tested are in the header record which is read at the
beginning
>of the program.  The IF statement is now reworked and a switch set if
>everything makes it thru the tangle and the switch checked where each of
the
>original IFs were.  Just my part of making this nightmare a bit more
managable.
>
>Eileen
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-13T15:27:10+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ad674e8_4@my.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com> <3ad47463_1@anonymous>`

```
I gave some more thought to this...

While most of the thread is wailing about what awful code it is, and
speculating about how it came to be and what would be a much better solution
(all fine, as far as it goes...) it occurred to me that there is a TON of
this stuff sitting around on mainframes (and PCs probably...) and it won't
go away and it won't get re-written (well, not overnight , anyway).

The 20 minute exercise I did which produced this...

if AIHD-SALES-CLS = 11 OR 34 OR 61 OR 35
                         OR
    SPECIAL-CHRY-CLS
                          OR
    AIHD-CUST-NO = 08221 OR 05659 OR 00879 OR
                     08228 OR 10418 OR 11010 OR
                     08288
                          OR
    (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 14 AND
    (WS-DEST-CD-1 = 'AP' OR 'WE' OR 'EE' OR 'EF' OR 'FD' OR
                                       'PP' OR 'CC' OR 'IE'  OR
    WS-DEST-CD     = 'TC04A' OR 'TC11A' OR '0100A'  ))
                          OR
    (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 30 AND
    (AIHD-CUST-NO  = 09937 OR 10100 OR 10181 OR 10190  OR
                                       11100 OR 09941 OR 09949 OR
                                       10222 OR 01552 OR 11109  OR
                                       10108 OR
     WS-DEST-CD = '04179' OR '12014' OR 'LSC07' OR '31   '))
                            OR
     (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 18 AND
      (WS-DEST-CD = '48063' OR '17576'))
        perform XXXX
end-if

from this...

>
>IF (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 11 OR 34 OR 61) OR
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

...could be automated and done in thousandths of a second.

It would involve converting the conditions to symbolic logic, applying
Boolean Algebra to the symbolic logic to simplify it, then converting the
simplified symbolic logic back to the original conditions, having removed
the unnecessary ones and added new ones which represent combinations of old
ones derived from the simplification.

Of course the same principles would handle much more complex tangles than
the above (especially where negations of existing conditions where also
thrown into the mix).

Three questions:

1. Does anybody know of any source manipulation packages which can do this?

2. Does anybody know of any potential sites where such a tool could be sold?
(I guess Eileen's would be one...<G>)

3. Anybody prepared to help write it, as a spare time back burner project
with the only return being a share in the profits (if any)?

(You would need to state that you have no objection to working with someone
who stands accused of having a retarded NZ education, and a questionable
grasp of logic...at least if this NG is anything to go by...(Don't bother
applying, Jerry...<G>))

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-13T05:05:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AD688FF.3C5A2E62@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com> <3ad47463_1@anonymous> <3ad674e8_4@my.newsfeeds.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> I gave some more thought to this...
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> go away and it won't get re-written (well, not overnight , anyway).

In view of what you've written I've given it some more thought too. The long
term, as Richard and I have suggested, is a flag(s) in records to get at these
conditons. That may or may not be possible.

Your suggestion may turn out a very elegant coding solution, in fact so elegant,
once introduced, a future coder may not want to even tinker with it - not being
sure what his current tinkering will do <G>

So some suggestions :-

1. Change the program to have table(s) to access the specifics used in the
'Ugly' code. Doesn't happen every day, but every time a new addition is made, it
does mean a re-compile. (An alternative to this would be as a forerunner, read
in parameters to currently fill the condition table).

2. Consider use of those file flags - some assumptions here, and it may even
necessitate file reads, which the 'Ugly' program doesn't currently do. Given
that the appropriate file record has a 'filler' either pic x or pic 9, or even
an existing field where you could 'pinch' the last 1 or 2 characters :-

(a).    - Read infile and initialize the new flag field as a pic x space or pic
9 zero

(b).    - 'Assuming' only one program 'edits' the file containing the new
flag(s) -  change the edit program.

(c).    -  Now use the revised edit program to put 'valid flags' into the file
generated    by  (a)  for records you wish to capture (records not appropriate
have either zero or  space flags).

OK (a) - Let's assume it is a file with 30 million customers, (to be extreme),
having tested on a reasonable sample file to prove it - it is only one exercise
to make that change - even if it takes 'x' hours.

And the result is you have something designed logically as well as programmed
logically.

But of course I see the gleam in your eye - let's come up with a nifty
programming solution <G>

Jimmy
```

It would definitely turn out an elegant coding solution; mathematically
pure. The point is you could tinker with it to your heart's content, adding
all the rules you (or the Business or anyone else) could think of, then run
it through the "logic engine" and get mathematically pure, non-redundant
code. It would still need manual optimizing for things the Engine could not
be aware of, like conditional priorities in on-line environments (if anybody
still cares about things like that...)

Actually, when I put it like that, it sounds a bit like a 4GL or
IEF...p'raps it isn't such a good idea after all...<G>

Also, the majority of programmers nowadays don't write stuff like that in
the first place, do they...? Not much point in a logic optimizer if  there's
nothing to optimize...

It is possible that such a tool could be a useful part of another tool which
was employed leveraging and re-factoring Legacy code. Maybe prior to
wrapping the old code as components for re-deployment on the Network (see my
article on www.aboutlegacycoding.com)
