<a id="t-033bb040ca"></a>
## Thread: NJE from Non IBM platforms — 6 msgs

### NJE from Non IBM platforms

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T12:32:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ae02bf2.121186351@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Does anyone have a link/resource for NJE between an IBM mainframe host
and "anything" else?  Our mainframe (P/390) can't do it with the
hardware/software installed -- and the PTB won't let us use it anyway
(it will be decomissioned today).  But we have an NJE need.  We can
use NT, VMS, UNIX - would even consider Linux I think.

Anyone have any suggestions?
```

#### ↳ Re: NJE from Non IBM platforms

- **From:** Merlin43PhD@netscape.net (Dwight Scott Miller)
- **Date:** 2001-04-22T00:21:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-dd6UcrXeLPdG@localhost>`
- **References:** `<3ae02bf2.121186351@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
On Sun, 20 Apr 3901 12:32:38, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) 
wrote:

> Does anyone have a link/resource for NJE between an IBM mainframe host
> and "anything" else?  Our mainframe (P/390) can't do it with the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone have any suggestions?

Thane,

NJE *REQUIRES* that both ends be JES2 or JES3 peers.  Anythng else is 
RJE.  I expect that anything that has gotten around IBM to the extent 
that it can be either, will be more expensive than keeping Roadrunner 
on-line.  Sorry, but I told them that long ago.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NJE from Non IBM platforms

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-04-22T01:02:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AE22DFE.87919928@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3ae02bf2.121186351@news1.attglobal.net> <B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-dd6UcrXeLPdG@localhost>`

```
Check on IBM-main.  I know that VM and VSE (I may have been at the first
site with JES3 talking to VSE given the bug in the signon process we
found) both can handle NJE and that the protocols have been documented
in excruciating detail somewhere (I think an IBM orange or redbook). I
believe that someone has implemented NJE for VMS and I would also check
to see if AIX can handle it.  It could be an interesting challenge for
the Linux folk.  They might even get IBM help.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, Inc.
   cfmtech@istar.ca or clark.morris@ns.sympatico.ca

Dwight Scott Miller wrote:
> 
> On Sun, 20 Apr 3901 12:32:38, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> on-line.  Sorry, but I told them that long ago.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NJE from Non IBM platforms

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-04-22T01:03:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AE22E0B.4E42DC27@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3ae02bf2.121186351@news1.attglobal.net> <B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-dd6UcrXeLPdG@localhost>`

```
Check on IBM-main.  I know that VM and VSE (I may have been at the first
site with JES3 talking to VSE given the bug in the signon process we
found) both can handle NJE and that the protocols have been documented
in excruciating detail somewhere (I think an IBM orange or redbook). I
believe that someone has implemented NJE for VMS and I would also check
to see if AIX can handle it.  It could be an interesting challenge for
the Linux folk.  They might even get IBM help.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, Inc.
   cfmtech@istar.ca or clark.morris@ns.sympatico.ca
```

<a id="t-a1431e239c"></a>
## Thread: Software production — 8 msgs

### Software production

- **From:** "Bill Gentry" <billgentry2@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T14:38:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ARXD6.42769$C55.4118046@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```
Hello COBOL'ers,

I've been a frequent lurker and occasional contributor to this news-group
the past year or so, and I have an issue that many of you may be interested
in and possibly may even be able to help me out with!

I've been writing COBOL for the past 11 years, and I've been an independent
consultant for the past 3.   I primarily do modifications to vendor COBOL
software packages.

In any case, I've recently come up with a few ideas for developing software
on my own.  I've been looking into getting some business financing so I can
build my company and explore these opportunities.

Unfortunately, I've run into some obstacles in getting a bank to underwrite
my project.   They want a very detailed business plan, including pro forma
projections, but only give me a vague idea of what that should contain.   I
like to think I'm competent with the technical stuff, but I'm in over my
head with the business planning issues.

Here are my questions:  Have any of you guys gone this route, or know anyone
who has?  How did they get financing?    What kind of business plan did they
put together?   Are there any good web-sites, or consulting firms, that I
could get some direction from?

( BTW, I subscribed to some of the "Business" newsgroups, but they were just
filled with worthless Spam! )

Thanks COBOL'ers
BG
```

#### ↳ Re: Software production

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T14:45:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0YXD6.609193$f36.17824063@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<ARXD6.42769$C55.4118046@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```
I've been on that course for about 20 years now, and have gone through the
same steps.  It is a huge job, in fact one that I have always looked at as a
full time job on its own.  Have you considered a full time partner that is
completely bussiness oriented, and will handle the marketing?

Writing the stuff is far less than 1/2 the work.  Running the business,
financing, and marketing are at least 75%.

"Bill Gentry" <billgentry2@home.com> wrote in message
news:ARXD6.42769$C55.4118046@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Software production

- **From:** "Bill Gentry" <billgentry2@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T15:04:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2eYD6.42860$C55.4125677@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`
- **References:** `<ARXD6.42769$C55.4118046@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <0YXD6.609193$f36.17824063@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Thanks for the reply... that's kind of what I thought.   I do know a couple
of people that would be good at that.   One problem is, it's the classic
chicken vs. egg argument:  I'd have to hire someone to handle the business
side to get financing, but at the same time I'd have to get some financing
to hire a person for the business side.   Also, once I get the financing,
I'd lock myself in my basement for about 6 months to work the stuff up, and
I'm not sure what I'd do with my "business guy" while all that was going on.

Well, I'm sure I'll work it out.

BG
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

```
Well, if you are a gambler, and your partner is a gambler, then the correct
method is for the marketing man to use the same 6 months to get the
marketing together.  Putting a brochure together, getting a web page in
place, arranging for the advertizing to be all set up the day the product is
ready, etc., will also be at least a six month job. Of course, if all that
gets done, and your software is not on a mailable CD at the end of it, then
you have just pissed away a whole lot of money.
```

```
price).
```

#### ↳ Re: Software production

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-04-21T19:23:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ae1d38a.1046545813@news>`
- **References:** `<ARXD6.42769$C55.4118046@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```
Hi, Bill,

My 2 cents' worth, having been involved in a couple of startups over
the years:

1.  Don't put all your money into a startup, no matter how convinced
you are that it's going to fly.  The principals are always far more
optimistic than outsiders.  Which, incidentally, is why your bank is
being somewhat recalcitrant about advancing funds.  Bear in mind that
most startups, fail within a couple of years.

What would you do if a stranger came to you with a similar idea and
wanted YOU to hand over some $$$ to get started?  Would you?

We were once involved in a joint venture where we provided hardware
and technical stuff and the other J/V partner provided the facility
for an educational venture.    He had a great looking business plan,
profit projections, etc., etc.  On paper it looked like a gold mine,
so we negotiated a contract and got to work with him.

The portion we were involved in was a huge success, but the partner's
business plan called for "hitting the ground running" so he cashed in
virtually everything he had to get the thing going.  He rented a huge
amount of space, put in lots of expensive furniture, spent tons on
advertising... and diverted the profits from the J/V into keeping the
non-J/V portion afloat.  He also talked a few other businesses into
extending rather large lines of credit for rent, furnishings, and so
on.  We came way too close to losing about $100K on that debacle.

Moral of the story?  Even the best-conceived startups are still
extremely risky, and there are always unanticipated problems.
Furthermore, your assumptions just may not reflect reality.  Don't
blow all your money on starting up; you might lose it all. 

2.  Do you have a real product, or just an idea?  I can tell you from
over fifteen years of proprietary software experience that it's one
thing to dream it up and quite another to actually put a product
together, complete with good documentation, tech support, etc.  

You also need to deal responsively with bugs you never dreamed of once
your product hits the street.  Beta testing never flushes them all
out, and you need to fix them - fast - once you start taking money for
your product.

Not to mention marketing, though that has become much easier via the
Internet, than it used to be.  It takes many talents to bring all of
the pieces together.  

3.  Is your idea unique?  Is it new?  Is it sufficiently complex that
you can be fairly safe from copycats?  Our first product (see below)
was all of the above.  The same market today has a number of players,
and our latest product, even though it's far more powerful, complex,
and sophisticated than the first (successful) one, is doing OK but not
making us millionaires - yet <G>.  

It's far, far more difficult to dominate a market where there are
superficially similar, competing, established products - regardless of
how good yours is.  

4.  Proprietary software can be a very good business.  It's great fun
once it takes off.  You write something once and, hopefully, sell it
many times over.  You get to control the product (within certain
limits).  It's both challenging and interesting.

Our first product, written in the mid-80's was a character mode screen
manager for COBOL on the PC.  It was the first one out there, and when
we showed it to Marc Sokol, president of Realia, he saw the potential
and encouraged us to take it to market.  (At the time, one of the big
obstacles to COBOL development on the PC was the lack of an
easy-to-use screen manager).   So, Marc realized our product could
help his COBOL sales.  

When the product was ready to hit the street, Realia began referring
people to us for the screen management, and sales took off like a
rocket - both ours and Realia's.  They ultimately marketed ScreenIO
under their label.

This was a great thing for us, because we had a symbiotic relationship
with a major player - which made our product profitable within a
matter of months.  We did not have any outside financing, all we had
in it was about 1,000 hours of our (spare?) time, and we didn't need
to spend a ton of money on advertising.  

If you can find a similar benefactor for your product, it could be one
of the better ways to get off the ground without taking a lot of
financial risk.

Oh, yeah, we worked our butts off that first year.  It was not at all
unusual for me to arrive at the office at 8AM and go home at 3, 4, or
5 the next morning - seven days a week.  I don't think I'd be willing
to make that kind of sacrifice today, but I was fifteen years younger
then.

I'm not trying to discourage you.  If you've got a great idea, go for
it by all means.

I would suggest, however, that if you can develop it in your spare
time and find a few customers who will work with you through the
development phase, you'll be risking far less - and if your product is
good, you'll have proof of it when you start going after a mass
market.  You'll have a track record that you can take to your bank or
to investors.  

If you can generate income as you go, you may not even need outside
funds - and, frankly, that's the best way to go, in my opinion...

Best of luck,
Kevin



On Fri, 20 Apr 2001 14:38:24 GMT, "Bill Gentry" <billgentry2@home.com>
wrote:

>In any case, I've recently come up with a few ideas for developing software
>on my own.  I've been looking into getting some business financing so I can
```

- **From:** "Bill Gentry" <billgentry2@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-22T14:37:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o0CE6.55822$C55.5239393@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`
- **References:** `<ARXD6.42769$C55.4118046@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3ae1d38a.1046545813@news>`

```
Hi Kevin,

Thanks for the input.   Actually, you're last point was something I'd
thought of... I know of one organization ( a current consulting client )
that would be interested in partnering up to develop this thing.

Also, at this point I've decided to consult part time just to keep the bills
paid, and spend the rest of my time working on the new product.   I think
I'd need to develop a working prototype that would at least give people an
IDEA of what it could do before I looked for any partners/underwriters.

I think at this point, trying to deal with a bank is a wash-out:   You're
right, from their perspective it's probably not a good investment of their
money.

I'll keep you posted.
BG
```

<a id="t-4878dca41a"></a>
## Thread: Another gentleman (?) of leisure — 1 msgs

### Another gentleman (?) of leisure

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T21:43:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r42E6.22419$181.4296324@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
Well - I finally went and did it. I decomissioned myself out of a job.

Let this be a lesson to all those who, like me, enjoy removing redundant
code, tuning processes and otherwise improving the world. I decomissioned
one finally piece of code, and got to the end date on my contract.

I even saw a $500,00 annual saving on an application that currently costs
about $800,000 - all for about 40 hours labor.They were busily asking me
how to do this, as they gently pushed me out the door.

I saw a friend outside, who'd been away until today - he said "this must be
your
last day; because you've got a box under your arm and a smile on you face!".

Now for some serious doing nothing for the Summer!
```

---

<a id="t-f58f6e2865"></a>
## Thread: Cobol, the legacy — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Cobol, the legacy

- **From:** chims1@aol.com (ChimS1)
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T23:44:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010420194401.11254.00000295@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
Back in the early 80's when I did my coding in
Assembler, I had the attitude that Cobol is not
a real computing language simply because it
has to go through many other translations and
it lacks the cryptic mystery of a binary machine.
With the shift of the U. S. economy, the proliferation
of PC's and its graphical user interface, and the
more accepted practice of structured programming,
Cobol has become the only computer language
that closely resembles its human counterpart of
English and is the most frequently used computing
instructions that direct business information in many
larger shops in America.

Some people complain that Cobol can be, at times,
verbose and it lacks the robust efficiency of lower level
machine language. The absence of arcane and esoteric
flavor allows non-practitioners to wade into the field of
exclusive association among computing brahmans,
thus polluting the environment, and adulterating the
professional ethics of real artists and practitioners.

Call it a legacy or call it a dying dinosaur. I call it a
practical approach to making a living. It's much easier
than working under the hot tropical sun and under the
guns of the Khmer Rouge in Cambodia. Since the early
80's, Cobol has provided me a steady flow of income to
pay my bills. Through the medium of Cobol, I've come
to learn the different concepts of business applications
such as insurance, banking, finance, and distribution.
Just before last year, during the Y2K bridge to the new
millennium, Cobol had been re-discovered by mainframe
enthusiasts and big time decision makers that it has
remained the main driver of many complex applications
in American business and industry.

With the financial collapse of e-commerce and the
reluctance of investors to throw in new money for
dot.com companies to expand their business operations,
Cobol has once again learned to survive the onslaught
of pc's novelty, glittering glamour of the web, and its
own abend-aids. With its new interface to relational
database, its usual coupling for online transactions of
CICS, Cobol is still the language of business computing
in most larger shops in America.

The last time I had the opportunity to use EVALUATE/
END-EVALUATE, in-line performed paragraphs, and
above the clouds storage allocation, I felt the glimpse
of eternal happiness to see that God had breathed new
life into Cobol over the years. In spite of the bad rap that
Cobol has got from its new brethren that used to be
window washers, coffee makers, and garage mechanics,
it's the computing language that has the semantics of
rich flavor and long tradition of values and proved ethical
practices.

I've made my living with Cobol. I've paid my bills,
supported the family, and raised the kids. But most
certainly, I've learned the business of living in a more
complex society such as America. Throughout the
years, I've come to meet interesting people in different
departments of the corporations, hobnob with business
managers at many levels, and exchange several long
life-threatening conversations with production control
operators. As of today, several thousands of executable
Cobol load modules are still running the business of
America. Contrary to the point of views of its opponents,
Cobol will not be dying in any immediate future. Some
20 years ago, I was told that Cobol was dying. As I left
the office this evening, I saw people were fine tuning their
Cobol codes and migrating their load modules to the
production environment. 
 
Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, we're here to share
and exchange the information about the vehicle of our
survival. I know through conversation with consultants,
head hunters, and simple folks across the streets in
America that Cobol programmers are out of work.
After putting some long years into the practices of
Cobol coding, people are getting laid off, summarily
dismissed, and being treated as a useless piece of
furniture.

Today, after more than 20 years later, I can assure
you that Cobol is still alive and kicking. The realities,
however, are challenging. Cobol is facing competitive
pressure from all sides. Just like someone has
suggested in this newsgroup, bring down the price
of Cobol compiler, borrow some features of Visual
Basic, and make it more portable across various
platforms, then we can be sure that competitive
threat will only create more challenging opportunities
for Cobol folks over the long run.

It has been an outstanding legacy. In Richardson,
Texas, roads are named after the legacy. Let's work
to make it last. Just like an old wine, I hope that
Cobol will continue to improve its flavor and taste
with the passing of time. Have Cobol will certainly
travel. And that's not the end of story.

Chim
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol, the legacy

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xi4E6.11665$Jh5.12401634@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<20010420194401.11254.00000295@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
nothing to add -- just wanted to say that i enjoyed reading that (extremely

thanks.

Gazaloo.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol, the legacy

- **From:** awkisall <awkisall@qwest.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-20T23:30:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AE128E9.AF84B52@qwest.net>`
- **References:** `<20010420194401.11254.00000295@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
wow!

Consider me a Y2K baby.  I was a network administrator and totally PC
oriented when our organization ran out of programmers and began
recruiting.  I'd always been captivated by the idea of being a
"programmer" and jumped at the chance and learned COBOL fast and on the
fly.  When the dust settled hundreds of people went elsewhere, not
necessarily goodwhere; I was fortunate enough that I'd done well enough
that I'm still working and learning and I really give a shit but also
don't give a shit about what people say about good-bye to COBOL, because
it's here and lasting.

Last week I was transferred.  Now I'm learning PowerBuilder and Sybase
and all this cool stuff, and it's really exciting and back to PC, so I
feel like a duck just landed, but  the tradeoff is the user front-end
for the programmer's backside.  The "English-like" aspects of COBOL will
never be equaled by any other language, and its (COBOL's) power remains.

Now we do open server, and guess what? after all the GUI and shit for
syntax, the gateway falls upon COBOL.

Please accept my most sincere regards.
```

```
Well said, sir...  After all these years you know that
people will say, and have said Cobol is going to die,
and the big iron is going to die.  These kinds of
predictions, like the psychic hotline, are repeated
routinely at regular intervals, usually once every
few months you may hear rumblings to that effect.
The perspective that is lacking in younger people
today is that they compare the shiniest and newest
languages and machines with the 1960's implemen-
tations of languages and machines.  I don't think
of Networking people as working on 1980's
286's with token-ring networks.  I think the two
camps need to work closer together so the
appropriate applications of EACH platform and
software can be carried out.
```

```
the default folder is ?

Bob Hennessey
```

```
Fujitsu did offer a version of Cobol 3.0 that ran on Windows 3.1 along
with the Win95 versions.  This was dropped in version 4.  The free
student version is Win95/NT only.

If you asked nicely at Fujitsu (www.adtools.com) they might send you an
old version 3 CD with the Windows 3.1 system on it, or they may no
longer have this.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-04-23T17:54:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9c1q91$67g$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<%XTE6.1559$Ln6.96436@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com>`

```
In article <%XTE6.1559$Ln6.96436@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com>, dw.norris
<dw.norris@ntlworld.com> writes:
>Hi Folkies
>
>I am about to start as a trainee for a major company doing COBOL.  I am not
>a techie person.  What should I expect, and what is the logical career
>progression for someone like me that just wants to "dip into" programming.

I wouldn't expect any company to expect that much out of a trainee.  It takes
months just to learn how their file system works and what kind of standards
they use in their shop, Not to mention the Operation System Specific Tasks.
Every shop is a little different.  In a large shop you might be doing SQL, or
database stuff.  They may force all new programmers to go through a training
program to learn the basics.
```

```
devices (single bit on/off for motors, lights, etc.).  I have been using
assembler routines to toggle that stuff from Cobol.
```

<a id="t-860d7dbf79"></a>
## Thread: XML and COBOL — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### XML and COBOL

- **From:** Charles Giguere<cgiguere@iname.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-23T20:29:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Cg0F6.2378$QV4.195924@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Hi everyone,

We are looking at implementing the usage of XML in our systems and since
most of our legacy data and applications (written mostly in COBOL) are on
an OS/390 platform, we need to move data from it to an NT box (and
eventually to partners).

On the NT side we plan to use MSXML 3.0 from Microsoft since it�s the most
advanced in terms of stability and conformance with the W3C standards
(XML, DTD, XSL).  But on the OS/390 side the solutions seems not so
trivial.

We plan to use the XMLife vocabulary (developed by ACORD) and we have
these solutions in mind and I would like to know your comments about them
and your recommendations :

1. Create XML files directly from a COBOL application (it is simple and
easy, but it is difficult to ensure the validity of the XML generated
according to a DTD).
2. Use the XML4COBOL parser/generator product (from MAAS) (we don�t know
if it complies with the W3C DOM specifications and if it is easy to use).
3. Install JAVA on the OS/390 to have access to the myriad of XML tools
available on this platform (we don�t have any JAVA expertise, but it could
allow us to concentrate more on business logic than on the creation of XML
files).
4. Convert the data from the OS/390 to XML on the NT platform (we are
limiting the benefits of using XML, since we cannot send an XML file
directly from the OS/390 to our partners, we must convert them on NT box
before).

What do you think of these solutions ? Am I missing something ?  Which is
our best choice considering we have a considerable amount of data and
applications on OS/390 ?

Charles.
```

#### ↳ Re: XML and COBOL

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-23T23:04:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ux2F6.38010$_P.10548604@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<Cg0F6.2378$QV4.195924@www.newsranger.com>`

```
You may also want to consider MQ Series. IBM was saying late last year that forthcoming versions would able to help with XML-to-structure conversions. 

If it were me, I'd probably not want to chew up mainframe MIPS doing data conversion, all things being equal. As well, there are some security benefits to be had hiding your mainframe behind another machine.

The last time I looked at XML4COBOL it didn't support XMLSchema. I think their approach offers some performance advantages at the expense of flexibility. Since it builds an assembler module to match a specific DTD, I don't know what happens if somebody starts sending you XML where the DTD differs from one  you're prepared to handle. Of course, the vendor (MAAS?)  would be able to provide more accurate information than me. Your mileage may vary.

- Karl


"Charles Giguere" <cgiguere@iname.com> wrote in message news:Cg0F6.2378$QV4.195924@www.newsranger.com...
> Hi everyone,
> 
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: XML and COBOL

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-04-28T13:32:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aeac469.127302488@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<Cg0F6.2378$QV4.195924@www.newsranger.com> <Ux2F6.38010$_P.10548604@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
There are a whole host of issues, but I am happy the original poster
understands them!

Some additional thoughts follow.  XML only a standard for defiing a
framework.  You or your partners will be responsible for defining the
actual "standard" for what DTD's your particular application will
support.  What's interesting so far is that the same data, fed to two
different XML converters, even when the "end result" definiton (the
DTD's you define) for your XML are the same - the XML itself is
different.

My take on this is that the best way to make USE of XML is actually in
the DOM.  The Document Object Model and XML are closely linked.  Once
you "feed" the XML into the DOM - you manipulate the data directly in
the DOM doing whatever you want with it - and then you generate XML,
or actually anything you like - from the DOM. 

To get the full advantage of this - you need to have some Object
Orientation knowledge and make use of the OO features offered on the
various platforms involved.  

The ENTIRE beauty of XML is tied up in the DOM.  Remove the DOM and
you are left with just another way to describe data.

On Mon, 23 Apr 2001 23:04:20 GMT, "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
wrote:

>You may also want to consider MQ Series. IBM was saying late last year =
>that forthcoming versions would able to help with XML-to-structure =
…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

### table help needed for homework

- **From:** philippemorin@videotron.ca (Philippe Morin)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1V2F6.316$231.163449@wagner.videotron.net>`

```
Hi

I'm newbie to cobol and i got a final homework to make to my university 
class.

The thing is that i dont know how to put values entered by the user in a 
table

the table is stored in a indexed-sequential file

like:

fd product.
    	01 line-procuct occurs 10 times.
    	    	05 product-id    	    	pic 9(3).
    	    	05 product-price    	pic 99V99.
    	    	05 product-name    	pic x(12).

...

accept product-id.
accept product-price.
accept product-name.

thanks!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: table help needed for homework

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-24T17:27:05+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<988090582.544793143@aklobs.org.nz>`
- **References:** `<1V2F6.316$231.163449@wagner.videotron.net> <Je3F6.2436$DW1.108573@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

>Philippe Morin <philippemorin@videotron.ca> wrote:
>I'm newbie to cobol and i got a final homework to make to my university 
>class.
>The thing is that i dont know how to put values entered by the user in a 
>table

>the table is stored in a indexed-sequential file

Why ?  What is the point of storing the _table_ in an ISAM file as a table?  In
any sensible system the ISAM file would be used to store _records_, each of
which is identified by the product-identity used as the key.

Are you sure this is your _final_ homework, you appear to have a ---l-o-n-g---
way to go yet.

> fd product.
>    	01 line-procuct occurs 10 times.

Occurs is illegal on 01 level.  You probably don't want to do this anyway, it
is most likely that you completely misunderstood the question.

>    	    	05 product-id    	    	pic 9(3).
>   	    	05 product-price    	pic 99V99.
>    	    	05 product-name    	pic x(12).

> accept product-id.
> accept product-price.
> accept product-name.

These won't work with the occurs clause because there are ten 'product-id's and
the compiler will complain that it doesn't know which one you mean.

Also what do you expect to be displayed when the user is expected to key in the
'product-id' do you expect the program to come up with a nice message such as
"Key in the product-id now:" ?  How would this happen ?
```

#### ↳ Re: table help needed for homework

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-04-24T00:47:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-dxw87Tq3M17d@24-108-102-82.ivideon.com>`
- **References:** `<1V2F6.316$231.163449@wagner.videotron.net>`

```
PERFORM the accept loop ten times using subscripting to place the
data into the table. Then WRITE the record to the file.

Lorne
```

#### ↳ Re: table help needed for homework

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-24T07:16:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AE57CA4.1C09AEEF@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<1V2F6.316$231.163449@wagner.videotron.net>`

```
Philippe Morin wrote:

> Hi
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the table is stored in a indexed-sequential file

An indexed file is used the same way as a table is, but I haven't heard
anybody call it a table.  But I get what you mean.

> like:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 05 product-name         pic x(12).

This seems odd.  What is the key of this indexed  file?  Why does this file
have an occurs 10 for its values?   I am inferring that you know that tables
have occurs, so you stuck one in the file description.   But in an indexed
file, each record is the equivalent of an occurs, with one (or more) of the
fields the key.  I suspect you want to remove the "occurs 10 times", and
make the product-id the unique key.


> ...
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks!

You need to decide whether you always create your file from scratch, or
whether this program adds records to an existing file when run repeatedly.
This will determine how you open the file.

Look up how to define the file with a key.  Look up how to open the file.
Decide how to tell when you are done entering products (to exit the
program).

Then write a program as follows:

Initialize - open the file, set up counters, display instructions, etc.
Loop - perform write-loop until exit conditions are met.
Finalize - close the file, display counts, etc. then stop

Write this, get it compiled cleanly, and let us know where you are having
problems.
```

#### ↳ Re: table help needed for homework

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-24T08:35:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ifF6.1419$NH5.94656@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<1V2F6.316$231.163449@wagner.videotron.net>`

```
"Philippe Morin" <philippemorin@videotron.ca> wrote:
>
> I'm newbie to cobol and i got a final homework to make to my university
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the table is stored in a indexed-sequential file

COBOL supports 'Indexed Files'.  The term 'indexed-sequential' refers
to a specific method of organizing index tables internally, which is
incidental and completely dependent on the compiler vendor.  Some
compilers generate code that store Indexed Files using indexed-sequential,
but some use b-tree or numerous other methods.  On computer systems that
support built-in database capability, their compilers often use the
built-in database support to store Indexed Files.  Some compilers permit
the use of third party database software (e.g. Btrieve) to store Indexed
Files.

> like:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>         05 product-name    pic x(12).

No key.
Can't use OCCURS on an 01 level entry.

> ...
>
> accept product-id.
> accept product-price.
> accept product-name.

Are you only going to get one entry from the user?

Sorry, but if this is your 'final' assignment, and you have been
faithfully studying, ask for your money back, because I could teach
someone more about COBOL in one day then you appear to have learned.

Read about subscripting, looping, PERFORM, file definition and I/O,
records, and a host of other topics that you should have already
covered in class.
```

#### ↳ Re: table help needed for homework

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-04-24T20:55:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9c4p8c$dcp$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<1V2F6.316$231.163449@wagner.videotron.net>`

```
In article <1V2F6.316$231.163449@wagner.videotron.net>, Philippe Morin
<philippemorin@videotron.ca> writes:
>Hi
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>thanks!

You probably want LINE-PRODUCT OCCURS 10 TIMES.

This means there are 10 items called LINE-PRODUCT, numbered
1 thru 10.  Accessing them requires the use of a "sub-script".
Example:  

    MOVE WS-LINE-PRODUCT TO LINE-PRODUCT (1).

However if you are using some variable for 1 thru 10 to indicate
which LINE-PRODUCT it is like a counter or some numeric value
in a field you can use a variable instead.
Example: 

    MOVE WS-LINE-PRODUCT TO LINE-PRODUCT (SUB).

This works best if you make a structure exacly like LINE-PRODUCT
and call it WS-LINE-PRODUCT without the occurance so that when you
accept the values it goes into a structure the same size as
your LINE-PRODUCT.  Then you move the group as a group move instead
of moving each individual field. It is a common practice to use the
prefix "WS" for temporary (Working-Storage) storage fields.
EXAMPLE:

>fd product.
>    	01 line-product occurs 10 times.
>    	    	05 product-id    	pic 9(3).
>    	    	05 product-price    	pic 99V99.
>    	    	05 product-name    	pic x(12).

        01 ws-counters.
                05 sub                  pic 9(02) value 1.

   	01 ws-line-product.
    	    	05 ws-product-id    	pic 9(3).
    	    	05 ws-product-price    	pic 99V99.
    	    	05 ws-product-name    	pic x(12).

accept ws-product-id.
accept ws-product-price.
accept ws-product-name.
MOVE WS-LINE-PRODUCT TO LINE-PRODUCT (sub).   (this is a group move)
compute sub = (sub + 1).

Something like that.

A Sub-Script must be numeric.
A Sub-Script must be larger than 1.
A Sub-Script must not be larger than the occurs clause.
Otherwise you will receive a sub-script error.

This is just one way to do it.  It might work fine using a subscript
in the accept line, but I have never tried that.

Charles

made through NewsOne.Net violate posting guidelines, email abuse@newsone.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: table help needed for homework

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-04-26T02:04:00+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ae6dda2_3@my.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<1V2F6.316$231.163449@wagner.videotron.net> <3AE5FD47.22D59A0C@home.com>`

```
Actually Jimmy,

All of the well-meaning posts here in response to Philippe's post are just a
waste of time.

Philippe is very naughty boy who has not done his COBOL homework (or even
his class work).

He posted the exact same notice into  the Tutoraid newsgroup which I
sometimes use to help students, and received a detailed piece of code (not
from me, I hasten to add) showing the use of Perform...Varying to load the
table into the indexed File. Although it was posted by a COBOLler who is
usually very good and has a deep and sincere desire to help students, on
this occasion it was, in fact, incorrect.
Normally I would've  (gently) pointed out the errors in it, and suggested an
alternative. But on this occasion I remained silent... Draw your own
conclusions. Philippe is getting what he deserves.

There are two things here:

1. Competent people who want to help, and at the same time demonstrate their
knowledgeability...vanity, if you like. See, if you REALLY wanted to help
students you would NOT spell out detailed solutions and point out errors
which they could get from a listing, if they even bothered to compile the
code.
("Occurs at an 01 level" should not need to be pointed out in this
forum...just as an example. I have never encountered a compiler which
doesn't flag this. So where has Philippe obtained his code from? Another
student, maybe? It is blatantly obvious that no attempt has been made to
compile this rubbish, and such a lack of understanding is completely
unacceptable in someone at "final" level)

As any Teacher knows, there are times when you MUST let the student find out
for themselves. To do otherwise is robbing them of a useful learning
experience. Point the direction, give clues, but don't just show how much
you know about the subject, where students are concerned. (Commercial
enquiries where someone is requesting detailed help, which may indeed
require specialised in-depth knowledge, are another kettle of fish
altogether...)

2. Students who believe the World owes them a living and they don't need to
do any work or apply themselves in any way. The Internet is there to get
answers. There are enough people on the Internet who are keen to demonstrate
their capability with a detailed solution, so why bother doing anything?

Sometimes it is difficult to draw the line, and I believe that on those
occasions it is better to err on the side of Doc Dwarf ..."Do your own
homework."

Doc's admonition may be unpalatable, but it is right on the button for a
certain percentage of students.

Sometimes no help is the best help that can be given.

Pete.

James J. Gavan wrote in message <3AE5FD47.22D59A0C@home.com>...

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: Fujitsu 6.0, alot to be desired..... — 11 msgs

### Fujitsu 6.0, alot to be desired.....

- **From:** yyjohnyy@aol.com (Yy JOHN yy)
- **Date:** 2001-04-24T02:00:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010423220030.24459.00000573@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
     I think I have read this, but loaded Fujitsu on my PC about a week ago,
went to open it up today, and got a message something to the extent : unable to
access it or something.....The sad part, I registered it on that PC, for that
PC when I installed it.  Emailed Fujitsu at 9am this morning, WOW look at that,
12 hours later, havent heard back from them....
     Guess putting Fujitsu on my PC at home is out of the question to?  Thank
god we are not a 24 hour operation.  But would like a little flexibility for
weekends, and nights.  I really dont want to drive an hour to work for a couple
of hours of work.
     I understand they want to collect money from people using thier software,
but the majority of companies are honest.
     I purchased SP2 from flexus,  and thin client from them (10 clients).  Oh
yea, we have 11 clients using thin client, and guess what? no problems at all
with Flexus products.  Called Bob Wolfe at flexus, and he explained they would
rather put out software that works.  While they realize some people are going
to "screw" them, they realize the majority wont.
     I kind of thought it was a good idea that Fujitsu was in bed with Billy
Gates, boy, what was I thinking ?????????
     Windows is unstable enough, why throw a monkey wrench in it Fujitsu ?  I
love using COBOL for window applications, but god, think I would rather just
use CICS, and the good ole mainframe.  I dont think i ever had to reboot the
mainframe.......
    Maybe I will look at using another 32 bit cobol compiler.   
    Just as a side note, had lots of success dealing with Flexus, and thier
consultants there.  Had a problem, emailed them, and had an answer within 2
hours.  By the way, the answer was correct, and worked.  Thanks Flexus.  Just
my 2 cents, if anyone cares........
```

```
tape, ran our "trick" registry restore program and everything was A-OK.

Denny
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu 6.0, alot to be desired.....

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <rwstyles@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-27T04:44:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DO6G6.36575$RF1.3137386@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
> Just curious.  Did you ever get a response back from Fujitsu?
Our company
> has a "scheduled" disaster backup test on May 14th so I was
just wondering
> if support ever got back to you as far as re-licensing.  We'll
be restoring
> our network backups and I need to go through the procedure to
re-license at
> out disaster recovery center, if the restore doesn't work as in
the past.
> Of course, this means that we can't "transfer" said license
since our
> auditors require that we pretend that out operations center was
totally out
> of commission on the day of the test.  I'll let everyone know
after May
> 14th, but in the past with Fujitsu Version 5, we just restored
the backup
> tape, ran our "trick" registry restore program and everything
was A-OK.
> Actually, I've got this "educated hunch" that this will work
with 6.0 also,
> but it would be nice to know ahead of time.
>
> Denny
>
>
    I am not using Fujitsu, but from what I have picked up in
this
newsgroup, it sounds like Fujitsu 6 requires certain small hidden
files to still be in the same exact spot they were before.

    Unless you were using an unusual restore program,
those files will not be on the same sectors as before.

    That said, doesn't it allow you to use it for 30 days?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu 6.0, alot to be desired.....

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-27T07:04:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xedG6.3580$5b1.233884@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<20010423220030.24459.00000573@ng-fx1.aol.com> <9cafuj$6lcg$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <DO6G6.36575$RF1.3137386@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Russell Styles" wrote:
>
>     I am not using Fujitsu, but from what I have picked up in this
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>     That said, doesn't it allow you to use it for 30 days?

Don't know how Fujitsu COBOL 6.0 does its thing (I never intend to
install the thing, even though I have a license), but if it's like
Net Express, you only get the 30 days the first time you install it
on a machine.  If you have ever installed and uninstalled Net Express
on a machine, even an earlier release, you get a 'license expired'
message if you install it again later, until you get a new response
key.  This, I believe, is inexcusable.  If you install a later version
on a machine after having uninstalled an earlier version, is it not
obvious that you aren't trying to simply extend the original 30 day
trial period, but are at least evaluating the new version?  It also
means that you cannot use a given machine for backup purposes more
than once, unless you completely reformat the hard drive.  Since few
of us have an unlimited number of backup computers, and none of us
want (or probably have the time to) format the hard drives and reload
all the software regularly on our backup machines, this severely
limits the 30 day trial period as a backup tool, in real practice.
```

```
same drive,
without removing windows first, will allow Netx to allow the 30
day trial without disturbing most applications, except for
protected
ones of course.  It can sometime be done in an hour or so.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu 6.0, alot to be desired.....

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-28T07:29:53+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AEA6361.BDC29E4D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010423220030.24459.00000573@ng-fx1.aol.com> <9cafuj$6lcg$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <DO6G6.36575$RF1.3137386@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <xedG6.3580$5b1.233884@news1.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Don't know how Fujitsu COBOL 6.0 does its thing (I never intend to
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> than once, unless you completely reformat the hard drive. 

There are utilities which will monitor an install of a product and then
produce a differences file that can be reversed.  The 30 day problem
with NetExpress is probably something left in the registry. These
utilities can sometimes be obtained in free versions, such as on
magazine disks.

> Since few
> of us have an unlimited number of backup computers, and none of us
> want (or probably have the time to) format the hard drives and reload
> all the software regularly on our backup machines, this severely
> limits the 30 day trial period as a backup tool, in real practice.

There should be no problems. with the right tools, of uninstalling and
reinstalling every 30 days.

Fujitsu isn't unbreakable either.
```

<a id="t-77bf969ba9"></a>
## Thread: Cobol on unix — 2 msgs

### Cobol on unix

- **From:** "paul" <paul@pwrr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-24T16:15:18-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tebni9eosfor99@corp.supernews.com>`

```
We are moving toward Oracle and are now running openvms. We may consider
unix, but all of our vms cobol uses simple indexed files...does unix support
indexed files?
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol on unix

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-24T16:41:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%rmF6.134$Lj7.59308@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<tebni9eosfor99@corp.supernews.com>`

```
paul <paul@pwrr.com> wrote in message
news:tebni9eosfor99@corp.supernews.com...
> We are moving toward Oracle and are now running openvms. We may consider
> unix, but all of our vms cobol uses simple indexed files...does unix
support
> indexed files?

Unix does not support indexed files in the same way as VMS.  However, all
COBOL vendors supply an indexed file system for their Unix implementations.
See the COBOL FAQ for a list of COBOL vendors.

Regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02

URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

```
What kind of effieciencies does one have to consider when
using INITIALIZE instead of MOVE to reset a field? Is it
mostly for readability, or really more efficient?
```

#### ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-25T02:13:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AE632DC.31F93BCC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com>`

```


Ubiquitous wrote:

> What kind of effieciencies does one have to consider when
> using INITIALIZE instead of MOVE to reset a field? Is it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    the Internet kind, but I'm sure it's just as tasty as the original"

01 Everything.
    05 CommonFields.
            10 common1    pic x.
            10 common2    pic 9(03).
            10 common3    pic x(05).
            10 common4    pic 9.
    05 flags.
         10  EndofFileFlag        pic 9.
                88  EndofFile        value 0.
                88 NotEndofFile   value 1.
           10 AreaFlag                pic xx.
                88 InvalidArea        value spaces.
                88 NYArea             value "NY".
                88 BostonArea        value "BS".

Initialize Everything   OR :-

 move zeroes to ..........
 move spaces to..........

You go figure !

And as regards your byline - if you had been old enough to remember when
dried egg powder and Spam from Uncle Sam substituted for good ole eggs and
bacon in Blighty - believe me - you wouldn't like Spam either !

A DP from the UK - now in the Great White North <G>
```

#### ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

- **From:** "Ross Burnett" <rossburnett@email.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-25T06:48:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JquF6.20368$Jh5.20037151@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com>`

```
As far as I know, an INITIALIZE results in the same object code as a MOVE.
(How else can a compiler generate a move-constant?) The real benefits are
readability when an INITIALIZE is applied to a group item, and the
elimination of uninitialized fields when items are added later to the group.

Note that an INITIALIZE will not update FILLER or unnamed fields within a
group.  Also, the items of an INITIALIZEd group will not show up in the
cross-reference for that statement (at least, not in IBM Cobol II).

"Ubiquitous" <weberm@polaris.net> wrote in message
news:9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com...
> What kind of effieciencies does one have to consider when
> using INITIALIZE instead of MOVE to reset a field? Is it
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <rwstyles@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-26T05:17:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CbOF6.4205$AU4.350555@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <JquF6.20368$Jh5.20037151@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`

```
    Some compilers on some platforms can produce some really
bad code with initialize.  Sometimes something as simple
(for a group item with both pic x and numeric items) as

MOVE SPACES TO GRP-ITEM.
followed by
INITALIZE GRP-ITEM REPLACING NUMERIC BY ZERO.
can be much more efficent.

You have to check it out case by case.

the

to the group.
>

fields within a

up in the
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-28T21:09:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cft6p$47ag$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <JquF6.20368$Jh5.20037151@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com> <CbOF6.4205$AU4.350555@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
    One advantage of INITIALIZE over MOVE, that no one has mentioned yet, is
if a data item changes its PICTURE clause from alphanumeric to numeric (or
vice versa), there is no need to change the INITIALIZE statement in the
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
```

```
LiamD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

- **From:** "awkisall" <awkisall@qswest.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-28T12:04:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wzEG6.629$qK1.223321@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <JquF6.20368$Jh5.20037151@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`

```
An example I recently encountered where INITIALIZE worked better than MOVE
SPACES TO at a group level:

Using UNSTRING  to convert  ASCII comma-delimited records from an uploaded
PC file, there were dates that were unstrung into their elements (DD, MM,
CCYY). There were FILLER elements (PIC X VALUE '/') in between the date
elements to make dates looking like 03/29/2001.  The records were variable
length so I had to initialize the "unstring-area"--if not just out of good
practice.  If I moved Spaces to the unstring-area prior to performing the
unstring I ended up with dates that looked like 03 29 2001.  As was said,
using Initialize didn't update Filler, so in this case Initialize was
preferable to Move Spaces To because it didn't make the slashes dissappear
and the routine made dates as intended.  Otherwise, it's probably just a
style (or perhaps shop standard) issue.

"Ross Burnett" <rossburnett@email.com> wrote in message
news:JquF6.20368$Jh5.20037151@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com...
> Note that an INITIALIZE will not update FILLER or unnamed fields within a
> group.  Also, the items of an INITIALIZEd group will not show up in the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > mostly for readability, or really more efficient?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-28T19:40:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U0FG6.2260$SZ5.171639@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <JquF6.20368$Jh5.20037151@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com> <wzEG6.629$qK1.223321@news.uswest.net>`

```
There have been perfomance problems with init. They've been working on them and
eventally they'll get it right. One huge advantage of init is that you can
initialize multi-level tables with 1 statement instead of a distracting
subroutine.

Also, though nobody asked, move corr is 100% efficient.

On to SPAM: I read somewhere that Hawaii is the #1 consumer, per capita, of SPAM
in the world. I went to an upscale restaurant there and a fancy SPAM dish was on
the menue. 

Jack
```

```
"awkisall" <awkisall@qswest.net> wrote in message
news:wzEG6.629$qK1.223321@news.uswest.net...
> An example I recently encountered where INITIALIZE worked
better than MOVE
> SPACES TO at a group level:
>
> Using UNSTRING  to convert  ASCII comma-delimited records from
an uploaded
> PC file, there were dates that were unstrung into their
elements (DD, MM,
> CCYY). There were FILLER elements (PIC X VALUE '/') in between
the date
> elements to make dates looking like 03/29/2001.  The records
were variable
> length so I had to initialize the "unstring-area"--if not just
out of good
> practice.  If I moved Spaces to the unstring-area prior to
performing the
> unstring I ended up with dates that looked like 03 29 2001.  As
was said,
> using Initialize didn't update Filler, so in this case
Initialize was
> preferable to Move Spaces To because it didn't make the slashes
dissappear
> and the routine made dates as intended.  Otherwise, it's
probably just a
> style (or perhaps shop standard) issue.
>
> "Ross Burnett" <rossburnett@email.com> wrote in message

fields within a
> > group.  Also, the items of an INITIALIZEd group will not show
up in the
> > cross-reference for that statement (at least, not in IBM
Cobol II).

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-29T01:41:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vjKG6.54890$vg5.2979578@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com>`

```
Well I did a test.

In a PERFORM loop, I contrasted:

   MOVE SPACES TO MYRECORD
   MOVE ALL '0' TO MYRECORD-NUMERICS

with

   INITIALIZE MYRECORD.

The first took 12.47 seconds (including loop overhead). The second
took 56.57 seconds.

Of course this was for a record with about 400 data items.

It was also for TEN MILLION iterations of the loop.

The comparative efficiency of multiple moves vs. INITIALIZE is
real-world irrelevant.


"Ubiquitous" <weberm@polaris.net> wrote in message
news:9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com...
> What kind of effieciencies does one have to consider when
> using INITIALIZE instead of MOVE to reset a field? Is it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    the Internet kind, but I'm sure it's just as tasty as the
original"
>
>
```

```
> Well I did a test.

…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> real-world irrelevant.

Just curious, and did following :-
------------------------------------
Program-id. InitIt.

Working-storage section.
01 myRecord.
   05 field1        pic 9(03).
   05 field2        pic 9(03) comp.
   05 field3        pic 9(03) comp-3.

Procedure Division.

  move spaces to myRecord.

STOP RUN.
-----------------------------------

Although it compiled 'clean' in Net Express, when I looked at fields in
the Animator I got "illegal data in numeric field', for fields 1 and 3
and a value of '224' in field2..

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-29T05:06:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rjNG6.57155$vg5.3090013@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <vjKG6.54890$vg5.2979578@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3AEB80A1.40E0D104@home.com>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

>
> Just curious, and did following :-
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Although it compiled 'clean' in Net Express, when I looked at fields
in
> the Animator I got "illegal data in numeric field', for fields 1 and
3
> and a value of '224' in field2..
>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

Of course you would. Poor record design (just kidding). In our shop,
we try to put alphanumerics at the start of the record and DISPLAY
numerics at the end. So, a typical record layout looks like:

01  MYCUST.
   02  MYCUST-ALPHA
      05  MYCUST-NAME
      05  MYCUST-ADDR1
      05  MYCUST-ADDR2
(etc. followed by)
      05  MCUST-SPARE1 [note 1]
   02  MYCUST-NUMERICS.
      05  MYCUST-CURRENT-BAL     PIC S9(7)V99.
      05  MYCUST-CREDIT-LIM           PIC S9(7)V99.
      05  MYCUST-LAST-PAYMENT
(etc. followed by)
      05  MYCUST-SPARE2 [note 1]

[Note 1] This notation "X-SPAREx' takes the place, in our shop, of the
traditional 'FILLER' used to reserve room in the record for future
expansion. This notation also gives the INITIALIZE verb something
tangible to chew on.

We've considered the possible downsides to this policy. The two
biggest disadvantages, efficiency and economy of disk space, just
don't seem to overcome the resultant ease of use/programming and
elimination of confusion (as you saw in your example). In debugging,
for instance, a human can actually READ the data files instead of
having to decode the elements. In programming, many controls can slurp
up a FILE and display or print the results with no programming or
conversion code on our part.

Maybe our goofy-meter is in the red. But I've seen programmers use a
COMP-3 field for a ZIP code because five-digits packed-decimal uses
two bytes less than DISPLAY and one byte less than BINARY. That, in my
view, seems not only extreme, but silly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-30T07:41:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AED6B7D.AA469D11@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <vjKG6.54890$vg5.2979578@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3AEB80A1.40E0D104@home.com> <rjNG6.57155$vg5.3090013@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Jerry P wrote:

> Maybe our goofy-meter is in the red. But I've seen programmers use a
> COMP-3 field for a ZIP code because five-digits packed-decimal uses
> two bytes less than DISPLAY and one byte less than BINARY. That, in my
> view, seems not only extreme, but silly.

Very silly.    Especially silly when we look at some foreign zips which
contain letters.   Storage is very cheap.  Sometimes performance is
important enough to use binary or packed, but in this case, performance is
hurt - no arithmetic will be done, so there is no performance advantage in
having a packed zip, but there is overhead in packing and unpacking it.

I wonder if this was done because the file didn't use to have zip, and was
added later.  Someone didn't want to restructure the file, and had to
squeeze the data in wherever it could fit.  This used to be common
programming thought a couple of decades ago.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>

```

```

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-29T11:42:39+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n2inetk1q1ijrmdj7nsnpei5nf13kvf4m8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <vjKG6.54890$vg5.2979578@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3AEB8307.6574B76@soltec.net>`

```
On Sat, 28 Apr 2001 21:57:11 -0500 "Steven O. Ellis" <soellis@soltec.net>
wrote:

:>At the risk of seeming to miss your point about the
:>irrelevance of it, it's an interesting test and I
:>have to ask:

:>Which compiler, O/S, and machine did you use?

My guess would be that it didn't matter.

He probably had a structure like.

  01  MYRECORD.
      05  Char1  PIC X(whatever).
      05  Char2  PIC X(whatever).
      05  Char3  PIC X(whatever).
      05  Char4  PIC X(whatever).
      05  Char5  PIC X(whatever).
      05  Char6  PIC X(whatever).
             .
             .
      05  MYRECORD-NUMERICS.
          10  Numeric-1  PIC 9(4).
          10  Numeric-2  PIC 9(4).
          10  Numeric-3  PIC 9(4).
          10  Numeric-4  PIC 9(4).
          10  Numeric-5  PIC 9(4).
             .
             .

Where the MOVE statements each generated on machine code instruction while the
initialize code generated an instruction for each field.

Of course, if the numerics where interspersed within the character fields or
if the numerics weren't USAGE DISPLAY one would not be able to initialize the
fields with those two MOVEs and the run time would approach equal.

:>Jerry P wrote:
 
:>> Well I did a test.
 
:>> In a PERFORM loop, I contrasted:
 
:>>    MOVE SPACES TO MYRECORD
:>>    MOVE ALL '0' TO MYRECORD-NUMERICS
 
:>> with
 
:>>    INITIALIZE MYRECORD.
 
:>> The first took 12.47 seconds (including loop overhead). The second
:>> took 56.57 seconds.
 
:>> Of course this was for a record with about 400 data items.
 
:>> It was also for TEN MILLION iterations of the loop.
 
:>> The comparative efficiency of multiple moves vs. INITIALIZE is
:>> real-world irrelevant.

:>> "Ubiquitous" <weberm@polaris.net> wrote in message
:>> news:9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com...
:>> > What kind of effieciencies does one have to consider when
:>> > using INITIALIZE instead of MOVE to reset a field? Is it
:>> > mostly for readability, or really more efficient?
```

## Thread: TinyCOBOL release 0.52 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Open-source COBOL (GnuCOBOL, OpenCOBOL, TinyCOBOL)`](topics.md#open-source)_

### TinyCOBOL release 0.52

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-24T22:17:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AE633BE.CA423F38@arvotek.net>`

```
Announce: TinyCOBOL release 0.52

The TinyCOBOL compiler version 0.52 is available at the TinyCOBOL home
page (1) and iBiblio (2).

This release contains bugs fixes, and enhancements, including updates to
the preprocessor, math verbs, MOVE (CORRESPONDING option) statement,
COMPUTE (exponent operator)
statement, enhancements to the regression test suite.

Support for the BLANK WHEN ZERO clause, SYNC clause, qualification,
OCCURS...DEPENDING ON... clause (variable-sized tables), RECORD VARYING
... DEPENDING ON ... (variable-sized records) clause, multiple record
defines for FD entries, sequence of source programs, have been added to
the compiler.


Please report any bugs and/or problems to the TinyCOBOL mailing list. 


1) TinyCOBOL Home Page
   http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/

2) iBiblio (formerly Metalab)
   http://www.ibiblio.org/pub/Linux/devel/lang/cobol/
```

### Cutting accepted string

- **From:** "Jarkko K���hk���nen" <jarkko.kahkonen@softageneraattori.fi>
- **Date:** 2001-04-25T10:13:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<grxF6.64$DR.4119@read2.inet.fi>`

```
Hiallo. I made a program which asks your name and prints string into screen.
Please read and help.

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. ACCPT01.

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

DATA DIVISION.
    WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01    THE-NAME                                PIC X(30).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

    PROGRAM-BEGIN.
        DISPLAY "ENTER YOUR NAME:".
        ACCEPT THE-NAME.
        DISPLAY "HIALLO, " THE-NAME ".".
    PROGRAM-DONE.
        STOP RUN.

This compiled correctly and MUST work a way like this (but doesn't work):

ENTER YOUR NAME:
Jake
HIALLO, Jake.

But when I run this I must write 30 characters long name that I program can
continue running, why's that?
THE-NAME -string must cut or something? Or I must add some option to
ACCEPT-clause?

Hope you understood, gimme help!

Jake
```

#### ↳ Re: Cutting accepted string

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-04-25T12:13:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mbzF6.286$OC1.35073@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<grxF6.64$DR.4119@read2.inet.fi>`

```
COBOL will always DISPLAY the full Working-Storage length of a dataname.

If you wish to truncate the name, you have to find the end of the meaningful
text yourself and only disply that portion of the dataname.

Since this looks like homework, all you get is a hint: Look up the INSPECT
verb with the TALLYING option; then look up "reference modification."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cutting accepted string

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-04-26T08:17:26+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AE7CB86.659B25BC@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<grxF6.64$DR.4119@read2.inet.fi> <mbzF6.286$OC1.35073@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> COBOL will always DISPLAY the full Working-Storage length of a dataname.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> verb with the TALLYING option; then look up "reference modification."

Alternately, make up the reply in another W-S item using STRING and use
DELIMITED BY to determine the end of each part as the reply is built up.

To handle multiple parts in a name (such as 'Jake Smith') you may need
to use 2 spaces as a terminator so that a single space doesn't terminate
the inspect tallying or string, or you could use an UNSTRING DELIMITED
BY ALL SPACES repeatedly to get each word separately and then STRING
these back together adding a single space between each.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cutting accepted string

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-04-25T23:47:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ae760c4.167502175@news>`
- **References:** `<grxF6.64$DR.4119@read2.inet.fi> <mbzF6.286$OC1.35073@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3AE7CB86.659B25BC@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
On Thu, 26 Apr 2001 08:17:26 +0100, Richard Plinston
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Michael Mattias wrote:
>> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>these back together adding a single space between each.
========
Or, you can do it manually like this (not guaranteed to compile
precisely as written, but it's a neat trick):

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER
  VARYING CHAR-COUNTER FROM LENGTH OF NAME-FIELD BY -1
  UNTIL NAME-FIELD (CHAR-COUNTER:1) NOT = SPACE AND LOW-VALUE
      OR CHAR-COUNTER < 2
CONTINUE
END-PERFORM

Now, CHAR-COUNTER is the number of characters in the field before the
trailing space(s).  Even if the text string contains  embedded spaces.


You need to deal with the special case of an empty field...

So, then you just:

DISPLAY NAME-FIELD (1:CHAR-COUNTER)

which will display only the number of characters you want to display.

You will probably want to limit test (e.g., verify that it works with
strings of length zero, 1, and field-length) because lots of code
looks good but breaks when you force it to minimum/maximum values.

There are many ways to manipulate strings in COBOL; you can use
INSPECT, STRING, UNSTRING verbs; all of them have their place, and
unfortunately, too many COBOL guys are unfamiliar with them.  

'Course you could say that about a bunch of verbs, I guess.

Anyway, you now have enough hints to see you through.

Good Luck!  (I hate it when people end with that phrase!)
Kevin


Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cutting accepted string

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** JD <pb@dazoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-26T17:53:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AE898EA.C33ED3F5@dazoo.com>`
- **References:** `<grxF6.64$DR.4119@read2.inet.fi> <mbzF6.286$OC1.35073@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3AE7CB86.659B25BC@Azonic.co.nz> <3ae760c4.167502175@news>`

```
If your compiler supports the REVERSE function, you could also do this:

INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE(NAM-LAST)
    TALLYING TRAILING-SPACES FOR LEADING SPACES

COMPUTE STRING-LENGTH = LENGTH OF NAM-LAST - TRAILING-SPACES

DISPLAY NAM-LAST(1:STRING-LENGTH)
```

#### ↳ Re: Network problem with RM/Cobol 85

- **From:** "John Bradley" <k5mwe@austin.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-25T13:34:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LnAF6.199067$wx.28325338@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<9c69q2$jd6$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```
Hi,

Your problem is likely the round-trip delay in the dial-up connections, or
the data rate, or both.  When you map a drive in this way over a WAN, every
file operation must be sent to the server, and a reply returned.  One COBOL
file operation can result in dozens of low-level file operations that must
traverse the net.

There are a few things that you should look at if you haven't already.  (for
files that are on the server and used by the PC's)  Be sure that you are
opening the files in the most restrictive mode possible for your
application.  In other words, if you can open a file for exclusive use, do
that.  If you only input from a file, open it for input.  Only when
necessary open the file input/output for shared use.  There are also runtime
configuration parameters that affect WAN performance (sometimes
dramatically).

In spite of this, you will probably find that your network configuration is
inherently slow.  You can try using the RM/InfoExpress server product on the
Windows 2000 server.  This will reduce the network traffic to exactly one
round-trip per COBOL file operation.  For write-intensive apps this usually
results in dramatic improvements.  For read-intensive apps, it has less
effect, and might even slow things down.

For much more detail, especially for the configuration issues, check the
RM/COBOL Users' Guide and contact Liant support wherever you are. (probably
the distributor in your country, or the London office, based on your email
address).  To find out who to contact, go to www.liant.com.

Good luck,

John

"�������� �������" <dksoft@otenet.gr> wrote in message
news:9c69q2$jd6$1@usenet.otenet.gr...
> I'm having a computer with Windows 2000 Server and runtime of  RM/Cobol85
> for 10 users.
> Also having 10 Pc's With Windows 95,Windows 98.
> Routers connect Pc's to Server with dial-up Connection.When I'm running
the
> application i wrote in RM/Cobol85 from Pc's with mapping drive on server
i'm
> having slow speed of application.How can make my application to get more
> speed???.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Network problem with RM/Cobol 85

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-04-29T01:56:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aeb749c.172395392@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<9c69q2$jd6$1@usenet.otenet.gr> <LnAF6.199067$wx.28325338@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Another option that will make the applications nearly as fast as local
ones (likely just as fast) is to use the Flexus Thin Client with COBOL
sp2.  http://www.flexus.com.  This allows you to run the interface
only on the client machine, while the application runs on the server.
It works with RM COBOL too!


On Wed, 25 Apr 2001 13:34:35 GMT, "John Bradley" <k5mwe@austin.rr.com>
wrote:

>Hi,
>
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** "John Bradley" <k5mwe@austin.rr.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

<a id="t-167f753c80"></a>
## Thread: help designing record layout — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help designing record layout

- **From:** goble@gtech (David. E. Goble)
- **Date:** 2001-04-25T11:53:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ae6bab4.9524547@news.adelaide.on.net>`

```
Hi all;

I have a problem. I need to design a record layout about a score
sheet. The score sheet has upto 4 stages, each stage has upto 8 series
and each series has upto 10 scores. Each score is out of 10

I need this info about the score sheets, so I can calculate the max
score possible for each stage. So that when the user enters the score
from each stage, I can validate if the score is too high.

An example;

A stage with 1 series and 5 scores has a possible max score of 50

The most complicated score sheet will have 4 stages, between 2 to 8
series and from 2 to 6 scores.

Has anyone any ideas? Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: help designing record layout

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-04-25T08:45:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AE6C701.433D07CF@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<3ae6bab4.9524547@news.adelaide.on.net>`

```
"David. E. Goble" wrote:
> 
> Hi all;
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Has anyone any ideas? Thanks.

Looks like you need a 3-dimensional table like:

01  SCORE-SHEET.
    05  STAGE		OCCURS 4 TIMES.
	10  SERIES	OCCURS 8 TIMES.
	    15  SCORES  OCCURS 10 TIMES  PIC S9(4).
```

- **From:** goble@gtech (David. E. Goble)
- **Date:** 2001-04-28T08:09:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aea0cb6.91541771@news.adelaide.on.net>`
- **References:** `<3ae6bab4.9524547@news.adelaide.on.net> <3AE6C701.433D07CF@yahoo.com>`

```
Hi Joe;

Yes, that would work, but it will have a lot of unused elements.

I have the following score sheets;

match		stages	series	shots
Center fire	  2	 12	5
Sport pistol	  2	 12	5
Rapid fire	  2	 12	5
Standard	  3	 12	5
Airpistol men	  1	  6	10
Airpistol women	  1       4	10
Free pistol	  2	  6	10
Blackpowder 25	  2	  8	5
Service		  4	 18	variable max 6 min 2

I ended up just using the number of stages and storing the max score
per stage.

But, I want to later have a program to enter the scores for each
individual shot. This will need the above info. 

ie for service

1	1	6
	2	6

	4	6

	5	3

	2	3
	3	6

	2	6
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Jake.
```

#### ↳ Re: Natural Cobol

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-04-25T21:46:50+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SlcQPQA6ez56EwoF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<LszF6.92$DR.5994@read2.inet.fi>`

```
I have not heard of Natural Cobol but you may mean the Software AG
product Natural which can be used against DB2, DL/I, Adabas and VSAM
files. Natural is a 4GL which is similar in syntax to cobol but has it's
own idiosyncratic editors, one of which is modelled on ISPF. Try looking
at Software AG's websites.

In article <LszF6.92$DR.5994@read2.inet.fi>, Jarkko Kï¿½hkï¿½nen <jarkko.kah
konen@softageneraattori.fi> writes
```
